cases.title: Chest pressure when climbing hills
cases.chiefComplaint: I get a tight band across my chest whenever I walk uphill, and it goes away when I stop.
cases.history: A 61-year-old man presents with 3 months of substernal chest pressure that comes on reliably after walking about two blocks uphill or climbing two flights of stairs. He describes a 'tight band' across the chest radiating to the left shoulder, lasting 3 to 5 minutes and resolving completely within a few minutes of rest. The episodes have not changed in frequency, duration, or threshold over the past month, and he has never had pain at rest or at night. He denies dyspnea at rest, syncope, palpitations, orthopnea, and leg swelling. His history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia. Medications are lisinopril 20 mg daily and atorvastatin 20 mg daily, though he admits to missing the statin several times a week. He has no drug allergies. He smokes half a pack per day with a 30 pack-year total history and drinks two beers on weekends. His father had a myocardial infarction at age 58.
cases.exam: Comfortable at rest, in no distress. Jugular venous pressure is not elevated. Heart is regular in rate and rhythm with normal S1 and S2; no murmurs, rubs, or gallops. Lungs are clear to auscultation bilaterally. No carotid or femoral bruits. Abdomen is soft without a pulsatile mass. Peripheral pulses are 2+ and symmetric, and there is no lower-extremity edema. The chest wall is nontender, and the pain is not reproduced by palpation, arm movement, or deep inspiration.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which of the following is the most appropriate first test to obtain in the clinic today?
options: Invasive coronary angiography
Resting 12-lead ECG
Stat troponin and immediate emergency department referral
Transthoracic echocardiogram
Coronary artery calcium scoring
stages.explanation: Every patient with suspected stable angina should first have a resting 12-lead ECG. It is inexpensive, may reveal prior infarction or conduction disease, and determines whether the baseline tracing is interpretable for exercise stress testing — which shapes the entire downstream workup. Invasive angiography is premature before noninvasive risk stratification in a patient with stable, predictable symptoms. Coronary calcium scoring is a screening tool for asymptomatic patients, not a diagnostic test for symptomatic ones. A stat troponin and ED referral are for acute coronary syndrome; his symptoms are exertional, reproducible, and fully relieved by rest, with no rest pain or crescendo pattern. Echocardiography is useful when heart failure, a murmur, or valvular disease is suspected, none of which applies here.

stages.label: BASELINE DATA
stages.content:
Resting 12-lead ECG: normal sinus rhythm at 74 bpm, normal axis, no Q waves, no ST-segment or T-wave abnormalities
Hemoglobin 14.6 g/dL
Creatinine 0.9 mg/dL (eGFR >60 mL/min/1.73 m²)
Fasting glucose 101 mg/dL, HbA1c 5.8%
Lipid panel: LDL-C 128 mg/dL, HDL-C 41 mg/dL, triglycerides 165 mg/dL
TSH 1.9 µIU/mL

stages.label: DIAGNOSTIC TEST
stages.question: He walks daily without orthopedic limitation, and his resting ECG is normal. Which is the most appropriate stress test to evaluate his chest pressure?
options: Adenosine myocardial perfusion imaging
Exercise treadmill ECG without imaging
48-hour Holter monitoring
Dobutamine stress echocardiography
Invasive coronary angiography
stages.explanation: When a patient can exercise adequately and the baseline ECG is interpretable (no LBBB, paced rhythm, pre-excitation, LVH with strain, or >1 mm resting ST depression), exercise treadmill ECG is the initial stress test of choice — it is functional, inexpensive, and provides prognostic data such as exercise capacity and the Duke treadmill score. Adenosine perfusion imaging is a pharmacologic stress option reserved for patients who cannot exercise. Dobutamine echocardiography is likewise for patients unable to exercise who need an imaging-based test. Invasive angiography is not a first-line tool for stable, low-to-intermediate risk chest pain. Holter monitoring detects arrhythmias and has no role in diagnosing exertional ischemia.

stages.label: STRESS TEST RESULTS
stages.content:
Exercise treadmill test (Bruce protocol): exercised 7 minutes, achieving 8.5 METs
At peak exercise: 1.5 mm horizontal ST-segment depression in leads V4–V6, resolving within 3 minutes of recovery
Typical chest pressure reproduced at peak exercise, resolving with rest
Blood pressure rose appropriately with exercise; no arrhythmias, no ST elevation
Duke treadmill score: intermediate risk

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which of the following best characterizes this patient's condition?
options: Vasospastic (Prinzmetal) angina as the primary diagnosis
Unstable angina as the primary diagnosis
Chronic stable angina due to obstructive coronary artery disease
Severe aortic stenosis with demand ischemia in this clinical context
Gastroesophageal reflux disease as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: He has chronic stable angina: a fixed, predictable exertional threshold for over 3 months, complete relief with rest within minutes, and a stress test reproducing his symptoms with ischemic ST depression — the classic picture of flow-limiting coronary atherosclerosis. Unstable angina requires rest pain, new-onset severe angina, or a crescendo pattern with decreasing threshold, none of which he reports. Vasospastic angina causes episodic rest pain, often at night, with transient ST elevation rather than exertional ST depression. Reflux disease does not produce a reproducible exertional threshold or ischemic ECG changes during exercise. Severe aortic stenosis can cause exertional angina, but his exam shows no systolic murmur, making it effectively excluded.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: In addition to daily aspirin and intensifying his statin to high-intensity dosing, which is the most appropriate initial antianginal regimen?
options: Combination diltiazem and verapamil as initial therapy
Ranolazine daily as initial therapy
Long-acting nitrate monotherapy as initial therapy
Immediate referral for percutaneous coronary intervention
Metoprolol succinate daily plus sublingual nitroglycerin
stages.explanation: Beta-blockers are first-line antianginal therapy in stable angina: they lower heart rate, contractility, and wall stress, raising the exertional threshold, and sublingual nitroglycerin provides on-demand relief and is given to every angina patient. Ranolazine is an add-on agent when symptoms persist despite first-line drugs, not initial monotherapy. Long-acting nitrate monotherapy is limited by tolerance, requires a nitrate-free interval, and offers no mortality benefit. Routine upfront PCI does not reduce death or myocardial infarction compared with optimal medical therapy in stable coronary disease (COURAGE, ISCHEMIA); revascularization is reserved for refractory symptoms or high-risk anatomy. Combining two nondihydropyridine calcium channel blockers is irrational and risks bradycardia and heart block.

cases.diagnosis: Chronic stable angina due to obstructive coronary artery disease
pearls: Exercise treadmill ECG is the stress test of choice when the patient can exercise and the baseline ECG is interpretable.
Beta-blockers are first-line antianginal therapy; sublingual nitroglycerin covers acute episodes.
In stable angina, optimal medical therapy is as good as upfront revascularization for preventing MI and death (COURAGE, ISCHEMIA).
Rest pain, a crescendo pattern, or new-onset severe angina redefines the syndrome as unstable angina and warrants emergency evaluation.

cases.title: High readings at the pharmacy kiosk
cases.chiefComplaint: The blood pressure machine at the pharmacy keeps saying my numbers are high, but I feel fine.
cases.history: A 47-year-old man presents after a pharmacy kiosk recorded blood pressures of 150/94 and 148/92 mmHg on two occasions over the past month. He feels entirely well, with no headaches, chest pain, dyspnea, palpitations, or visual changes. A reading at a work health fair three months ago was 142/88 mmHg. In the office today his blood pressure is 146/92 mmHg, and 144/90 mmHg when repeated after five minutes of quiet rest with proper cuff technique. He has no known medical history and takes no prescription medications, NSAIDs, decongestants, or stimulants. He works a sedentary desk job, eats takeout most days, and drinks two to three beers most evenings. He has never smoked. His body mass index is 31 kg/m². He denies loud snoring, witnessed apneas, episodic flushing, sweating attacks, or muscle weakness. Family history is notable for hypertension in both parents and a stroke in his father at age 70. He has no drug allergies.
cases.exam: Obese man in no distress. Fundoscopic exam shows no arteriovenous nicking, hemorrhages, or papilledema. Heart is regular with normal S1 and S2; no S4 gallop and no murmurs. Lungs are clear bilaterally. Abdomen is soft and obese with no abdominal or flank bruits. Radial and femoral pulses are full and symmetric without radio-femoral delay. There is no peripheral edema. No moon facies, abdominal striae, or thyromegaly. Blood pressure is similar in both arms.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which of the following is the most appropriate next step to establish the diagnosis?
options: Plasma free metanephrines and renal artery Doppler
24-hour ambulatory blood pressure monitoring
Start lisinopril today without further testing
Transthoracic echocardiogram in this clinical context
Repeat office measurement in 12 months
stages.explanation: Guidelines recommend confirming elevated office readings with out-of-office measurement — 24-hour ambulatory monitoring is the reference standard, with serial home readings an acceptable alternative — before committing a patient to lifelong therapy. This excludes white-coat hypertension, which affects up to a quarter of patients with elevated office readings. Starting lisinopril immediately skips confirmation, although treatment will likely be needed. Waiting 12 months to recheck stage 2-range readings is inappropriate delay. Screening for pheochromocytoma or renovascular disease is reserved for suggestive features such as paroxysmal symptoms, hypokalemia, resistant hypertension, abdominal bruit, or very young onset — none present. Echocardiography can assess for end-organ effects like LVH but does not establish the diagnosis of hypertension itself.

stages.label: AMBULATORY MONITORING & LABS
stages.content:
24-hour ambulatory BP monitoring: mean 24-hour BP 138/86 mmHg (daytime mean 142/88 mmHg, nighttime mean 129/79 mmHg)
Basic metabolic panel: Na 139 mEq/L, K 4.1 mEq/L, creatinine 0.9 mg/dL
Fasting glucose 99 mg/dL, HbA1c 5.6%
Lipid panel: LDL-C 118 mg/dL, HDL-C 44 mg/dL, triglycerides 150 mg/dL
Urinalysis: no protein, no blood
12-lead ECG: normal sinus rhythm, no left ventricular hypertrophy criteria
TSH 2.3 µIU/mL

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which of the following is the most accurate diagnosis?
options: Masked hypertension
Primary hypertension
Renovascular hypertension
Primary hyperaldosteronism
White-coat hypertension
stages.explanation: His blood pressure is elevated both in the office and on ambulatory monitoring (mean 24-hour 138/86 mmHg exceeds the ambulatory threshold of 125/75–130/80 mmHg), confirming sustained hypertension. With onset in middle age, a strong family history, obesity, high sodium intake, regular alcohol use, normal potassium, normal creatinine, and no suggestive features, this is primary (essential) hypertension — the cause in over 90% of adults. White-coat hypertension requires normal out-of-office readings, which he does not have. Masked hypertension is the reverse pattern: normal office readings with elevated ambulatory values. Primary hyperaldosteronism typically shows hypokalemia or resistant hypertension. Renovascular disease is suggested by abrupt or resistant hypertension, flash pulmonary edema, an abdominal bruit, or a creatinine rise after ACE inhibition — none of which are present.

stages.label: LIFESTYLE COUNSELING
stages.question: Which single lifestyle intervention is expected to produce the largest reduction in his blood pressure?
options: Daily fish-oil supplementation
Stopping caffeine entirely
Adopting the DASH diet
Stress-reduction breathing exercises
Switching from beer to red wine
stages.explanation: Of the listed options, the DASH dietary pattern — rich in fruits, vegetables, and low-fat dairy with reduced saturated fat — produces the largest expected systolic reduction, roughly 8 to 11 mmHg, rivaled only by substantial weight loss (about 1 mmHg per kg lost). Sodium restriction and moderating alcohol to no more than two drinks daily each add several mmHg and should also be advised given his takeout-heavy diet and nightly beers. Stopping caffeine causes only small, transient changes in habitual users. Fish-oil supplementation has a minimal effect at usual doses. Structured breathing exercises produce modest, inconsistent reductions. Simply switching the type of alcoholic beverage does not lower blood pressure — total alcohol quantity is what matters.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Along with lifestyle modification, which of the following is the most appropriate initial antihypertensive agent?
options: Clonidine as initial therapy
Chlorthalidone
Metoprolol tartrate as monotherapy
Furosemide as initial therapy
Doxazosin as initial therapy
stages.explanation: First-line antihypertensives are thiazide-type diuretics, ACE inhibitors, ARBs, and dihydropyridine calcium channel blockers. Chlorthalidone is the only first-line agent listed; it is long-acting and reduced cardiovascular events in ALLHAT, making it an excellent choice for a patient with stage 2 office readings and confirmed ambulatory hypertension. Beta-blockers such as metoprolol are no longer first-line for uncomplicated hypertension because they prevent stroke and cardiovascular events less effectively, and he has no compelling indication such as coronary disease or heart failure. Clonidine is a last-resort central agent with sedation and rebound hypertension on withdrawal. Doxazosin was inferior to chlorthalidone in ALLHAT, with more heart failure. Furosemide is a short-acting loop diuretic used for volume overload or advanced CKD, not routine hypertension.

cases.diagnosis: Primary (essential) hypertension, confirmed by ambulatory blood pressure monitoring
pearls: Confirm elevated office readings with ambulatory or home BP monitoring before diagnosing hypertension — white-coat hypertension is common.
First-line drug classes are thiazides, ACE inhibitors, ARBs, and dihydropyridine calcium channel blockers; beta-blockers are not first-line without a compelling indication.
The DASH diet and weight loss produce the largest nonpharmacologic BP reductions (~8–11 mmHg systolic).
Screen for secondary hypertension only when red flags exist: young onset, resistant hypertension, hypokalemia, bruits, or paroxysmal symptoms.

cases.title: An abnormal cholesterol panel at a wellness visit
cases.chiefComplaint: My screening blood work came back with high cholesterol and I want to know if I need medication.
cases.history: A 52-year-old woman presents for an annual wellness visit. She feels well, with no chest pain, dyspnea, claudication, or neurologic symptoms. A screening fasting lipid panel drawn last week showed total cholesterol 242 mg/dL, LDL-C 158 mg/dL, HDL-C 46 mg/dL, and triglycerides 170 mg/dL. Her history includes hypertension, well controlled on lisinopril 10 mg daily. She has smoked half a pack of cigarettes daily for 25 years and has been unable to quit despite two attempts. She does not have diabetes. She went through menopause at age 50 and takes no hormone therapy. She drinks a glass of wine once or twice a week and exercises rarely. Her body mass index is 29 kg/m². Family history includes a stroke in her mother at age 72; there is no premature coronary disease or known familial cholesterol disorder. She has never taken a lipid-lowering medication, has no muscle complaints, and has no drug allergies.
cases.exam: Well-appearing woman in no distress. There are no tendon xanthomas, eruptive xanthomas, or xanthelasma, and no premature corneal arcus. Thyroid is normal in size without nodules. Heart is regular with normal S1 and S2 and no murmurs. Lungs are clear. Abdomen is soft and nontender without hepatomegaly. No carotid, abdominal, or femoral bruits. Distal pulses are 2+ and symmetric, and there is no peripheral edema. Skin and deep tendon reflexes are normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in evaluating her elevated cholesterol?
options: Begin high-intensity statin therapy without further evaluation
Order invasive coronary angiography as the first step today
Calculate her 10-year ASCVD risk using the pooled cohort equations
Start ezetimibe monotherapy as the first step today
Repeat the lipid panel in 5 years as the first step today
stages.explanation: For primary prevention in adults aged 40–75 with LDL-C between 70 and 189 mg/dL and no diabetes, the statin decision is anchored to the estimated 10-year ASCVD risk from the pooled cohort equations, which integrate age, sex, blood pressure, cholesterol, smoking, and diabetes. Secondary causes (hypothyroidism, diabetes, liver disease) should be excluded at the same time. High-intensity statin without risk calculation is reserved for clinical ASCVD or LDL-C ≥190 mg/dL; her LDL of 158 mg/dL does not automatically qualify. Repeating the panel in 5 years ignores an actionable result in a smoker with hypertension. Ezetimibe is an add-on agent, not initial monotherapy. Invasive angiography has no role in an asymptomatic patient.

stages.label: RISK CALCULATION & LABS
stages.content:
10-year ASCVD risk (pooled cohort equations): 12.4% — intermediate risk (7.5–19.9%)
HbA1c 5.5%
TSH 1.6 µIU/mL
AST 22 U/L, ALT 25 U/L
Creatinine 0.8 mg/dL
Confirmed fasting lipid panel: LDL-C 158 mg/dL, HDL-C 46 mg/dL, triglycerides 170 mg/dL

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Based on her 10-year ASCVD risk of 12.4%, which of the following is the most appropriate management?
options: Fenofibrate as initial therapy
Moderate-intensity statin therapy
PCSK9 inhibitor therapy as initial therapy
Lifestyle changes alone, with repeat lipid panel in 5 years
Ezetimibe monotherapy as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Her risk of 12.4% places her in the intermediate-risk category (7.5–19.9%), for which guidelines recommend a moderate-intensity statin — such as atorvastatin 20 mg or rosuvastatin 10 mg — targeting a 30–49% LDL-C reduction, alongside lifestyle change and aggressive smoking-cessation support. Her active smoking and hypertension are risk enhancers that strengthen the case for treatment. Lifestyle alone is insufficient at this risk level and delays proven event reduction. PCSK9 inhibitors are reserved for clinical ASCVD or familial hypercholesterolemia inadequately controlled on maximal statin therapy. Fenofibrate is used mainly for triglycerides ≥500 mg/dL to prevent pancreatitis, not for LDL lowering. Ezetimibe is an adjunct when statins are not tolerated or LDL goals are unmet, not first-line monotherapy.

stages.label: SHARED DECISION
stages.question: She is hesitant about taking a daily medication for life. Which test is most appropriate to refine her risk estimate and inform the decision?
options: Coronary artery calcium (CAC) scoring
Exercise stress echocardiography
High-sensitivity troponin in this setting
Carotid intima-media thickness measurement
Resting transthoracic echocardiogram
stages.explanation: When the statin decision is uncertain in intermediate-risk patients, guidelines endorse coronary artery calcium scoring as the best tiebreaker. A CAC score of 100 or more, or at or above the 75th percentile for age and sex, favors starting a statin, while a score of zero generally supports deferral — with the important caveat that deferral does not apply to active smokers, patients with diabetes, or those with a strong family history, so her smoking already tempers a zero result. Stress echocardiography detects flow-limiting ischemia, not subclinical atherosclerosis, and is inappropriate in an asymptomatic patient. Carotid intima-media thickness is no longer recommended for routine risk refinement. High-sensitivity troponin is not a guideline tool for statin decisions. A resting echocardiogram evaluates structure and function, not atherosclerotic burden.

stages.label: CAC RESULT
stages.content:
Coronary artery calcium score: 142 Agatston units (82nd percentile for age and sex)
After reviewing the result, the patient agrees to start atorvastatin 20 mg daily and sets a quit date for smoking with varenicline support

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP
stages.question: Which of the following is the most appropriate monitoring plan after starting the statin?
options: Repeat fasting lipid panel in 4–12 weeks to assess response
Monthly liver function tests for the first year in this setting
Routine creatine kinase every 3 months before discharge planning
No follow-up lipid testing is needed once a statin is started
Repeat lipid panel in 5 years as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: A repeat lipid panel 4–12 weeks after statin initiation confirms the expected 30–49% LDL-C reduction for a moderate-intensity agent and serves as the single best adherence check; thereafter, testing every 3–12 months is reasonable. Baseline transaminases are recommended before starting, but routine serial liver function tests are not — they are checked only if symptoms such as fatigue, anorexia, or jaundice develop, since serious hepatotoxicity is rare. Routine creatine kinase monitoring is likewise not recommended in asymptomatic patients; CK is reserved for those with muscle pain or weakness. Skipping follow-up testing forfeits the chance to detect nonadherence or inadequate response. Waiting 5 years defeats the purpose of confirming treatment effect.

cases.diagnosis: Primary hyperlipidemia warranting moderate-intensity statin therapy for intermediate 10-year ASCVD risk
pearls: For primary prevention (age 40–75, LDL 70–189, no diabetes), the statin decision is driven by the pooled cohort equations: intermediate risk (7.5–19.9%) merits a moderate-intensity statin.
Coronary artery calcium scoring is the guideline-endorsed tiebreaker when the statin decision is uncertain — but CAC 0 does not justify deferral in smokers or diabetics.
Automatic high-intensity statin indications: clinical ASCVD or LDL-C ≥190 mg/dL.
Recheck lipids 4–12 weeks after starting a statin; do not order routine CK or serial LFTs in asymptomatic patients.

cases.title: Passed out during a blood draw
cases.chiefComplaint: I fainted while they were drawing my blood yesterday and my mom wants to make sure my heart is okay.
cases.history: A 19-year-old college student is seen the day after fainting during a routine blood draw at student health. While the phlebotomist was filling the second tube, she felt suddenly warm and nauseated, heard ringing in her ears, became sweaty, and her vision 'closed in like a tunnel' before she slumped over in the chair. Staff reported she was pale and unresponsive for roughly 20–30 seconds, with a few brief jerks of her arms, then woke immediately and knew where she was. There was no tongue biting, no incontinence, and no confusion afterward. She had skipped breakfast because the draw was fasting. At age 16 she nearly fainted while watching a friend get a vaccination. She has never had syncope during exercise, palpitations preceding symptoms, or chest pain. She takes no medications, has no medical history, and does not use alcohol or drugs. Family history is negative for sudden cardiac death, congenital deafness, or epilepsy. Her last menstrual period was two weeks ago.
cases.exam: Well-appearing young woman in no distress. Heart is regular in rate and rhythm with normal S1 and S2; no murmurs at rest or with standing and Valsalva. No carotid bruits. Lungs are clear bilaterally. Neurologic exam is fully intact: cranial nerves, strength, sensation, reflexes, coordination, and gait are all normal. The tongue shows no lacerations, and there are no bruises or signs of injury from the episode. Mucous membranes are moist.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Beyond a careful history and physical examination, which test should be obtained in essentially every patient presenting after syncope?
options: Transthoracic echocardiogram
12-lead ECG
CT scan of the head
48-hour Holter monitor
Electroencephalogram
stages.explanation: History, physical examination with orthostatic vital signs, and a 12-lead ECG constitute the core evaluation of syncope in every patient. The ECG is inexpensive and screens for the dangerous mimics of benign syncope: long QT syndrome, Brugada pattern, Wolff-Parkinson-White pre-excitation, hypertrophic cardiomyopathy voltage criteria, arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy (epsilon waves), and conduction disease. Head CT has essentially no yield in syncope without head trauma or focal neurologic findings. An EEG is for suspected seizure, which her immediate recovery without confusion argues strongly against. Echocardiography is reserved for an abnormal cardiac exam, abnormal ECG, or exertional symptoms. Prolonged ambulatory monitoring is for suspected arrhythmic syncope — typically sudden collapse without prodrome — not a classic reflex faint.

stages.label: ECG & BEDSIDE DATA
stages.content:
12-lead ECG: normal sinus rhythm at 72 bpm, PR 150 ms, QRS 86 ms, QTc 408 ms, no delta wave, no epsilon wave, no Brugada pattern, normal voltages
Orthostatic vitals: supine 110/70 mmHg, HR 72 → standing 3 min 106/68 mmHg, HR 80 (no significant drop)
Point-of-care glucose: 88 mg/dL
Hemoglobin 13.4 g/dL
Urine hCG: negative

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Orthostatic hypotension
Generalized tonic-clonic seizure
Congenital long QT syndrome with arrhythmic syncope
Hypoglycemia as the primary diagnosis
Vasovagal (reflex) syncope
stages.explanation: She has textbook vasovagal syncope: a recognized emotional/noxious trigger (venipuncture), a classic prodrome of warmth, nausea, diaphoresis, and tunnel vision, brief loss of consciousness, and immediate, complete recovery — plus a prior near-faint with a similar trigger. The few brief myoclonic jerks represent convulsive syncope from transient cerebral hypoperfusion, a common and benign finding often mistaken for seizure. A true generalized seizure typically causes a sustained tonic-clonic phase, tongue biting, incontinence, and postictal confusion, all absent here. Orthostatic hypotension is excluded by normal orthostatic vitals and by the fact that she was seated when she fainted. Long QT syndrome is unlikely with a QTc of 408 ms, no exertional or startle-triggered events, and no family history. Her normal glucose excludes hypoglycemia.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in her workup?
options: MRI of the brain with EEG in this setting
30-day external event monitor in this setting
Tilt-table testing as the immediate next step
No further testing
Transthoracic echocardiogram in this setting
stages.explanation: A classic vasovagal story with a normal physical examination, normal ECG, and normal orthostatic vitals requires no additional testing — the diagnosis is clinical, the prognosis is excellent, and further workup adds cost and false positives without benefit. Reassurance that this is common, benign, and manageable is itself therapeutic. Tilt-table testing is reserved for recurrent unexplained syncope when the diagnosis remains uncertain; it neither confirms nor excludes vasovagal syncope reliably enough to be routine. Echocardiography is indicated for murmurs, abnormal ECGs, or exertional syncope. Brain MRI and EEG address seizure or structural neurologic disease, which the history excludes. An event monitor is for suspected arrhythmia — abrupt syncope without prodrome, palpitation-triggered events, or high-risk features, none of which she has.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which of the following is the most appropriate management recommendation?
options: Daily midodrine as initial therapy
Metoprolol succinate as initial therapy
Permanent pacemaker placement as initial therapy
Education on hydration, trigger awareness,
Fludrocortisone as initial therapy
stages.explanation: First-line management of vasovagal syncope is education: recognize the prodrome, immediately sit or lie down with legs elevated, maintain generous fluid and salt intake, and use physical counterpressure maneuvers — leg crossing with muscle tensing, handgrip, and arm tensing — which raise blood pressure and can abort an impending faint. Asking to lie supine for future blood draws addresses her specific trigger directly. Midodrine is reserved for frequent, refractory episodes and requires dosing around supine hypertension. Beta-blockers are ineffective for vasovagal syncope in patients under 40 (POST trial). Fludrocortisone has only modest evidence in selected recurrent cases and is not first-line for a single typical episode. Pacemakers are considered only for older patients with recurrent syncope and a documented cardioinhibitory (asystolic) mechanism.

cases.diagnosis: Vasovagal (reflex) syncope triggered by venipuncture
pearls: Brief myoclonic jerks during a faint are common (convulsive syncope) and do not mean seizure — absence of postictal confusion is the key discriminator.
Every syncope patient gets a history, exam, orthostatic vitals, and a 12-lead ECG; classic vasovagal syncope with a normal ECG needs nothing more.
Physical counterpressure maneuvers (leg crossing, handgrip, muscle tensing) can abort an impending vasovagal faint.
Red flags that demand further workup: exertional syncope, no prodrome, syncope while supine, palpitations before the event, abnormal ECG, or family history of sudden death.

cases.title: Two falls and morning dizziness
cases.chiefComplaint: Daughter reports: 'Mom has fallen twice this month, always right after she stands up.'
cases.history: An 82-year-old woman is brought in by her daughter after two falls in the past month, both occurring within a minute of standing — once getting out of bed in the morning and once rising from the couch after lunch. She describes lightheadedness and 'graying out' of her vision that resolves when she sits back down. There was no loss of consciousness with either fall, no palpitations, no chest pain, no vertigo with rolling over in bed, and no focal weakness or speech difficulty. Symptoms are worst in the morning and after meals. Her history includes hypertension, insomnia, hyperlipidemia, and osteoarthritis. Medications: lisinopril 40 mg daily, hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily, amitriptyline 25 mg nightly (started two months ago for insomnia), atorvastatin 40 mg nightly, calcium with vitamin D, and acetaminophen as needed. She drinks little fluid in the evening to avoid nocturia and admits her appetite has declined recently. She lives alone, uses no assistive device, and has no drug allergies.
cases.exam: Thin elderly woman, alert and oriented, in no distress. Mucous membranes are slightly dry. Heart is regular with normal S1 and S2; no murmurs or gallops. Lungs are clear. A resolving bruise overlies the left hip; there is no bony tenderness. Neurologic exam shows normal strength, intact sensation, symmetric reflexes, and no nystagmus with positional testing; gait is cautious but steady with normal base. No carotid bruits. There is no peripheral edema, and capillary refill is brisk.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which of the following is the most appropriate initial test in the clinic today?
options: Transthoracic echocardiogram as the first step today
Tilt-table testing as the first step today
Carotid duplex ultrasound as the first step today
Orthostatic vital signs
CT scan of the head as the first step today
stages.explanation: Her story — lightheadedness and visual graying within a minute of standing, worst in the morning and after meals, in an elderly woman on multiple vasoactive drugs — points directly to orthostatic hypotension, and the diagnostic test is simply measuring blood pressure and heart rate supine and then after one and three minutes of standing. It is free, immediate, and definitive. Head CT is for head trauma or focal neurologic deficits, which she lacks. Carotid ultrasound evaluates stroke risk, not positional lightheadedness, and carotid disease does not cause syncope or presyncope on standing. Tilt-table testing is reserved for unexplained recurrent syncope after basic evaluation, not as a first step when bedside orthostatics will answer the question. Echocardiography is indicated for murmurs, exertional symptoms, or heart-failure signs, none of which are present.

stages.label: ORTHOSTATIC VITALS & LABS
stages.content:
Supine (after 5 minutes): BP 148/78 mmHg, HR 70 bpm
Standing 1 minute: BP 122/64 mmHg, HR 76 bpm
Standing 3 minutes: BP 114/60 mmHg, HR 78 bpm — patient reports her typical lightheadedness
Basic metabolic panel: Na 134 mEq/L, K 3.6 mEq/L, creatinine 1.1 mg/dL, BUN 28 mg/dL
Hemoglobin 11.9 g/dL
12-lead ECG: normal sinus rhythm at 72 bpm, no conduction disease, no ischemic changes

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Benign paroxysmal positional vertigo
Medication-induced orthostatic hypotension
Vasovagal syncope as the primary diagnosis
Transient ischemic attacks
Cardiac arrhythmia in this clinical context
stages.explanation: Her systolic pressure falls 34 mmHg by three minutes of standing — far beyond the diagnostic threshold of a 20 mmHg systolic or 10 mmHg diastolic drop — and reproduces her exact symptoms, confirming orthostatic hypotension. The cause is almost certainly her medications: two antihypertensives including a diuretic, plus amitriptyline, an alpha-1-blocking, sedating tricyclic started two months ago, coinciding with symptom onset, all compounded by poor fluid intake (note the elevated BUN/creatinine ratio and borderline sodium). The blunted heart-rate rise reflects age-related baroreflex impairment and drug effects. Vasovagal syncope requires a trigger and prodrome, not a purely positional pattern. BPPV causes true spinning vertigo with head movement and was excluded by positional testing. An arrhythmia would not be reliably positional, and her ECG is normal. TIAs cause focal deficits, not isolated positional lightheadedness.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which of the following is the best initial management step?
options: Start midodrine as initial therapy
Refer for permanent pacemaker placement
Reduce or discontinue the contributing medications
Admit to the hospital for IV fluids and telemetry
Start fludrocortisone as initial therapy
stages.explanation: The first and highest-yield intervention in medication-induced orthostatic hypotension is deprescribing: tapering or stopping the offending agents, which here means addressing the tricyclic and lightening the antihypertensive burden (for example, stopping hydrochlorothiazide and reassessing the lisinopril dose), combined with counseling on slow position changes, morning fluid intake of 1.5–2 liters daily, and rising in stages. Fludrocortisone and midodrine are reserved for persistent neurogenic or refractory orthostatic hypotension after offending drugs are removed — adding pressors on top of causative medications treats the symptom while feeding the cause, and both worsen supine hypertension. She is stable with normal electrolytes and ECG, so hospitalization is unnecessary. There is no bradyarrhythmia or conduction disease, so a pacemaker has no role.

stages.label: DEPRESCRIBING
stages.question: Which of her medications is the highest priority to discontinue?
options: Abruptly stopping all antihypertensives at once
Amitriptyline
Acetaminophen as needed
Calcium with vitamin D
Atorvastatin in this setting
stages.explanation: Amitriptyline is the highest-priority target: tricyclics cause orthostatic hypotension through alpha-1 adrenergic blockade, add anticholinergic effects and sedation that further raise fall risk, and appear on the Beers Criteria as potentially inappropriate in older adults — and it was started two months ago, matching the onset of her falls. Safer insomnia strategies (sleep hygiene, cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia) should replace it. Atorvastatin, acetaminophen, and calcium with vitamin D do not lower standing blood pressure; vitamin D may actually support fall prevention. Stopping every antihypertensive abruptly overshoots — her supine pressure is 148/78, so the antihypertensives should be reduced stepwise (the diuretic is a sensible first cut) with orthostatic vitals rechecked in two to four weeks rather than withdrawn wholesale.

cases.diagnosis: Medication-induced orthostatic hypotension (polypharmacy: tricyclic antidepressant plus antihypertensives) causing recurrent falls
pearls: Diagnose orthostatic hypotension with a ≥20 mmHg systolic or ≥10 mmHg diastolic drop within 3 minutes of standing; symptom reproduction clinches the link to falls.
In older adults with falls, review the medication list first — antihypertensives, diuretics, tricyclics, alpha-blockers, and sedatives are the usual culprits (Beers Criteria).
Deprescribe and use nonpharmacologic measures (slow position changes, hydration, compression stockings) before ever adding midodrine or fludrocortisone.
A blunted compensatory heart-rate rise on standing suggests baroreflex impairment from age, neuropathy, or rate-controlling drugs.

cases.title: Pounding heartbeat since breakfast
cases.chiefComplaint: My heart has been pounding and fluttering since breakfast and I can't make it stop.
cases.history: A 68-year-old man presents to the emergency department with palpitations that began abruptly six hours ago at breakfast. He describes a fluttering, pounding sensation in his chest with mild breathlessness walking in from the parking lot, and he feels fatigued. He has had no chest pain, no syncope or near-syncope, no fever, and no cough. He attended a wedding the night before and drank five or six drinks, considerably more than his usual one or two. He has never had a similar episode. His history includes hypertension and type 2 diabetes mellitus. Medications are amlodipine 5 mg daily and metformin 1,000 mg twice daily; he takes no over-the-counter decongestants or stimulants. He quit smoking 10 years ago after a 20 pack-year history and drinks socially. He has no history of stroke, heart failure, or peripheral vascular disease. Family history is noncontributory, and he has no drug allergies.
cases.exam: Anxious but alert man in no acute distress, speaking in full sentences. Cardiac auscultation reveals an irregularly irregular tachycardic rhythm with variable intensity of S1; no murmurs, rubs, or gallops. The radial pulse is irregular with a pulse deficit compared with the apical rate. Jugular venous pressure is not elevated. Lungs are clear bilaterally without crackles. There is no peripheral edema. The thyroid is normal in size without nodules, and there is no tremor. Extremities are warm and well perfused.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which of the following is the most appropriate first step?
options: Immediate synchronized cardioversion
IV adenosine 6 mg
12-lead ECG
Carotid sinus massage
CT pulmonary angiography
stages.explanation: The rhythm must be defined before it is treated, so a 12-lead ECG is the first step in any stable patient with palpitations and tachycardia; it distinguishes atrial fibrillation from flutter, multifocal atrial tachycardia, and other supraventricular rhythms, and screens for ischemia and pre-excitation. He is hemodynamically stable — normal blood pressure, no chest pain, no pulmonary edema, normal mentation — so immediate synchronized cardioversion is not indicated and would also raise thromboembolic questions. Adenosine terminates AV-node-dependent reentrant tachycardias but will not convert atrial fibrillation and is premature before rhythm diagnosis. CT pulmonary angiography is not warranted without pleuritic pain, hypoxia, or PE risk factors. Carotid sinus massage is a diagnostic vagal maneuver for regular narrow-complex tachycardias, and his pulse is plainly irregular.

stages.label: ECG & LABS
stages.content:
12-lead ECG: irregularly irregular rhythm at 138–152 bpm, no discernible P waves, coarse fibrillatory baseline, narrow QRS, no ST-segment deviation
Troponin I: <0.01 ng/mL
BMP: Na 138 mEq/L, K 4.0 mEq/L, Mg 1.9 mg/dL, creatinine 1.0 mg/dL, glucose 162 mg/dL
TSH 1.4 µIU/mL
CBC: hemoglobin 14.8 g/dL, WBC 7.9 ×10³/µL
Chest X-ray: normal cardiac silhouette, clear lung fields

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which of the following is the most likely rhythm diagnosis?
options: Atrial flutter with 2:1 conduction
AV nodal reentrant tachycardia in this clinical context
Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response
Multifocal atrial tachycardia in this clinical context
Sinus tachycardia with frequent premature atrial contractions
stages.explanation: An irregularly irregular narrow-complex rhythm with no P waves and a fibrillatory baseline is atrial fibrillation, here with a rapid ventricular response near 145 bpm — and the preceding alcohol binge is a classic precipitant ('holiday heart'). Multifocal atrial tachycardia is also irregular but shows at least three distinct P-wave morphologies and occurs mainly in severe pulmonary disease. Atrial flutter with 2:1 conduction produces a regular rate close to 150 bpm with sawtooth flutter waves, not an irregular rhythm without atrial activity. AV nodal reentrant tachycardia is a regular tachycardia with abrupt onset and offset. Sinus tachycardia with premature atrial contractions retains visible sinus P waves and an underlying regular rhythm interrupted by early beats, unlike the continuous irregularity seen here.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which of the following is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: IV amiodarone as initial therapy
IV adenosine as initial therapy
Oral digoxin loading
IV diltiazem for rate control
Immediate synchronized cardioversion
stages.explanation: In hemodynamically stable atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response, the first-line strategy is rate control with an AV-nodal blocker — IV diltiazem or IV metoprolol — which reliably slows conduction and relieves symptoms. Synchronized cardioversion is reserved for instability (hypotension, ischemic chest pain, pulmonary edema, shock) or for an elective rhythm-control strategy after anticoagulation issues are addressed. Adenosine transiently blocks the AV node but lasts seconds and cannot control atrial fibrillation. Amiodarone can pharmacologically cardiovert, which in a patient without established anticoagulation creates avoidable stroke risk; it is reserved for refractory cases or those with decompensated heart failure where diltiazem is undesirable. Digoxin has a slow onset over hours and is an adjunct, mainly useful in hypotensive or heart-failure patients, not first-line monotherapy here.

stages.label: STROKE PREVENTION
stages.question: After IV diltiazem, his rate is 92 bpm and he feels better. What is the most appropriate next step regarding stroke prevention?
options: Start a DOAC based on his CHA2DS2-VASc score of 3
Aspirin plus clopidogrel as the immediate next step
Aspirin 81 mg daily as the immediate next step
Warfarin, but only if rate control fails in this setting
No antithrombotic therapy is needed because the episode lasted under 48 hours
stages.explanation: His CHA2DS2-VASc score is 3 — one point each for hypertension, diabetes, and age 65–74 — which carries an annual stroke risk of roughly 3% and mandates oral anticoagulation; direct oral anticoagulants are preferred over warfarin in nonvalvular atrial fibrillation for equal or better stroke prevention with about half the intracranial hemorrhage. Aspirin alone is no longer recommended for stroke prevention in atrial fibrillation — it adds bleeding risk with minimal protection. Episode duration under 48 hours does not eliminate stroke risk and does not exempt a patient with this score from anticoagulation; even brief or subclinical episodes confer risk. The anticoagulation decision is independent of whether a rate- or rhythm-control strategy is chosen. Dual antiplatelet therapy was inferior to anticoagulation in the ACTIVE-W trial.

cases.diagnosis: New-onset atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response, precipitated by an alcohol binge ('holiday heart')
pearls: An irregularly irregular rhythm with no P waves is atrial fibrillation until proven otherwise.
Stable AF with RVR gets rate control (IV diltiazem or metoprolol); cardioversion is for the unstable patient.
Adenosine does not work in atrial fibrillation — it only blocks the AV node for seconds.
Anticoagulate by CHA2DS2-VASc score, not by symptom duration or rhythm strategy; DOACs are preferred in nonvalvular AF.

cases.title: An irregular pulse found at her checkup
cases.chiefComplaint: The nurse said my pulse felt irregular when she checked my vitals today.
cases.history: A 74-year-old woman presents for her annual examination, where the medical assistant notes an irregular pulse. She reports six months of mild fatigue and slightly reduced stamina on her daily walks, which she attributed to aging, and occasional brief 'fluttering' sensations in her chest. She denies chest pain, syncope, lightheadedness, orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, and leg swelling. She has never been told she has an arrhythmia. Her history includes hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus, and osteoarthritis. Medications are losartan 50 mg daily, metformin 500 mg twice daily, and acetaminophen as needed. She has no history of stroke, transient ischemic attack, heart failure, or bleeding problems, and she has never had a serious fall. She has never smoked and drinks a rare glass of wine. She lives independently, manages her own pillbox reliably, and drives. Her sister has atrial fibrillation. She has no drug allergies.
cases.exam: Well-appearing woman in no distress. Cardiac exam reveals an irregularly irregular rhythm at approximately 88 bpm with variable intensity of S1; no murmurs — specifically no diastolic rumble — and no rubs or gallops. Jugular venous pressure is normal. Lungs are clear bilaterally. There is no peripheral edema and no calf tenderness. The thyroid is normal in size. Neurologic examination is nonfocal with normal speech, strength, and gait. Distal pulses are irregular but full.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which of the following is the most appropriate next step to confirm the rhythm?
options: 12-lead ECG now in the office
Exercise treadmill test
30-day external event monitor
Carotid duplex ultrasound
Immediate emergency department referral for cardioversion
stages.explanation: Her irregular pulse is present right now, so an immediate office 12-lead ECG will capture and define the rhythm — the fastest, cheapest definitive step. Prolonged ambulatory monitoring is for paroxysmal symptoms that are absent at the time of evaluation; it adds nothing when the arrhythmia is ongoing during the visit. Emergency referral for cardioversion is unwarranted: she is hemodynamically stable with a controlled rate of 88 bpm, and an arrhythmia of unknown (likely months-long) duration must not be cardioverted before anticoagulation issues are addressed. An exercise treadmill test evaluates ischemia, not an irregular resting rhythm. Carotid ultrasound assesses atherosclerotic stroke risk and plays no role in diagnosing an arrhythmia.

stages.label: ECG, LABS & ECHO
stages.content:
12-lead ECG: atrial fibrillation with a ventricular rate of 86 bpm, no ST-T abnormalities
TSH 2.0 µIU/mL
CBC: hemoglobin 13.1 g/dL, platelets 240 ×10³/µL
BMP: creatinine 0.9 mg/dL (CrCl ≈ 58 mL/min), K 4.3 mEq/L
AST 24 U/L, ALT 21 U/L
Transthoracic echocardiogram: LVEF 60%, mildly dilated left atrium, no mitral stenosis, no prosthetic valve, no LV hypertrophy

stages.label: RISK ASSESSMENT
stages.question: What is her CHA2DS2-VASc score?
options: 5 as the immediate next step
2 as the immediate next step
3 as the immediate next step
6 as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Walking through the score: congestive heart failure — none (0); hypertension — yes (1); age 75 or older — no, she is 74, but age 65–74 earns 1 point; diabetes — yes (1); prior stroke or TIA — none (0); vascular disease (prior MI, peripheral arterial disease, aortic plaque) — none (0); sex category female — yes (1). Total: 4. A score of 4 corresponds to an annual stroke risk of roughly 4%, well above the threshold at which anticoagulation clearly benefits. Guidelines recommend oral anticoagulation for a score of 2 or greater in men and 3 or greater in women, since the female-sex point acts as a risk modifier rather than an independent indication. Scores of 2, 3, 5, and 6 each miscount one or more of her risk factors.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which of the following is the most appropriate therapy for stroke prevention in this patient?
options: Apixaban 5 mg twice daily
No antithrombotic therapy as monotherapy
Aspirin 81 mg daily as initial therapy
Warfarin with INR goal 2–3 as the preferred first-line agent
Clopidogrel plus aspirin as monotherapy
stages.explanation: With a CHA2DS2-VASc score of 4 and nonvalvular atrial fibrillation — her echo shows no mitral stenosis and no mechanical valve — a direct oral anticoagulant such as apixaban is the guideline-preferred choice: DOACs match or beat warfarin for stroke prevention, cause roughly half the intracranial hemorrhage, require no INR monitoring, and have fewer food and drug interactions. Her renal function (CrCl ≈ 58 mL/min) permits full dosing; she meets only one of the three apixaban dose-reduction criteria (age ≥80, weight ≤60 kg, creatinine ≥1.5 mg/dL — and actually none apply). Warfarin remains first-line only for mechanical valves or moderate-to-severe mitral stenosis. Aspirin alone and dual antiplatelet therapy are both inferior and not recommended for AF stroke prevention. Withholding therapy at a 4% annual stroke risk is unacceptable.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: Which statement about her new anticoagulant is most accurate?
options: Warfarin causes less intracranial bleeding than DOACs in this setting
DOACs cannot be used in patients over age 70 as the immediate next step
She can stop the anticoagulant once her heart rate is well controlled
She will need INR checks every 4 weeks as the immediate next step
Missed doses are more consequential with a DOAC because of its short half-life
stages.explanation: DOACs have half-lives of roughly 8–14 hours, so protection fades within a day of a missed dose — strict adherence matters more than with warfarin, whose days-long effect buffers occasional lapses. This is the key counseling point for a patient managing her own pillbox. INR monitoring is a warfarin requirement; DOACs need only periodic renal function checks. Age alone is not a contraindication — trials enrolled large elderly populations, and apixaban in particular showed favorable bleeding outcomes in older adults. Warfarin causes about twice the intracranial hemorrhage of DOACs, not less. And anticoagulation is lifelong, driven by her stroke risk profile, not by rate or even rhythm control — stroke risk persists even if sinus rhythm is restored.

cases.diagnosis: Chronic (likely persistent) atrial fibrillation with CHA2DS2-VASc score of 4, treated with a DOAC
pearls: Anticoagulate atrial fibrillation when CHA2DS2-VASc is ≥2 in men or ≥3 in women; her score of 4 makes the decision clear.
DOACs are preferred over warfarin unless the patient has a mechanical valve or moderate-to-severe mitral stenosis.
Anticoagulation is independent of rate- versus rhythm-control strategy and continues even if sinus rhythm returns.
DOAC counseling essentials: no INR checks, but short half-life makes adherence critical; reassess renal function periodically.

cases.title: Sudden racing heart while studying
cases.chiefComplaint: My heart suddenly started racing like a switch flipped — I can feel it pounding in my neck.
cases.history: A 24-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with palpitations that began abruptly 45 minutes ago while she was sitting at her desk studying — 'like a switch flipped.' She describes rapid pounding in her chest and neck with mild lightheadedness but no chest pain, no dyspnea at rest, and no syncope. She has had two or three similar episodes over the past year, each starting and stopping suddenly after a few minutes; one ended when she bent over to tie her shoes. It is finals week: she has had four espresso drinks today and slept five hours last night. She takes no medications, has no medical history, and does not use tobacco or recreational drugs. Family history is negative for sudden cardiac death or arrhythmia. She has no allergies. A triage 12-lead ECG shows a regular, narrow-complex tachycardia at 186 bpm without clearly visible P waves.
cases.exam: Anxious but alert young woman, conversant in full sentences. Cardiac exam reveals a rapid, regular tachycardia without murmurs; rapid regular pulsations are visible in the neck ('frog sign'). Blood pressure is maintained, extremities are warm with brisk capillary refill, and there is no diaphoresis or mottling. Lungs are clear bilaterally with no accessory muscle use. There is no peripheral edema. Neurologic exam is grossly intact. No tremor, lid lag, or thyromegaly.

stages.label: INITIAL MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She remains hemodynamically stable. Which of the following is the most appropriate first intervention?
options: Immediate synchronized cardioversion
Adenosine 12 mg rapid IV push
IV metoprolol in this setting
IV amiodarone in this setting
Modified Valsalva maneuver
stages.explanation: For a stable, regular, narrow-complex tachycardia, vagal maneuvers are the first-line intervention. The modified Valsalva — a 15-second strain followed immediately by lying flat with the legs raised to 45 degrees — converted about 43% of SVT episodes in the REVERT trial, versus 17% for the standard technique, and it is free and risk-free. Synchronized cardioversion is reserved for hemodynamic instability: hypotension, altered mentation, ischemic chest pain, or pulmonary edema, none of which she has. Amiodarone has no role in stable SVT. IV metoprolol is a later option if adenosine and vagal maneuvers fail. Adenosine is the correct next drug after failed vagal maneuvers, but the starting dose is 6 mg, not 12 mg — and drugs come after vagal maneuvers, not before.

stages.label: RESPONSE TO VAGAL MANEUVERS
stages.content:
Modified Valsalva maneuver attempted twice: transient slowing on the monitor, but the tachycardia persists at 184 bpm
BP remains 112/70 mmHg; patient remains alert
Large-bore IV placed in the right antecubital fossa
Urine hCG: negative
Point-of-care K 4.0 mEq/L, Mg 2.1 mg/dL

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Vagal maneuvers have failed. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?
options: Continue repeating Valsalva maneuvers for another 30 minutes as the immediate next step
IV digoxin as the immediate next step
Oral diltiazem as the immediate next step
Synchronized cardioversion at 50 J as the immediate next step
Adenosine 6 mg rapid IV push through the antecubital line, followed immediately by a saline flush
stages.explanation: After failed vagal maneuvers in stable SVT, adenosine 6 mg by rapid push through a large proximal vein with an immediate flush is the drug of choice — its half-life is under 10 seconds, so delivery technique matters. Warn her first: a few seconds of chest pressure, flushing, and an alarming 'impending doom' sensation are expected and brief, and a rhythm strip should run during administration because the termination pattern is diagnostically valuable. If 6 mg fails, give 12 mg. Synchronized cardioversion is premature in a stable patient with pharmacologic options remaining. Oral diltiazem is far too slow for an ongoing tachycardia at 184 bpm. Digoxin has a delayed onset and is not used for acute SVT termination. Endless Valsalva repetition merely delays definitive therapy.

stages.label: POST-CONVERSION
stages.content:
After adenosine 6 mg: brief pause on the monitor, then conversion to normal sinus rhythm at 88 bpm
Symptoms fully resolved; patient feels 'back to normal'
Post-conversion 12-lead ECG: sinus rhythm, PR 148 ms, QRS 84 ms, QTc 412 ms, no delta wave, no pre-excitation, normal axis
Repeat BP 118/74 mmHg

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Atrial flutter with 2:1 conduction in this clinical context
Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response
Manifest Wolff-Parkinson-White syndrome with orthodromic AVRT
Sinus tachycardia from anxiety and caffeine
AV nodal reentrant tachycardia (AVNRT)
stages.explanation: Abrupt 'switch-like' onset and offset, a regular narrow-complex tachycardia near 186 bpm with no visible P waves, termination with vagal maneuvers and adenosine, and pounding in the neck all point to AVNRT — the most common paroxysmal SVT, with a predilection for young women. The neck pounding ('frog sign') comes from cannon A waves as the atria contract against closed AV valves during simultaneous atrioventricular activation. Atrial flutter with 2:1 block runs close to 150 bpm with sawtooth waves and rarely terminates with adenosine. Sinus tachycardia builds and resolves gradually, retains P waves, and rarely reaches 186 bpm at rest. The normal post-conversion ECG without a delta wave excludes manifest WPW (a concealed pathway remains possible but is less likely than AVNRT). Atrial fibrillation is irregularly irregular.

stages.label: DISPOSITION & LONG-TERM
stages.question: She is ready for discharge and asks what can be done to keep this from happening again. Which option offers definitive treatment?
options: Electrophysiology referral for catheter ablation
Implantable cardioverter-defibrillator
Daily aspirin as the immediate next step
Daily amiodarone as the immediate next step
Lifelong avoidance of all vigorous exercise
stages.explanation: Catheter ablation of the slow AV-nodal pathway cures AVNRT in over 95% of patients with a low complication rate, and it is first-line for recurrent symptomatic episodes or by patient preference — appropriate here given several episodes in a year, one requiring an ED visit. She should also be taught self-administered vagal maneuvers and advised to cut back on caffeine and sleep deprivation, which lower the threshold for episodes. Amiodarone carries thyroid, pulmonary, and hepatic toxicity and is never the answer for AVNRT in a healthy young patient. An ICD treats ventricular arrhythmias and sudden-death risk, not SVT. AVNRT carries no thromboembolic risk, so aspirin has no role. Exercise restriction is unnecessary — AVNRT is not exertionally dangerous in a structurally normal heart.

cases.diagnosis: AV nodal reentrant tachycardia (AVNRT)
pearls: Stable regular narrow-complex tachycardia: vagal maneuvers first, then adenosine 6 mg → 12 mg; cardioversion only for instability.
The modified Valsalva (strain, then supine with legs up) nearly triples conversion rates versus the standard technique (REVERT trial).
Neck pounding or the 'frog sign' reflects cannon A waves — a classic clue to AVNRT.
Always obtain a post-conversion ECG to look for a delta wave; slow-pathway catheter ablation cures AVNRT in >95% of cases.

cases.title: Skipped beats in the evening
cases.chiefComplaint: I keep feeling my heart skip a beat and then thump hard, mostly when I'm relaxing at night.
cases.history: A 35-year-old software engineer presents with two months of intermittent 'skipped beats' — a pause followed by a single hard thump in his chest — occurring mostly in the evenings while relaxing on the couch. Episodes last only seconds and occur several times an hour on bad days. He notices far fewer during his morning runs and none at peak exertion. He has had no syncope, presyncope, exertional chest pain, or dyspnea, and he runs 25 km per week without limitation. During a stressful product launch he has been drinking three to four energy drinks plus two coffees daily and sleeping five to six hours per night. He takes no medications or supplements, does not smoke, and drinks two to three beers a week. He has no medical history. Family history is negative for sudden cardiac death, cardiomyopathy, and unexplained drowning or single-car accidents in young relatives. He has no drug allergies.
cases.exam: Athletic-appearing man in no distress. Cardiac exam reveals a regular rhythm with occasional premature beats, each followed by a brief pause; normal S1 and S2 with no murmurs, rubs, or gallops, and no click. The point of maximal impulse is nondisplaced. Jugular venous pressure is normal. Lungs are clear bilaterally. There is no peripheral edema. The thyroid is normal in size with no tremor or lid lag. Peripheral pulses are full and symmetric.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which of the following is the most appropriate initial evaluation?
options: 12-lead ECG with electrolytes and TSH
Coronary CT angiography
Implantable loop recorder
Reassurance and discharge without any testing
Invasive electrophysiology study
stages.explanation: The initial workup of palpitations is a 12-lead ECG plus targeted labs — electrolytes including potassium and magnesium, and TSH — which together identify the ectopy, screen for channelopathies and structural clues (Q waves, epsilon waves, long QT, pre-excitation), and exclude metabolic drivers. An invasive electrophysiology study is reserved for sustained or dangerous arrhythmias, not benign-sounding skipped beats. Coronary CT angiography evaluates chest pain and coronary disease in a symptomatic patient, not isolated palpitations in a fit 35-year-old runner. An implantable loop recorder is for infrequent, unexplained syncope or palpitations after noninvasive monitoring fails — far too aggressive as a first step. Skipping all testing is also wrong: an ECG is cheap, and documenting the ectopy and screening for red flags is the standard of care before reassurance.

stages.label: ECG & LABS
stages.content:
12-lead ECG: sinus rhythm at 64 bpm with occasional unifocal premature ventricular complexes showing left bundle branch block morphology and an inferior axis (suggesting right ventricular outflow tract origin); otherwise normal intervals, no Q waves, no epsilon waves, QTc 404 ms
K 4.2 mEq/L, Mg 2.1 mg/dL
TSH 1.7 µIU/mL
Hemoglobin 15.2 g/dL
Creatinine 0.9 mg/dL

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?
options: Coronary angiography as the immediate next step
Cardiac MRI with gadolinium as the immediate next step
Empiric amiodarone as the immediate next step
24–48-hour Holter monitor to quantify PVC burden
Invasive electrophysiology study in this setting
stages.explanation: Once PVCs are documented, ambulatory Holter monitoring quantifies the daily burden, confirms whether the beats are unifocal or multifocal, detects runs of nonsustained ventricular tachycardia, and correlates symptoms with rhythm — all of which drive every downstream decision, including whether imaging is needed. The LBBB-with-inferior-axis morphology suggests a right ventricular outflow tract origin, the classic benign idiopathic PVC. An electrophysiology study is reserved for sustained arrhythmias or planned ablation. Cardiac MRI looks for scar or arrhythmogenic cardiomyopathy when the echo is abnormal, the burden is high, or red flags exist — premature now. Amiodarone is a toxic drug with no role in benign ectopy. Coronary angiography addresses ischemia, and nothing here — a fit runner with no exertional symptoms — suggests coronary disease.

stages.label: AMBULATORY MONITORING
stages.content:
48-hour Holter: PVC burden 3.1% (≈3,300 PVCs per 24 hours), all unifocal with the same RVOT morphology
Rare couplets; no runs of nonsustained ventricular tachycardia
Symptom diary: every logged 'skip' corresponds to an isolated PVC
Heart rate range 48 bpm (sleep) to 162 bpm (running) with appropriate sinus response
PVCs nearly disappear during exercise and peak in the late evening

stages.label: RISK STRATIFICATION
stages.question: Which of the following findings, if present, would most strongly indicate the need for echocardiography and further structural evaluation?
options: PVC suppression during exercise
PVC burden exceeding 10–15% of total beats
A single PVC morphology on the Holter
Evening predominance of symptoms
Ongoing caffeine and energy-drink use
stages.explanation: A PVC burden above roughly 10–15% of total daily beats is the strongest listed indication for echocardiography, because sustained high-burden ectopy can itself cause a reversible PVC-induced cardiomyopathy — and detecting early ventricular dysfunction changes management toward beta-blockers or catheter ablation. Other features that warrant imaging include multifocal PVCs, runs of nonsustained VT, exertional increase in ectopy, or any abnormal baseline ECG or exam. Evening predominance is typical of benign, catecholamine-withdrawal-related ectopy. Suppression with exercise is reassuring — the worrisome pattern is PVCs that multiply with exertion, raising concern for ischemia or catecholaminergic arrhythmias. Caffeine use is a modifiable trigger, not an imaging indication. A single (unifocal) morphology is a reassuring feature suggesting one benign focus, typically the right ventricular outflow tract.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: His burden is 3.1%, unifocal, with no nonsustained VT and a normal exam and ECG apart from the PVCs. Which of the following is the most appropriate management?
options: Flecainide as initial therapy
Catheter ablation now as initial therapy
Amiodarone as initial therapy
Implantable cardioverter-defibrillator as initial therapy
Reassurance with caffeine
stages.explanation: A low PVC burden with reassuring features — unifocal RVOT morphology, suppression with exercise, no nonsustained VT, no family history of sudden death, and a structurally normal-appearing heart — carries an excellent prognosis. First-line management is reassurance plus trigger reduction: cutting the three to four daily energy drinks and two coffees, restoring sleep, and addressing stress, with follow-up to confirm improvement. A beta-blocker can be added later if symptoms remain bothersome. Flecainide is avoided without first excluding structural and coronary disease (CAST trial harm) and is unnecessary here. Catheter ablation is reserved for high-burden PVCs, PVC-induced cardiomyopathy, or refractory symptoms. Amiodarone's toxicity profile is indefensible for benign ectopy. An ICD prevents sudden death in high-risk structural disease and has no role whatsoever in this patient.

cases.diagnosis: Benign premature ventricular complexes (RVOT morphology) in a structurally normal heart, exacerbated by caffeine, stress, and sleep deprivation
pearls: Unifocal PVCs that suppress with exercise in a structurally normal heart are benign; PVCs that increase with exertion are a red flag.
Quantify PVC burden with ambulatory monitoring — burden >10–15% risks PVC-induced cardiomyopathy and warrants echocardiography.
First-line treatment for benign symptomatic PVCs is trigger reduction (caffeine, sleep, stress) and reassurance; beta-blockers are second-line.
LBBB morphology with an inferior axis points to a right ventricular outflow tract origin — the classic benign idiopathic PVC.

cases.title: Chest pain that eases when he leans forward
cases.chiefComplaint: I've had sharp chest pain for two days — it's worse when I breathe deep or lie down, and better when I sit up and lean forward.
cases.history: A 28-year-old man presents to the emergency department with two days of sharp, central chest pain. The pain is worse with deep inspiration and when lying flat, improves when he sits up and leans forward, and radiates to the ridge of his left trapezius. It is constant, rated 6/10, and unrelated to exertion or meals. About a week before the pain began he had a 'flu-like' illness with low-grade fever, rhinorrhea, and muscle aches that resolved on its own. He took a single 200 mg ibuprofen yesterday with modest relief. He has no medical history, takes no medications, and has no allergies. He does not smoke, drinks socially, and denies cocaine or other recreational drug use. He has had no recent long travel, leg swelling, immobilization, surgery, or trauma to the chest. Family history is negative for premature coronary artery disease and sudden death. He works as a graphic designer and exercises twice a week.
cases.exam: Uncomfortable-appearing young man sitting upright and leaning forward on the stretcher. Cardiac auscultation reveals a scratchy, squeaky friction rub at the left lower sternal border, loudest with the patient leaning forward in end-expiration; heart sounds are otherwise normal without murmur. Jugular venous pressure is not elevated, and pulsus paradoxus is 6 mmHg. Lungs are clear bilaterally. The chest wall is nontender, and the pain is not reproduced by palpation. There is no calf swelling or tenderness, and no peripheral edema.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which of the following is the most appropriate initial diagnostic step?
options: Empiric proton-pump inhibitor trial
Exercise treadmill stress test
CT pulmonary angiography as the first step today
12-lead ECG with troponin
Activate the cath lab for emergent coronary angiography
stages.explanation: Acute chest pain in the emergency department always starts with a 12-lead ECG, and in this presentation it is paired with troponin and inflammatory markers (CRP, ESR) — the combination that confirms pericardial inflammation, screens for myocardial involvement, and excludes infarction. The pleuritic, positional pattern with a friction rub after a viral prodrome predicts the diagnosis but still requires objective confirmation. Cath lab activation is for ST-elevation myocardial infarction; committing to it before the ECG is read in context risks an unnecessary invasive procedure in a 28-year-old. CT pulmonary angiography targets pulmonary embolism, but he has no risk factors, no hypoxia, and a positional component atypical for PE. Stress testing has no role in acute chest pain. An empiric PPI trial ignores objective findings of fever, tachycardia, and a rub.

stages.label: ECG, LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
12-lead ECG: diffuse concave ('saddle-shaped') ST-segment elevation in leads I, II, III, aVF, and V2–V6 with PR-segment depression; PR elevation and ST depression in aVR; no reciprocal ST depression elsewhere
Troponin I: 0.02 ng/mL (normal <0.04)
CRP 64 mg/L; ESR 48 mm/h
WBC 11.6 ×10³/µL
Chest X-ray: normal cardiac silhouette, no infiltrate or effusion
Bedside echocardiogram: trivial circumferential pericardial effusion, LVEF 60%, no chamber collapse

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Costochondritis
Pulmonary embolism
Acute myocarditis
Acute ST-elevation myocardial infarction
Acute viral pericarditis
stages.explanation: He meets all four diagnostic criteria for acute pericarditis (two are required): characteristic pleuritic, positional chest pain; a pericardial friction rub; typical ECG changes — diffuse concave ST elevation with PR depression and reciprocal PR elevation in aVR; and a new pericardial effusion. The preceding viral illness, young age, fever, and elevated inflammatory markers complete the classic picture of viral (or idiopathic) pericarditis. STEMI produces convex, territorial ST elevation with reciprocal ST depression — absent here — and would elevate troponin. Pulmonary embolism lacks a rub, PR depression, and positional relief, and he has no risk factors or hypoxia. Myocarditis is the key mimic, but his troponin is normal and ventricular function preserved, arguing against significant myocardial involvement. Costochondritis is reproducible with chest-wall palpation, explicitly absent on his exam.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which of the following is the most appropriate treatment regimen?
options: Urgent pericardiocentesis as initial therapy
Heparin infusion with serial troponins as initial therapy
High-dose aspirin alone for 2 days as initial therapy
Ibuprofen 600 mg three times daily for 1–2 weeks plus colchicine 0.5 mg twice daily for 3 months
Prednisone taper as initial therapy
stages.explanation: First-line therapy for acute viral or idiopathic pericarditis is a full-dose NSAID — ibuprofen 600–800 mg three times daily for one to two weeks, tapered as symptoms and CRP normalize — plus colchicine for three months, which roughly halves the recurrence rate (ICAP trial). Strenuous exercise should be restricted until symptoms resolve and CRP normalizes. High-dose aspirin is an acceptable NSAID alternative, but two days is far too short and it omits colchicine. Glucocorticoids increase recurrence risk and are reserved for NSAID contraindication or failure, autoimmune etiologies, or pregnancy. Pericardiocentesis is for cardiac tamponade or suspected purulent or malignant effusion — his effusion is trivial with no chamber collapse. Anticoagulation is avoided in pericarditis when feasible because it may promote hemorrhagic transformation of the effusion, and there is no ACS to treat.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which of the following is the most appropriate disposition?
options: Admit for urgent cardiac MRI with appropriate follow-up
Admit to the ICU for tamponade monitoring with appropriate follow-up
Admit for electrophysiology consultation with appropriate follow-up
Discharge home on ibuprofen plus colchicine with activity restriction
Discharge with a 2-week course of azithromycin with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: He has none of the high-risk features that mandate admission for acute pericarditis: fever above 38 °C (his is 37.9 °C), subacute onset, large effusion greater than 20 mm or tamponade, failure of NSAID therapy after a week, significant troponin elevation suggesting myopericarditis, immunosuppression, recent trauma, or oral anticoagulant use. Low-risk patients do well with outpatient NSAID-plus-colchicine therapy, activity restriction until symptoms and CRP resolve, and reassessment in roughly one week. ICU admission is unwarranted with a trivial effusion and no chamber collapse. Cardiac MRI is reserved for suspected myopericarditis or constrictive physiology, not an uncomplicated first episode. Antibiotics treat nothing in a viral illness. There is no arrhythmia to justify electrophysiology consultation.

cases.diagnosis: Acute viral (idiopathic) pericarditis
pearls: Diagnosis requires 2 of 4: typical positional/pleuritic pain, friction rub, diffuse ST elevation with PR depression, or new pericardial effusion.
PR depression with PR elevation in aVR and the absence of reciprocal ST depression distinguish pericarditis from STEMI.
Treatment is NSAID for 1–2 weeks plus colchicine for 3 months — colchicine halves recurrence (ICAP); steroids increase it.
Admit only for high-risk features: fever >38 °C, large effusion or tamponade, elevated troponin, NSAID failure, immunosuppression, or anticoagulant use.

cases.title: Breathless Months After a Blood Clot
cases.chiefComplaint: Progressive shortness of breath and exercise intolerance that have not improved since a pulmonary embolism eight months ago.
cases.history: A 57-year-old man presents with worsening dyspnea on exertion over the past 6 months. Eight months ago he was diagnosed with an acute submassive pulmonary embolism after a long-haul flight, treated with rivaroxaban 20 mg daily, which he continues to take reliably. He initially improved but over the last several months notes he can now walk only one block before stopping, has lightheadedness climbing stairs, and had one episode of exertional near-syncope. He denies chest pain, orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, fever, or hemoptysis. PMH: prior provoked PE, mild obesity (BMI 31), no prior cardiac or lung disease. Medications: rivaroxaban 20 mg daily. Allergies: none. Social: retired accountant, never-smoker, 2 drinks weekly, no recreational drugs. Family history: a brother with unprovoked DVT. He has never had a repeat imaging study since his initial PE diagnosis and was told his clot would 'dissolve over time.' Review of systems is notable for progressive fatigue and mild bilateral ankle swelling but no calf pain or asymmetry.
cases.exam: Comfortable at rest but mildly tachypneic with exertion. JVP elevated to 9 cm with prominent a-wave. Cardiac exam: regular rhythm, a loud P2 and a right-sided S4; a soft pansystolic murmur at the left lower sternal border that increases with inspiration. Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally, no crackles or wheeze. Left parasternal heave palpable. Abdomen soft, liver edge palpable 2 cm below costal margin, non-tender. Trace-to-1+ symmetric pedal edema. No calf tenderness, no cords, no asymmetric leg swelling. No clubbing or cyanosis.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature of this presentation is most concerning for a chronic, fixed pulmonary vascular process rather than residual deconditioning after his PE?
options: Progressive dyspnea with exertional near-syncope, a loud P2,
The presence of trace bilateral ankle edema alone as the first step today
His prior long-haul flight as a provoking factor as the first step today
His ongoing adherence to rivaroxaban as the first step today
stages.explanation: Progressive symptoms with signs of pulmonary hypertension and right ventricular pressure overload (loud P2, parasternal heave, near-syncope) persisting despite adequate anticoagulation point to a chronic, fixed obstruction of the pulmonary vasculature rather than simple deconditioning, which should improve over months. Continued rivaroxaban adherence makes recurrent acute clot less likely but does not explain fixed obstruction. A remote provoking flight explains the original event, not persistent symptoms. Trace edema in isolation is nonspecific and could reflect many causes; it is the constellation of RV pressure-overload findings plus near-syncope that raises alarm for an established pulmonary vascular disease such as CTEPH.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial diagnostic test to evaluate for chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension in this patient?
options: Lower-extremity venous duplex ultrasound in this clinical context
High-resolution CT of the chest for interstitial lung disease
Repeat CT pulmonary angiogram looking for new acute clot
Ventilation–perfusion (V/Q) scan
stages.explanation: The V/Q scan is the recommended screening test for CTEPH: it is highly sensitive and a normal scan effectively excludes the diagnosis. CTEPH produces segmental or larger mismatched perfusion defects. A standard CTPA can miss the chronic, organized, web-like and eccentric thrombi of CTEPH and is less sensitive for screening, though dedicated CT/MR pulmonary angiography is later used to define operability.
