Venous duplex evaluates for recurrent DVT but does not assess the pulmonary vasculature for chronic obstruction. High-resolution CT screens for parenchymal lung disease, which his clear lungs and clear chest film make unlikely. The V/Q scan is the correct first step before confirmatory angiography and right heart catheterization.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
BNP 540 pg/mL
Troponin T <0.01 ng/mL
Hemoglobin 15.1 g/dL
INR 1.1 (on rivaroxaban)
ECG: right-axis deviation, right bundle branch block, RV strain pattern (T-wave inversions V1–V3)
V/Q scan: multiple bilateral segmental and subsegmental MISMATCHED perfusion defects with normal ventilation
Transthoracic echo: RV dilation and hypokinesis, estimated PASP 68 mmHg, flattened interventricular septum (D-shaped LV), normal LV size and function (EF 60%)

stages.label: CONFIRMATION
stages.question: After the V/Q scan and echo, what is the most appropriate next step to confirm the diagnosis and assess operability?
options: Start a phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitor empirically and reassess in 3 months
Right heart catheterization with pulmonary angiography
Switch rivaroxaban to warfarin and repeat the V/Q scan in 6 months
Refer directly for lung transplantation evaluation as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: The diagnosis of CTEPH requires hemodynamic confirmation of precapillary pulmonary hypertension (mean PA pressure ≥20 mmHg, PVR elevated, pulmonary capillary wedge pressure ≤15 mmHg) by right heart catheterization, plus dedicated pulmonary angiography to map the chronic thromboembolic lesions and judge surgical accessibility. Empiric PDE-5 inhibitors are not appropriate before confirming the diagnosis and assessing for the potentially curative surgical option. Switching anticoagulants and watchful waiting delays definitive care; lifelong anticoagulation is required but is not the definitive treatment. Lung transplantation is reserved for inoperable, medically refractory disease — premature here before operability is even assessed at an expert center.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Right heart catheterization confirms precapillary pulmonary hypertension (mPAP 42 mmHg, PVR 7 Wood units, wedge 10 mmHg) with proximal, surgically accessible disease at an expert center. What is the most appropriate definitive treatment?
options: Balloon pulmonary angioplasty as first-line therapy
Lifelong anticoagulation alone as initial therapy
Riociguat monotherapy indefinitely as monotherapy
Pulmonary endarterectomy
stages.explanation: Pulmonary endarterectomy (PEA) is the treatment of choice and is potentially curative for patients with surgically accessible (proximal) chronic thromboembolic disease and acceptable operative risk. All CTEPH patients require lifelong anticoagulation, but anticoagulation alone does not relieve fixed obstruction. Balloon pulmonary angioplasty and the soluble guanylate cyclase stimulator riociguat are reserved for inoperable disease, for patients who are poor surgical candidates, or for persistent/recurrent pulmonary hypertension after PEA — not as first-line when the patient is operable. Recognizing operable CTEPH matters because PEA can dramatically improve or normalize hemodynamics and survival. Referral to a specialized CTEPH center for the operability decision is essential.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Regarding lifelong anticoagulation in this patient with CTEPH, which statement is most accurate?
options: All CTEPH patients require indefinite anticoagulation, even after pulmonary endarterectomy
Anticoagulation is only needed if a new DVT is found with appropriate follow-up
A direct oral anticoagulant is contraindicated and only aspirin should be used
Anticoagulation can be stopped after successful pulmonary endarterectomy
stages.explanation: Lifelong (indefinite) anticoagulation is recommended for all patients with CTEPH and is continued even after a successful pulmonary endarterectomy, because the underlying thrombotic predisposition persists and recurrent thromboembolism would jeopardize the result. Stopping anticoagulation after surgery is therefore incorrect. Anticoagulation is not contingent on finding a new DVT — it is a baseline requirement of the diagnosis. The choice of agent (vitamin K antagonist versus a direct oral anticoagulant) is individualized; an underlying antiphospholipid syndrome, if present, favors warfarin, but anticoagulation itself is never replaced by aspirin alone in CTEPH. Evaluation for an underlying hypercoagulable state (including antiphospholipid antibodies) is also part of the workup.

cases.diagnosis: Chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension (CTEPH)
pearls: Suspect CTEPH in any patient with persistent or progressive dyspnea and signs of pulmonary hypertension months after an acute PE despite adequate anticoagulation.
The V/Q scan is the screening test of choice — a normal scan rules out CTEPH; CTEPH shows segmental mismatched perfusion defects. CTPA can miss chronic organized thrombus.
Diagnosis requires right heart catheterization showing precapillary PH (mPAP ≥20 mmHg, wedge ≤15 mmHg) plus pulmonary angiography to define operability.

Pulmonary endarterectomy is potentially curative for surgically accessible disease; all patients need lifelong anticoagulation, and riociguat or balloon pulmonary angioplasty are options for inoperable disease.

cases.title: Swollen Legs and Breathlessness in a Lifelong Smoker
cases.chiefComplaint: Worsening leg swelling, abdominal fullness, and breathlessness over several weeks in a man with severe emphysema.
cases.history: A 68-year-old man with severe COPD (GOLD group E, FEV1 32% predicted) is admitted with two weeks of progressive bilateral leg swelling, abdominal bloating, and worsening dyspnea. He has chronic productive cough and uses home short-acting bronchodilators frequently. He has had two COPD exacerbations in the past year. He denies chest pain, orthopnea limited to lung disease, fever, or hemoptysis. He has never used home oxygen and has not had formal oxygen assessment. PMH: COPD, hypertension, 50-pack-year smoking history (still smoking ~half a pack daily). Medications: tiotropium inhaler, albuterol PRN, amlodipine 5 mg daily. Allergies: penicillin (rash). Social: lives alone, retired machinist. Family history: noncontributory. He reports that his ankles are 'the worst they've ever been,' his shoes no longer fit, and his belt feels tight. Review of systems notable for early satiety and increased fatigue. He sleeps in a recliner mostly due to breathlessness when lying flat.
cases.exam: Barrel-chested man in mild respiratory distress, using accessory muscles, pursed-lip breathing. JVP elevated to 12 cm. Cardiac: distant heart sounds, loud P2, left parasternal heave, a soft tricuspid regurgitation murmur at the lower sternal border increasing with inspiration. Lungs: markedly decreased breath sounds, prolonged expiratory phase, scattered expiratory wheezes, no focal consolidation. Abdomen: distended, mild hepatomegaly with tender liver edge, positive hepatojugular reflux, no shifting dullness clearly. Extremities: 3+ pitting edema to the knees bilaterally. No calf tenderness. Warm, well-perfused, mild central cyanosis of the lips.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which combination of findings best explains this patient's leg swelling and elevated JVP in the context of his lung disease?
options: Right heart failure driven by chronic hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction from severe COPD
Left ventricular systolic dysfunction causing pulmonary congestion in this clinical context
Nephrotic syndrome causing hypoalbuminemia and edema as the first step today
Bilateral deep vein thrombosis causing dependent edema as the first step today
stages.explanation: Elevated JVP, hepatomegaly with hepatojugular reflux, a parasternal heave, loud P2, tricuspid regurgitation, and bilateral pitting edema in a patient with severe COPD and chronic hypoxemia describe cor pulmonale — right heart failure from a primary pulmonary process. Chronic alveolar hypoxia causes pulmonary vasoconstriction and remodeling, raising pulmonary pressures and overloading the RV. Left heart failure would typically produce pulmonary crackles and an S3, less prominent isolated right-sided signs. Nephrotic syndrome causes edema but not elevated JVP or RV pressure-overload signs. Bilateral DVT does not raise the JVP or cause hepatomegaly. The clinical picture is classic right-sided failure secondary to advanced lung disease.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
ABG (room air): pH 7.36, PaCO2 58 mmHg, PaO2 52 mmHg, HCO3 31 mEq/L
Hemoglobin 18.4 g/dL, Hematocrit 55%
BNP 290 pg/mL
Basic metabolic panel: Na 138, K 4.4, Cr 1.0 mg/dL
ECG: P pulmonale (peaked P waves in II), right-axis deviation, right ventricular hypertrophy
Chest X-ray: hyperinflation, flattened diaphragms, enlarged central pulmonary arteries, no infiltrate
Echo: dilated, hypertrophied right ventricle, RV systolic dysfunction, estimated PASP 60 mmHg, normal LV size and EF 60%, no significant valvular stenosis

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The ABG shows chronic hypoxemia with compensated respiratory acidosis and secondary polycythemia. Which finding most directly confirms the mechanism linking his lung disease to right heart failure?
options: Chronic hypoxemia with echo evidence of pulmonary hypertension
Hemoglobin of 18.4 g/dL as the primary diagnosis
Normal LV ejection fraction of 60% as the primary diagnosis
Elevated BNP of 290 pg/mL as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Chronic hypoxemia (PaO2 52 mmHg) drives hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction and remodeling, producing the pulmonary hypertension and RV hypertrophy/dysfunction seen on echo — this is the direct mechanistic link of cor pulmonale. The elevated BNP reflects RV/ventricular wall stress but is nonspecific. Secondary polycythemia (Hgb 18.4) is a consequence of chronic hypoxemia, supporting the picture but not itself the mechanism. The preserved LV EF helps exclude left heart failure as the cause but does not confirm the pulmonary-vascular mechanism. The hypoxemia–pulmonary hypertension–RV overload sequence is the defining pathophysiology of cor pulmonale from COPD.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Beyond optimizing his COPD and gentle diuresis for volume overload, which intervention has the greatest proven mortality benefit and is the cornerstone of managing cor pulmonale from COPD?
options: A pulmonary vasodilator such as sildenafil as initial therapy
High-dose loop diuretics to keep him net negative daily as initial therapy
Long-term continuous supplemental oxygen therapy for chronic resting hypoxemia
Therapeutic phlebotomy to normalize his hematocrit as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Long-term oxygen therapy (≥15 hours/day) is the only intervention proven to improve survival in COPD patients with chronic resting hypoxemia (PaO2 ≤55 mmHg, or ≤59 mmHg with cor pulmonale/polycythemia). It also reduces pulmonary artery pressures over time by relieving hypoxic vasoconstriction — making it the cornerstone of cor pulmonale management. Pulmonary vasodilators like sildenafil are not recommended for COPD-related (group 3) pulmonary hypertension and can worsen V/Q matching and hypoxemia. Aggressive diuresis treats edema but can reduce RV preload excessively and cause prerenal injury; it is adjunctive, not mortality-altering. Phlebotomy is reserved for symptomatic severe polycythemia and does not address the underlying hypoxia.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: He qualifies for home oxygen. Which additional intervention offers the most important long-term benefit for his underlying disease and survival?
options: Starting prophylactic anticoagulation in this setting
Smoking cessation support and pharmacotherapy
Beginning a calcium channel blocker for pulmonary hypertension
Adding a daily oral corticosteroid in this setting
stages.explanation: Smoking cessation is the single most effective intervention to slow COPD progression and improve long-term survival, and this patient is still smoking. Combining counseling with pharmacotherapy (nicotine replacement, varenicline, or bupropion) maximizes success. Chronic daily oral corticosteroids are not recommended in stable COPD due to systemic toxicity. Empiric anticoagulation is not indicated for cor pulmonale from COPD in the absence of thromboembolic disease. Calcium channel blockers benefit only a small minority of patients with idiopathic pulmonary arterial hypertension who are vasoreactive, and they are not used for COPD-related pulmonary hypertension, where they can worsen oxygenation. Cessation, oxygen, and COPD optimization are the priorities.

cases.diagnosis: Cor pulmonale secondary to severe COPD
pearls: Cor pulmonale is right heart failure caused by a primary pulmonary process; look for elevated JVP, hepatomegaly, parasternal heave, loud P2, TR murmur, and pedal edema in advanced lung disease.
Chronic alveolar hypoxia drives hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction and remodeling, leading to pulmonary hypertension and RV failure; secondary polycythemia and P pulmonale on ECG are clues.
Long-term oxygen therapy for chronic resting hypoxemia (PaO2 ≤55, or ≤59 with cor pulmonale/polycythemia) is the only therapy proven to improve survival and lowers pulmonary pressures.
Avoid pulmonary vasodilators in COPD-related (group 3) pulmonary hypertension; smoking cessation and COPD optimization are the foundation, with diuretics used cautiously for volume overload.

cases.title: A Sudden Cold, Painful Leg
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden onset of severe pain, numbness, and coldness in the right leg over the past three hours.
cases.history: A 73-year-old woman presents to the ED with abrupt onset of severe right leg pain that began three hours ago while watching television. The pain is constant and accompanied by numbness and a sensation that the leg is 'frozen.' She has noticed she can barely move her toes. She denies trauma, back pain, or prior similar episodes. PMH: atrial fibrillation diagnosed two years ago, hypertension, type 2 diabetes. She admits she stopped her apixaban about a month ago because she 'kept forgetting' and disliked the cost. Medications (prescribed): apixaban 5 mg twice daily (non-adherent), metoprolol 50 mg twice daily, lisinopril 20 mg daily, metformin 1000 mg twice daily. Allergies: none. Social: former smoker, quit 10 years ago, lives with her husband. Family history: father had a stroke. Review of systems negative for fever, chest pain, palpitations awareness, or recent immobilization. She reports the leg looked normal earlier today and the change was dramatic and sudden, distinguishing it from the gradual claudication some of her friends describe.
cases.exam: Anxious woman in pain. Cardiac: irregularly irregular rhythm, no murmurs, no S3. Right lower extremity: pale and cool from mid-thigh distally compared with the warm, pink left leg. Capillary refill markedly delayed in the right foot. Right femoral pulse palpable but popliteal, dorsalis pedis, and posterior tibial pulses absent on the right; all pulses present and 2+ on the left. Decreased pinprick sensation over the right foot and reduced light touch. Right ankle and toe dorsiflexion weak. Calf soft, not tense, mild tenderness. No skin mottling yet. Left leg exam entirely normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Based on the abrupt presentation and exam, which diagnosis is most likely and what is the underlying mechanism?
options: Deep vein thrombosis of the right leg as the first step today
Chronic peripheral arterial disease with worsening claudication
Acute limb ischemia from a cardioembolic occlusion arising from atrial fibrillation
Lumbar spinal stenosis causing neurogenic claudication as the first step today
stages.explanation: A sudden cold, pale, painful, pulseless leg with sensory and motor deficits in a patient with atrial fibrillation who stopped anticoagulation is classic acute limb ischemia from arterial embolism. The clot most often originates in the left atrial appendage and lodges at an arterial bifurcation. Chronic PAD causes gradual claudication and rest pain over months, often with collateral development and a less dramatic onset. DVT causes a swollen, warm, often tender leg with palpable pulses — not a cold, pulseless limb. Neurogenic claudication from spinal stenosis is positional, relieved by flexion, and does not abolish pulses or cause an acutely cold limb. The embolic source and abruptness are key.

stages.label: ASSESSMENT
stages.question: Which set of findings constitutes the 'six P's' used to assess acute limb ischemia, and which two are the most ominous signs of advanced ischemia?
options: Pain, pallor, pulselessness, poikilothermia, paresthesia, paralysis
Pain, pallor, pulselessness, polyuria, paresthesia, paralysis — with polyuria most ominous as the immediate next step
Pain, pyrexia, pallor, pulselessness, pruritus, paralysis — with pyrexia and pruritus most ominous in this setting
Pain, pallor, palpitations, pulselessness, paresthesia, paralysis — with palpitations most ominous in this setting
stages.explanation: The six P's are Pain, Pallor, Pulselessness, Poikilothermia (coolness), Paresthesia, and Paralysis. Of these, paresthesia (sensory loss) and paralysis (motor deficit) signal that the limb is threatened and ischemia is advancing toward irreversible damage — they mandate urgent revascularization. The other options insert spurious terms (pyrexia, pruritus, palpitations, polyuria) that are not part of the classic mnemonic and are not markers of limb ischemia. Recognizing sensory and motor compromise is critical because it separates a viable limb from a threatened or irreversibly damaged one (Rutherford classification) and dictates how urgently the limb must be reperfused.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Before definitive vascular imaging or revascularization, what is the single most important immediate medication to administer?
options: Intravenous fluids and potassium as monotherapy
Oral aspirin 81 mg only as initial therapy
Intravenous unfractionated heparin bolus
Intravenous broad-spectrum antibiotics
stages.explanation: Immediate systemic anticoagulation with an IV unfractionated heparin bolus and infusion is the first step in acute limb ischemia: it prevents thrombus propagation, limits clot extension distally, and protects collateral and downstream vessels while the patient is prepared for revascularization. It also reduces the risk of recurrent embolism. Heparin is preferred for its rapid onset and reversibility before a procedure. Antibiotics are not indicated absent infection or established gangrene. Aspirin alone provides inadequate anticoagulation for an acute embolic occlusion. IV fluids and electrolyte management are supportive and may matter later (e.g., with reperfusion), but they do not address the propagating thrombus. Anticoagulation buys time to revascularize within the narrow window.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Heparin started. INR 1.0, aPTT now therapeutic on infusion
Creatinine 1.1 mg/dL, K 4.2 mEq/L
Lactate 1.8 mmol/L, CK 180 U/L
ECG: atrial fibrillation, rate 110, no acute ST changes
CT angiography of the runoff: abrupt cutoff of the right popliteal artery with an intraluminal filling defect; reconstituted distal vessels via collaterals; patent contralateral arterial tree
Leg remains with intact but diminished sensation, weak but present motor function (Rutherford IIa–IIb, threatened but viable)

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: The leg is threatened but still viable (Rutherford IIa–IIb). What is the most appropriate definitive management given the embolic occlusion?
options: Begin long-term warfarin and discharge home with vascular follow-up
Urgent revascularization
Admit for observation and continue heparin alone, reimaging in 48 hours
Immediate above-knee amputation with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: A threatened but viable limb requires urgent revascularization within hours to salvage the limb — typically surgical (Fogarty balloon) embolectomy for an embolic occlusion, or catheter-directed thrombolysis/thrombectomy depending on anatomy and timing. The window to reperfuse before irreversible muscle and nerve injury is narrow (roughly 6 hours), so heparin-only observation risks limb loss and is inappropriate for a category II limb. Amputation is reserved for an irreversibly ischemic (Rutherford III) limb with profound, fixed deficits and muscle rigidity — not present here. Long-term anticoagulation is essential afterward to prevent recurrence given her atrial fibrillation, but it is not the acute limb-saving treatment. Watch for reperfusion compartment syndrome after revascularization.

cases.diagnosis: Acute limb ischemia from arterial (cardioembolic) embolism in atrial fibrillation
pearls: A sudden cold, pale, painful, pulseless leg in a patient with atrial fibrillation is acute limb ischemia from cardioembolism until proven otherwise — embolism causes abrupt onset, unlike gradual atherosclerotic claudication.
Assess the six P's: Pain, Pallor, Pulselessness, Poikilothermia, Paresthesia, Paralysis. Sensory and motor deficits mark a threatened limb needing emergent revascularization.
Give an IV heparin bolus and infusion immediately to prevent thrombus propagation while arranging revascularization.

A threatened-but-viable limb (Rutherford II) needs urgent surgical embolectomy or catheter-directed therapy within hours; watch for reperfusion injury and compartment syndrome, and anticoagulate long-term to prevent recurrence.

cases.title: Recurrent Bleeding and a Harsh Heart Murmur
cases.chiefComplaint: Recurrent episodes of dark stools and anemia requiring transfusions, now admitted with another bleed.
cases.history: A 79-year-old man is admitted for the third time in eight months with melena and symptomatic anemia. Each prior admission required transfusion; two upper endoscopies and one colonoscopy showed only scattered mucosal vascular lesions in the small bowel and colon without a single bleeding ulcer or mass. He has been treated with proton pump inhibitors without benefit. He also reports increasing exertional dyspnea and one episode of exertional lightheadedness over the past year. He denies NSAID use, anticoagulants, alcohol excess, or weight loss. PMH: severe aortic stenosis followed for two years, hypertension, prior diverticulosis. Medications: pantoprazole 40 mg daily, amlodipine 5 mg daily, ferrous sulfate. Allergies: none. Social: retired teacher, never-smoker. Family history: noncontributory. He is frustrated that no source of bleeding has been definitively found despite multiple scopes, and his hemoglobin keeps drifting down between admissions despite oral iron. He has not had a valve intervention because he was initially considered asymptomatic.
cases.exam: Pale, well-nourished man, comfortable at rest. Cardiac: a harsh late-peaking crescendo–decrescendo systolic murmur at the right upper sternal border radiating to the carotids; soft, single second heart sound with diminished A2; sustained apical impulse; delayed and diminished carotid upstrokes (pulsus parvus et tardus). Lungs clear. Abdomen soft, non-tender, no masses, no hepatosplenomegaly. Rectal exam: melenic stool, no mass. Skin: pale conjunctivae, no telangiectasias on the lips or oral mucosa, no spider angiomata. No peripheral edema. Neurologic exam normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given recurrent GI bleeding from mucosal vascular lesions and a murmur consistent with severe aortic stenosis, what unifying diagnosis should be suspected?
options: Peptic ulcer disease from NSAID use as the first step today
Heyde syndrome — aortic stenosis with acquired von Willebrand disease
Colon cancer with occult bleeding as the first step today
Hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia (Osler–Weber–Rendu) in this clinical context
stages.explanation: Heyde syndrome is the triad of severe aortic stenosis, an acquired von Willebrand syndrome (type 2A), and bleeding from GI angiodysplasia. High shear stress across the stenotic valve unfolds and cleaves the largest, most hemostatically active von Willebrand factor multimers, impairing platelet adhesion at sites of fragile angiodysplastic vessels. Hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia causes mucocutaneous telangiectasias and epistaxis with a positive family history, absent here (no lip/oral lesions, no family history). Colon cancer is excluded by the prior negative colonoscopy and absence of mass or weight loss. There is no NSAID use or ulcer on endoscopy, making peptic ulcer disease unlikely. The valve–bleeding link is the unifying explanation.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Hemoglobin 7.6 g/dL (baseline ~12), MCV 74 fL (microcytic)
Ferritin 9 ng/mL, transferrin saturation 8% (iron deficiency)
Platelets 240 ×10³/µL, INR 1.0, aPTT 30 s
von Willebrand factor antigen normal, but loss of high-molecular-weight vWF multimers on gel electrophoresis; reduced vWF activity-to-antigen ratio
Echocardiogram: aortic valve area 0.8 cm², mean gradient 48 mmHg, peak velocity 4.3 m/s, calcified trileaflet valve, LV ejection fraction 60%, LVH
Capsule endoscopy: multiple small-bowel angiodysplasias, one with active oozing

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which laboratory finding most specifically confirms the acquired von Willebrand abnormality of Heyde syndrome?
options: Microcytic anemia with low ferritin as the primary diagnosis
Normal platelet count and normal PT/PTT as the primary diagnosis
Loss of high-molecular-weight von Willebrand factor multimers with a reduced vWF activity-to-antigen ratio
Elevated aortic valve mean gradient on echocardiography as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: The hallmark of the acquired von Willebrand syndrome in Heyde is selective loss of high-molecular-weight vWF multimers with a disproportionately reduced vWF activity (e.g., ristocetin cofactor or collagen-binding) relative to vWF antigen — even when total antigen is normal. This reflects shear-mediated proteolysis of the most active large multimers. Microcytic, iron-deficiency anemia confirms chronic blood loss but is nonspecific. Normal platelet count and standard coagulation times are expected because vWF disorders often do not prolong routine PT/PTT, so they cannot confirm the diagnosis. The elevated valve gradient confirms severe aortic stenosis (the cause) but not the bleeding diathesis itself. Multimer analysis is the specific confirmatory test.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: After stabilization with transfusion and iron, what is the definitive treatment most likely to resolve his recurrent GI bleeding?
options: Long-term octreotide infusions as monotherapy
Endoscopic ablation of every angiodysplastic lesion alone
Aortic valve replacement (surgical or transcatheter)
Lifelong scheduled blood transfusions as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Aortic valve replacement (surgical or transcatheter) is the definitive treatment: relieving the high transvalvular shear stress restores the normal high-molecular-weight vWF multimers, correcting the acquired coagulopathy, and the GI bleeding from angiodysplasia typically resolves. This patient now has documented symptoms (dyspnea, exertional lightheadedness) plus severe AS, so he meets criteria for valve replacement regardless of bleeding. Chronic transfusion is supportive only and does not fix the cause. Endoscopic ablation may temporarily control individual lesions but new angiodysplasias recur because the underlying vWF defect and shear stress persist. Octreotide can reduce angiodysplastic bleeding in some patients but is an adjunct, not a cure, and does not correct the valve lesion driving the syndrome.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: His echocardiogram shows severe AS and he now reports exertional symptoms. Why does this finding change his management urgency compared with his initial 'asymptomatic' designation?
options: Asymptomatic severe AS always requires immediate surgery regardless of symptoms with appropriate follow-up
His symptoms are unrelated to the valve and do not affect timing with appropriate follow-up
Symptoms make valve replacement contraindicated due to bleeding risk with appropriate follow-up
The onset of symptoms in severe AS markedly worsens prognosis
stages.explanation: The emergence of symptoms — dyspnea, angina, or syncope/presyncope — in severe aortic stenosis dramatically worsens prognosis (untreated symptomatic severe AS has a poor 2–3 year survival) and constitutes a Class I indication for aortic valve replacement. He was previously deemed asymptomatic, but his new exertional dyspnea and lightheadedness reclassify him and create urgency. Asymptomatic severe AS does not universally require immediate surgery; intervention depends on additional criteria (e.g., reduced EF, very severe AS, abnormal stress test). His acquired bleeding diathesis is a reason to fix the valve, not a contraindication. The symptoms arise directly from outflow obstruction and are central to timing the intervention.

stages.label: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
stages.question: Why does the high shear stress of severe aortic stenosis specifically cause loss of the largest von Willebrand factor multimers?
options: Antibodies against von Willebrand factor are produced in response to the valve in this setting
The liver stops synthesizing von Willebrand factor in aortic stenosis as the immediate next step
High shear stress unfolds large vWF multimers, exposing them to cleavage by the protease ADAMTS13
Calcium from the valve binds and inactivates von Willebrand factor as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: The turbulent, high-shear flow across a stenotic aortic valve mechanically unfolds the largest, most hemostatically active high-molecular-weight vWF multimers, exposing a cleavage site that is then cut by the plasma metalloprotease ADAMTS13. This selectively depletes the large multimers needed for platelet adhesion, producing an acquired type 2A von Willebrand syndrome and bleeding from fragile angiodysplastic vessels. Hepatic and endothelial synthesis of vWF is not shut off — total antigen is often normal. Valvular calcium does not directly bind or inactivate circulating vWF. This is a mechanical/proteolytic process, not an autoimmune one, so antibody-mediated destruction is incorrect. Relieving shear stress by replacing the valve restores the multimers and corrects the defect.

cases.diagnosis: Heyde syndrome — severe aortic stenosis with acquired von Willebrand syndrome and GI bleeding from angiodysplasia
pearls: Heyde syndrome links severe aortic stenosis, acquired (type 2A) von Willebrand disease, and bleeding from GI angiodysplasia; suspect it with recurrent unexplained GI bleeds and an AS murmur.

High transvalvular shear cleaves high-molecular-weight vWF multimers; the specific test is loss of these multimers with a reduced vWF activity-to-antigen ratio, despite often-normal PT/PTT and platelet count.
Aortic valve replacement is the definitive cure — it restores vWF multimers and resolves the bleeding; endoscopic ablation and octreotide are temporizing adjuncts.
Onset of symptoms in severe AS (dyspnea, angina, syncope) is a Class I indication for valve replacement and signals a steep drop in survival without intervention.

cases.title: Severe Valve, Weak Pump, Confusing Numbers
cases.chiefComplaint: Worsening fatigue and breathlessness with a calcified aortic valve and reduced heart function on echo.
cases.history: A 77-year-old man is referred to cardiology for progressive exertional dyspnea and fatigue over six months. An outside echocardiogram reported a small aortic valve area but a 'low gradient,' creating uncertainty about severity. He can now walk only half a block. He denies syncope but reports two episodes of exertional chest tightness. PMH: ischemic cardiomyopathy with reduced ejection fraction, prior anterior MI five years ago, hypertension, hyperlipidemia. Medications: carvedilol 12.5 mg twice daily, sacubitril–valsartan 49/51 mg twice daily, spironolactone 25 mg daily, atorvastatin 40 mg daily, furosemide 40 mg daily. Allergies: none. Social: former smoker (quit 5 years ago), occasional alcohol. Family history: father with coronary disease. Review of systems notable for orthopnea (two pillows) and mild ankle swelling. He is on guideline-directed medical therapy for heart failure but remains symptomatic. His referring physician is unsure whether the aortic valve disease is truly severe enough to warrant intervention given the low gradient.
cases.exam: Comfortable at rest. Cardiac: a soft mid-peaking systolic ejection murmur at the right upper sternal border, only grade 2/6, with normal-intensity but slightly diminished A2; displaced, sustained apical impulse; an audible S4. Carotid upstrokes mildly delayed. Lungs: faint bibasilar crackles. JVP mildly elevated at 8 cm. Abdomen soft, non-tender. Extremities: trace bilateral pedal edema. No cyanosis or clubbing. The murmur is notably softer than expected for severe aortic stenosis, a clue that low transvalvular flow may be masking severity.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The echo shows aortic valve area 0.9 cm² but a mean gradient of only 28 mmHg with an LV ejection fraction of 30%. How can a valve area in the severe range coexist with a low gradient?
options: The patient must have aortic regurgitation rather than stenosis as the first step today
A low gradient always means the stenosis is not severe as the first step today
The valve is only mildly stenotic and the small area is a measurement error as the first step today
Low transvalvular flow from reduced LV systolic function produces a low gradient despite a truly small valve area
stages.explanation: Gradient depends on flow: pressure gradient is proportional to the square of flow velocity across the valve. With a depressed ejection fraction (30%), stroke volume and transvalvular flow are reduced, so the gradient can be low (<40 mmHg) even when the valve area is genuinely severe (≤1.0 cm²). This is classic low-flow, low-gradient severe AS with reduced EF. A low gradient does not reliably exclude severe stenosis when flow is low — that reasoning is the trap. The discrepancy is not simply measurement error, and the murmur and valve findings indicate stenosis rather than regurgitation. The key is to recognize that low flow can mask true severity.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate test to distinguish true severe aortic stenosis from 'pseudo-severe' AS and to assess contractile reserve in this low-flow, low-gradient patient?
options: Repeat resting echocardiogram in three months
Exercise treadmill stress test
Low-dose dobutamine stress echocardiography
Coronary CT angiography in this clinical context
stages.explanation: Low-dose dobutamine stress echocardiography is the standard test in low-flow, low-gradient AS with reduced EF. By augmenting stroke volume, it distinguishes true severe AS (valve area stays ≤1.0 cm² while gradient/velocity rises into the severe range) from pseudo-severe AS (valve area increases >1.0 cm² as the weak ventricle opens a mildly diseased valve). It also assesses contractile reserve — an increase in stroke volume ≥20% — which carries prognostic and procedural significance. Exercise stress testing is contraindicated or unsafe in symptomatic severe AS. Coronary CT angiography evaluates coronary anatomy and valve calcium but does not resolve the flow-dependent severity question. Repeating a resting echo provides the same ambiguous data and delays diagnosis.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
BNP 720 pg/mL
Creatinine 1.3 mg/dL
Resting echo: AVA 0.9 cm², mean gradient 28 mmHg, peak velocity 3.4 m/s, EF 30%, stroke volume index 28 mL/m²
Dobutamine stress echo: with increasing dobutamine, stroke volume rises by ~25% (contractile reserve PRESENT); mean gradient increases to 46 mmHg and peak velocity to 4.2 m/s while valve area remains 0.9 cm²
CT aortic valve calcium score: markedly elevated, consistent with severe AS
Coronary angiography: patent prior territories, no new flow-limiting lesion requiring revascularization

stages.label: CONFIRMATION
stages.question: On dobutamine stress echo, the valve area stays at 0.9 cm² while the mean gradient rises to 46 mmHg and stroke volume increases by ~25%. How should these results be interpreted?
options: True severe AS with preserved contractile reserve
The test is uninterpretable and should be repeated
Mild AS that does not require intervention
Pseudo-severe AS with absent contractile reserve
stages.explanation: When dobutamine raises flow and the gradient climbs into the severe range (mean ≥40 mmHg, velocity ≥4 m/s) while the valve area remains fixed and small (≤1.0 cm²), the stenosis is truly severe — this is true severe AS, not pseudo-severe. The ≥20% rise in stroke volume demonstrates preserved contractile reserve, which is associated with better surgical outcomes. Pseudo-severe AS would show the valve area opening above 1.0 cm² with little gradient change. Mild AS is excluded by the fixed small area, severe-range stress gradient, and high calcium score. The test is clearly interpretable and confirms severe disease, supporting valve intervention.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given confirmed true severe low-flow, low-gradient AS with preserved contractile reserve and persistent symptoms on optimal heart failure therapy, what is the most appropriate management?
options: Aortic valve replacement (surgical or transcatheter)
Balloon aortic valvuloplasty as definitive therapy as monotherapy
Add a calcium channel blocker to lower the gradient as monotherapy
Continue medical therapy alone and avoid intervention because the EF is low
stages.explanation: Confirmed true severe symptomatic AS is an indication for aortic valve replacement (surgical or transcatheter) — relieving the obstruction can improve symptoms and often the ejection fraction, particularly when contractile reserve is preserved. A low EF caused by afterload from severe AS is a reason to fix the valve, not to withhold intervention; medical therapy alone will not relieve the fixed obstruction. Balloon aortic valvuloplasty provides only transient benefit and high restenosis rates, used mainly as a bridge or palliation, not as definitive therapy. Calcium channel blockers do not treat fixed valvular stenosis and could worsen hemodynamics in this patient. The Heart Team should select surgical versus transcatheter replacement based on risk.

stages.label: PROGNOSIS
stages.question: Which finding from his workup most favorably influences his expected outcome after aortic valve replacement?
options: His elevated BNP of 720 pg/mL as the immediate next step
The trace pedal edema on exam as the immediate next step
The presence of contractile reserve on dobutamine stress echo
His age of 77 years as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Demonstrated contractile reserve (a stroke-volume rise ≥20% during dobutamine) predicts lower operative mortality and greater likelihood of LV functional recovery after valve replacement, making it the most favorable prognostic feature here. An elevated BNP reflects severity of heart failure and is generally associated with worse, not better, outcomes. Advanced age increases procedural risk and tends to favor transcatheter over surgical approaches but is not a favorable prognostic factor. Trace pedal edema is a nonspecific sign of volume status and does not predict post-procedure recovery. Notably, even patients lacking contractile reserve may still benefit from valve replacement, but the presence of reserve is the more reassuring prognostic marker.

cases.diagnosis: Low-flow, low-gradient severe aortic stenosis with reduced ejection fraction
pearls: In low-flow, low-gradient severe AS, a depressed EF reduces transvalvular flow so the gradient is low (<40 mmHg) despite a truly small valve area (≤1.0 cm²) — a low gradient does not exclude severe AS.

Low-dose dobutamine stress echo distinguishes true severe AS (area stays ≤1.0 cm², gradient/velocity rises into severe range) from pseudo-severe AS (area opens >1.0 cm²) and assesses contractile reserve.
Contractile reserve (stroke volume increase ≥20%) predicts better surgical outcomes and LV recovery; its absence raises risk but does not necessarily preclude benefit.
Confirmed true severe symptomatic AS warrants aortic valve replacement; a low EF from valvular afterload is a reason to operate, not to withhold intervention.

cases.title: A Young Woman with a Murmur and a Click
cases.chiefComplaint: Asymptomatic, referred for evaluation of a heart murmur noted at a routine physical before starting graduate school.
cases.history: A 22-year-old woman is referred after a primary care physician heard a systolic murmur during a pre-enrollment physical. She is entirely asymptomatic — no chest pain, dyspnea, palpitations, syncope, or exercise limitation. She runs recreationally without difficulty. She has never been told of a heart problem before, though she recalls a 'murmur' as a child that was not investigated. PMH: none. Medications: combined oral contraceptive pill. Allergies: none. Social: graduate student, nonsmoker, occasional alcohol, no drugs. Family history: no congenital heart disease, no sudden cardiac death, no known syndromes. Review of systems negative. She specifically denies cyanotic episodes, squatting as a child, or recurrent respiratory infections. She is fit and active, and her main concern is whether the murmur will prevent her from participating in a half-marathon she has been training for. She has no symptoms of right heart failure and no history of rheumatic fever.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, comfortable young woman. Cardiac: a systolic ejection click at the left upper sternal border that DECREASES with inspiration; a crescendo–decrescendo systolic ejection murmur best heard at the left second–third intercostal space (pulmonic area), grade 3/6, radiating toward the left clavicle; widely split S2 with a soft, delayed P2. A palpable thrill at the left upper sternal border and a left parasternal lift. Lungs clear. Abdomen soft. No cyanosis, clubbing, or edema. Peripheral pulses normal and symmetric. JVP normal with a prominent a-wave. No hepatomegaly.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A systolic ejection click that decreases with inspiration, a pulmonic-area ejection murmur, and a widely split S2 with a soft P2 most strongly suggest which lesion?
options: Aortic stenosis
Mitral valve prolapse
Atrial septal defect
Pulmonary valve stenosis
stages.explanation: A right-sided ejection click that uniquely decreases with inspiration (because increased venous return pre-opens the mobile pulmonic valve, lessening the snap) plus a crescendo–decrescendo murmur maximal in the pulmonic area and a widely split S2 with a delayed, soft P2 are classic for pulmonary valve stenosis. Aortic stenosis produces a right-upper-sternal-border murmur radiating to the carotids and an aortic ejection click that does not vary with respiration. Mitral valve prolapse gives a mid-systolic click and late murmur that move with maneuvers altering LV volume, not an ejection click. An atrial septal defect causes a fixed (not widely-variable) split S2 and a flow murmur, without an ejection click or thrill. The inspiratory variation of the click is the giveaway.

stages.label: DIAGNOSTICS
stages.content:
ECG: right-axis deviation with evidence of right ventricular hypertrophy (tall R in V1)
Chest X-ray: normal heart size, post-stenotic dilation of the main pulmonary artery, normal pulmonary vascularity
Echocardiogram: doming, thickened pulmonic valve leaflets; peak instantaneous gradient 45 mmHg (moderate); normal RV size and systolic function; no significant pulmonic regurgitation; intact interatrial and interventricular septa

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Her ECG shows right-axis deviation and RV hypertrophy. Why are these findings expected in significant pulmonary valve stenosis?
options: Because an associated atrial septal defect causes left-axis deviation as the primary diagnosis
Because the left ventricle hypertrophies to overcome the obstruction as the primary diagnosis
Because chronic hypoxemia causes biventricular hypertrophy as the primary diagnosis
Because the right ventricle faces increased afterload pumping against the stenotic pulmonic valve
stages.explanation: In pulmonary valve stenosis, the right ventricle must generate higher pressures to eject blood across the narrowed valve, producing right ventricular hypertrophy and a resultant right-axis deviation on ECG. This is the expected electrical and structural consequence of chronic RV pressure overload. The left ventricle is not the chamber facing this obstruction, so LV hypertrophy is not the mechanism. Isolated pulmonic stenosis with a competent septum does not cause hypoxemia, and there is no biventricular hypertrophy here. There is no atrial septal defect in this patient (intact septa on echo), and ASD does not produce left-axis deviation in this scenario — right-axis deviation reflects the RV strain of the stenotic lesion.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Her peak gradient is 45 mmHg (moderate) and she is asymptomatic with normal RV function. What is the most appropriate management at this time?
options: Immediate surgical pulmonary valve replacement as initial therapy
Balloon pulmonary valvuloplasty now regardless of symptoms as initial therapy
Clinical and echocardiographic surveillance, reserving intervention for severe gradients or symptoms
Start a beta-blocker to reduce the gradient as initial therapy
stages.explanation: An asymptomatic patient with moderate pulmonic stenosis and preserved RV function is managed with periodic clinical and echocardiographic surveillance; intervention is deferred until criteria are met. Intervention (balloon valvuloplasty) is indicated for severe stenosis — typically a peak gradient ≥60 mmHg (or a peak velocity/mean gradient in the severe range) — or for symptoms attributable to the lesion. Surgical valve replacement is reserved for valves unsuitable for ballooning (e.g., dysplastic valves or significant regurgitation), not first-line for typical domed pulmonic stenosis. Beta-blockers do not relieve a fixed valvular gradient and are not standard therapy. Reassurance and surveillance, with attention to symptoms and progression, is appropriate here.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Two years later she develops exertional dyspnea, and her peak gradient has progressed to 68 mmHg with a typical mobile, domed (non-dysplastic) valve. What is the treatment of choice?
options: Surgical valve replacement with a mechanical prosthesis
Lifelong endocarditis antibiotic prophylaxis without intervention
Pulmonary vasodilator therapy as the immediate next step
Balloon pulmonary valvuloplasty
stages.explanation: Balloon pulmonary valvuloplasty is the treatment of choice for severe, symptomatic pulmonary valve stenosis with a typical mobile, domed valve — it is highly effective, durable, and less invasive than surgery. She now meets severe criteria (peak gradient ≥60 mmHg) plus symptoms. Surgical valve replacement is reserved for dysplastic valves, significant associated pulmonic regurgitation, or when valvuloplasty is unsuitable — not the first option for a classic domed valve. Endocarditis prophylaxis alone does not relieve the obstruction and is not routinely indicated for isolated native pulmonic stenosis. Pulmonary vasodilators treat pulmonary arterial hypertension, a different entity, and do nothing for a fixed valvular obstruction. Balloon valvuloplasty is the standard intervention.

cases.diagnosis: Pulmonary (pulmonic) valve stenosis
pearls: Pulmonary valve stenosis classically presents with a pulmonic-area ejection murmur and an ejection click that DECREASES with inspiration (the only right-sided sound that softens with inspiration), plus a widely split S2 with a soft P2.
RV pressure overload produces right-axis deviation and RV hypertrophy on ECG; the chest X-ray shows post-stenotic dilation of the main pulmonary artery.
Asymptomatic moderate stenosis with normal RV function is followed with surveillance; intervene for severe gradients (peak ≥60 mmHg) or symptoms.
Balloon pulmonary valvuloplasty is the treatment of choice for typical domed valves; surgery is reserved for dysplastic valves or significant pulmonic regurgitation.

cases.title: Pulsating Liver and Swollen Legs
cases.chiefComplaint: Progressive leg swelling, abdominal fullness, and fatigue over several months.
cases.history: A 70-year-old woman presents with several months of worsening bilateral leg swelling, abdominal bloating, and fatigue. She also notes early satiety and right-upper-quadrant discomfort. She has a long history of left-sided heart disease and atrial fibrillation. She denies fever, chest pain, or recent infection. Her dyspnea has been stable. PMH: hypertension, prior diastolic heart failure (HFpEF), long-standing atrial fibrillation, mitral regurgitation, prior smoking. Medications: apixaban 5 mg twice daily, metoprolol succinate 100 mg daily, furosemide 40 mg daily, lisinopril 10 mg daily. Allergies: sulfa (hives). Social: lives with daughter, former smoker (quit 15 years ago), no alcohol. Family history: hypertension. Review of systems notable for increasing abdominal girth and ankle swelling that worsens as the day progresses. She has needed escalating diuretic doses with limited relief. She has no jaundice, no melena, and her appetite is reduced mostly from abdominal fullness rather than nausea.
cases.exam: Comfortable at rest. JVP markedly elevated with prominent, large v-waves. Cardiac: irregularly irregular rhythm; a holosystolic murmur at the left lower sternal border that increases with inspiration (Carvallo sign); a right ventricular heave. Lungs clear. Abdomen: distended with a pulsatile, tender, enlarged liver; positive hepatojugular reflux; shifting dullness suggesting ascites. Extremities: 3+ pitting edema to the thighs bilaterally. No cyanosis. The combination of a pulsatile liver, giant v-waves, and an inspiratory-augmented holosystolic murmur points to the right-sided valve as the source of her systemic congestion.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A pulsatile hepatomegaly, giant v-waves in the JVP, and a holosystolic murmur that increases with inspiration are most characteristic of which valvular lesion?
options: Aortic stenosis
Mitral regurgitation
Severe tricuspid regurgitation
Pulmonary regurgitation
stages.explanation: Pulsatile hepatomegaly, prominent (giant) v-waves in the jugular venous pulse, and a holosystolic murmur at the left lower sternal border that increases with inspiration (Carvallo sign) are the classic triad of severe tricuspid regurgitation. Regurgitant flow into the right atrium and vena cava during systole produces the giant v-wave and the systolic pulsation of the liver. Mitral regurgitation produces an apical murmur radiating to the axilla and does not augment with inspiration or cause a pulsatile liver. Aortic stenosis is a systolic ejection murmur at the right upper sternal border with delayed carotid upstrokes. Pulmonary regurgitation is a diastolic murmur. The inspiratory augmentation localizes the lesion to the right heart.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
BNP 410 pg/mL
AST 60 U/L, ALT 55 U/L, total bilirubin 1.6 mg/dL, albumin 3.4 g/dL (congestive hepatopathy)
Creatinine 1.2 mg/dL
ECG: atrial fibrillation, right-axis deviation
Echo: severe functional tricuspid regurgitation with annular dilation, dilated right atrium and right ventricle; estimated PASP 52 mmHg; moderate mitral regurgitation; left atrial enlargement; LV ejection fraction 55%; structurally normal tricuspid leaflets (no rheumatic or vegetative changes)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The tricuspid leaflets are structurally normal but the annulus and RV are dilated, and there is pulmonary hypertension from left-heart disease and atrial fibrillation. What is the mechanism of her tricuspid regurgitation?
options: Infective endocarditis of the tricuspid valve as the primary diagnosis
Functional tricuspid regurgitation from RV/annular dilation due to left-heart disease, pulmonary hypertension,
Primary (organic) tricuspid valve disease from rheumatic scarring as the primary diagnosis
Carcinoid heart disease as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: With structurally normal leaflets but a dilated annulus and right ventricle, the regurgitation is functional (secondary): left-heart disease and atrial fibrillation raise pulmonary and right-heart pressures, the RV and tricuspid annulus dilate, and the leaflets fail to coapt — even though the valve itself is normal. Primary tricuspid disease (e.g., rheumatic) would show thickened, scarred, or restricted leaflets. Infective endocarditis would produce vegetations and typically fever, often in injection-drug users — absent here. Carcinoid heart disease causes leaflet thickening and retraction with characteristic plaque and is accompanied by flushing/diarrhea and elevated 5-HIAA. The normal leaflets plus annular/RV dilation in the setting of left-heart disease and AF defines functional TR.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial management for her symptomatic severe functional tricuspid regurgitation and systemic congestion?
options: Start a pulmonary vasodilator as monotherapy
Diuretics for volume overload plus treatment of the underlying left-heart disease, pulmonary hypertension,
Immediate isolated tricuspid valve surgery as initial therapy
Begin digoxin and discharge without further evaluation as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Functional TR is driven by the underlying conditions, so initial management targets the cause: diuretics (often with attention to aldosterone antagonists) relieve systemic venous congestion, and optimizing left-heart failure, controlling pulmonary hypertension, and managing atrial fibrillation (rate/rhythm control) can reduce RV and annular dilation and lessen the regurgitation. Isolated tricuspid surgery as a first step carries high operative risk and is generally reserved for severe symptomatic disease refractory to medical therapy, progressive RV dysfunction, or when performed alongside left-sided valve surgery; transcatheter options are emerging. Pulmonary vasodilator monotherapy is not appropriate for group 2 (left-heart) pulmonary hypertension and can worsen congestion. Digoxin without addressing congestion or the underlying disease is inadequate.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Her liver enzymes and bilirubin are mildly elevated with a pulsatile, tender liver. What does this most likely represent?
options: Acute viral hepatitis as the leading concern
Congestive hepatopathy from chronic systemic venous congestion
Drug-induced liver injury from apixaban as the leading concern
Choledocholithiasis with biliary obstruction
stages.explanation: Chronic elevation of systemic venous pressure from severe tricuspid regurgitation and right heart failure causes passive hepatic congestion (congestive hepatopathy), producing a tender, pulsatile, enlarged liver with mildly elevated transaminases and bilirubin and, over time, hypoalbuminemia and ascites. Acute viral hepatitis typically causes markedly higher transaminases and is unrelated to her cardiac congestion. Choledocholithiasis produces an obstructive pattern with high alkaline phosphatase, often colicky pain and a non-pulsatile liver, not the venous-congestion picture. Apixaban is not a typical cause of hepatocellular injury and does not explain a pulsatile liver. Relieving congestion with diuresis and treating the underlying disease usually improves the hepatic findings.

cases.diagnosis: Severe functional (secondary) tricuspid regurgitation
pearls: Severe tricuspid regurgitation classically causes pulsatile hepatomegaly, giant v-waves in the JVP, and a holosystolic murmur that increases with inspiration (Carvallo sign).
Functional (secondary) TR has structurally normal leaflets with annular/RV dilation, usually driven by left-heart disease, pulmonary hypertension, and atrial fibrillation.

Initial management targets the underlying cause and congestion (diuretics, treat left-heart failure and pulmonary hypertension, control AF); isolated tricuspid surgery is high-risk and reserved for refractory or progressive disease.
Chronic systemic venous congestion causes congestive hepatopathy — a tender, pulsatile liver with mild transaminase and bilirubin elevation that often improves with decongestion.

cases.title: A Heart Attack at 41 with Normal Cholesterol
cases.chiefComplaint: Follow-up after a recent heart attack, seeking to understand why it happened despite a healthy lifestyle.
cases.history: A 41-year-old man presents for cardiology follow-up six weeks after a non-ST-elevation myocardial infarction treated with a drug-eluting stent to the LAD. He is troubled because he exercises regularly, eats well, has never smoked, and was told his cholesterol was 'normal.' He has no diabetes or hypertension. His father had a myocardial infarction at age 45 and a paternal uncle had a stroke in his early 50s. PMH: recent NSTEMI. Medications: aspirin 81 mg daily, ticagrelor 90 mg twice daily, atorvastatin 80 mg daily, metoprolol 50 mg daily, lisinopril 10 mg daily. Allergies: none. Social: software engineer, never-smoker, rare alcohol, runs three times weekly. Family history: premature coronary disease (father MI at 45) and a paternal uncle with early stroke; no known familial hypercholesterolemia diagnosis. Review of systems is otherwise negative. He wants to know what additional risk factor explains his early event and how to reduce his future risk, since his LDL was not strikingly high.
cases.exam: Fit, well-appearing man in no distress. Cardiac: regular rate and rhythm, normal S1 and S2, no murmurs, rubs, or gallops. Lungs clear bilaterally. No xanthelasma, no tendon xanthomas, no corneal arcus. Abdomen soft, non-tender, no bruits. Peripheral pulses 2+ and symmetric, no carotid bruits. No peripheral edema. Healed radial access site from catheterization. Body habitus lean, BMI 23. The absence of tendon xanthomas and arcus makes overt familial hypercholesterolemia phenotype less obvious, prompting a look for other inherited atherogenic drivers.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: He had a premature MI with a strong family history of early cardiovascular disease but only modestly elevated LDL. Which inherited factor best explains his residual atherogenic risk?
options: Elevated lipoprotein(a) [Lp(a)]
Low HDL cholesterol alone
Mildly elevated fasting glucose
Elevated homocysteine as the primary driver
stages.explanation: Lipoprotein(a) is a genetically determined, LDL-like particle carrying apolipoprotein(a) that is independently atherogenic and prothrombotic. Elevated Lp(a) is a leading explanation for premature coronary disease with a strong family history despite near-normal LDL, and it is the most common monogenic-like lipid disorder linked to early events. Low HDL is associated with risk but is a weaker, less specific explanation and is not a strong causal target. Mildly elevated glucose alone does not explain a premature MI in a lean, non-diabetic patient. Homocysteine elevations have not proven to be a major causal, treatable driver of coronary events in trials. Lp(a), being heritable and atherogenic, best reframes his prevention strategy.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Total cholesterol 168 mg/dL
LDL-C 78 mg/dL (on high-intensity statin)
HDL-C 48 mg/dL
Triglycerides 110 mg/dL
Lipoprotein(a) 180 nmol/L (markedly elevated; normal <75 nmol/L)
Hemoglobin A1c 5.3%
Apolipoprotein B 92 mg/dL
hs-CRP 1.2 mg/L

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: His Lp(a) is markedly elevated at 180 nmol/L. Which statement about lipoprotein(a) is correct and clinically relevant here?
options: Statins are highly effective at lowering Lp(a) as the primary diagnosis
Lp(a) is only relevant in patients who also have diabetes as the primary diagnosis
Lp is largely genetically determined
Lp(a) levels fluctuate widely day to day and are mainly diet-dependent as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Lp(a) concentration is roughly 80–90% genetically determined, remains relatively stable across life, and is minimally affected by diet or lifestyle — so a single measurement generally suffices for risk assessment. Elevated Lp(a) independently increases risk of atherosclerotic events and calcific aortic stenosis. It does not fluctuate widely day to day. Statins do NOT reliably lower Lp(a) and may modestly raise it, although statins remain essential for lowering LDL and overall ASCVD risk. Lp(a) risk is not confined to diabetics; it is an independent risk marker across populations. Recognizing this reframes prevention toward aggressive LDL/apoB lowering and emerging Lp(a)-directed therapies, plus cascade screening of relatives.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given his elevated Lp(a) and very high cardiovascular risk after an MI, what is the most appropriate management focus now?
options: Start high-dose niacin to lower Lp(a) as initial therapy
Intensify LDL/apoB lowering toward aggressive targets
Rely on diet and exercise alone to lower Lp(a) as initial therapy
Stop the statin since LDL is already below 100 as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Because there is no widely approved therapy yet that lowers Lp(a) with proven outcome benefit, the cornerstone is aggressively reducing the modifiable atherogenic burden: maximize LDL/apoB lowering (high-intensity statin, add ezetimibe and/or a PCSK9 inhibitor to reach very-low LDL targets, e.g., <55 mg/dL in very-high-risk patients), control all other risk factors, and screen first-degree relatives because Lp(a) is heritable. Stopping the statin is dangerous in a post-MI patient. Niacin lowers Lp(a) modestly but lacks outcome benefit and has adverse effects, so it is not recommended. Diet and exercise do not meaningfully lower genetically determined Lp(a). Novel RNA-based Lp(a)-lowering agents are in trials but not yet standard of care.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: Why is cascade screening of his first-degree relatives recommended?
options: Because Lp(a) screening is only useful in symptomatic relatives as the immediate next step
Because relatives will all need stents as the immediate next step
Because Lp is heritable, so relatives may have elevated levels
Because Lp(a) is contagious as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Lp(a) is inherited in a largely autosomal codominant fashion, so relatives of a person with markedly elevated Lp(a) are at higher likelihood of also having elevated levels and therefore unrecognized premature atherosclerotic risk. Identifying them early allows aggressive risk-factor modification and intensive LDL/apoB lowering before an event occurs. Lp(a) is not infectious. Relatives do not automatically need stents; the goal is risk stratification and prevention, not procedures. Screening is valuable in asymptomatic relatives precisely because it detects risk before symptoms develop. Cascade testing leverages the heritable nature of Lp(a) to extend prevention to the family, which is why it is recommended after identifying an affected individual.

cases.diagnosis: Premature coronary artery disease driven by elevated lipoprotein(a)
pearls: Suspect elevated lipoprotein(a) in premature coronary disease with a strong family history despite normal or only modestly elevated LDL.
Lp(a) is largely genetically determined and stable, so one measurement usually suffices; elevated levels independently raise risk of atherosclerotic events and calcific aortic stenosis.
Statins do not reliably lower Lp(a); management centers on aggressive LDL/apoB lowering (statin plus ezetimibe and/or PCSK9 inhibitor) and controlling all other risk factors.
Because Lp(a) is heritable, perform cascade screening of first-degree relatives to identify and treat unrecognized premature cardiovascular risk early.

cases.title: Chest Pain in a New Mother
cases.chiefComplaint: Severe, crushing chest pain radiating to the left arm three weeks after delivering her first child.
cases.history: A 39-year-old woman presents to the ED with sudden, severe substernal chest pressure radiating to her left arm, with diaphoresis and nausea, that began an hour ago at rest while caring for her newborn. She delivered her first child three weeks ago via uncomplicated vaginal delivery. She has no diabetes, hypertension, or hyperlipidemia and has never smoked. She does not use cocaine or other drugs. PMH: recent term pregnancy, mild fibromuscular features noted incidentally on a prior renal imaging study. Medications: prenatal vitamins. Allergies: none. Social: marketing manager, never-smoker, no alcohol while breastfeeding, no drugs. Family history: no premature coronary disease. Review of systems negative for fever, cough, or leg swelling. She is frightened and emphasizes she has 'no reason' to have a heart attack given her youth, fitness, and absence of traditional risk factors. The peripartum timing and lack of atherosclerotic risk factors are notable and should shape the differential.
cases.exam: Anxious woman in moderate distress, diaphoretic. Cardiac: regular rhythm, normal S1 and S2, no murmurs or gallops, no rubs. Lungs clear bilaterally. No JVD. Abdomen soft, gravid uterus involuting, non-tender. Extremities warm, well-perfused, 2+ symmetric pulses, no edema or calf tenderness. No carotid or abdominal bruits appreciated on this exam. Neurologic exam normal. No signs of heart failure. She remains hemodynamically stable but with ongoing chest pain during evaluation.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: She has chest pain with diaphoresis. What is the most appropriate immediate initial step?
options: Reassure her that MI is unlikely given her age and discharge
Start empiric thrombolytics for presumed STEMI before any testing
Order a CT pulmonary angiogram first to rule out PE
Obtain a 12-lead ECG and troponin immediately while giving aspirin
stages.explanation: Any patient with acute chest pain and features suggestive of ischemia needs an immediate 12-lead ECG and troponin, with aspirin given while acute coronary syndrome is evaluated — this is true regardless of age. Reassurance and discharge would be dangerous and ignores a real, life-threatening possibility; young women can and do have MIs. A CT pulmonary angiogram targets pulmonary embolism, which is also a peripartum concern, but the priority is to first capture the ECG and biomarkers for ACS in a patient with classic ischemic symptoms. Empiric thrombolytics before any ECG/biomarkers and before defining the mechanism are inappropriate and potentially harmful, especially given the differential includes coronary dissection. The ECG/troponin/aspirin step comes first.

stages.label: INITIAL WORKUP
stages.content:
ECG: ST-segment elevation in leads II, III, aVF (inferior)
Troponin T 0.85 ng/mL (elevated, rising)
Hemoglobin 12.6 g/dL
Creatinine 0.8 mg/dL
Urine pregnancy test: negative (delivered 3 weeks ago)
Bedside echo: inferior wall hypokinesis, EF 50%, no pericardial effusion
Patient taken urgently for coronary angiography

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: At angiography, the right coronary artery shows a long, smooth, tapering narrowing with a radiolucent intimal flap and contrast staining within the wall, rather than a discrete atherosclerotic plaque. In this peripartum woman with no risk factors, what is the diagnosis?
options: Coronary vasospasm as the primary diagnosis
Atherosclerotic plaque rupture
Spontaneous coronary artery dissection
Coronary embolism from a cardiac thrombus
stages.explanation: A long, smooth, tapering stenosis with an intramural hematoma or intimal flap and contrast staining in the vessel wall — in a young, peripartum woman without atherosclerotic risk factors and with features such as fibromuscular dysplasia — is characteristic of spontaneous coronary artery dissection. SCAD results from a tear or intramural hematoma that compresses the true lumen, not from plaque. Atherosclerotic plaque rupture shows irregular, calcified, focal lesions with thrombus in a patient who usually has risk factors. Vasospasm produces transient, reversible narrowing that resolves with nitroglycerin, not a fixed flap or intramural hematoma. Coronary embolism shows an abrupt cutoff with intraluminal filling defect and a source (e.g., AF, endocarditis), absent here. Recognizing SCAD fundamentally changes management.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She is hemodynamically stable with preserved distal flow (TIMI 3) in the dissected RCA and resolving pain. What is the preferred management strategy for SCAD in this setting?
options: Routine stenting of the entire dissected segment as initial therapy
Emergency coronary artery bypass grafting for all SCAD as initial therapy
Conservative medical management with close monitoring, reserving revascularization for ongoing ischemia or instability
Full-dose thrombolytics to dissolve the obstruction as initial therapy
stages.explanation: For hemodynamically stable SCAD with preserved flow, conservative management with close inpatient monitoring is preferred because most dissections heal spontaneously over weeks, and intervention carries higher risk of propagating the dissection. Revascularization (PCI or, in select cases, CABG) is reserved for ongoing or recurrent ischemia, hemodynamic instability, left main or proximal multivessel involvement, or ventricular arrhythmia. Routine stenting is discouraged: manipulating a dissected, fragile vessel can extend the hematoma and stents often malposition as the hematoma resorbs. Emergency CABG is not first-line for stable single-vessel SCAD. Thrombolytics are contraindicated in SCAD — they can worsen intramural hemorrhage and extend the dissection, and the problem is not intraluminal thrombus.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: While monitored on telemetry during conservative management, she suddenly develops recurrent severe chest pain with new, persistent ST elevation and hypotension. What is the most appropriate response?
options: Discharge her on a beta-blocker with outpatient follow-up as the leading concern
Reassess urgently with repeat angiography
Administer full-dose thrombolytics immediately as the leading concern
Continue conservative management and increase analgesia as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Although conservative management is preferred for stable SCAD, the development of hemodynamic instability and ongoing/recurrent ischemia (here, persistent ST elevation with hypotension) signals propagation or compromise of flow and mandates urgent reassessment with repeat angiography and revascularization — PCI when feasible, or CABG for unfavorable anatomy or failure of PCI. Simply increasing analgesia ignores active, evolving infarction and shock. Thrombolytics remain contraindicated in SCAD because they can extend the intramural hematoma and worsen the dissection. Discharging an unstable, actively ischemic patient is unsafe. The key concept is that the conservative-versus-invasive decision in SCAD is dynamic: stability allows watchful waiting, but instability or ongoing ischemia triggers intervention.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Beyond acute care, which evaluation and counseling point is most appropriate for this patient with SCAD?
options: Screen for extracoronary fibromuscular dysplasia
Initiate lifelong high-intensity statin and dual antiplatelet therapy identical to atherosclerotic ACS for all patients
Reassure her there is no recurrence risk and no need for follow-up with appropriate follow-up
Recommend immediate coronary CT calcium scoring to confirm atherosclerosis with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: SCAD is strongly associated with fibromuscular dysplasia, so screening the extracoronary vasculature (e.g., renal, iliac, carotid, cerebral arteries) for FMD and aneurysms is recommended. Patients should be counseled that SCAD can recur and that subsequent pregnancy carries an elevated risk of recurrent SCAD, informing reproductive counseling. Management is NOT identical to atherosclerotic ACS — statins are not routinely indicated unless there is another indication, and the role/duration of antiplatelet therapy differs and is individualized. Telling her there is no recurrence risk is false and unsafe. Calcium scoring to 'confirm atherosclerosis' is inappropriate because her event was a dissection, not plaque-mediated disease. Cardiac rehabilitation and avoidance of extreme exertion and hormonal triggers are also appropriate.

cases.diagnosis: Spontaneous coronary artery dissection (SCAD)
pearls: Suspect SCAD in young or peripartum women with acute MI and few or no traditional atherosclerotic risk factors; angiography shows a smooth tapering narrowing with intramural hematoma or intimal flap, not plaque.
Hemodynamically stable SCAD with preserved flow is managed conservatively because most dissections heal spontaneously; intervention risks extending the dissection.
Avoid thrombolytics in SCAD — they can worsen intramural hemorrhage; reserve PCI/CABG for ongoing ischemia, instability, or high-risk anatomy.
SCAD is linked to fibromuscular dysplasia — screen extracoronary arteries — and counsel patients on recurrence risk and the elevated risk of future pregnancy.

cases.title: Chest Pain and a Positive Troponin, Clean Arteries
cases.chiefComplaint: Recurrent episodes of chest pain at rest with a positive troponin but no blockages found on catheterization.
cases.history: A 47-year-old woman is admitted after recurrent episodes of substernal chest pain, several occurring at rest and a few in the early morning hours. One prolonged episode prompted ED evaluation, where troponin was elevated and the ECG showed transient ST changes that resolved. She underwent coronary angiography that revealed no obstructive coronary disease. She has migraines and Raynaud phenomenon. She is a current smoker. She denies cocaine use. PMH: migraine with aura, Raynaud phenomenon, anxiety. Medications: oral contraceptive pill, sumatriptan PRN for migraines, no regular cardiac medications. Allergies: none. Social: current smoker (one pack daily for 25 years), social alcohol, denies illicit drugs. Family history: mother with coronary disease in her 60s. Review of systems notable for episodic chest pain that sometimes wakes her from sleep and resolves spontaneously within 15 minutes. The combination of rest/nocturnal angina, smoking, migraine, and Raynaud raises suspicion for a vasomotor coronary disorder despite angiographically normal vessels.
cases.exam: Comfortable and pain-free at the time of exam. Cardiac: regular rate and rhythm, normal S1 and S2, no murmurs, rubs, or gallops. Lungs clear bilaterally. No JVD. Abdomen soft, non-tender. Extremities warm with intact pulses; cool fingertips with mild color change reported on cold exposure. No peripheral edema, no calf tenderness. During one monitored episode of chest pain on the ward, telemetry captured transient ST-segment elevation in the inferior leads that resolved as the pain subsided spontaneously.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: She has troponin-positive chest pain with transient ST changes but non-obstructive coronaries on angiography. What is the correct overarching term for this clinical entity?
options: Stable exertional angina as the first step today
Non-cardiac chest pain as the first step today
Takotsubo cardiomyopathy by definition in this clinical context
Myocardial infarction with non-obstructive coronary arteries
stages.explanation: MINOCA describes a myocardial infarction (ischemic symptoms plus a rise/fall in troponin meeting MI criteria) in the setting of angiographically non-obstructive coronary arteries (<50% stenosis). It is a working diagnosis that mandates a search for the underlying mechanism. Stable exertional angina implies reproducible exertional symptoms with obstructive disease and would not explain a troponin-positive rest event. Takotsubo (stress) cardiomyopathy is one possible cause within the MINOCA differential but cannot be assumed without characteristic apical ballooning and a trigger; it is not synonymous with the term. Non-cardiac chest pain is excluded by the elevated troponin and dynamic ECG changes. Labeling it MINOCA correctly frames the next diagnostic steps to find the cause.

stages.label: MONITORING & LABS
stages.content:
Troponin T peak 0.42 ng/mL, now down-trending
ECG during pain: transient inferior ST-segment elevation, fully resolved between episodes
Lipid panel: LDL 110 mg/dL, HDL 50 mg/dL
Hemoglobin 13.5 g/dL, TSH normal
Urine toxicology: negative for cocaine and amphetamines
Echocardiogram: normal LV size and systolic function, no apical ballooning, no wall-motion abnormality at rest
Coronary angiography (reviewed): no stenosis >30% in any vessel, no dissection, no thrombus

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given transient ST-segment elevation during rest/nocturnal pain that resolves spontaneously, with clean coronaries and negative toxicology, which mechanism is most likely?
options: Demand ischemia from anemia
Pulmonary embolism as the primary diagnosis
Aortic dissection as the primary diagnosis
Coronary artery vasospasm
stages.explanation: Rest and nocturnal/early-morning angina with transient ST-segment elevation that resolves spontaneously, in a smoker with migraine and Raynaud (vasomotor) phenomena and non-obstructive coronaries, is classic for coronary artery vasospasm (Prinzmetal/variant angina). Transient ST elevation reflects reversible transmural ischemia from focal spasm. Demand ischemia from anemia is excluded by a normal hemoglobin and would not cause focal ST elevation at rest. Pulmonary embolism causes dyspnea, tachycardia, and right-heart strain patterns, not transient inferior ST elevation that resolves with pain. Aortic dissection causes tearing pain with pulse/BP differentials and a widened mediastinum, not self-resolving ST elevation with clean coronaries. The vasospastic pattern fits the picture best.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: To confirm the mechanism in MINOCA when vasospasm or microvascular dysfunction is suspected, which approach is most appropriate?
options: Functional/provocative coronary testing
Immediate coronary artery bypass grafting as the immediate next step
Empiric long-term anticoagulation as the immediate next step
Repeat standard coronary angiography only as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: MINOCA is a call to identify the mechanism. Invasive functional testing — acetylcholine or ergonovine provocation to elicit spasm and measurement of coronary flow reserve/microvascular resistance — can confirm epicardial vasospasm or microvascular dysfunction, while cardiac MRI helps distinguish infarction from myocarditis or Takotsubo and detects edema/scar. Simply repeating standard angiography adds little because the epicardial vessels are already known to be non-obstructive and the question is functional. Empiric anticoagulation does not address a vasomotor mechanism and adds bleeding risk without diagnostic value. CABG is illogical with non-obstructive coronaries. Pivoting to functional and imaging studies is precisely how the MINOCA workup proceeds once obstructive plaque, dissection, and thrombus are excluded.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
