stages.explanation: White coat hypertension without target-organ damage or compelling cardiovascular risk is not treated with antihypertensive drugs — trials show treatment guided by office readings in these patients yields no outcome benefit while exposing them to hypotension, cost, and side effects. Correct management is healthy lifestyle reinforcement (her habits are already good: normal BMI, regular exercise, low alcohol, sensible diet, modest sodium) plus continued periodic out-of-office monitoring to detect any transition to sustained hypertension. Starting amlodipine could drop her true daytime pressure of 122/77 into symptomatic hypotension. Abandoning surveillance for a decade ignores that her phenotype carries an elevated risk of converting to sustained hypertension, particularly with a hypertensive mother. Pre-visit anxiolytics medicate the clinic encounter rather than the patient's health — relaxation techniques and automated unattended office measurement are reasonable, but a benzodiazepine prescription for checkups is not standard care.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP
stages.question: Which statement about her long-term outlook is most accurate?
options: She cannot develop sustained hypertension in the future as the immediate next step
She is at higher risk of progressing to sustained hypertension than normotensive individuals, so periodic out-of-office monitoring every 1–2 years is warranted
Her office readings should be permanently disregarded as the immediate next step
White coat hypertension carries identical risk to sustained hypertension, so drug therapy is still mandatory as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: White coat hypertension is not entirely benign: cohort data show these patients progress to sustained hypertension at substantially higher rates than truly normotensive people — roughly 1 to 5 percent per year — and untreated white coat hypertension carries a modestly increased long-term cardiovascular risk. The practical consequence is surveillance: repeat out-of-office assessment (home monitoring or ABPM) every one to two years, with annual office checks, so any conversion is caught early. Saying her risk equals sustained hypertension overstates it and would wrongly mandate drugs; her cardiovascular risk sits between normotension and sustained hypertension. Permanently disregarding office readings discards useful data — persistent office elevation is precisely what flags her for ongoing monitoring. And asserting she can never develop true hypertension contradicts the natural history, especially with her family history; reassurance should be paired with a concrete follow-up plan, not a promise of immunity.

cases.diagnosis: White coat hypertension
pearls: Never diagnose hypertension or start lifelong therapy on office readings alone — confirm out-of-office with ABPM (reference standard) or home monitoring.
White coat HTN = elevated office, normal out-of-office; masked HTN is the reverse and carries higher untreated risk.
ABPM thresholds: daytime mean ≥130/80, 24-hour mean ≥125/75 mmHg (ACC/AHA).
White coat hypertension progresses to sustained hypertension in many patients — lifestyle measures plus out-of-office re-checks every 1–2 years.

cases.title: Pounding heart after a long weekend
cases.chiefComplaint: My heart has been racing and flopping around in my chest since I woke up this morning.
cases.history: A 30-year-old man presents Monday morning with palpitations that began abruptly around 6 AM, about six hours ago. He describes an irregular fluttering sensation with mild lightheadedness but denies chest pain, dyspnea, syncope, or diaphoresis. He spent the weekend at a bachelor party where he drank heavily — roughly 12 beers plus multiple shots on both Friday and Saturday nights, with several more drinks and energy drinks on Sunday — and slept fewer than five hours each night. He has never had palpitations before. He has no known medical problems, takes no medications or supplements, and has no allergies. He works as a software engineer and normally drinks two to three beers per week. He does not smoke or vape, denies cocaine, amphetamine, or other stimulant use, and uses marijuana once or twice a month. Family history is notable for hypertension in his father; there is no family history of sudden cardiac death, cardiomyopathy, or arrhythmia. Review of systems is negative for fever, weight loss, heat intolerance, tremor, and recent illness.
cases.exam: Anxious but well-appearing young man in no acute distress. Cardiac exam reveals an irregularly irregular tachycardia with variable intensity of S1; no murmurs, rubs, or gallops. Lungs are clear to auscultation bilaterally. JVP is not elevated and there is no peripheral edema. Thyroid is normal in size without nodules; no tremor, lid lag, or proptosis. No stigmata of chronic liver disease. Peripheral pulses are irregular but strong and symmetric. Neurologic exam is grossly intact.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which of the following is the best initial step to characterize this patient's rhythm?
options: Order serum troponin and observe on telemetry before any other testing
Send a urine drug screen and discharge if negative
Administer adenosine 6 mg IV push empirically
Obtain an urgent transthoracic echocardiogram
Obtain a 12-lead ECG
stages.explanation: A 12-lead ECG is the essential first step in any patient with palpitations and an irregular pulse — it defines the rhythm, rate, QRS width, and screens for pre-excitation or ischemia, all of which drive subsequent management. Troponin and telemetry alone delay rhythm diagnosis and troponin is low-yield in a young man without chest pain. Echocardiography is important later to assess for structural disease but does not define the rhythm and should not precede the ECG. Adenosine is used diagnostically or therapeutically in regular narrow-complex tachycardias; this pulse is irregularly irregular, and empiric adenosine before an ECG is inappropriate and potentially dangerous if pre-excited atrial fibrillation is present. A urine drug screen may be a reasonable adjunct given the history, but it neither diagnoses the rhythm nor justifies discharge.

stages.label: ECG, LABS & ECHO
stages.content:
12-lead ECG: atrial fibrillation, ventricular rate 128 bpm, no ST-segment deviation, no delta wave or pre-excitation, QTc 410 ms
TSH 1.8 µIU/mL
K 3.6 mEq/L
Mg 1.9 mg/dL
Troponin I <0.01 ng/mL
Hgb 15.1 g/dL
Creatinine 0.9 mg/dL
Serum ethanol 12 mg/dL
Transthoracic echocardiogram: normal LV size and systolic function (EF 60%), no valvular disease, left atrium normal size, no pericardial effusion

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely explanation for this patient's arrhythmia?
options: Thyrotoxicosis-induced atrial fibrillation as the primary diagnosis
Alcohol-triggered atrial fibrillation in a structurally normal heart
Pre-excited atrial fibrillation due to Wolff-Parkinson-White syndrome
Atrial fibrillation from underlying dilated cardiomyopathy in this clinical context
Cocaine-induced supraventricular tachycardia as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: New-onset atrial fibrillation in a young patient after an acute alcohol binge, with a structurally normal heart on echo and normal thyroid function, is classic holiday heart syndrome. Acute alcohol excess shortens atrial refractoriness, increases sympathetic tone, and is compounded here by sleep deprivation and energy drinks. Thyrotoxicosis is excluded by the normal TSH and the absence of hyperthyroid signs. WPW is excluded because the ECG shows no delta wave or short PR, and pre-excited AF typically produces a wide, bizarre, very rapid rhythm. Cocaine-induced arrhythmia is unlikely given his explicit denial of stimulants and the dominant alcohol history, and SVT would be regular. Dilated cardiomyopathy is ruled out by the normal LV size and ejection fraction of 60% on echocardiography.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He remains hemodynamically stable with a rate of 128 bpm. Symptom onset was approximately 6 hours ago. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Immediate synchronized electrical cardioversion as monotherapy
Start an amiodarone infusion for chemical cardioversion
Initiate lifelong apixaban before anything else as monotherapy
Refer for same-day catheter ablation as initial therapy
Rate control with IV metoprolol
stages.explanation: In a hemodynamically stable patient with alcohol-triggered AF of clear onset under 48 hours, rate control (a beta-blocker or nondihydropyridine calcium channel blocker) plus observation is appropriate, because a majority of holiday heart episodes convert spontaneously within 24 hours as the adrenergic and alcohol effects wear off. Immediate electrical cardioversion is reserved for hemodynamic instability, ongoing ischemia, or acute heart failure — none of which is present. Amiodarone carries substantial toxicity and is a poor first choice in a young patient likely to self-convert. Lifelong anticoagulation is not indicated before risk stratification; his CHA2DS2-VASc score is 0, and peri-conversion anticoagulation decisions differ from chronic therapy. Catheter ablation is a consideration for recurrent symptomatic AF refractory to or intolerant of medical therapy, not for a first reversible-trigger episode.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Eight hours later he spontaneously converts to sinus rhythm at 72 bpm and feels back to normal. His CHA2DS2-VASc score is 0. What is the best discharge plan?
options: Discharge with a flecainide 'pill-in-the-pocket' prescription with appropriate follow-up
Counsel on alcohol moderation and sleep
Discharge on apixaban for 3 months 'just to be safe' with appropriate follow-up
Schedule elective electrical cardioversion in 48 hours with appropriate follow-up
Start daily metoprolol indefinitely with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: The cornerstone of management for holiday heart is trigger modification: counsel on reducing or abstaining from binge alcohol, improving sleep, and limiting stimulants such as energy drinks. With a CHA2DS2-VASc score of 0, a structurally normal heart, and spontaneous conversion, neither long-term anticoagulation nor chronic antiarrhythmic or rate-control therapy is indicated. Pill-in-the-pocket flecainide is an option for selected patients with recurrent paroxysmal AF, not a first episode with an obvious reversible trigger. Three months of apixaban has no evidence basis here and exposes him to bleeding risk without stroke-risk benefit. Elective cardioversion is moot — he is already in sinus rhythm. Indefinite metoprolol treats a problem that has resolved and would not address the underlying cause; follow-up with primary care and return precautions for recurrent palpitations complete the plan.

cases.diagnosis: Holiday heart syndrome — alcohol-triggered paroxysmal atrial fibrillation in a structurally normal heart
pearls: Holiday heart = new atrial fibrillation after acute alcohol excess in a structurally normal heart; most episodes self-convert within 24 hours.
Always get a 12-lead ECG first in palpitations — an irregularly irregular rhythm without P waves is AF, and you must rule out pre-excitation before AV-nodal blockers.
With CHA2DS2-VASc 0 and a reversible trigger, neither lifelong anticoagulation nor chronic antiarrhythmics are indicated after a single self-terminating episode.
Alcohol reduction is disease-modifying in AF — abstinence significantly lowers recurrence, so counseling is the highest-yield 'prescription' at discharge.

cases.title: Crushing chest pain while mowing the lawn
cases.chiefComplaint: It feels like an elephant is sitting on my chest — it started an hour and a half ago and won't let up.
cases.history: A 58-year-old man is brought in by EMS with 90 minutes of severe, crushing substernal chest pain that began while he was mowing his lawn. The pain is 9/10, pressure-like, radiates to his left arm and jaw, and is accompanied by diaphoresis and nausea. It has been constant since onset and is not affected by position or breathing. He has never had pain like this before, though he recalls milder chest tightness climbing stairs over the past month that resolved with rest. Past medical history includes hypertension treated with lisinopril 20 mg daily and hyperlipidemia for which he stopped taking atorvastatin a year ago. He has a 30-pack-year smoking history and continues to smoke a pack per day. He drinks two beers most evenings. He has no known drug allergies and takes no other medications, including no recent sildenafil use. Family history is significant for his father suffering a fatal myocardial infarction at age 55 and a brother with coronary stents at 60. EMS administered 325 mg of chewed aspirin en route.
cases.exam: Diaphoretic, anxious man clutching his chest, in obvious distress. JVP is not elevated. Heart is tachycardic and regular with an audible S4; no murmurs or rubs, no S3. Lungs have faint bibasilar crackles but are otherwise clear. Abdomen is soft and nontender. Extremities are warm with symmetric radial and femoral pulses and no pulse deficit; blood pressure is similar in both arms. No lower-extremity edema. Chest wall is nontender, and pain is not reproduced by palpation or inspiration.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most important immediate diagnostic step on arrival?
options: Give sublingual nitroglycerin and reassess the pain in 30 minutes
Obtain and interpret a 12-lead ECG within 10 minutes of arrival
Order a stat CT pulmonary angiogram as the first step today
Send a troponin and wait for the result before further decisions
Perform a bedside echocardiogram before any other testing
stages.explanation: Guidelines mandate a 12-lead ECG interpreted within 10 minutes of first medical contact for any patient with suspected acute coronary syndrome, because ST-elevation myocardial infarction is a time-critical, ECG-based diagnosis that triggers immediate reperfusion — you do not wait for biomarkers. Troponin takes an hour or more to result and may be normal early in STEMI; waiting on it delays reperfusion. CT pulmonary angiography targets pulmonary embolism, which is far less likely than ACS given exertional crushing pain, radiation to the arm and jaw, and his heavy risk-factor burden. Nitroglycerin may be given as an adjunct but is symptomatic therapy, not a diagnostic strategy, and 'reassessing in 30 minutes' wastes myocardium. Bedside echo can show wall-motion abnormalities but should never delay the ECG, which is faster, cheaper, and definitive for STEMI.

stages.label: ECG & INITIAL LABS
stages.content:
12-lead ECG: 3 mm ST-segment elevation in V1–V4 with hyperacute T waves; 1 mm ST depression in II, III, aVF; occasional PVCs
Troponin I (initial) 0.09 ng/mL (URL <0.04)
Creatinine 1.0 mg/dL
K 4.1 mEq/L
Hgb 14.6 g/dL
Glucose 138 mg/dL
Portable CXR: no widened mediastinum, mild pulmonary vascular congestion

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Based on the ECG and presentation, what is the diagnosis?
options: Type A aortic dissection as the primary diagnosis
Massive pulmonary embolism as the primary diagnosis
Acute pericarditis with diffuse ST changes as the primary diagnosis
Non-ST-elevation myocardial infarction (NSTEMI) as the primary diagnosis
Acute anterior STEMI from occlusion of the left anterior descending artery
stages.explanation: ST elevation in the precordial leads V1–V4 localizes a transmural infarction to the anterior wall, the territory of the left anterior descending artery; reciprocal inferior ST depression strongly supports true STEMI rather than mimics. Pericarditis causes diffuse concave ST elevation with PR depression and no reciprocal depression — reciprocal change is the key discriminator here. Pulmonary embolism produces sinus tachycardia, sometimes S1Q3T3 or right heart strain, not regional anterior ST elevation with reciprocal changes. NSTEMI is incorrect by definition because diagnostic ST elevation is present; the modest initial troponin reflects early presentation, not a lesser diagnosis. Aortic dissection is argued against by equal arm pressures, no pulse deficit, a normal mediastinum on chest radiograph, and pressure-like rather than tearing pain — though dissection can occlude a coronary ostium, nothing here suggests it.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Your hospital has a 24/7 catheterization lab. What is the definitive management?
options: Order an urgent exercise stress test to confirm ischemia first as initial therapy
Activate the cath lab for primary PCI with a goal door-to-balloon time of 90 minutes or less
Give high-dose IV metoprolol to reduce infarct size before deciding on reperfusion
Administer fibrinolytics because they are always preferable when given quickly
Start a heparin infusion and admit for angiography tomorrow morning as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Primary PCI is the reperfusion strategy of choice for STEMI when it can be performed by an experienced team within 90 minutes of first medical contact at a PCI-capable hospital — it achieves higher patency rates and lower rates of reinfarction, stroke, and intracranial hemorrhage than fibrinolysis. Fibrinolytics are reserved for settings where timely PCI is unavailable (anticipated first-medical-contact-to-device time over 120 minutes); they are not 'always preferable.' Heparin with next-day angiography is an NSTEMI strategy and would condemn viable anterior myocardium — time is muscle. Stress testing is absolutely contraindicated in acute STEMI. Routine early IV beta-blockade is avoided in patients with any heart-failure signs (he has basilar crackles) because it increases the risk of cardiogenic shock; oral beta-blockers are started within 24 hours once stable.

stages.label: ADJUNCT THERAPY
stages.question: While the cath lab is being activated, which adjunctive pharmacologic regimen is most appropriate?
options: High-flow oxygen by face mask regardless of saturation as the immediate next step
IV nitroglycerin and morphine alone, holding all antiplatelets until after angiography as the immediate next step
Immediate IV beta-blocker bolus to blunt tachycardia despite the basilar crackles as the immediate next step
Confirm chewed aspirin 325 mg, load a P2Y12 inhibitor ,
Fibrinolytic plus glycoprotein IIb/IIIa inhibitor combination therapy as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Standard adjuncts before primary PCI are dual antiplatelet therapy — aspirin 162–325 mg chewed (already given by EMS) plus a P2Y12 inhibitor load such as ticagrelor 180 mg or prasugrel — and parenteral anticoagulation, most commonly unfractionated heparin. Holding antiplatelets until after angiography deprives him of proven mortality-reducing therapy; nitroglycerin and morphine are symptomatic adjuncts only, and morphine may even delay P2Y12 absorption. Supplemental oxygen is indicated only for SpO2 below roughly 90 percent; routine oxygen in normoxic MI shows no benefit and possible harm. IV beta-blockade is risky with heart-failure signs and relative hemodynamic vulnerability, raising cardiogenic shock risk. Combining full-dose fibrinolytics with GP IIb/IIIa inhibitors increases major bleeding without mortality benefit and has no role when primary PCI is imminent.

cases.diagnosis: Acute anterior ST-elevation myocardial infarction (proximal LAD occlusion) treated with primary PCI
pearls: ECG within 10 minutes of arrival for any suspected ACS — STEMI is an ECG diagnosis; never wait for troponin to activate reperfusion.
ST elevation in V1–V4 = anterior wall = LAD territory; reciprocal ST depression strongly favors true STEMI over pericarditis.
Primary PCI with door-to-balloon ≤90 minutes beats fibrinolysis when available; fibrinolytics are for when PCI is delayed >120 minutes.
Adjuncts before PCI: chewed aspirin + P2Y12 load + heparin; avoid routine oxygen if SpO2 ≥90% and avoid early IV beta-blockers when heart-failure signs are present.

cases.title: Slow, irregular pulse at a sports physical
cases.chiefComplaint: I'm just here for my pre-season physical — the nurse said my pulse was skipping beats.
cases.history: A 21-year-old collegiate cross-country runner presents for his annual pre-participation sports physical. He is entirely asymptomatic: he denies palpitations, lightheadedness, presyncope, syncope, chest pain, and exertional intolerance, and he is currently running 60–70 miles per week with improving race times. He has never blacked out during exercise. During intake, the nurse noted a slow pulse with occasional 'dropped beats,' prompting a closer look. He has no significant past medical history, no prior cardiac evaluation, and takes no medications or supplements, including no performance-enhancing drugs, stimulants, or beta-blockers. He has no allergies. He does not smoke, vape, or use recreational drugs, and drinks one or two beers on weekends. He has not had recent febrile illness, rash, or known tick exposure, and he has not traveled to Lyme-endemic regions recently. Family history is negative for sudden cardiac death, cardiomyopathy, congenital heart disease, and pacemaker placement at a young age. His last physical a year ago was unremarkable apart from resting bradycardia in the 50s, attributed to athletic conditioning.
cases.exam: Fit, athletic-appearing young man in no distress. Cardiac auscultation reveals bradycardia with grouped beating — runs of several beats followed by a brief pause. S1 intensity varies slightly; no murmurs, rubs, or gallops, and no fixed splitting. PMI is nondisplaced. Lungs are clear bilaterally. JVP is normal without cannon A waves. No peripheral edema, cyanosis, or clubbing. Pulses are symmetric and strong. No rash, joint swelling, or stigmata of connective tissue disease. Marfanoid features are absent.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial test to evaluate this irregular bradycardia?
options: Resting 12-lead ECG
Tilt-table testing
Exercise treadmill stress test
Invasive electrophysiology study
Implantable loop recorder
stages.explanation: A resting 12-lead ECG is the cheapest, fastest, and most informative first step for any newly detected rhythm abnormality — it will usually capture the conduction pattern outright in a patient whose pulse is irregular in the office. An exercise test may be useful later to demonstrate that a vagally mediated block resolves with activity, but it is not the initial step before the rhythm is even characterized. An implantable loop recorder is reserved for infrequent, symptomatic events that elude noninvasive monitoring — this patient is asymptomatic with an abnormality present right now. An invasive EP study is far too aggressive for an asymptomatic athlete and is reserved for risk-stratifying ambiguous or infranodal block. Tilt-table testing evaluates reflex syncope, which he does not have. Always start with the 12-lead.

stages.label: ECG & LABS
stages.content:
12-lead ECG: sinus rhythm at 54 bpm with progressive PR prolongation across consecutive beats (200 → 240 → 290 ms) followed by a nonconducted P wave, then the cycle repeats; grouped beating pattern
QRS duration 88 ms (narrow), normal axis
No delta wave; QTc 408 ms; voltage criteria for early repolarization, no T-wave inversions beyond V1
TSH 2.1 µIU/mL
K 4.2 mEq/L, Ca 9.4 mg/dL, Mg 2.0 mg/dL
Lyme serology: not sent (no exposure or suggestive features)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What rhythm does this ECG demonstrate?
options: Mobitz type I second-degree AV block
Mobitz type II second-degree AV block
First-degree AV block as the primary diagnosis
Complete (third-degree) AV block
Sinus arrhythmia with blocked premature atrial contractions
stages.explanation: Progressive PR-interval prolongation culminating in a single nonconducted P wave, with the cycle then resetting and producing grouped beating, is the signature of Mobitz type I (Wenckebach) second-degree AV block, which localizes to the AV node. Mobitz type II shows abrupt dropped beats with a fixed, unchanging PR interval and frequently a wide QRS, reflecting dangerous infranodal (His-Purkinje) disease — the progressive PR lengthening here excludes it. Third-degree block shows complete AV dissociation with independent P waves and escape QRS complexes, not a repeating conducted pattern. Blocked PACs cause early, morphologically different P waves; these P waves march out regularly. First-degree AV block is a fixed prolonged PR over 200 ms with every P conducted — here, beats are actually dropped, making it second-degree by definition.

stages.label: RISK STRATIFICATION
stages.question: Which finding most strongly supports a benign, vagally mediated AV-nodal mechanism in this athlete?
options: A wide QRS complex accompanying the block
Coexisting bifascicular block on the ECG
A family history of early pacemaker implantation
Worsening of the block during exercise testing
Resolution of the block with light exercise or atropine
stages.explanation: Wenckebach block in endurance athletes results from high resting vagal tone acting on the AV node. Anything that withdraws vagal tone or adds sympathetic drive — light exercise, atropine — improves AV-nodal conduction and abolishes the block, confirming a benign nodal mechanism. The opposite pattern is ominous: infranodal (His-Purkinje) block paradoxically worsens with exercise or atropine because faster atrial rates stress the diseased distal conduction system. A wide QRS suggests infranodal disease and would push toward Mobitz II physiology, not reassurance. Bifascicular block similarly marks distal conduction disease and raises concern for progression to complete heart block. A family history of early pacemakers raises suspicion for inherited progressive cardiac conduction disease (e.g., SCN5A-related), which is a red flag rather than a reassuring feature. His narrow QRS and asymptomatic status fit the benign athletic phenotype.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: A brief jog in the hallway abolishes the block, with 1:1 conduction at a heart rate of 95 bpm. He remains asymptomatic. What is the appropriate management?
options: Order serial weekly ECGs and bench him until the block disappears at rest as initial therapy
Start daily theophylline to enhance AV conduction as initial therapy
Restrict him from sports pending an invasive electrophysiology study as initial therapy
Implant a permanent pacemaker before the season starts as initial therapy
Clear him for full athletic participation with reassurance
stages.explanation: Asymptomatic Mobitz I block that resolves with exercise in a trained endurance athlete is a normal variant of athletic conditioning — a manifestation of physiologic vagotonia, alongside sinus bradycardia and first-degree AV block. Guidelines do not recommend pacing for asymptomatic type I second-degree AV block at the AV-nodal level; pacemakers are indicated for symptomatic bradycardia, Mobitz II, high-grade, or third-degree block. Restricting sport or pursuing an EP study is unnecessary when noninvasive evaluation already localizes the block to the node and exercise normalizes conduction. Theophylline is an occasional niche option for symptomatic bradycardia in patients refusing or unsuitable for pacing — irrelevant here. Serial ECGs and benching impose burden without benefit; the resting block reflects fitness, not disease. He should simply be told to report syncope, presyncope, or exertional symptoms if they ever develop.

cases.diagnosis: Mobitz type I (Wenckebach) second-degree AV block due to high vagal tone in an endurance athlete — benign, no pacemaker indicated
pearls: Wenckebach = progressive PR prolongation then a dropped beat with grouped beating; it localizes to the AV node and is usually benign.
In athletes, high vagal tone produces sinus bradycardia, first-degree AV block, and Mobitz I — all normal variants when asymptomatic.
Block that improves with exercise or atropine is nodal and benign; block that worsens with exercise suggests infranodal disease and is dangerous.
Pacemakers are for symptomatic bradycardia, Mobitz II, high-grade, or complete block — not asymptomatic Wenckebach.

cases.title: Nighttime collapse in the bathroom
cases.chiefComplaint: My wife found me on the bathroom floor at 3 AM — I passed out while using the toilet.
cases.history: A 77-year-old man is brought in by his wife after a syncopal episode at 3 AM. He awoke to urinate, walked to the bathroom, and lost consciousness while standing at the toilet midway through voiding. His wife heard the fall and found him on the floor; he was unconscious for less than 30 seconds and was immediately oriented when he came to, with no confusion afterward. He recalls feeling warm and queasy for a few seconds before everything 'went gray.' There was no tongue biting, no limb jerking beyond a brief shudder per his wife, no bowel or bladder incontinence beyond the interrupted voiding, and no chest pain, palpitations, or dyspnea before or after. He has never fainted before and has no exertional symptoms. Past medical history: benign prostatic hyperplasia treated with tamsulosin 0.4 mg nightly (started 3 weeks ago) and hypertension on amlodipine 5 mg daily. He had two glasses of wine with dinner. He does not smoke. No known drug allergies. Family history is negative for sudden cardiac death.
cases.exam: Alert, well-appearing elderly man, fully oriented. Small left forehead contusion without laceration; cervical spine nontender with full range of motion. Cardiac exam: regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs — specifically no crescendo-decrescendo systolic murmur to suggest aortic stenosis — and no carotid bruits. Lungs clear. Tongue without lateral bite marks. Neurologic exam is nonfocal with normal gait. Supine-to-standing orthostatic vitals at the bedside: BP 138/76 supine to 130/72 standing at 3 minutes, HR 72 to 78, without symptoms. No melena on rectal exam; stool is brown and guaiac-negative.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: In addition to the history and physical, what is the most appropriate initial workup for this syncopal episode?
options: 12-lead ECG plus orthostatic vital signs
CT head and EEG for everyone who loses consciousness
Direct admission for 72-hour telemetry before any testing
Urgent echocardiogram and nuclear stress test
Carotid duplex ultrasonography as the first step today
stages.explanation: Every syncope evaluation rests on three pillars: a detailed history, a physical exam including orthostatic vitals, and a 12-lead ECG — together these identify the cause or risk-stratify the majority of patients, and basic labs (glucose, hemoglobin, electrolytes) screen for mimics like hypoglycemia and anemia from occult bleeding. CT head and EEG are low-yield without head trauma preceding the event, focal neurologic deficits, or features of seizure; reflexive neuroimaging in syncope is a classic low-value practice. Carotid disease causes focal TIA or stroke, essentially never isolated transient loss of consciousness, so duplex is not indicated. Echo and stress testing are reserved for patients with murmurs, exertional syncope, abnormal ECGs, or suspected structural disease. Admission for prolonged telemetry is premature before initial risk stratification, which often shows a benign cause that needs no monitoring at all.

stages.label: ECG & LABS
stages.content:
12-lead ECG: normal sinus rhythm at 70 bpm, PR 178 ms, QRS 92 ms, QTc 432 ms
No delta wave, no epsilon wave, no Brugada pattern, no Q waves or ST-T abnormalities
Hgb 13.8 g/dL
Glucose 102 mg/dL
Na 139 mEq/L, K 4.3 mEq/L, Creatinine 1.1 mg/dL
Troponin I <0.01 ng/mL
Orthostatic vitals (repeated): no significant BP drop, appropriate HR response

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely cause of this patient's syncope?
options: Generalized tonic-clonic seizure
Situational (micturition) reflex syncope
Orthostatic hypotension caused by tamsulosin
Intermittent high-grade AV block (Stokes-Adams attack)
Critical aortic stenosis in this clinical context
stages.explanation: Loss of consciousness during urination, preceded by a warm, queasy prodrome, with rapid and complete recovery, is classic situational syncope — a reflex (neurally mediated) syncope triggered by micturition. Nocturnal voiding combines vagal activation from bladder emptying, standing posture, straining, and vasodilation from alcohol and a warm bed, producing transient bradycardia and hypotension. Tamsulosin can contribute and likely lowered his reserve, but documented normal orthostatic vitals make pure orthostatic hypotension less likely as the primary mechanism; the trigger was voiding itself. Seizure is argued against by the brief duration, absence of tongue biting and true convulsive activity, and instant reorientation without postictal confusion — brief myoclonic shudders are common in any syncope. Stokes-Adams attacks from AV block typically strike without warning in any position; his prodrome, trigger, and normal ECG argue against it. Aortic stenosis is excluded by the absence of a murmur and the non-exertional context.

stages.label: RED FLAGS
stages.question: Which feature, had it been present, would most strongly suggest a dangerous cardiac cause rather than reflex syncope?
options: Occurrence of the episode at night
A prodrome of warmth and nausea
A clear trigger such as urination
Syncope during exertion or while supine
Rapid return to baseline mental status
stages.explanation: Syncope that occurs during exertion or while supine is a cardinal red flag for cardiac syncope — exertional syncope suggests fixed obstruction (aortic stenosis, hypertrophic cardiomyopathy) or exercise-induced arrhythmia, and supine syncope cannot be explained by the gravitational pooling that underlies reflex and orthostatic mechanisms, so an arrhythmia must be suspected. Other red flags include absent prodrome, palpitations immediately before the event, family history of sudden death, abnormal ECG, and structural heart disease. The remaining options all point the opposite way: nocturnal timing around voiding is typical of micturition syncope; a warm, nauseated prodrome is the classic autonomic prodrome of reflex syncope; immediate reorientation distinguishes syncope from seizure; and an identifiable situational trigger is precisely what defines situational syncope and predicts a benign course.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: His ECG, labs, and observation period are unremarkable. What is the most appropriate disposition?
options: Place an implantable loop recorder before discharge with appropriate follow-up
Order outpatient EEG and MRI of the brain with appropriate follow-up
Discharge home with counseling: sit while urinating at night, rise from bed slowly, limit evening alcohol,
Admit for 48 hours of telemetry monitoring with appropriate follow-up
Discontinue both tamsulosin and amlodipine immediately and start fludrocortisone with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Situational syncope with a classic story, benign exam, normal ECG, and no red flags carries an excellent prognosis and is managed with counseling alone: sitting to void at night eliminates the postural component, rising slowly reduces preload stress, and limiting evening alcohol removes a vasodilating contributor; reviewing the alpha-blocker's dose timing with his prescriber is sensible since tamsulosin lowers his hemodynamic reserve. Admission with telemetry adds cost without benefit in low-risk syncope — risk scores and guidelines support discharge when the mechanism is clearly reflex. An implantable loop recorder is for recurrent unexplained syncope, not a first episode with an obvious mechanism. EEG and MRI address seizure or structural brain disease, neither of which is suggested. Abruptly stopping both his BPH and antihypertensive therapy and adding fludrocortisone treats orthostatic hypotension he does not have and risks urinary retention and hypertension.

cases.diagnosis: Situational (micturition) reflex syncope, with tamsulosin and evening alcohol as contributing factors
pearls: Situational syncope is reflex syncope tied to a specific trigger — micturition, defecation, cough, swallow — and classically strikes older men voiding at night.
History, exam with orthostatics, and a 12-lead ECG risk-stratify most syncope; routine head CT, EEG, and carotid ultrasound are low-value without focal findings.
Red flags for cardiac syncope: exertional or supine onset, no prodrome, palpitations, abnormal ECG, structural heart disease, or family history of sudden death.
Management of micturition syncope is behavioral — sit to void, rise slowly, limit nighttime alcohol — and a hard look at alpha-blockers and antihypertensive timing.

cases.title: A question before a tooth extraction
cases.chiefComplaint: My dentist wants to pull a molar in two weeks and told me to ask you whether I need antibiotics first.
cases.history: A 55-year-old man presents to cardiology clinic for a routine visit and a specific question. Three years ago he underwent mechanical aortic valve replacement for a stenotic bicuspid aortic valve; he has done well since, with no fevers, embolic events, or heart failure symptoms. He takes warfarin 5 mg daily with a goal INR of 2.5–3.5 (most recent INR 2.8, historically stable), metoprolol succinate 50 mg daily, and lisinopril 10 mg daily for hypertension. His dentist has scheduled extraction of a carious lower molar (tooth #19) in two weeks and asked him to clarify antibiotic prophylaxis. He reports an allergy to penicillin: as an adult he developed diffuse hives and lip swelling within an hour of taking amoxicillin. He denies recent dental infections, fevers, night sweats, or weight loss. He does not smoke and drinks rarely. He exercises without limitation. Family history is notable for a brother with mitral valve prolapse. Review of systems is otherwise negative; his last echocardiogram 8 months ago showed a well-functioning prosthesis.
cases.exam: Well-appearing man in no distress. Crisp mechanical click at S2 best heard at the right upper sternal border with a soft grade 1/6 early systolic flow murmur; no diastolic murmur, no new regurgitant murmur. Lungs clear bilaterally. No splinter hemorrhages, Janeway lesions, Osler nodes, or conjunctival petechiae. Dentition shows a visibly carious lower left molar without surrounding erythema, fluctuance, or tenderness. No lymphadenopathy. Abdomen soft, no splenomegaly. No peripheral edema; pulses full and symmetric.

stages.label: RISK ASSESSMENT
stages.question: Which feature of this patient's history is the reason he qualifies for infective endocarditis antibiotic prophylaxis before dental work?
options: The fact that any dental procedure in any adult warrants prophylaxis
His age over 50 as the immediate next step
His history of a bicuspid aortic valve alone, even if it had never been replaced
His prosthetic (mechanical) aortic valve
His history of hypertension as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Current AHA/ACC guidance restricts endocarditis prophylaxis to patients at highest risk of an adverse outcome from endocarditis, and prosthetic cardiac valves — mechanical or bioprosthetic, including transcatheter valves and prosthetic material used for repair — top that list. Other qualifying conditions are prior infective endocarditis, certain congenital heart diseases (unrepaired cyanotic CHD, repaired CHD with residual defects near prosthetic material, or within 6 months of repair with prosthetic material), and cardiac transplant recipients with valvulopathy. Hypertension confers no endocarditis risk. The sweeping 'antibiotics for every dental procedure' approach was abandoned in 2007 because bacteremia from daily activities dwarfs procedural bacteremia and prophylaxis was overused. Age alone is not a criterion. An unrepaired bicuspid aortic valve, while a long-term endocarditis risk factor, does not qualify for prophylaxis under current guidelines — it is the prosthesis that changes the calculus.

stages.label: RECORDS REVIEW
stages.content:
Echocardiogram (8 months ago): well-seated mechanical aortic valve, mean gradient 12 mmHg, no paravalvular leak, EF 62%
INR today: 2.8 (therapeutic; goal 2.5–3.5)
Dental plan from oral surgeon: extraction of tooth #19 — involves manipulation of gingival tissue and perforation of oral mucosa
Documented allergy: amoxicillin → diffuse urticaria and lip angioedema within 1 hour (consistent with an immediate-type, IgE-mediated reaction)
CBC: WBC 6.8 ×10³/µL, Hgb 14.2 g/dL, Plt 232 ×10³/µL
No fevers; blood cultures not indicated

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given his immediate-type penicillin allergy, which prophylactic regimen is most appropriate before the extraction?
options: Azithromycin 500 mg orally 30–60 minutes before the procedure
IV vancomycin infusion completed before the procedure as initial therapy
Cephalexin 2 g orally — cephalosporins are safe in every type of penicillin allergy
Amoxicillin 2 g orally 30–60 minutes before the procedure as monotherapy
Clindamycin 600 mg orally 30–60 minutes before the procedure
stages.explanation: For patients who need prophylaxis but have an immediate-type (IgE-mediated) penicillin allergy, the 2021 AHA update recommends azithromycin or clarithromycin 500 mg, or doxycycline 100 mg, taken 30–60 minutes before the procedure. Amoxicillin 2 g is the standard first-line agent but is contraindicated by his hives and angioedema. Clindamycin was removed from the AHA regimens in 2021 because it causes more severe adverse events — particularly Clostridioides difficile infection — than any other prophylactic antibiotic, a frequently missed update. Cephalexin is an acceptable alternative only in patients whose penicillin reaction was NOT anaphylaxis, angioedema, or urticaria; his immediate-type reaction makes a first-generation cephalosporin inadvisable per the guideline. IV vancomycin is not part of routine dental prophylaxis regimens and adds infusion logistics and toxicity without indication.

stages.label: CONCEPT CHECK
stages.question: His brother, who has mitral valve prolapse with regurgitation, asks whether he too needs antibiotics before his own upcoming extraction. Which of the following conditions DOES warrant prophylaxis under current guidelines?
options: Rheumatic mitral stenosis of a native valve
A previous episode of infective endocarditis
Mitral valve prolapse with regurgitation
A secundum atrial septal defect, unrepaired
An unrepaired bicuspid aortic valve
stages.explanation: Prior infective endocarditis is one of the highest-risk conditions and firmly warrants prophylaxis — recurrent endocarditis is both more likely and more lethal. Mitral valve prolapse, even with regurgitation, was removed from prophylaxis recommendations in 2007: although it carries some lifetime endocarditis risk, the absolute procedural risk is tiny and prophylaxis showed no favorable risk-benefit balance, so the brother does not qualify. Unrepaired bicuspid aortic valve is similarly a moderate-risk native-valve lesion that no longer qualifies. Isolated secundum ASD is explicitly low risk — it is one of the few congenital lesions that never qualified even under older guidelines. Native-valve rheumatic mitral stenosis, despite its historical association with endocarditis, also does not meet current high-risk criteria. The memorable shortlist: prosthetic valves or prosthetic repair material, prior endocarditis, specific congenital heart disease categories, and transplant valvulopathy.

stages.label: PERIPROCEDURAL PLAN
stages.question: He asks whether he should stop his warfarin before the extraction. What do you advise?
options: Stop warfarin 5 days before the extraction with no bridging as the immediate next step
Continue warfarin without interruption
Hold warfarin only on the morning of the procedure as the immediate next step
Stop warfarin and bridge with therapeutic enoxaparin around the procedure as the immediate next step
Switch him from warfarin to apixaban for the periprocedural period as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Simple dental extractions are minor, low-bleeding-risk procedures that can be performed safely with a therapeutic INR (generally up to about 3.5) using local hemostatic measures — gauze pressure, sutures, and tranexamic acid mouthwash if needed. Interrupting anticoagulation in a patient with a mechanical aortic valve exposes him to valve thrombosis and stroke for the sake of a procedure that rarely bleeds significantly. Stopping warfarin for 5 days without bridging creates an unprotected window that is unnecessary here. Bridging with enoxaparin adds bleeding risk and complexity for a procedure that does not require interruption at all. Switching to apixaban is dangerous and contraindicated: direct oral anticoagulants are inferior to warfarin in mechanical valves (the RE-ALIGN trial of dabigatran showed excess thrombosis and bleeding) and are not approved for this indication. Holding a single morning dose meaningfully changes nothing pharmacologically and signals misunderstanding of warfarin kinetics.

cases.diagnosis: Prosthetic (mechanical) aortic valve requiring infective endocarditis prophylaxis before dental extraction — azithromycin given immediate-type penicillin allergy; warfarin continued
pearls: Endocarditis prophylaxis is reserved for the highest-risk patients: prosthetic valves/material, prior endocarditis, select congenital heart disease, and transplant valvulopathy.
It applies only to dental procedures that manipulate gingiva, the periapical region, or perforate oral mucosa — standard regimen amoxicillin 2 g, 30–60 minutes prior.
Since the 2021 AHA update, clindamycin is out (C. difficile risk); immediate-type penicillin allergy gets azithromycin, clarithromycin, or doxycycline.

Do not interrupt warfarin for simple extractions in mechanical-valve patients — therapeutic INR plus local hemostasis is safer than any interruption, and DOACs are contraindicated with mechanical valves.

cases.title: An alarming blood pressure reading
cases.chiefComplaint: The pharmacy machine said my blood pressure was 208 over 112 and told me to go to the ER — I feel completely fine.
cases.history: A 59-year-old man presents after a pharmacy kiosk reading of 208/112 mmHg. He feels entirely well: no chest pain, dyspnea, headache, visual changes, focal weakness, numbness, confusion, abdominal or back pain, or decreased urine output. He was diagnosed with hypertension six years ago and was taking amlodipine 10 mg and lisinopril-hydrochlorothiazide 20/25 mg daily, but his prescriptions ran out about three weeks ago when he changed jobs and lost insurance coverage; he has taken nothing since. He has no history of coronary disease, stroke, kidney disease, or diabetes. He smokes half a pack per day (20 pack-years) and drinks two or three beers on weekends. He denies cocaine, amphetamines, decongestant use, and NSAIDs. He has no drug allergies. He works as a warehouse supervisor. Family history includes hypertension in both parents and a stroke in his mother at age 74. Review of systems is otherwise negative; he specifically denies episodic headaches, palpitations, and sweating spells. His last routine labs a year ago were reportedly normal, including creatinine.
cases.exam: Comfortable, well-appearing man in no distress. Fundoscopic exam shows mild arteriolar narrowing without papilledema, hemorrhages, or exudates. Cardiac exam: regular rhythm, prominent but nondisplaced PMI, no S3, no murmurs. Lungs clear bilaterally without crackles. Abdomen soft and nontender without bruits or pulsatile mass. Neurologic exam fully intact: normal speech, strength, sensation, and gait. No peripheral edema. Pulses symmetric in all extremities with no radial-femoral delay.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial approach to this patient?
options: Recheck the BP after quiet rest
Start IV labetalol immediately to normalize the blood pressure within the hour as the first step today
Obtain an immediate CT of the head because of the BP level alone as the first step today
Discharge him from triage without any evaluation since he is asymptomatic as the first step today
Give sublingual immediate-release nifedipine to bring the pressure down quickly as the first step today
stages.explanation: The pivotal question in severe hypertension is not the number but whether acute target-organ damage is present — that distinction separates hypertensive emergency (requiring IV therapy and ICU care) from asymptomatic severe hypertension. The correct approach is to confirm the reading after rest and screen the brain, heart, kidneys, and retina with a focused history, exam, ECG, creatinine, and urinalysis. Immediate IV labetalol is premature and potentially harmful: rapid BP reduction in chronic hypertension can drop pressure below the shifted cerebral autoregulatory range and cause ischemic stroke. Sublingual nifedipine is infamous for precipitating stroke and myocardial infarction through uncontrolled pressure drops and was abandoned decades ago. CT head is indicated for neurologic symptoms or signs, not a number. Discharging without any assessment misses the chance to detect occult organ injury and to restart therapy.

stages.label: WORKUP RESULTS
stages.content:
BP after 60 minutes of quiet rest: 196/106 mmHg
ECG: sinus rhythm, voltage criteria for LV hypertrophy with strain pattern in V5–V6, no acute ST-T changes
Creatinine 1.0 mg/dL (baseline 1.0 one year ago)
BUN 16 mg/dL, Na 140 mEq/L, K 4.0 mEq/L
Urinalysis: no protein, no blood, no casts
Troponin I <0.01 ng/mL
CXR: normal cardiac silhouette, no pulmonary edema, no mediastinal widening

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: How should this presentation be classified?
options: White-coat hypertension as the primary diagnosis
Renovascular hypertension confirmed by these findings as the primary diagnosis
Asymptomatic severe hypertension
Pheochromocytoma crisis as the primary diagnosis
Hypertensive emergency requiring immediate IV therapy as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: He has severely elevated blood pressure (≥180/110–120 mmHg) with no clinical or laboratory evidence of acute target-organ injury — normal neurologic exam, no papilledema, stable creatinine, bland urinalysis, negative troponin, and no pulmonary edema — which defines asymptomatic severe hypertension (traditionally 'hypertensive urgency'). The chronic LVH on ECG reflects long-standing disease, not acute injury. Hypertensive emergency requires acute end-organ damage — encephalopathy, retinal hemorrhages or papilledema, acute kidney injury, aortic dissection, pulmonary edema, or ACS — none of which is present. Pheochromocytoma crisis classically features paroxysms of headache, palpitations, and diaphoresis, all specifically denied. Renovascular hypertension is a consideration in resistant hypertension or with abdominal bruits and unexplained renal dysfunction, but nothing here confirms it, and his elevation is well explained by medication nonadherence. White-coat hypertension is incorrect because the reading was first obtained outside the medical setting and he has established, previously treated hypertension.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management of his blood pressure?
options: Restart his oral regimen with the goal of gradual BP reduction over days to weeks
Sublingual nifedipine now, repeated every 30 minutes until BP is below 160/100 as initial therapy
ICU admission for IV nicardipine titrated via arterial line as initial therapy
Observe in the ED until the BP is below 140/90 before any disposition decision as initial therapy
Reduce mean arterial pressure by 25% within one hour using IV labetalol boluses as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Asymptomatic severe hypertension is treated by resuming or intensifying oral antihypertensive therapy with gradual reduction over days to weeks — his elevation is fully explained by three weeks off two effective medications, so restarting them is both the simplest and the most durable fix. Rapid normalization offers no benefit and real harm: chronic hypertension shifts cerebral autoregulation rightward, and abrupt drops can cause cerebral or myocardial hypoperfusion. ICU nicardipine and the 'reduce MAP 25% in one hour' target are strategies for true hypertensive emergencies with organ damage. Sublingual nifedipine causes precipitous, uncontrollable drops and is contraindicated. Requiring BP below 140/90 before disposition is a common ED myth — observed in-department BP reduction does not improve outcomes, prolongs stays, and often prompts unnecessary IV dosing; what matters is reliable therapy and follow-up.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What is the best disposition plan?
options: Discharge with no follow-up since he feels well with appropriate follow-up
Hold him in the ED until his BP is documented below 140/90 mmHg with appropriate follow-up
Refer urgently for renal artery stenting with appropriate follow-up
Discharge on his restarted oral regimen with BP recheck
Admit to the ICU for arterial-line BP monitoring with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: After excluding target-organ damage, the patient can be discharged on oral therapy with early follow-up — ideally within days — for a BP recheck and titration; equally important is fixing the root cause of nonadherence, so connecting him to low-cost generic programs (amlodipine and lisinopril-HCTZ cost a few dollars monthly) and social work directly prevents recurrence. ICU admission wastes resources on a patient with no acute organ injury. Holding for an arbitrary in-ED BP target delays care without improving outcomes. Renal artery stenting is not indicated — there is no evidence of renovascular disease, and even when present, the CORAL and ASTRAL trials showed stenting rarely beats medical therapy. Discharge without follow-up squanders the opportunity: untreated severe hypertension carries substantial short-term risk of progression to true emergency, stroke, and heart failure, and arranged follow-up is what converts an ED visit into durable BP control.

cases.diagnosis: Asymptomatic severe hypertension (hypertensive urgency) due to medication nonadherence, without acute target-organ damage
pearls: The number alone never defines a hypertensive emergency — acute target-organ damage does; screen brain, eyes, heart, and kidneys before deciding.
Asymptomatic severe hypertension is treated with oral agents and gradual reduction over days; rapid IV normalization risks watershed cerebral and myocardial ischemia.
Sublingual nifedipine is contraindicated — uncontrolled precipitous drops have caused strokes and MIs.
Medication nonadherence is the most common cause of severe BP elevation; fixing access (cost, insurance, refills) is more valuable than any drug given in the ED.

cases.title: Burning chest pain after dinner
cases.chiefComplaint: For three months I've had burning pain behind my breastbone after big meals, and I'm scared it's my heart.
cases.history: A 42-year-old woman presents with three months of retrosternal burning chest pain. Episodes occur two to four times weekly, typically 30–60 minutes after large, spicy, or fatty meals and when lying down soon after dinner; they last 30–90 minutes and are accompanied by a sour taste in her mouth and occasional belching. Over-the-counter calcium carbonate provides partial relief within 15 minutes. The pain never occurs with exertion — she walks briskly 30 minutes most days and recently helped a friend move furniture without symptoms. She denies dysphagia, odynophagia, weight loss, vomiting, hematemesis, melena, early satiety, and food impaction. She has no prior cardiac history, no diabetes, and no hypertension. She takes no daily medications and only rare ibuprofen. No drug allergies. She has never smoked, drinks one glass of wine with dinner most nights, and drinks two cups of coffee daily. BMI is 29. Her mother has hypothyroidism; there is no family history of premature coronary disease or esophageal cancer. She is anxious because a coworker recently had a heart attack.
cases.exam: Well-appearing woman in no distress. Cardiac exam: regular rate and rhythm, normal S1 and S2, no murmurs, rubs, or gallops; PMI nondisplaced. Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally. Abdomen soft with mild epigastric tenderness to deep palpation, no guarding, no masses, no hepatosplenomegaly; bowel sounds normal. Chest wall is nontender and palpation does not reproduce her pain. No lower-extremity edema; distal pulses intact. Dentition normal without enamel erosion. No supraclavicular lymphadenopathy.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial diagnostic approach?
options: Same-day exercise stress test as the first step today
Coronary CT angiography to definitively exclude coronary disease as the first step today
Estimate pretest probability of coronary disease clinically, obtain a baseline office ECG,
Referral for diagnostic coronary catheterization as the first step today
Refer to the ED for serial troponins as the first step today
stages.explanation: Chest-pain evaluation begins with structured pretest-probability assessment. Her pain is non-exertional, positional/postprandial, burning, and relieved by antacids — a noncardiac pattern — in a premenopausal woman without diabetes, smoking, hypertension, or family history, putting her coronary pretest probability in the very low range where further cardiac testing causes more harm (false positives, incidentalomas, radiation, cost, downstream catheterization) than benefit. A baseline ECG is cheap and reasonable; screening for GI alarm features directs the gastrointestinal workup. Coronary CTA in a very-low-probability patient mostly generates false leads. Stress testing the very-low-risk violates appropriate-use criteria for the same reason. ED referral for serial troponins is for suspected acute coronary syndrome, not three months of stable postprandial burning. Catheterization is the most invasive option and is reserved for high-probability or high-risk presentations.

stages.label: OFFICE STUDIES
stages.content:
12-lead ECG: normal sinus rhythm at 74 bpm, normal axis, no ST-T abnormalities, no Q waves, QTc 418 ms
Lipid panel: LDL 102 mg/dL, HDL 58 mg/dL, TG 110 mg/dL
Fasting glucose 92 mg/dL
Hgb 13.6 g/dL
TSH 1.6 µIU/mL
ASCVD 10-year risk estimate: 1.8%
Alarm-feature screen: no dysphagia, no odynophagia, no weight loss, no GI bleeding, no anemia, no persistent vomiting

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely cause of her chest pain?
options: Achalasia as the primary diagnosis
Acute coronary syndrome as the primary diagnosis
Gastroesophageal reflux disease causing noncardiac chest pain
Costochondritis as the primary diagnosis
Microvascular angina as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Postprandial burning retrosternal pain worsened by recumbency, accompanied by sour regurgitation and belching, and partially relieved by antacids is classic gastroesophageal reflux — the single most common cause of noncardiac chest pain. Her risk-factor profile (large meals, evening wine, coffee, BMI 29, lying down after dinner) fits perfectly. Microvascular angina causes exertional pain, typically in patients with risk factors, and her vigorous activity tolerance without symptoms argues strongly against any anginal syndrome. Achalasia presents with progressive dysphagia to both solids and liquids with regurgitation of undigested food — she has no dysphagia at all. Acute coronary syndrome is excluded by the three-month stable course, non-exertional pattern, normal ECG, and very low pretest probability. Costochondritis produces sharp, well-localized pain reproduced by chest-wall palpation; her chest wall is explicitly nontender and palpation does not reproduce the pain.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Daily lorazepam for presumed anxiety-related chest pain as initial therapy
Helicobacter pylori stool antigen testing and empiric quadruple therapy as initial therapy
Stress echocardiography to 'be thorough' despite the low pretest probability as initial therapy
An empiric once-daily proton pump inhibitor trial for 4–8 weeks plus lifestyle measures
Urgent upper endoscopy before any treatment as initial therapy
stages.explanation: For typical GERD symptoms without alarm features, guidelines recommend an empiric once-daily PPI for 4–8 weeks combined with lifestyle modification; symptom response is both therapeutic and diagnostic (the 'PPI test'), and her triggers — late large meals, evening wine, coffee, elevated BMI — are each addressable. Upper endoscopy as the first move is reserved for alarm features (dysphagia, weight loss, bleeding, anemia, persistent vomiting), refractory symptoms after PPI optimization, or screening for Barrett esophagus in patients with chronic GERD plus multiple risk factors; she has none today. H. pylori test-and-treat is the strategy for uninvestigated dyspepsia/ulcer disease — eradication does not reliably improve GERD and is not indicated for reflux symptoms. Stress echo in a patient with a 1.8% ASCVD risk and noncardiac symptoms is low-value testing likely to produce false positives. Benzodiazepines do not treat reflux, carry dependence risk, and dismiss a treatable diagnosis.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP
stages.question: At her 6-week follow-up she is 90% improved on omeprazole. Which new symptom, if it developed, would most strongly warrant prompt upper endoscopy?
options: Occasional nighttime sour regurgitation after a late dinner
Symptom recurrence after eating spicy food in this setting
Continued relief of symptoms with antacids on occasional breakthrough days
Her BMI remaining at 29 as the immediate next step
Progressive difficulty swallowing solid foods
stages.explanation: Progressive solid-food dysphagia is a cardinal alarm feature in a reflux patient: it raises concern for peptic stricture, eosinophilic esophagitis, or esophageal adenocarcinoma arising in Barrett esophagus, and it mandates prompt endoscopy with biopsy regardless of how well the reflux itself is controlled. The other options are expected parts of the GERD course rather than warning signs: antacid-responsive breakthrough symptoms confirm the acid-related mechanism; occasional nocturnal regurgitation after late meals reflects a lifestyle trigger to reinforce, not investigate; spicy-food-provoked recurrence is typical symptom behavior; and a stable BMI of 29, while a target for ongoing weight-loss counseling because adiposity drives reflux, is not an indication for endoscopy. The full alarm list worth memorizing: dysphagia, odynophagia, unintentional weight loss, GI bleeding or iron-deficiency anemia, persistent vomiting, and new symptoms after age 60.

cases.diagnosis: Gastroesophageal reflux disease presenting as noncardiac chest pain — managed with an empiric PPI trial and lifestyle modification, avoiding unnecessary cardiac testing
pearls: Pretest probability drives chest-pain workup: non-exertional, positional, burning, antacid-responsive pain in a low-risk patient should not trigger reflexive cardiac testing.
GERD is the most common cause of noncardiac chest pain; an empiric 4–8 week once-daily PPI trial is both treatment and diagnostic test.
Reserve upper endoscopy for alarm features: dysphagia, odynophagia, weight loss, bleeding, anemia, or persistent vomiting.
Testing very-low-risk patients (stress tests, coronary CTA) generates false positives and harm — the best test is often a careful history.

cases.title: Collapse with tearing back pain
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden terrible pain in my belly and lower back about an hour ago — then I blacked out at home.
cases.history: A 74-year-old man is brought in by EMS after sudden onset of severe abdominal and lower back pain one hour ago, followed by a brief syncopal episode at home. The pain is constant, deep, and 'ripping,' radiating from the periumbilical region to the back and left flank. He feels lightheaded and nauseated. He denies chest pain, dyspnea, hematemesis, melena, or urinary symptoms; he has never had pain like this. Past medical history includes hypertension on amlodipine 10 mg daily (admits to missing doses), COPD on tiotropium, and hyperlipidemia on rosuvastatin 20 mg. Three years ago an ultrasound reportedly showed a 4.8 cm abdominal aortic aneurysm; he was told to get surveillance scans but never followed up. He has smoked one and a half packs daily for 55 years and continues to smoke. He drinks little alcohol. No known drug allergies. No prior abdominal surgery. Family history: a brother died of a 'burst blood vessel in the belly' at 70. En route, EMS noted BP 88/56 mmHg, placed one IV, and gave a 250 mL saline bolus.
cases.exam: Pale, diaphoretic, anxious man who is alert but slow to answer. Skin is cool with thready peripheral pulses. Abdomen: a tender, expansile pulsatile mass is palpable in the mid-abdomen, periumbilical and to the left of midline; involuntary guarding without rigidity. No flank ecchymosis visible. Femoral pulses are present but diminished bilaterally and symmetric. Heart sounds rapid and regular without murmur; lungs with scattered expiratory wheezes but no crackles. No leg swelling or asymmetry. Rectal exam: brown stool, guaiac-negative.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate course of action?
options: Send him to CT angiography first to confirm the diagnosis before involving surgery as the first step today
Two large-bore IVs, type
Analgesia and serial abdominal exams over the next several hours as the first step today
Diagnostic peritoneal lavage to look for intra-abdominal blood as the first step today
Aggressive crystalloid boluses to restore systolic BP above 120 mmHg before any imaging as the first step today
stages.explanation: A hypotensive elderly smoker with sudden abdominal/back pain, syncope, and a pulsatile abdominal mass has a ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm until proven otherwise — a diagnosis with minute-to-minute mortality. The correct response is simultaneous resuscitation and mobilization: large-bore access, type and crossmatch with massive transfusion protocol activation, immediate vascular surgery notification, and a bedside ultrasound, which can confirm an aneurysm in under a minute without moving the patient. Sending an unstable patient to CT before surgery is consulted is the classic fatal delay — 'death in the scanner.' Flooding him with crystalloid to normal pressures worsens hemorrhage by diluting clotting factors and popping the fragile retroperitoneal tamponade. Serial exams have no role in suspected vascular catastrophe. Diagnostic peritoneal lavage is an obsolete trauma tool with no place here.

stages.label: BEDSIDE IMAGING & LABS
stages.content:
Bedside ultrasound: infrarenal abdominal aorta measures 8.2 cm in maximal diameter with mural thrombus; free fluid in the left paracolic gutter; limited retroperitoneal visualization
FAST: positive for free intraperitoneal fluid
Hgb 11.2 g/dL (reported baseline 13.9 g/dL)
Lactate 4.8 mmol/L
Creatinine 1.4 mg/dL
INR 1.1, platelets 210 ×10³/µL
Type and crossmatch: 6 units PRBC ordered; massive transfusion protocol activated
ECG: sinus tachycardia at 116 bpm, no ST elevation

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the diagnosis?
options: Renal colic from an obstructing ureteral stone
Perforated peptic ulcer
Acute mesenteric ischemia
Acute pancreatitis
Ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm
stages.explanation: The triad of hypotension, abdominal/back pain, and a pulsatile abdominal mass — here with an 8.2 cm aneurysm and free fluid on ultrasound, a falling hemoglobin, elevated lactate, syncope, and every major risk factor (male sex, age over 65, heavy smoking, hypertension, family history, and a known neglected 4.8 cm AAA) — is diagnostic of ruptured AAA. Renal colic is the most notorious mimic in older men (flank pain, hematuria), and any first 'kidney stone' after age 60 should prompt aortic imaging, but stones do not cause shock or free fluid. Mesenteric ischemia causes pain out of proportion to exam with risk factors like atrial fibrillation, not a pulsatile mass with hemoperitoneum. Perforated ulcer produces rigid peritonitis and free air, with guaiac findings often positive. Pancreatitis rarely causes immediate shock with a pulsatile mass and would show epigastric pain with elevated lipase.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Vascular surgery is at the bedside. His BP is now 78/50 mmHg despite two units of blood. What is the definitive management?
options: ICU admission for stabilization on norepinephrine before any repair as initial therapy
Place bilateral percutaneous drains to decompress the retroperitoneal hematoma as initial therapy
Immediate transfer to the operating room for emergent repair without further imaging
IV esmolol and labetalol for anti-impulse therapy as you would for aortic dissection as initial therapy
CT angiography first, to map anatomy for endovascular repair, despite the deteriorating pressure as monotherapy
stages.explanation: A hemodynamically unstable patient with a confirmed ruptured AAA goes straight to the operating room — the only therapy that stops the bleeding is repair, whether open or endovascular, and every minute of delay compounds mortality, which already approaches 50% even among patients who reach the hospital alive. CT angiography is appropriate only for stable patients in whom EVAR planning is feasible without risking arrest in the scanner; with falling pressure despite transfusion, it is contraindicated. ICU 'stabilization' is an illusion: no amount of vasopressor or volume can stabilize ongoing arterial hemorrhage, and norepinephrine raises pressure at the cost of accelerating bleeding. Anti-impulse therapy with beta-blockade is the strategy for aortic dissection, where reducing dP/dt limits propagation — in rupture with shock it would be lethal. Draining the retroperitoneal hematoma removes the tamponade restraining the hemorrhage and would cause immediate exsanguination.

stages.label: RESUSCITATION STRATEGY
stages.question: While the OR is readied, which resuscitation strategy is most appropriate?
options: Norepinephrine titrated to a MAP of 85 mmHg as the immediate next step
Withhold all transfusion until the formal crossmatch is complete, even if he deteriorates as the immediate next step
Permissive hypotension
Transfusion targeting a hemoglobin above 12 g/dL before incision as the immediate next step
Crystalloid boluses targeting a systolic BP above 120 mmHg as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Permissive (hypotensive) resuscitation is the accepted strategy in ruptured AAA before surgical control: a systolic pressure around 80–90 mmHg with preserved mentation maintains critical organ perfusion while avoiding pressures that blow out the fragile clot and retroperitoneal tamponade. Resuscitation should use blood products — red cells with plasma and platelets in balanced ratios — because large-volume crystalloid dilutes clotting factors, induces hypothermia and acidosis, and worsens bleeding. Driving systolic BP above 120 with crystalloid does exactly that. Vasopressor-driven MAP targets of 85 similarly increase hemorrhage without addressing volume loss. A hemoglobin above 12 is an arbitrary, supraphysiologic transfusion goal that delays the OR. Withholding blood pending formal crossmatch is wrong because uncrossmatched type O (or type-specific) blood is the standard for exsanguinating patients — the massive transfusion protocol exists precisely for this scenario.

cases.diagnosis: Ruptured infrarenal abdominal aortic aneurysm (8.2 cm) — emergent operative repair with permissive hypotension and blood-product resuscitation
pearls: Hypotension + abdominal/back pain + pulsatile mass in an older male smoker = ruptured AAA; unstable patients go to the OR, never to the CT scanner.
Bedside ultrasound confirms the aneurysm in seconds without moving the patient; a first 'renal colic' after age 60 deserves aortic imaging.
Use permissive hypotension (SBP ~80–90 with intact mentation) and blood products, not crystalloid floods — normalizing BP pops the tamponade.
Prevention works: one-time ultrasound screening for AAA in men 65–75 who have ever smoked, with surveillance and elective repair at ≥5.5 cm.

cases.title: A heart rate stuck at 150
cases.chiefComplaint: My heart has been racing since yesterday and I get winded just walking to the mailbox.
cases.history: A 65-year-old man presents with about 36 hours of palpitations, fatigue, and exertional breathlessness. The racing sensation began fairly abruptly while watching television, has been continuous since, and feels rapid and regular rather than fluttery. He denies chest pain, syncope, fever, or cough, though he felt lightheaded climbing the stairs this morning. He has never had palpitations before. Past medical history includes hypertension on losartan 100 mg daily and type 2 diabetes on metformin 1000 mg twice daily; his last A1c was 7.2%. He has no known coronary disease or heart failure. He quit smoking 10 years ago after 25 pack-years. He drinks one or two beers weekly and had nothing unusual recently; he denies stimulants and energy drinks. No known drug allergies. His wife notes he snores heavily and gasps at night, but he has never been tested for sleep apnea. Family history includes atrial fibrillation in an older brother. Review of systems is negative for heat intolerance, tremor, weight loss, and recent illness.
cases.exam: Mildly fatigued-appearing man in no acute distress, speaking full sentences. Cardiac exam: rapid, strikingly regular rhythm at 150 bpm; no murmurs, rubs, or gallops appreciated at this rate. JVP shows rapid undulations but no cannon A waves clearly identified. Lungs clear bilaterally without crackles or wheezes. No thyromegaly, tremor, or lid lag. Abdomen soft and nontender. Extremities warm, no edema; pulses rapid, regular, and symmetric. Neurologically intact.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the best first diagnostic step?
options: Stat CT pulmonary angiogram for his dyspnea
Immediate synchronized cardioversion
Send a D-dimer and await the result
Empiric IV diltiazem bolus before any rhythm documentation
Obtain a 12-lead ECG
stages.explanation: A 12-lead ECG is mandatory before treating any sustained tachycardia in a stable patient — it establishes whether the rhythm is narrow or wide, regular or irregular, and often reveals the mechanism outright, which dictates entirely different therapies. He is hemodynamically stable (BP 124/78, mentating, no ischemic pain, no pulmonary edema), so immediate cardioversion is not indicated; electricity first is for unstable tachyarrhythmias. Giving empiric diltiazem before documenting the rhythm risks treating blind — and AV-nodal blockers can be catastrophic in pre-excited rhythms or misdiagnosed ventricular tachycardia. CT pulmonary angiography chases pulmonary embolism, but his dyspnea is readily explained by 36 hours of tachycardia, and PE rarely produces a fixed regular rate of exactly 150. A D-dimer in a 65-year-old adds nothing actionable and delays the one test that will actually identify the problem.

stages.label: ECG & ADENOSINE RESPONSE
stages.content:
12-lead ECG: regular narrow-complex tachycardia at exactly 150 bpm; possible undulations in the baseline of leads II, III, aVF but distinct P waves not identifiable; QRS 92 ms
Troponin I <0.01 ng/mL; TSH 1.4 µIU/mL; K 4.0 mEq/L; Mg 2.0 mg/dL; Hgb 14.4 g/dL; creatinine 1.0 mg/dL
Vagal maneuvers: no change
Adenosine 6 mg rapid IV push: transient AV block unmasks continuous sawtooth flutter waves at ~300/min in the inferior leads (negative deflections in II, III, aVF, positive in V1); ventricular rate then resumes at 150 bpm
Portable CXR: normal heart size, clear lungs

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the rhythm diagnosis?
options: AV-nodal reentrant tachycardia (AVNRT) as the primary diagnosis
Sinus tachycardia from dehydration as the primary diagnosis
Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response in this clinical context
Multifocal atrial tachycardia as the primary diagnosis
Typical atrial flutter with 2:1 AV conduction
stages.explanation: A regular narrow-complex tachycardia fixed at almost exactly 150 bpm should always prompt the thought 'flutter with 2:1 block' — the typical flutter circuit cycles at about 300 per minute, and 2:1 AV conduction yields 150. Adenosine confirmed it: transient AV block stripped away the QRS complexes and revealed the continuous sawtooth at ~300/min, negative in the inferior leads and positive in V1, the signature of counterclockwise cavotricuspid isthmus-dependent flutter. AVNRT would typically terminate abruptly with adenosine rather than merely unmasking persistent atrial activity. Sinus tachycardia varies with hydration, pain, and position, gradually trending rather than sitting fixed at 150, and adenosine would show slowing with visible sinus P waves. Atrial fibrillation is excluded by the strict regularity and organized flutter waves. Multifocal atrial tachycardia is an irregular rhythm with three or more P-wave morphologies, classically in severe pulmonary disease.

stages.label: ANTICOAGULATION
stages.question: Rate control is started with diltiazem. What is the correct approach to stroke prevention?
options: No anticoagulation — atrial flutter carries a much lower stroke risk than atrial fibrillation in this setting
Anticoagulate only if a transesophageal echo first shows a thrombus as the immediate next step
Anticoagulate for 48 hours only, then stop once the rate is controlled as the immediate next step
Aspirin 81 mg daily is sufficient as the immediate next step
Start a direct oral anticoagulant
stages.explanation: Atrial flutter is risk-stratified and anticoagulated exactly like atrial fibrillation: the fluttering atria still generate stasis and thrombus, patients frequently have coexisting AF, and guidelines apply identical CHA2DS2-VASc thresholds. His score is 3 (age 65–74 = 1, hypertension = 1, diabetes = 1), well above the threshold at which anticoagulation is recommended for men, so a DOAC such as apixaban should begin now — also essential because cardioversion (electrical, chemical, or ablative) is likely in his near future and he has been in the rhythm over 24 hours. The 'flutter is safer' belief is a dangerous myth. Aspirin provides inadequate stroke protection in atrial arrhythmias and is no longer recommended for this purpose. TEE is used to expedite early cardioversion, not as a gatekeeper for anticoagulation itself. Stopping after 48 hours ignores both his ongoing arrhythmia and his chronic risk profile.

stages.label: DEFINITIVE THERAPY
stages.question: He undergoes successful cardioversion two days later after TEE excludes thrombus. What is the best long-term rhythm strategy to discuss?
options: Referral for catheter ablation of the cavotricuspid isthmus, which is first-line for typical flutter with a success rate above 90–95%
Permanent pacemaker implantation as the immediate next step
Lifelong amiodarone for rhythm maintenance as the immediate next step
No further therapy or follow-up once sinus rhythm is restored as the immediate next step
Flecainide monotherapy without any AV-nodal blocking agent as the immediate next step
