stages.explanation: Typical atrial flutter depends on a single, anatomically fixed reentrant circuit through the cavotricuspid isthmus, making it uniquely curable: catheter ablation of the isthmus succeeds in over 90–95% of cases with low complication rates, and guidelines endorse it as first-line therapy for symptomatic typical flutter — often preferable to any long-term drug strategy. Lifelong amiodarone trades a curable arrhythmia for cumulative thyroid, hepatic, pulmonary, and ocular toxicity and is reserved for patients with limited options. Flecainide without AV-nodal blockade is a classic hazard: it can slow the flutter circuit to ~200/min and permit 1:1 AV conduction, producing a faster, hemodynamically dangerous ventricular response. A pacemaker treats bradycardia, not flutter. Doing nothing invites recurrence — flutter recurs frequently after cardioversion alone — and he also needs ongoing anticoagulation decisions, evaluation of his probable obstructive sleep apnea, and risk-factor management regardless of strategy.

cases.diagnosis: Typical (cavotricuspid isthmus-dependent) atrial flutter with 2:1 AV conduction — anticoagulated per CHA2DS2-VASc and referred for catheter ablation
pearls: A regular narrow-complex tachycardia fixed at ~150 bpm is atrial flutter with 2:1 block until proven otherwise; adenosine unmasks the sawtooth.
Atrial flutter gets the same CHA2DS2-VASc-guided anticoagulation as atrial fibrillation — the 'flutter is safer' myth causes strokes.
Cavotricuspid isthmus ablation cures typical flutter in >90–95% of cases and is first-line therapy.
Never give flecainide for flutter without AV-nodal blockade — slowing the circuit can allow 1:1 conduction and hemodynamic collapse.

cases.title: New aches after a new prescription
cases.chiefComplaint: Ever since I started that cholesterol pill my thighs and shoulders ache — I want to stop it.
cases.history: A 63-year-old woman returns to clinic reporting three weeks of bilateral, symmetric aching in her thighs and shoulders that began about two weeks after starting atorvastatin 40 mg nightly. The statin was initiated for primary prevention after a 10-year ASCVD risk estimate of 12.5% (LDL 148 mg/dL). The aching is worse climbing stairs and after her morning walk, improves with rest, and is not associated with true weakness — she can still rise from chairs and carry groceries. She denies dark urine, fever, rash, joint swelling, prolonged morning stiffness, scalp tenderness, jaw claudication, or visual symptoms. Past medical history includes hypothyroidism on levothyroxine 75 µg daily (stable dose for years) and hypertension on chlorthalidone 25 mg daily. She takes no fibrates, no antifungals, no macrolides, and drinks no grapefruit juice. No known drug allergies. She does not smoke and drinks wine rarely. Family history includes a sister who 'couldn't tolerate statins' and a father with a stroke at 70. She is otherwise active and walks two miles most mornings.
cases.exam: Well-appearing woman in no distress. Musculoskeletal exam: strength 5/5 in deltoids, hip flexors, and quadriceps bilaterally; mild diffuse tenderness to palpation of the thigh muscles; no fasciculations or atrophy. She rises from a chair without using her arms. Reflexes 2+ and symmetric. No proximal joint synovitis, no temporal artery tenderness, normal shoulder range of motion. Skin: no heliotrope rash, Gottron papules, or other rashes. Cardiopulmonary exam unremarkable. No thyromegaly. Gait normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial evaluation of her muscle symptoms?
options: Start empiric prednisone for presumed inflammatory myopathy
Stop the statin permanently and document a statin allergy in the chart
Switch her immediately to red yeast rice as a 'natural' alternative
Check creatine kinase
Order MRI of both thighs to look for myositis as the first step today
stages.explanation: The workup of suspected statin-associated muscle symptoms starts with a creatine kinase to distinguish benign myalgia (normal or mildly elevated CK) from true myopathy, rhabdomyolysis, or immune-mediated necrotizing myopathy; checking TSH matters doubly here because hypothyroidism both causes myopathy and potentiates statin muscle toxicity, and a medication review hunts for CYP3A4 inhibitors and fibrates that raise statin levels. Permanently abandoning statins and labeling an 'allergy' forfeits proven cardiovascular benefit — most patients with muscle symptoms ultimately tolerate some statin regimen. MRI is reserved for suspected inflammatory myopathy with weakness or markedly elevated CK. Empiric steroids treat a diagnosis not yet made and carry real harms. Red yeast rice contains unregulated, variable amounts of a natural lovastatin (monacolin K) — it is neither safer nor reliably dosed, and it can cause the same myopathy.

stages.label: LABORATORY RESULTS
stages.content:
Creatine kinase 102 U/L (normal 26–192)
TSH 2.4 µIU/mL (on stable levothyroxine)
Creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, eGFR >60 mL/min/1.73m²
AST 24 U/L, ALT 22 U/L
25-hydroxyvitamin D 28 ng/mL
ESR 14 mm/hr, CRP 0.3 mg/dL
Urinalysis: no blood, no myoglobin
Medication review: no fibrates, azoles, macrolides, cyclosporine, or grapefruit juice

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Hypothyroid myopathy as the primary diagnosis
Rhabdomyolysis as the primary diagnosis
Statin-induced immune-mediated necrotizing myopathy (anti-HMGCR myopathy)
Statin-associated muscle symptoms
Polymyalgia rheumatica as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Bilateral symmetric proximal aching beginning weeks after statin initiation, with preserved strength and a normal CK, is statin-associated muscle symptoms with normal CK — by far the most common statin muscle complaint, affecting perhaps 5–10% of users in practice (though blinded trials show much of this is nocebo effect). Rhabdomyolysis requires a dramatically elevated CK (typically more than 10 times the upper limit, often in the thousands) with myoglobinuria — her CK of 102 and bland urine exclude it. Immune-mediated necrotizing myopathy presents with progressive proximal weakness and CK in the thousands that persists or worsens after stopping the statin; she has neither weakness nor CK elevation. Polymyalgia rheumatica causes shoulder and hip girdle stiffness in older adults but with markedly elevated ESR/CRP — hers are normal. Hypothyroid myopathy is excluded by her normal TSH on stable replacement.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Discontinue all statins permanently and document intolerance after this single trial as initial therapy
Add coenzyme Q10 to the current regimen as proven therapy for statin myalgia as initial therapy
Hold the statin for 2–4 weeks
Order a muscle biopsy before any medication change as initial therapy
Continue atorvastatin at the same dose and add scheduled NSAIDs for the pain as initial therapy
stages.explanation: The guideline-endorsed strategy for SAMS with normal CK is a structured washout-rechallenge: hold the statin for 2–4 weeks to confirm symptoms resolve (establishing causality and excluding the substantial nocebo contribution), then rechallenge with an alternative agent or regimen — commonly low-dose rosuvastatin, which is hydrophilic and long-acting enough to allow alternate-day or even twice-weekly dosing. With this approach, the large majority of 'statin-intolerant' patients ultimately tolerate some statin, preserving the drug class with the best outcome evidence. Declaring permanent intolerance after one trial of one statin abandons that benefit prematurely. Continuing the offending dose while masking symptoms with chronic NSAIDs adds renal and GI risk without solving anything. Muscle biopsy is for suspected necrotizing or inflammatory myopathy — weakness with high CK. Coenzyme Q10, despite its popularity, showed no benefit over placebo in randomized trials and should not be presented as proven therapy.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Symptoms resolve off the statin but recur with low-dose rosuvastatin and again with alternate-day pravastatin. Her LDL remains 145 mg/dL. What is the best next step?
options: Start ezetimibe; if LDL remains above goal, add a PCSK9 inhibitor
Fenofibrate as her primary LDL-lowering agent as the immediate next step
Rechallenge with the original atorvastatin 40 mg a third time
Accept the LDL of 145 with no lipid-lowering therapy at all
Niacin to lower LDL as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: After documented intolerance to multiple statins at the lowest feasible doses, the evidence-based ladder of non-statin therapy begins with ezetimibe — well tolerated, lowering LDL about 18–20%, with outcome support from IMPROVE-IT — and escalates to a PCSK9 inhibitor (evolocumab or alirocumab, each lowering LDL roughly 60% with outcome benefit) if she remains above goal; bempedoic acid is a further option with proven benefit specifically in statin-intolerant patients (CLEAR Outcomes) and minimal muscle toxicity because it is not activated in skeletal muscle. Niacin failed to improve outcomes when added in modern trials (AIM-HIGH, HPS2-THRIVE) and causes flushing, hyperglycemia, and gout. Fenofibrate targets triglycerides, not LDL, and itself causes myopathy. A third rechallenge with the same atorvastatin dose that provoked symptoms twice is illogical. Abandoning lipid-lowering entirely leaves a 12.5% 10-year risk and LDL of 145 untreated when effective alternatives exist.

cases.diagnosis: Statin-associated muscle symptoms (myalgia with normal CK), managed with washout, structured statin rechallenge, then ezetimibe with PCSK9-inhibitor escalation after confirmed multi-statin intolerance
pearls: Statin myalgia with normal CK is common and benign; true myopathy, rhabdomyolysis, and anti-HMGCR necrotizing myopathy feature weakness and markedly elevated CK.
Always check CK and TSH and review interacting drugs (fibrates, azoles, macrolides, grapefruit) before declaring statin intolerance.
Washout then rechallenge with a different statin or intermittent low-dose regimen — most 'intolerant' patients tolerate some statin; much of SAMS is nocebo.
After genuine multi-statin failure: ezetimibe first, then PCSK9 inhibitor or bempedoic acid; coenzyme Q10 and niacin lack outcome evidence.

cases.title: Pressure in the chest that won't quit
cases.chiefComplaint: I've had a heavy pressure in my chest for the past two hours and it just won't let up.
cases.history: A 66-year-old woman presents with two hours of substernal chest pressure that began at rest while she was watching television. The pressure radiates to her left arm and jaw, is rated 7/10, and is accompanied by diaphoresis, nausea, and mild shortness of breath. Nothing makes it better or worse; it has been constant since onset. One week ago she had a similar but milder episode while carrying groceries that resolved within five minutes of rest, which she attributed to indigestion. Past medical history includes hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus, and hyperlipidemia. Medications are lisinopril 20 mg daily, metformin 1,000 mg twice daily, and atorvastatin 40 mg nightly; she admits she often misses the statin. She has no drug allergies. She smoked one pack per day for 30 years and quit five years ago; she drinks wine socially. Family history is notable for a brother who had a myocardial infarction at age 58.
cases.exam: Anxious, diaphoretic woman in moderate distress, clutching her chest. No jugular venous distention. Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally. Cardiac exam: regular rate and rhythm, normal S1 and S2, an audible S4, no murmurs or rubs. Chest wall is nontender, and the pain is not reproducible with movement or inspiration. Abdomen soft and nontender. Extremities warm with 2+ symmetric radial and pedal pulses; no peripheral edema. No focal neurologic deficits.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate step in this patient's evaluation?
options: Schedule an exercise treadmill stress test in this clinical context
Obtain a 12-lead ECG within 10 minutes
Send a D-dimer and order CT pulmonary angiography
Order CT coronary angiography to evaluate for coronary disease
Begin empiric fibrinolytic therapy as the first step today
stages.explanation: Any patient with possible ACS needs a 12-lead ECG within 10 minutes of arrival to triage for STEMI, and chewable aspirin should be given immediately because it reduces mortality in ACS. CT coronary angiography can help exclude coronary disease in low-risk chest pain but should never delay the ECG in an ongoing, high-probability presentation. D-dimer and CT pulmonary angiography target pulmonary embolism, which is far less likely with pressure-like pain radiating to the arm and jaw plus multiple coronary risk factors. Fibrinolysis is only indicated for STEMI when timely PCI is unavailable — there is no ECG yet, so it cannot be justified. Exercise treadmill testing is contraindicated during ongoing rest pain with suspected acute coronary syndrome.

stages.label: LABS & ECG
stages.content:
12-lead ECG: 1.5 mm horizontal ST depression in V4-V6, T-wave inversion in I and aVL, no ST elevation
hs-Troponin I (0 h): 0.21 ng/mL (URL <0.04)
hs-Troponin I (2 h): 0.48 ng/mL
CBC: WBC 9.8 ×10³/µL, Hgb 13.1 g/dL, Plt 265 ×10³/µL
BMP: Na 138 mEq/L, K 4.2 mEq/L, Cr 1.0 mg/dL, glucose 188 mg/dL
Chest X-ray: clear lungs, normal cardiac silhouette

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best explains this presentation?
options: ST-elevation myocardial infarction
Takotsubo (stress) cardiomyopathy
Non-ST-elevation myocardial infarction
Acute pericarditis
Unstable angina in this clinical context
stages.explanation: She has an acute coronary syndrome with a rising troponin (0.21 to 0.48 ng/mL) but no ST-segment elevation, which defines NSTEMI. Unstable angina is the same clinical syndrome without myocardial necrosis — her positive troponin excludes it. STEMI requires ST elevation in two contiguous leads (or new left bundle branch block); she has only ST depression and T-wave inversion. Takotsubo cardiomyopathy can mimic ACS but classically follows an emotional or physical stressor in a postmenopausal woman, typically with anterior ST elevation or deep T-wave inversions, and remains a diagnosis of exclusion after angiography shows apical ballooning without culprit disease. Acute pericarditis causes pleuritic, positional pain with diffuse concave ST elevation and PR depression, none of which is present here.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Beyond aspirin and a high-intensity statin, what is the best management strategy?
options: Ischemia-guided strategy with discharge and outpatient stress testing
Calcium channel blocker monotherapy and observation as initial therapy
Antiplatelet therapy plus anticoagulation, with an early invasive strategy
Immediate fibrinolytic therapy as initial therapy
Emergent catheterization within 2 hours as initial therapy
stages.explanation: NSTEMI management starts with dual antiplatelet therapy, anticoagulation (for example, heparin), high-intensity statin, and a beta-blocker once stable. Because she has high-risk features — an elevated and rising troponin with dynamic ST depression — an early invasive strategy with angiography within 24 hours improves outcomes compared with an ischemia-guided approach. Fibrinolysis has no role in NSTEMI; the infarct artery is usually not totally occluded, so lysis adds bleeding risk without benefit. Discharge for outpatient stress testing is appropriate only for low-risk, troponin-negative patients. Immediate catheterization within 2 hours is reserved for refractory angina, hemodynamic instability, or electrical instability, none of which she has. Calcium channel blocker monotherapy does not address the underlying plaque rupture and thrombus.

stages.label: RISK STRATIFICATION
stages.question: Which feature of her presentation most strongly supports choosing an early invasive strategy?
options: Age over 65 alone as the immediate next step
Elevated troponin with dynamic ST-segment depression
Persistent anxiety despite treatment in this setting
Family history of coronary artery disease
Diabetes mellitus alone as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Risk scores such as GRACE and TIMI guide the timing of invasive management in NSTE-ACS, and elevated troponin with dynamic ST-segment changes are the strongest predictors of benefit from early angiography within 24 hours. Age, diabetes, and other comorbidities add risk points, but taken alone they do not mandate an early invasive approach the way ongoing myonecrosis with ECG changes does. Family history of premature CAD increases pretest probability of disease but carries no weight in the GRACE score and does not determine catheterization timing. Anxiety is nonspecific and has no prognostic value. Recognizing which features make a patient high risk is the key decision point separating the early invasive strategy from a delayed invasive (25-72 hours) or ischemia-guided strategy.

cases.diagnosis: Non-ST-elevation myocardial infarction (NSTEMI)
pearls: NSTEMI is ACS with a positive troponin but no ST elevation; unstable angina is the identical picture with negative biomarkers.
Elevated troponin and dynamic ST changes are high-risk features that warrant an early invasive strategy (angiography within 24 hours).
Fibrinolytics are never indicated in NSTE-ACS — the culprit artery is rarely completely occluded and bleeding risk outweighs benefit.
GRACE and TIMI scores formalize risk stratification and drive the timing of catheterization in NSTE-ACS.

cases.title: Chest pain that crashed after a nitro tab
cases.chiefComplaint: Crushing chest pain for 90 minutes; blood pressure dropped sharply after EMS gave nitroglycerin.
cases.history: A 63-year-old man develops crushing substernal chest pain while shoveling mulch, persisting for 90 minutes and radiating to the epigastrium, with nausea, belching, and profuse sweating. He initially blamed heartburn, but the pain did not respond to antacids, so his wife called EMS. Paramedics gave 324 mg of chewable aspirin and one sublingual nitroglycerin tablet, after which his blood pressure fell from 128/80 to 78/50 mmHg and he became lightheaded and pale; no further nitroglycerin was given. Past medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia. Medications are amlodipine 10 mg daily and rosuvastatin 20 mg daily, taken inconsistently. He has no known drug allergies. He has smoked one pack per day for 40 years and drinks two beers most evenings. His father died of a heart attack at age 67. Review of systems is negative for fever, cough, leg swelling, recent travel, or immobilization.
cases.exam: Pale, cool, diaphoretic man lying flat and uncomfortable. Jugular venous pressure elevated to 10 cm with a positive Kussmaul sign (JVP rises on inspiration). Lungs completely clear to auscultation bilaterally — no crackles or wheezes. Heart: bradycardic, regular, normal S1 and S2, no murmurs, rubs, or gallops. Abdomen soft with mild epigastric discomfort, no rebound. Extremities cool with thready but symmetric pulses; no edema. Blood pressures equal in both arms; no back pain.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: His 12-lead ECG shows 2 mm ST elevation in leads II, III, and aVF. Given the profound hypotension after nitroglycerin, what is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Give IV metoprolol to reduce myocardial oxygen demand
Start a dobutamine infusion as the first step today
Obtain a right-sided ECG (lead V4R)
Administer a second dose of sublingual nitroglycerin
Give IV furosemide for the elevated jugular venous pressure
stages.explanation: Inferior ST elevation in II, III, and aVF plus profound hypotension after nitroglycerin should immediately raise suspicion for right ventricular infarction, which complicates roughly a third of inferior MIs. The fastest confirmatory step is a right-sided ECG: ST elevation of 1 mm or more in V4R is the most predictive single finding. Additional nitroglycerin would further reduce preload and deepen the hypotension. Furosemide also strips preload and is doubly wrong in a patient with clear lungs and no congestion. Dobutamine may eventually be needed for RV failure refractory to volume, but diagnosis and fluid loading come first. IV metoprolol is dangerous in a hypotensive, bradycardic patient with an evolving infarct and possible nodal ischemia.

stages.label: RIGHT-SIDED ECG & LABS
stages.content:
Right-sided ECG: 1.5 mm ST elevation in V4R
Standard ECG: 2 mm ST elevation in II, III, aVF with elevation in lead III greater than lead II; reciprocal ST depression in I and aVL
Troponin I: 2.3 ng/mL (URL <0.04)
Lactate: 2.4 mmol/L
BMP: Cr 1.1 mg/dL, K 4.4 mEq/L
Portable chest X-ray: clear lung fields, normal mediastinal width

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best explains the hypotension, elevated JVP, and clear lungs?
options: Massive pulmonary embolism in this clinical context
Cardiac tamponade as the primary diagnosis
Inferior STEMI with acute severe mitral regurgitation
Acute aortic dissection with coronary involvement
Inferior STEMI with right ventricular infarction
stages.explanation: The triad of hypotension, elevated jugular venous pressure, and clear lung fields in the setting of inferior ST elevation is classic for right ventricular infarction, confirmed here by ST elevation in V4R — the single most specific lead. ST elevation in lead III greater than lead II points to a proximal right coronary artery culprit, the vessel that usually supplies the RV. Pulmonary embolism can cause hypotension with elevated JVP but does not produce focal inferior ST elevation with a markedly positive troponin in this pattern. Tamponade would feature distant heart sounds, pulsus paradoxus, and low ECG voltage rather than regional ST elevation. Dissection is argued against by equal arm pressures, the absence of tearing back pain, and a normal mediastinum. Acute severe mitral regurgitation causes flash pulmonary edema — his lungs are clear.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first intervention for his hypotension?
options: IV furosemide as initial therapy
IV morphine for pain control as initial therapy
Bolus IV isotonic crystalloid to restore right ventricular preload
Oral metoprolol as initial therapy
IV nitroglycerin infusion as initial therapy
stages.explanation: An infarcted right ventricle cannot generate adequate output without high filling pressures, making the patient exquisitely preload dependent. The first hemodynamic intervention is bolus isotonic crystalloid — typically 500 mL aliquots, reassessing after each, up to 1-2 L — to restore RV preload and left-sided filling. A nitroglycerin infusion would repeat the mistake that caused the crash, since venodilation strips preload. Furosemide does the same and targets congestion he does not have. Morphine blunts sympathetic drive and dilates veins, worsening hypotension. Beta-blockade is contraindicated with hypotension and bradycardia; the RCA frequently supplies the SA and AV nodes, so conduction disease may also be evolving. If fluid boluses fail to restore perfusion, an inotrope such as dobutamine is the next step.

stages.label: REPERFUSION
stages.question: After initial stabilization, what is the definitive next step?
options: Fibrinolysis only if pain persists beyond 12 hours in this setting
Admit for serial troponins and a next-day stress test in this setting
CT coronary angiography to define the anatomy first in this setting
Place an intra-aortic balloon pump before considering reperfusion
Emergent primary PCI with a door-to-balloon time of 90 minutes or less
stages.explanation: This is an acute STEMI well within the reperfusion window, and primary PCI with a door-to-balloon time of 90 minutes or less is the standard of care; reopening the right coronary artery is also the definitive therapy for the failing right ventricle. Serial troponins with stress testing are for low-risk chest pain, not an evolving STEMI. Fibrinolysis is the fallback when PCI cannot be achieved within about 120 minutes of first medical contact — it is not something to defer until 12 hours of symptoms, when benefit has largely evaporated. CT coronary angiography has no role once STEMI is established and only delays treatment. An intra-aortic balloon pump is not first-line therapy and should never postpone reperfusion.

cases.diagnosis: Inferior STEMI with right ventricular infarction
pearls: Inferior STEMI + hypotension + clear lungs + elevated JVP = right ventricular infarction until proven otherwise; confirm with V4R.
Avoid preload reducers (nitrates, morphine, diuretics) in RV infarction; treat hypotension with IV fluid boluses first.
ST elevation in lead III greater than lead II suggests a proximal RCA occlusion — the artery that usually perfuses the RV, SA node, and AV node.
Definitive treatment is emergent reperfusion: primary PCI with door-to-balloon time of 90 minutes or less.

cases.title: Breathless, queasy, and worn out
cases.chiefComplaint: I just can't catch my breath and I feel sick to my stomach — but my chest doesn't hurt at all.
cases.history: An 81-year-old woman is brought in by her daughter for six hours of shortness of breath, nausea with one episode of vomiting, and overwhelming fatigue that began this morning while she was making breakfast. She explicitly denies chest pain, pressure, or tightness when asked multiple times. She also denies fever, cough, abdominal pain, diarrhea, and urinary symptoms. She has had type 2 diabetes mellitus for 20 years complicated by peripheral neuropathy, along with hypertension and stage 3a chronic kidney disease. At baseline she lives alone, cooks for herself, and walks to church weekly. Medications: insulin glargine 24 units nightly, metformin 500 mg twice daily, losartan 50 mg daily, and simvastatin 20 mg nightly. She has no drug allergies. She has never smoked and does not drink alcohol. Her daughter says she looked gray this morning and has never complained like this before. Family history includes a sister with a stroke in her seventies.
cases.exam: Elderly woman in mild respiratory distress, speaking in full sentences but visibly fatigued. JVP mildly elevated at 8-9 cm. Lungs: fine bibasilar crackles. Heart: tachycardic, regular, normal S1 and S2 with an S4, no murmurs or rubs. Abdomen soft, nontender, no guarding. Extremities: trace bilateral pitting edema, warm, 2+ pulses. Decreased vibratory sensation in both feet. Chest wall nontender. No focal neurologic deficits.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial diagnostic step?
options: Give an albuterol nebulizer for presumed obstructive lung disease
Obtain a 12-lead ECG
Send a D-dimer to rule out pulmonary embolism as the first step today
Order a non-contrast CT of the abdomen for nausea and vomiting
Treat for viral gastroenteritis with ondansetron and oral rehydration, then discharge
stages.explanation: Elderly patients, women, and diabetics frequently present with anginal equivalents — dyspnea, nausea, fatigue, or syncope — instead of chest pain, so acute MI must be actively excluded with an ECG and serial troponins even when pain is absent. Treating presumed gastroenteritis would anchor on the nausea and miss a potentially fatal diagnosis; she has no diarrhea or sick contacts. CT of the abdomen is premature with a completely benign abdominal exam. Albuterol targets bronchospasm, but she has crackles rather than wheezes and no obstructive lung disease history. A D-dimer in an 81-year-old is almost always elevated and nonspecific; pulmonary embolism is conceivable, but the crackles, S4, and heavy risk-factor burden make myocardial ischemia with early pulmonary congestion the diagnosis to chase first.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
12-lead ECG: 1.5 mm ST depression with biphasic T waves in V2-V5, no ST elevation
hs-Troponin I (0 h): 1.8 ng/mL (URL <0.04)
hs-Troponin I (3 h): 3.2 ng/mL
BNP: 890 pg/mL
BMP: Na 136 mEq/L, K 4.6 mEq/L, Cr 1.4 mg/dL (baseline 1.3), glucose 246 mg/dL
CBC: WBC 10.9 ×10³/µL, Hgb 11.8 g/dL
Chest X-ray: mild pulmonary vascular congestion, no consolidation

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best explains her presentation?
options: Diabetic ketoacidosis as the primary diagnosis
Pulmonary embolism as the primary diagnosis
Acute gastritis as the primary diagnosis
Community-acquired pneumonia as the primary diagnosis
Acute myocardial infarction presenting without chest pain
stages.explanation: A rising troponin (1.8 to 3.2 ng/mL) with dynamic anterior ST depression establishes acute myocardial infarction — an NSTEMI — despite the complete absence of chest pain. Her dyspnea, nausea, and fatigue were anginal equivalents, and the elevated BNP with crackles reflects ischemic left ventricular dysfunction causing early pulmonary congestion. Diabetic ketoacidosis is excluded by a glucose of only 246 mg/dL without acidosis, and it would not cause regional ST depression with a climbing troponin. Pneumonia is unlikely without fever, cough, or consolidation on chest X-ray. Gastritis causes neither troponin elevation nor ECG changes nor pulmonary congestion. Pulmonary embolism can modestly elevate troponin, but the regional anterior ST depressions, S4, and radiographic congestion point firmly to a coronary event rather than an embolic one.

stages.label: MECHANISM
stages.question: Which mechanism best explains why she had no chest pain during her infarction?
options: Age-related decline in pain perception alone as the immediate next step
Female sex preventing typical anginal pain as the immediate next step
A small infarct size producing minimal symptoms before discharge planning
Beta-blocker therapy masking her symptoms as the immediate next step
Diabetic cardiac autonomic neuropathy blunting ischemic pain transmission
stages.explanation: Longstanding diabetes damages cardiac autonomic afferent fibers — the same process that caused her peripheral neuropathy — so ischemic pain signals never reach conscious perception. This is why a substantial fraction of infarcts in elderly diabetics are silent or atypical. Pain thresholds do change with age, but the specific, well-described mechanism in a diabetic with documented neuropathy is autonomic sensory denervation, not aging alone. She takes no beta-blocker, and beta-blockers blunt heart rate and contractility, not pain perception. Female sex is associated with atypical presentations but does not abolish pain through any defined mechanism. Infarct size does not determine whether pain is felt — silent infarcts can be large, exactly as her markedly elevated and rising troponin suggests.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management and disposition?
options: Immediate fibrinolytic therapy with appropriate follow-up
Discharge with outpatient cardiology follow-up within one week
Oral antibiotics for presumed pneumonia with appropriate follow-up
Stress echocardiography today to confirm ischemia with appropriate follow-up
Admit for antiplatelet therapy, anticoagulation, gentle diuresis,
stages.explanation: She has NSTEMI with high-risk features — a markedly elevated and rising troponin, dynamic ST changes, and signs of heart failure — so the correct disposition is admission with antiplatelet therapy, anticoagulation, a high-intensity statin, gentle diuresis for congestion, and early coronary angiography. Advanced age alone is not a contraindication to an invasive strategy, and elderly patients often derive the greatest absolute benefit. Discharge would send home an evolving infarct. Fibrinolysis is reserved for STEMI without timely PCI access and has no role in NSTEMI. Antibiotics treat a pneumonia that the normal temperature, clear history, and congestion-only chest X-ray argue against. Stress testing is contraindicated during an acute infarction and adds nothing once the diagnosis is biochemically established.

cases.diagnosis: Acute myocardial infarction (NSTEMI) with atypical presentation — silent ischemia in an elderly diabetic
pearls: Elderly diabetic patients often present with anginal equivalents — dyspnea, nausea, fatigue — rather than chest pain; keep a low threshold for ECG and troponin.
Diabetic cardiac autonomic neuropathy blunts ischemic pain perception, producing silent infarction.
A rising troponin with dynamic ST changes defines myocardial infarction regardless of symptoms.
Advanced age alone should not exclude patients from an early invasive strategy in high-risk NSTEMI.

cases.title: Chest tightness that keeps coming back
cases.chiefComplaint: The tightness used to come only when I climbed stairs — now it wakes me up at night.
cases.history: A 59-year-old man presents with three weeks of substernal chest tightness. Initially it occurred only when climbing two flights of stairs, lasted about five minutes, and resolved with rest. Over the past three days the episodes have become more frequent, now occurring at rest, lasting up to 15 minutes, and twice waking him from sleep. The most recent episode ended an hour before arrival; he is currently pain-free. The tightness radiates to his left shoulder and is accompanied by mild diaphoresis but no nausea or dyspnea. He has hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and obesity with a BMI of 33. Medications: amlodipine 5 mg daily and atorvastatin 20 mg nightly, which he admits to taking only a few times a week. He has no drug allergies. He smokes half a pack per day and has done so for 35 years; he drinks beer on weekends. His father underwent coronary bypass surgery at age 60. He denies recent heavy lifting, trauma, or reflux symptoms.
cases.exam: Comfortable, obese man, currently pain-free. JVP normal. Lungs clear bilaterally. Heart: regular rate and rhythm, normal S1 and S2, no murmurs, gallops, or rubs. Chest wall nontender; the pain is not reproduced by palpation or position change. Abdomen soft and nontender without epigastric discomfort. Extremities: no edema, 2+ symmetric pulses, no femoral bruits. No xanthomas or xanthelasma. Neurologic exam grossly intact.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: He is pain-free on arrival. What is the most appropriate initial approach?
options: Order CT pulmonary angiography as the first step today
Obtain an ECG, give aspirin, start serial troponins,
Check a single troponin and discharge if it is negative
Discharge with an outpatient stress test since he is currently pain-free
Activate the catheterization lab immediately as the first step today
stages.explanation: Crescendo angina — increasing frequency and duration with new rest episodes — is unstable angina until proven otherwise, even if the patient is currently pain-free. The correct approach is an immediate ECG, aspirin, serial troponins, and continuous telemetry monitoring. Discharging for an outpatient stress test risks infarction or sudden death before the test ever happens. Catheterization lab activation is for STEMI or hemodynamic or electrical instability, not a currently pain-free patient without ECG data. A single troponin cannot exclude ACS: necrosis markers take hours to rise, and unstable angina never elevates them at all. CT pulmonary angiography targets pulmonary embolism, which is unlikely with exertional crescendo pain, no pleuritic features, and no thromboembolic risk factors.

stages.label: SERIAL TESTING
stages.content:
12-lead ECG on arrival (pain-free): normal sinus rhythm, no ST or T-wave abnormalities
Repeat ECG during a 10-minute episode of chest tightness in the ED: 1 mm horizontal ST depression in V5-V6, resolving completely as the pain subsides
hs-Troponin I at 0, 3, and 6 hours: <0.010 ng/mL (URL <0.014) — all negative
Lipid panel: LDL 162 mg/dL, HDL 38 mg/dL
CBC and BMP: unremarkable
Chest X-ray: normal

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best fits these findings?
options: Unstable angina
Musculoskeletal chest pain
Stable angina in this clinical context
Non-ST-elevation myocardial infarction
Gastroesophageal reflux disease
stages.explanation: He has an acute coronary syndrome — rest angina with dynamic, reversible ST depression — but serially negative troponins at 0, 3, and 6 hours exclude infarction. ACS without myonecrosis is unstable angina. NSTEMI requires a rise and/or fall of troponin above the 99th percentile, which never occurred. Stable angina is exertional, predictable, and unchanged in pattern; his symptoms have escalated to rest and nocturnal pain, the definition of instability. Reflux does not produce transient ischemic ST depression that tracks precisely with symptoms. Musculoskeletal pain is reproducible with palpation or movement — his is not — and causes no ECG changes. The key lesson: a normal troponin rules out infarction, not acute coronary syndrome. The unstable plaque is still sitting in his coronary artery.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management?
options: Outpatient Holter monitoring as initial therapy
Increase amlodipine and discharge with follow-up as initial therapy
Admit; dual antiplatelet therapy, anticoagulation, high-intensity statin,
Fibrinolytic therapy as initial therapy
Discharge with a proton pump inhibitor trial as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Unstable angina with dynamic ECG changes is high-risk ACS and is managed like NSTEMI: admission, dual antiplatelet therapy, anticoagulation with heparin, a high-intensity statin, beta-blockade, and coronary angiography — typically within 24-72 hours, and sooner if symptoms recur or hemodynamics deteriorate. A proton pump inhibitor trial outsources an unstable plaque to gastroenterology and risks completed infarction. Fibrinolysis is reserved for STEMI; in unstable angina and NSTEMI it adds intracranial bleeding risk without any mortality benefit. Holter monitoring evaluates arrhythmias, not ischemia, and would delay definitive care. Increasing amlodipine treats blood pressure rather than plaque instability, and discharging him without antithrombotic therapy and risk stratification would be a critical error given his accelerating symptom trajectory.

stages.label: KEY CONCEPT
stages.question: Which statement about troponin testing in this patient is most accurate?
options: Serially negative troponins exclude infarction but do not exclude unstable angina
A normal troponin at any time point excludes all acute coronary syndromes
Troponin elevation is specific for atherosclerotic plaque rupture in this setting
Troponin becomes detectable within 30 minutes of ischemia onset in this setting
A single negative troponin at arrival reliably rules out myocardial infarction
stages.explanation: Troponin is a marker of myocardial necrosis, not ischemia. In unstable angina the plaque has ruptured or eroded and flow is critically reduced, but no cell death has occurred yet — so troponins remain negative while the patient stays at high risk of progressing to infarction. Serially negative high-sensitivity troponins effectively exclude MI but never exclude unstable angina; that diagnosis is clinical, resting on rest pain, crescendo pattern, and dynamic ECG changes. Troponin requires roughly one to three hours after necrosis begins to become detectable even with high-sensitivity assays, not 30 minutes. A single arrival value misses early infarcts, which is precisely why serial measurement is standard. Troponin also rises in myocarditis, pulmonary embolism, sepsis, and renal failure — it is not specific for plaque rupture.

cases.diagnosis: Unstable angina
pearls: Unstable angina is ACS with negative biomarkers; serially normal troponins rule out infarction, never ACS.
Crescendo or rest angina mandates admission, antithrombotic therapy, and risk stratification — not outpatient stress testing.
Dynamic ST depression during pain that resolves with symptoms is objective evidence of ischemia and marks high risk.
Unstable angina with high-risk features is managed identically to NSTEMI, including early invasive angiography.

cases.title: Agitated and clutching his chest at 3 a.m.
cases.chiefComplaint: My chest is pounding and it feels like it's going to explode.
cases.history: A 27-year-old man is brought in by friends at 3 a.m. from a nightclub with one hour of substernal chest pressure, palpitations, and a feeling that his heart is going to explode. He is pacing, anxious, and diaphoretic. After initial reluctance, he admits to insufflating cocaine roughly 90 minutes before symptom onset; he uses it most weekends. He also smokes half a pack of cigarettes daily and drank three vodka drinks tonight. He denies opioid, methamphetamine, or synthetic cannabinoid use. He has no known medical history, takes no medications, and has no drug allergies. He has never had chest pain before. There is no family history of premature coronary disease or sudden cardiac death. Review of systems is positive for anxiety, tremor, and palpitations; negative for fever before tonight, cough, vomiting, focal weakness, severe headache, or back pain. He is worried his parents will find out and initially asks to leave.
cases.exam: Agitated, diaphoretic young man pacing the room, oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Pupils 6 mm and reactive bilaterally. Skin flushed and warm. Heart: tachycardic, regular, no murmurs. Lungs clear bilaterally. Abdomen soft, nontender. Neurologic: fine tremor of outstretched hands, no focal deficits, normal gait. No chest wall tenderness. No track marks. Nasal septum intact with mild erythema.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial pharmacologic management?
options: Physical restraints and intramuscular haloperidol alone
Immediate endotracheal intubation for agitation
Start a labetalol infusion as the first step today
IV benzodiazepines plus aspirin
IV metoprolol to control heart rate and blood pressure
stages.explanation: Benzodiazepines are first-line in cocaine-associated chest pain: they blunt central sympathetic outflow and simultaneously treat agitation, tachycardia, hypertension, and myocardial oxygen demand. Aspirin and nitroglycerin address possible coronary thrombosis and cocaine-induced vasoconstriction. Beta-blockers, including metoprolol, are avoided in acute cocaine intoxication because blocking beta-mediated vasodilation can leave alpha-mediated coronary vasoconstriction unopposed, worsening spasm and hypertension. Labetalol, despite mixed alpha and beta activity, is predominantly a beta-blocker and is not first-line therapy. Restraints with an antipsychotic alone treat behavior rather than the underlying sympathetic surge and possible ischemia, and antipsychotics can lower the seizure threshold and impair heat dissipation in a hyperadrenergic, mildly hyperthermic patient. There is no airway or oxygenation indication for intubation.

stages.label: LABS & ECG
stages.content:
12-lead ECG: sinus tachycardia at 122 bpm, 1 mm ST depression in V4-V6, no ST elevation
hs-Troponin I (0 h): 0.09 ng/mL (URL <0.04)
Urine toxicology: positive for cocaine metabolites
BMP: Na 140 mEq/L, K 3.8 mEq/L, Cr 1.0 mg/dL, glucose 102 mg/dL
CK: 380 U/L
Chest X-ray: normal
After lorazepam 2 mg IV ×2, aspirin 325 mg, and sublingual nitroglycerin: BP 152/88 mmHg, HR 98 bpm, chest pain improved from 8/10 to 4/10

stages.label: PHARMACOLOGY
stages.question: Which of the following medications is contraindicated at this stage of his care?
options: Diltiazem in this setting
Aspirin as the immediate next step
Nitroglycerin in this setting
Lorazepam in this setting
Propranolol
stages.explanation: Propranolol, a nonselective beta-blocker, is the classic agent to avoid in acute cocaine toxicity. Cocaine stimulates both alpha and beta receptors; blocking beta-2-mediated vasodilation leaves alpha-mediated coronary and systemic vasoconstriction unopposed, which can paradoxically raise blood pressure and worsen coronary spasm. Aspirin is indicated because cocaine activates platelets and can precipitate plaque rupture even in young patients. Nitroglycerin directly reverses cocaine-induced coronary vasoconstriction. Diltiazem, a calcium channel blocker, is an acceptable second-line agent for persistent hypertension or ischemia after benzodiazepines. Lorazepam is the cornerstone of therapy, not a hazard. If refractory hypertension persists despite benzodiazepines and nitrates, phentolamine — a pure alpha-blocker — or nitroprusside is preferred over any beta-blocker in the acute phase.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Two hours later his pain persists at 4/10 and the repeat troponin returns at 0.31 ng/mL. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Add oral metoprolol now that the blood pressure has improved in this setting
Give empiric fibrinolytics without further imaging as the immediate next step
Treat as ACS: anticoagulation, continued nitrates
Discharge home once the agitation resolves as the immediate next step
Observe for six more hours without additional therapy as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: His troponin is rising (0.09 to 0.31 ng/mL) with persistent ST depression and ongoing pain — this is now a cocaine-associated NSTEMI and should be managed as ACS: anticoagulation, continued nitrates and benzodiazepines, and urgent coronary angiography to define the anatomy, since young cocaine users can have in-situ thrombosis, severe spasm, or surprisingly premature atherosclerosis. Discharge with a climbing troponin would be indefensible. Beta-blockade remains inappropriate in the acute intoxication phase even with improved blood pressure. Empiric fibrinolysis is not indicated without ST elevation, and cocaine-associated hypertension raises intracranial hemorrhage risk. Passive observation while myocardium infarcts wastes the window for intervention. Angiography is preferred precisely because it distinguishes thrombus, which needs PCI, from spasm, which needs vasodilators.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Angiography shows a small distal thrombus managed medically, and he stabilizes. Which discharge plan is most appropriate?
options: No follow-up is needed given his age with appropriate follow-up
Counsel cocaine cessation, refer to substance-use treatment,
Recommend a daily nitroglycerin patch and no other changes based on current risk
Prescribe metoprolol at discharge for routine post-MI cardioprotection
Discharge with a benzodiazepine prescription for recurrent chest pain
stages.explanation: The single strongest intervention for his long-term cardiovascular risk is cocaine cessation, with referral to substance-use treatment and motivational counseling — recurrent use is the dominant driver of recurrent infarction in these patients — plus structured cardiology follow-up after a troponin-positive event at age 27. Routine discharge beta-blockade in patients expected to continue cocaine use is controversial; many experts avoid it unless there is another compelling indication and sustained abstinence, so metoprolol for generic cardioprotection is not the best answer. Youth does not waive follow-up after myocardial infarction. Benzodiazepines are an acute ED therapy; prescribing them as a take-home remedy for chest pain in a patient with a substance-use disorder invites misuse. A nitroglycerin patch alone ignores the actual problem.

cases.diagnosis: Cocaine-associated chest pain with myocardial infarction (cocaine-induced acute coronary syndrome)
pearls: Benzodiazepines are first-line for cocaine-associated chest pain — they treat the sympathetic surge driving ischemia, agitation, and hypertension.
Avoid beta-blockers in acute cocaine intoxication: unopposed alpha stimulation can worsen coronary vasospasm and hypertension.
Cocaine causes MI via vasospasm, in-situ thrombosis, increased oxygen demand, and accelerated atherosclerosis — angiography sorts them out.
Phentolamine or nitroglycerin treats refractory cocaine-induced hypertension; cessation counseling is the key long-term intervention.

cases.title: Chest pain that strikes before dawn
cases.chiefComplaint: I keep waking up at 4 a.m. with crushing chest pain that goes away on its own.
cases.history: A 41-year-old woman presents to clinic with two months of recurrent chest pain that wakes her from sleep, almost always between 3 and 5 a.m. Episodes are substernal, pressure-like, last 10-15 minutes, and resolve spontaneously. She has no symptoms during the day and completes a 45-minute spin class three times weekly without any chest discomfort. Last week an episode frightened her enough to visit an emergency department, where she was told an ECG captured during the pain showed ST elevation that normalized within minutes; two troponins were negative and she was discharged with a referral. Past medical history: migraine with aura and Raynaud phenomenon. Medications: sumatriptan as needed, roughly twice monthly. No drug allergies. She has smoked half a pack per day for 20 years. She drinks wine occasionally and firmly denies cocaine or other stimulant use. Family history: mother with migraines; no premature coronary disease or sudden death.
cases.exam: Well-appearing woman in no distress. JVP normal. Lungs clear bilaterally. Heart: regular rate and rhythm, normal S1 and S2, no murmurs, rubs, or gallops. Chest wall nontender. Fingertips show mild pallor consistent with her reported Raynaud phenomenon; no digital ulcers or sclerodactyly. No peripheral edema. Pulses 2+ and symmetric throughout. BMI 23. No stigmata of hyperlipidemia.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given transient ST elevation during pain with negative troponins, what is the best next diagnostic step?
options: Exercise treadmill stress testing as the first step today
Upper endoscopy for presumed reflux as the first step today
Reassurance; this is likely anxiety given her age and negative troponins
Ambulatory ECG monitoring
D-dimer testing as the first step today
stages.explanation: Transient ST elevation during rest pain with negative troponins demands two things: documentation of the episodic ECG changes with ambulatory monitoring, and exclusion of obstructive coronary disease with angiography (invasive or CT). Vasospastic angina is diagnosed by demonstrating the spasm-ischemia correlation while proving the arteries are not fixed and critically narrowed. An exercise treadmill test is typically normal in this condition because symptoms are not exertional — she already exercises vigorously without any discomfort. Attributing documented transient ST elevation to anxiety would dismiss objective evidence of transmural ischemia. Endoscopy targets reflux, which cannot elevate ST segments. A D-dimer addresses venous thromboembolism, which fits neither the stereotyped circadian pattern nor the dynamic ECG findings.

stages.label: MONITORING & ANGIOGRAPHY
stages.content:
Ambulatory ECG monitor (14 days): two episodes of 2 mm ST elevation in II, III, aVF at 4:10 a.m. and 4:50 a.m., each resolving within 10 minutes, coinciding with diary-documented chest pain
Coronary angiography: smooth coronary arteries, no stenosis greater than 20%
hs-Troponin I: <0.010 ng/mL
Lipid panel: LDL 96 mg/dL, HDL 58 mg/dL
TSH: 1.8 µIU/mL
Urine drug screen: negative

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best explains her presentation?
options: Vasospastic (Prinzmetal/variant) angina
Takotsubo (stress) cardiomyopathy
Gastroesophageal reflux disease
Unstable angina from plaque rupture
Panic disorder with nocturnal panic attacks
stages.explanation: Recurrent rest angina clustered in the early morning hours, transient ST elevation that resolves with symptoms, angiographically near-normal coronaries, and associations with migraine, Raynaud phenomenon, and smoking form the textbook picture of vasospastic (Prinzmetal) angina — focal coronary smooth-muscle hyperreactivity causing brief transmural ischemia. Plaque-rupture unstable angina is excluded by clean coronaries and would not produce stereotyped circadian episodes over two months without infarction. Reflux causes burning pain but never ST elevation. Panic disorder can produce nocturnal chest tightness, but it generates no objective ECG changes. Takotsubo cardiomyopathy is a single stress-triggered event with troponin elevation and apical ballooning on imaging, not months of recurrent, self-terminating episodes with normal biomarkers and normal wall motion.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate treatment plan?
options: A calcium channel blocker plus as-needed sublingual nitroglycerin
Percutaneous coronary intervention with stenting as monotherapy
Daily prophylactic sumatriptan as initial therapy
Warfarin anticoagulation as initial therapy
Nightly propranolol as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Calcium channel blockers such as diltiazem or amlodipine are first-line for vasospastic angina — they directly relax coronary smooth muscle and prevent spasm — combined with sublingual nitroglycerin for acute episodes and, critically, smoking cessation, since smoking is the strongest modifiable trigger. Propranolol is relatively contraindicated: nonselective beta-blockade leaves alpha-mediated vasoconstriction unopposed and can intensify spasm. PCI has no target; her arteries contain no obstructive lesions to stent. Daily sumatriptan would be actively harmful — triptans are vasoconstrictors that can themselves provoke coronary spasm, and her migraine regimen should be reconsidered. Warfarin treats thrombosis, which is not the operative mechanism. If episodes persist on an adequate calcium channel blocker dose, a long-acting nitrate can be added.

stages.label: PATIENT COUNSELING
stages.question: Which of the following should she be told to avoid because they can provoke her episodes?
options: Influenza vaccination in this setting
Amlodipine as the immediate next step
Sublingual nitroglycerin in this setting
Statin therapy as the immediate next step
Nonselective beta-blockers, triptans,
stages.explanation: Three provocateurs matter here. Nonselective beta-blockers such as propranolol can intensify vasospasm through unopposed alpha tone. Triptans and ergot derivatives are direct vasoconstrictors that can trigger coronary spasm — her migraine therapy needs to change, which is an easy detail to miss. And smoking is the dominant modifiable risk factor for vasospastic angina; cessation alone substantially reduces episode frequency. Amlodipine and sublingual nitroglycerin are her treatments, not triggers. Statins are not contraindicated and may improve endothelial function, although her LDL is already acceptable. Influenza vaccination has no relationship to coronary spasm. Managing vasospastic angina is as much about removing triggers — smoking, beta-blockers, triptans, cocaine — as it is about prescribing calcium channel blockers.

cases.diagnosis: Vasospastic (Prinzmetal) angina
pearls: Vasospastic angina: rest pain, often nocturnal or early morning, with transient ST elevation and non-obstructive coronaries.
First-line therapy is a calcium channel blocker plus PRN sublingual nitroglycerin; smoking cessation is essential.
Avoid nonselective beta-blockers, triptans, and ergots — all can provoke or worsen coronary vasospasm.
Associated conditions include migraine and Raynaud phenomenon, reflecting generalized vascular smooth-muscle hyperreactivity.

cases.title: Sharp chest pain three weeks after a stent
cases.chiefComplaint: I have a sharp pain in my chest that gets worse when I breathe in or lie flat — it feels nothing like my heart attack.
cases.history: A 64-year-old man returns to clinic three weeks after an anterior ST-elevation myocardial infarction treated with a drug-eluting stent to the left anterior descending artery. For the past three days he has had sharp left-sided chest pain that worsens with deep inspiration and lying flat and improves when he sits up and leans forward. He reports low-grade fevers, malaise, and aching joints. The pain feels completely different from his infarct, which was a heavy pressure. He denies cough, sputum, leg swelling, and any recurrence of exertional pressure. He has been fully adherent to his medications: aspirin 81 mg daily, ticagrelor 90 mg twice daily, metoprolol succinate 50 mg daily, atorvastatin 80 mg nightly, and lisinopril 10 mg daily. Past history otherwise includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia. No drug allergies. He quit smoking the day of his infarct after 30 pack-years. Family history is notable for a brother with rheumatoid arthritis.
cases.exam: Mildly uncomfortable man sitting upright and leaning forward. A scratchy, triphasic friction rub is heard at the left lower sternal border, loudest with the patient leaning forward in end-expiration. JVP normal at 6 cm; pulsus paradoxus measured at 6 mmHg. Lungs: faint dullness at the left base, otherwise clear. No peripheral edema. Radial access site well healed. Heart sounds otherwise normal with no new murmur.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial workup?
options: Activate the catheterization lab for presumed stent thrombosis
Start empiric antibiotics for community-acquired pneumonia
Order CT pulmonary angiography as the first test
Stop dual antiplatelet therapy immediately as the first step today
Obtain an ECG, troponin, ESR/CRP, CBC,
stages.explanation: Pleuritic, positional chest pain with fever and a friction rub three weeks after MI suggests post-MI pericarditis, but the first obligation is to exclude the dangerous mimics with objective data: an ECG to distinguish a pericarditis pattern from new ischemic changes, a troponin (a re-rise suggests reinfarction or stent thrombosis), inflammatory markers, and an echocardiogram to size any effusion and compare wall motion. Activating the cath lab without data subjects him to an invasive procedure for what is most likely an inflammatory syndrome — and his pain is nothing like his infarct. Empiric antibiotics treat a pneumonia not yet demonstrated. Stopping dual antiplatelet therapy three weeks after a drug-eluting stent risks catastrophic stent thrombosis. CT pulmonary angiography can wait; the rub, positional character, and fever make pericarditis far more likely than embolism.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
12-lead ECG: diffuse concave ST elevation with PR-segment depression in multiple leads; PR elevation in aVR; no reciprocal ST depression; anterior Q waves unchanged from discharge ECG
hs-Troponin I: 0.06 ng/mL, unchanged on repeat 3 hours later (trending down from in-hospital peak of 48 ng/mL)
WBC: 12.4 ×10³/µL
ESR: 78 mm/h; CRP: 96 mg/L
Transthoracic echo: small circumferential pericardial effusion (0.8 cm), no chamber collapse, EF 45% with anterior hypokinesis unchanged from discharge study
Chest X-ray: small left pleural effusion

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best explains these findings?
options: Post-myocardial infarction immune-mediated pericarditis
Pulmonary embolism as the primary diagnosis
Acute aortic dissection as the primary diagnosis
Stent thrombosis with reinfarction as the primary diagnosis
Bacterial pneumonia with parapneumonic effusion
stages.explanation: Fever, malaise, arthralgias, pleuritic positional pain, a friction rub, diffuse concave ST elevation with PR depression, markedly elevated inflammatory markers, and new pericardial plus pleural effusions weeks after infarction define Dressler syndrome — an immune-mediated polyserositis triggered by exposure of myocardial antigens. The flat, down-trending troponin and unchanged wall motion exclude stent thrombosis and reinfarction, which would produce a troponin re-rise and regional ST elevation with reciprocal depression. Pneumonia lacks support without cough, sputum, or consolidation — a small effusion alone is insufficient. Pulmonary embolism rarely causes fever with a friction rub and never diffuse ST elevation with PR depression. Dissection presents with tearing pain and pulse or pressure differentials, none of which are present. Distinguishing diffuse pericarditis changes from focal ischemic ST elevation is the crux of this case.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate treatment?
options: Urgent pericardiocentesis as initial therapy
Prednisone as first-line therapy as initial therapy
High-dose aspirin tapered over weeks, plus colchicine
Discontinue ticagrelor and observe as initial therapy
High-dose ibuprofen as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Post-MI pericarditis is treated with high-dose aspirin (650-1,000 mg every 6-8 hours, then tapered as symptoms and CRP improve) plus colchicine for roughly three months to reduce recurrence. Aspirin is preferred specifically because other NSAIDs such as ibuprofen and indomethacin impair infarct scar healing and can interfere with the antiplatelet binding of his low-dose aspirin — so high-dose ibuprofen is the classic wrong answer in the post-MI setting. Glucocorticoids are second-line: they increase recurrence risk and are reserved for refractory disease or contraindications to first-line therapy. Pericardiocentesis is unnecessary for a small effusion without tamponade physiology. Discontinuing ticagrelor three weeks after a drug-eluting stent invites stent thrombosis and does nothing to treat pericardial inflammation.

stages.label: MONITORING
stages.question: Which development would mandate urgent reevaluation during treatment?
options: Mild diarrhea from colchicine as the immediate next step
Normalization of CRP on treatment as the immediate next step
A persistent soft friction rub as inflammation resolves as the immediate next step
New hiccups as the immediate next step
Worsening dyspnea, hypotension, or rising jugular venous pressure suggesting tamponade
stages.explanation: The feared complication of pericarditis with an effusion is progression to cardiac tamponade, heralded by worsening dyspnea, hypotension, rising JVP, and a pulsus paradoxus above 10 mmHg. His risk is amplified because ongoing dual antiplatelet therapy can convert an inflammatory effusion into a hemorrhagic one, so he needs explicit return precautions and interval echocardiography to track the effusion. Hiccups are nonspecific and benign. A soft residual rub during recovery is expected as inflammation subsides and does not signal deterioration. CRP normalization is the therapeutic goal — it guides the aspirin taper — not a warning sign. Mild diarrhea is the most common colchicine adverse effect and is managed with dose reduction rather than an emergency visit.

cases.diagnosis: Dressler syndrome (post-myocardial infarction immune-mediated pericarditis)
pearls: Dressler syndrome is immune-mediated pericarditis weeks after MI: fever, pleuritic positional pain, friction rub, elevated ESR/CRP, and effusion.
Differentiate from reinfarction: diffuse concave ST elevation with PR depression and a flat troponin, versus regional ST elevation with a troponin re-rise.
Treat with high-dose aspirin plus colchicine; avoid other NSAIDs and early steroids after MI — they impair scar healing and increase recurrence.
Watch any pericardial effusion for tamponade, especially in patients on antiplatelet or anticoagulant therapy.

cases.title: Winded and swollen months after a heart attack
cases.chiefComplaint: I get short of breath walking one block now, and my ankles swell by evening.
cases.history: A 70-year-old man presents to cardiology clinic with progressive exertional dyspnea four months after an anterior ST-elevation myocardial infarction. He presented late during the infarct — more than 24 hours after symptom onset — and underwent PCI to the left anterior descending artery. Over the past six weeks he has become short of breath walking one block, sleeps on two pillows, and has noticed ankle swelling by evening. He denies chest pain, pressure, palpitations, and syncope since discharge. Medications: aspirin 81 mg daily, clopidogrel 75 mg daily, metoprolol succinate 100 mg daily, atorvastatin 80 mg nightly, and lisinopril 10 mg daily; he reports good adherence. No drug allergies. He quit smoking at the time of his infarct after 40 pack-years and does not drink. Family history: father developed heart failure in his eighties. In clinic, the routine ECG catches your attention: persistent ST elevation across the precordial leads.
cases.exam: Comfortable at rest. JVP 10 cm at 45 degrees. Lungs: fine bibasilar crackles. Cardiac: apical impulse diffuse and laterally displaced, with a distinct sustained systolic bulge palpable medial to the apex; S3 gallop present; soft 1/6 holosystolic murmur at the apex; no rub. Abdomen soft, no hepatomegaly. Extremities: 1+ pitting edema to the mid-shins bilaterally, warm and well perfused. No pulsus paradoxus.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: His ECG shows 2 mm ST elevation in V1-V4 with deep Q waves in the same leads. He is pain-free. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Activate emergency services for primary PCI of an acute anterior STEMI
Administer fibrinolytic therapy in clinic as the first step today
Order an exercise treadmill stress test today as the first step today
Reassure him that this is a normal post-MI finding requiring no further evaluation
Check a troponin, compare with prior ECGs,
stages.explanation: Persistent ST elevation months after an anterior infarct in a pain-free patient most often reflects a left ventricular aneurysm rather than acute injury, but that conclusion requires confirmation: compare with prior tracings (stable, unchanged ST elevation favors aneurysm), check a troponin (it should be flat), and obtain an echocardiogram to visualize the apex — especially given his new heart failure symptoms and the palpable dyskinetic bulge. Activating EMS treats an acute STEMI he does not clinically have; pain-free, months-stable ST elevation is not an indication for emergent reperfusion. Fibrinolysis would add hemorrhage risk with no target. Pure reassurance ignores his progressive heart failure and the need to characterize the apex and screen for thrombus. Stress testing a decompensating patient with an undefined apical abnormality is premature and potentially hazardous.

stages.label: LABS & ECHO
stages.content:
hs-Troponin I: <0.010 ng/mL (undetectable)
NT-proBNP: 3,100 pg/mL
ECG: ST-T morphology identical to the discharge ECG from four months ago
Transthoracic echo: large apical aneurysm — thin, dyskinetic apical wall bulging outward in systole with a wide neck; EF 30%; no thrombus identified; mild functional mitral regurgitation
Chest X-ray: mild cardiomegaly with a focal bulge along the left heart border, mild pulmonary venous congestion

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best explains the persistent ST elevation and new heart failure?
options: Dressler syndrome as the primary diagnosis
Takotsubo cardiomyopathy as the primary diagnosis
Recurrent anterior ST-elevation myocardial infarction
Left ventricular aneurysm with new heart failure
Left ventricular pseudoaneurysm requiring emergent surgery
stages.explanation: A thin-walled, wide-necked apical segment that bulges outward in systole (dyskinesis), persistent ST elevation unchanged from discharge, an undetectable troponin, and gradually progressive heart failure four months after a large anterior infarct define a true left ventricular aneurysm — mature scar that balloons with each contraction, wasting stroke volume. Recurrent STEMI is excluded by the absence of symptoms, the flat troponin, and an ECG identical to prior tracings. Pseudoaneurysm — a contained free-wall rupture — classically has a narrow neck (neck-to-aneurysm diameter ratio under 0.5) and threatens catastrophic rupture, demanding urgent surgery; his wide neck and indolent four-month course argue strongly against it. Dressler syndrome causes pericarditis with effusion and rub, not a dyskinetic bulge. Takotsubo would have recovered, not progressed, over four months.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management at this visit?
options: Refer for surgical aneurysmectomy now as initial therapy
Implant a primary-prevention ICD today as initial therapy
Administer fibrinolytic therapy as initial therapy
Increase aspirin to 325 mg daily as initial therapy
Optimize guideline-directed heart failure therapy
stages.explanation: His immediate problem is symptomatic HFrEF (EF 30%) from a large infarct with aneurysm: optimize guideline-directed medical therapy — titrate the ACE inhibitor and beta-blocker, add a mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist and an SGLT2 inhibitor — plus a loop diuretic for congestion. Most true aneurysms are managed medically; surgical aneurysmectomy is reserved for heart failure refractory to medical therapy, intractable ventricular arrhythmias, or recurrent embolism, not offered routinely. Fibrinolysis has no thrombotic target and only adds bleeding risk. Raising aspirin to 325 mg increases gastrointestinal bleeding without cardiovascular benefit. An ICD may eventually be appropriate, but ejection fraction should be reassessed after at least three months of optimized medical therapy before committing to a primary-prevention device.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which complications is he at greatest risk for from this lesion?
options: Constrictive pericarditis as the leading concern
Mural thrombus with systemic embolism
Complete heart block as the leading concern
Free wall rupture, which is common in true aneurysms
Recurrent infarction caused by the aneurysm itself
stages.explanation: True aneurysms create a stagnant, dyskinetic pocket that predisposes to mural thrombus formation and systemic embolism — warranting anticoagulation if thrombus is found on surveillance imaging — and the rim of scar tissue is an arrhythmogenic substrate for reentrant ventricular tachycardia. These, along with progressive heart failure, are the major complications to anticipate. Free wall rupture is the feared complication of pseudoaneurysm; a true aneurysm is mature fibrous scar and rarely ruptures, which is a classic distinction on examinations. An aneurysm is a consequence of infarction, not a cause of reinfarction. Constrictive pericarditis arises from pericardial disease and has no relationship to an apical aneurysm. Complete heart block reflects conduction-system ischemia, typically in inferior infarcts supplied by the right coronary artery, not an apical scar.

cases.diagnosis: Left ventricular aneurysm following anterior myocardial infarction
pearls: Persistent ST elevation more than 2-3 weeks after MI with a flat troponin suggests LV aneurysm, not ongoing infarction.
True aneurysm: wide neck, dyskinetic mature scar, low rupture risk. Pseudoaneurysm: narrow neck, contained rupture, surgical emergency.
Aneurysm complications: progressive heart failure, mural thrombus with embolism, and ventricular arrhythmias from the scar border zone.
Management is GDMT for HFrEF; surgery is reserved for refractory failure, arrhythmia, or recurrent embolism.

cases.title: Routine check on post-infarct day three
cases.chiefComplaint: Hospital day 3 after an anterior infarct treated with a stent; the team is finalizing his predischarge evaluation.
cases.history: A 57-year-old man is on hospital day 3 after an anterior ST-elevation myocardial infarction. He presented 12 hours after symptom onset and underwent primary PCI with a drug-eluting stent to the proximal left anterior descending artery; peak troponin I was 92 ng/mL. His course has been uncomplicated: no recurrent chest pain, no arrhythmias on telemetry, and he is ambulating the halls with physical therapy. He reports mild fatigue but no dyspnea at rest, orthopnea, or palpitations. Current medications: aspirin 81 mg daily, ticagrelor 90 mg twice daily, metoprolol tartrate 25 mg twice daily, atorvastatin 80 mg nightly, and lisinopril 5 mg daily. Past medical history: hypertension. He smoked one pack per day for 35 years until admission and is enrolled in the inpatient cessation program. No drug allergies. Family history: mother with a stroke at 74. Discharge planning is underway and the team is deciding what evaluation he still needs.
cases.exam: Well-appearing man sitting in a chair, in no distress. JVP 7 cm. Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally. Cardiac: regular rate and rhythm; apical impulse mildly displaced laterally; normal S1 and S2 with a soft S4; no S3, murmur, or rub. Radial access site clean and soft, no hematoma or bruit. Extremities warm with no edema and 2+ distal pulses. Neurologic exam nonfocal.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which predischarge evaluation is most appropriate after this large anterior infarct?
options: Transesophageal echocardiography as the first-line study in this setting
Transthoracic echocardiography to assess LV function
Routine cardiac CT angiography as the immediate next step
Repeat invasive coronary angiography as the immediate next step
Discharge without imaging and obtain an outpatient echocardiogram in six months
stages.explanation: After a large anterior MI — especially with late presentation, a proximal LAD culprit, and a very high peak troponin — predischarge transthoracic echocardiography is essential to quantify left ventricular function, which drives prognosis, medication choices, and future device decisions, and to screen for apical mural thrombus, a recognized complication of anterior infarcts with apical akinesis. Discharging without imaging and waiting six months misses both. Cardiac CT is a reasonable problem-solver when echocardiographic findings are indeterminate, not a routine screen. Repeat angiography is unnecessary in an asymptomatic patient after a successful PCI. Transesophageal echocardiography is actually inferior to transthoracic imaging for the LV apex — the apex lies far from the esophageal probe and is often foreshortened — a classic testing point.

stages.label: ECHO & LABS
stages.content:
Transthoracic echo: EF 30%; akinesis of the apex and distal anterior wall; 1.4 × 1.0 cm protruding, mildly mobile echodensity adherent to the apical endocardium, consistent with mural thrombus; confirmed with an ultrasound-enhancing (contrast) agent
INR: 1.0; aPTT: 28 s
CBC: Hgb 13.8 g/dL, Plt 240 ×10³/µL
BMP: Cr 1.0 mg/dL, K 4.2 mEq/L

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He is on aspirin and ticagrelor for his new drug-eluting stent. What is the most appropriate antithrombotic strategy now?
options: Add warfarin to antiplatelet therapy, switch ticagrelor to clopidogrel,
Stop both antiplatelets and start warfarin monotherapy as initial therapy
Start IV alteplase to dissolve the thrombus as initial therapy
Refer for urgent surgical thrombectomy as initial therapy
Continue dual antiplatelet therapy alone; the thrombus will resolve as initial therapy
stages.explanation: A left ventricular thrombus after recent stenting requires adding oral anticoagulation to antiplatelet therapy. The guideline-supported regimen is a vitamin K antagonist targeting an INR of 2-3; because triple antithrombotic therapy substantially raises bleeding risk, standard moves are to switch ticagrelor to clopidogrel (ticagrelor and prasugrel carry higher bleeding rates in triple therapy and lack data in this setting), add a proton pump inhibitor for gastroprotection, and keep the course as short as safely possible. Dual antiplatelet therapy alone is insufficient — antiplatelet agents do not reliably resolve fibrin-rich ventricular thrombi. Warfarin monotherapy would leave his fresh drug-eluting stent unprotected against stent thrombosis. Systemic fibrinolytics risk fragmenting and embolizing the thrombus. Surgical thrombectomy is reserved for large mobile thrombi causing recurrent embolism despite anticoagulation.

stages.label: DURATION
stages.question: How long should anticoagulation continue, and what follow-up is needed?
options: Continue anticoagulation lifelong in all cases as the immediate next step
No repeat imaging is necessary as the immediate next step
Anticoagulate for two weeks, then stop as the immediate next step
Continue anticoagulation until dual antiplatelet therapy ends at 12 months, regardless of imaging
Anticoagulate for about three months, then repeat echocardiography to confirm resolution
stages.explanation: Standard practice is to anticoagulate for approximately three months and then reimage with echocardiography, ideally using an ultrasound-enhancing agent — by then the infarct has matured into scar and most thrombi have resolved or endothelialized, allowing anticoagulation to stop and trimming the bleeding risk of the remaining antiplatelet regimen. Lifelong anticoagulation is reserved for persistent thrombus, recurrent embolism, or a separate ongoing indication such as atrial fibrillation. Two weeks is far too short for thrombus organization and resolution. Skipping repeat imaging forfeits exactly the data needed to discontinue anticoagulation safely. Tying the anticoagulant duration to the 12-month antiplatelet clock has no physiologic basis — the thrombus, not the stent, dictates how long anticoagulation continues.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: What is the greatest immediate risk posed by this finding?
options: Infective endocarditis
Free wall rupture caused by the thrombus
Cardiac tamponade as the leading concern
Embolic stroke or other systemic embolism
Pulmonary embolism as the leading concern
stages.explanation: A protruding, mobile left ventricular thrombus sits in the systemic circulation, so its dominant danger is systemic embolization — most catastrophically embolic stroke, but also renal, splenic, mesenteric, or limb ischemia. Mobility and protrusion into the cavity are the echocardiographic features that raise embolic risk the most, which is why anticoagulation is added despite the bleeding cost of triple therapy. Pulmonary embolism arises from the venous circulation and right heart, not the left ventricle. Tamponade complicates free wall rupture or pericarditis, processes unrelated to an intracavitary thrombus. A thrombus is sterile; endocarditis requires bacteremia and typically valve involvement. Free wall rupture is a mechanical complication of necrotic myocardium itself, usually three to seven days after infarction, and is not caused by the thrombus.

cases.diagnosis: Left ventricular mural thrombus after anterior myocardial infarction
pearls: Large anterior MI with apical akinesis is the highest-risk setting for LV mural thrombus; screen with predischarge TTE.
LV thrombus on DAPT: add warfarin (INR 2-3), switch ticagrelor to clopidogrel, and add a PPI — then reimage at about 3 months.
TTE outperforms TEE for the LV apex; use an ultrasound-enhancing agent when image quality is limited.
Mobile, protruding thrombi carry the highest risk of systemic embolism, especially stroke.

cases.title: Three flights used to be easy
cases.chiefComplaint: I've been getting winded on the stairs for two months, and now I wake up at night gasping for air.
cases.history: A 49-year-old man presents to clinic to review the results of an echocardiogram ordered for two months of progressive exertional dyspnea. He used to climb three flights of stairs easily; now he is winded after one. He describes orthopnea requiring two pillows, two episodes of waking at night gasping for air, and a 4-kg weight gain with ankle swelling by evening. He denies chest pain, palpitations, syncope, and any recent viral illness. Past medical history: hypertension, treated inconsistently. Medication: hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily. No drug allergies.
