He drinks two to three beers most nights and denies cocaine, methamphetamine, or other stimulant use; he has never smoked. He works as an electrician. Family history: his father died at 55 of an enlarged heart. The echocardiogram report shows a dilated left ventricle with an ejection fraction of 30%, global hypokinesis without regional variation, and mild functional mitral regurgitation.
cases.exam: Comfortable at rest, speaking in full sentences. JVP 9 cm at 45 degrees with mild hepatojugular reflux. Lungs: faint bibasilar crackles. Cardiac: apical impulse displaced laterally; regular rhythm; S3 gallop; soft 2/6 holosystolic murmur at the apex radiating toward the axilla; no rub. Abdomen soft, no pulsatile liver. Extremities: 1+ pitting edema at the ankles, warm and well perfused. No cyanosis or clubbing.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Beyond the echocardiogram, which workup is most appropriate to establish the etiology of his ventricular dysfunction?
options: Cardiac MRI before any laboratory testing as the first step today
48-hour Holter monitoring alone as the first step today
Endomyocardial biopsy as the first step today
Send a genetic cardiomyopathy panel as the first step as the first step today
ECG, natriuretic peptide, screening labs ,
stages.explanation: Every new diagnosis of HFrEF needs an etiologic workup: an ECG; a natriuretic peptide level for baseline severity; laboratory screening including CBC, chemistry, TSH, iron studies, lipids, and HIV; and — most importantly — evaluation for coronary artery disease, the single most common cause of HFrEF, using coronary CT angiography or invasive angiography. His family history and nightly alcohol use offer alternative explanations, but ischemia must be excluded even without chest pain. Endomyocardial biopsy is reserved for suspected giant cell myocarditis or infiltrative disease with rapid deterioration. Cardiac MRI is a useful second-line tool for tissue characterization but does not replace basic labs and an ischemic evaluation. Genetic testing may follow if the initial workup points to familial disease. A Holter assesses rhythm, not etiology.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
ECG: sinus rhythm at 94 bpm, left atrial enlargement, nonspecific T-wave flattening, QRS 96 ms
NT-proBNP: 3,400 pg/mL
BMP: Na 138 mEq/L, K 4.3 mEq/L, Cr 1.0 mg/dL
CBC: normal
TSH: 2.1 µIU/mL; ferritin and transferrin saturation: normal; HIV: negative
Coronary CT angiography: no obstructive coronary artery disease (no stenosis >25%)
Lipid panel: LDL 118 mg/dL

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best fits the complete picture?
options: Constrictive pericarditis as the primary diagnosis
Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy as the primary diagnosis
Ischemic cardiomyopathy as the primary diagnosis
Heart failure with preserved ejection fraction
New-onset HFrEF due to nonischemic dilated cardiomyopathy
stages.explanation: He has heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (EF 30%) from a nonischemic dilated cardiomyopathy: a dilated, globally hypokinetic left ventricle with clean coronaries, and plausible contributors in chronic undertreated hypertension, nightly alcohol, and a family history suggesting heritable dilated cardiomyopathy — his father's death at 55 from an enlarged heart also warrants discussing genetic counseling and screening echocardiograms for first-degree relatives. Ischemic cardiomyopathy is excluded by the unobstructed coronary CT and by the global, non-regional pattern of dysfunction. HFpEF requires a preserved ejection fraction of 50% or more. Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy shows a thickened, not dilated, ventricle with preserved systolic function. Constrictive pericarditis causes right-sided predominant failure with preserved EF and distinctive hemodynamics, not global hypokinesis with a dilated chamber.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the best initial pharmacologic strategy?
options: Begin low doses of all four pillars
Start digoxin as first-line therapy as initial therapy
Begin ivabradine now for heart rate control as initial therapy
Start an ACE inhibitor alone and titrate to maximum dose before adding other agents as initial therapy
Start verapamil for afterload reduction as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Modern guidelines favor early initiation of all four pillars at low doses — a renin-angiotensin inhibitor, preferably sacubitril/valsartan; an evidence-based beta-blocker (carvedilol, metoprolol succinate, or bisoprolol); a mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist; and an SGLT2 inhibitor — within weeks, rather than maximizing one drug before starting the next, because each class independently reduces mortality and the benefits emerge within about 30 days. A loop diuretic relieves his congestion but does not improve survival. The old strategy of fully titrating an ACE inhibitor first needlessly delays three other life-prolonging therapies. Digoxin reduces hospitalizations but not mortality and is not first-line. Verapamil is a negative inotrope contraindicated in HFrEF. Ivabradine is considered only after beta-blocker optimization if the sinus rate remains 70 or higher. Alcohol cessation should be strongly advised alongside the medications.

stages.label: DEVICE THERAPY
stages.question: Regarding an implantable cardioverter-defibrillator, what is the correct plan?
options: Plan to stop the beta-blocker once the EF recovers as the immediate next step
No repeat echocardiography is needed as the immediate next step
Refer for heart transplant evaluation now as the immediate next step
Implant a primary-prevention ICD today as the immediate next step
Reassess EF after at least 3 months of optimized therapy
stages.explanation: Primary-prevention ICD criteria require an EF of 35% or less despite at least three months of optimized guideline-directed medical therapy, because many newly diagnosed patients recover substantial ejection fraction on the four pillars — particularly with alcohol cessation and blood pressure control in a nonischemic cardiomyopathy. The correct plan is to optimize and titrate therapy, repeat the echocardiogram in about three months, and implant an ICD only if the EF remains 35% or less in a patient with reasonable expected survival. Implanting today commits him to a device he may never need. GDMT, including the beta-blocker, continues indefinitely even if the EF normalizes — withdrawal frequently precipitates relapse. Transplant evaluation is for refractory advanced disease, not a treatment-naive patient. Skipping reassessment would make rational device and titration decisions impossible.

cases.diagnosis: New-onset heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (nonischemic dilated cardiomyopathy)
pearls: The four pillars of HFrEF therapy — ARNI/ACEi, evidence-based beta-blocker, MRA, and SGLT2 inhibitor — each independently cut mortality; start all four early at low doses.
Diuretics treat congestion and symptoms but do not improve survival.
Defer primary-prevention ICD decisions until EF is reassessed after at least 3 months of optimized GDMT.
Every new HFrEF diagnosis needs an etiologic workup: exclude coronary disease and screen with TSH, iron studies, and HIV testing.

cases.title: Short of breath on the stairs
cases.chiefComplaint: I can't make it up my stairs anymore without stopping to catch my breath.
cases.history: A 78-year-old woman is referred for six months of progressively worsening shortness of breath with exertion. She can no longer finish her usual walk around the block and must stop twice climbing the single flight of stairs to her bedroom. She sleeps on two pillows and describes occasional nighttime breathlessness relieved by sitting up. Her ankles swell by evening. She denies chest pain, palpitations, syncope, fever, wheezing, or productive cough. Past medical history includes hypertension for more than 20 years, type 2 diabetes mellitus, obesity (BMI 36 kg/m²), and obstructive sleep apnea for which she abandoned CPAP two years ago. Medications: amlodipine 10 mg daily, lisinopril 20 mg daily, and metformin 1,000 mg twice daily. No known drug allergies. She has never smoked and does not drink alcohol. Family history is notable for hypertension in both parents with no premature coronary disease or sudden death.
cases.exam: Obese woman in no distress at rest. JVP 9 cm at 45 degrees. Carotid upstrokes normal without bruits. Lungs with faint bibasilar crackles; no wheezes. Cardiac exam: regular rate and rhythm, sustained nondisplaced PMI, an S4 at the apex, no S3, no murmurs or rubs. Abdomen obese and nontender without hepatomegaly. Extremities show 1+ pitting edema to the mid-shins bilaterally. Distal pulses 2+ and symmetric. No clubbing or cyanosis.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which initial diagnostic strategy is most appropriate for this patient's exertional dyspnea?
options: NT-proBNP, 12-lead ECG, and transthoracic echocardiography
Spirometry alone, since obesity suggests a pulmonary cause
Exercise treadmill stress testing for occult ischemia
Left and right heart catheterization as the first step today
CT pulmonary angiography to exclude chronic pulmonary embolism
stages.explanation: Subacute exertional dyspnea with orthopnea, nocturnal dyspnea, and edema in an elderly hypertensive, obese woman should first be evaluated for heart failure with a natriuretic peptide level, ECG, and transthoracic echocardiogram — noninvasive tests that establish whether ventricular function is reduced or preserved and screen for valve disease. CT pulmonary angiography is not indicated without pleuritic pain, hemoptysis, acute onset, or venous thromboembolism risk factors. Invasive catheterization may eventually be needed if noninvasive testing is equivocal, but it is not the first step. A stress test targets angina-equivalent symptoms and would not explain her congestion. Spirometry may be a reasonable adjunct, but relying on it alone would miss the heart failure signs already evident on her exam (elevated JVP, crackles, edema, S4).

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
NT-proBNP 820 pg/mL
Hemoglobin 12.8 g/dL
Creatinine 1.1 mg/dL (eGFR 52 mL/min/1.73 m²)
HbA1c 7.4%
TSH 2.1 µIU/mL
ECG: left ventricular hypertrophy with repolarization abnormality, sinus rhythm, no Q waves
Chest X-ray: mild cardiomegaly, cephalization of pulmonary vessels, no effusion
TTE: LVEF 62%, concentric LV hypertrophy, left atrial volume index 41 mL/m², E/e' 15, RVSP 42 mmHg, no significant valve disease

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best explains her presentation and test results?
options: Heart failure with preserved ejection fraction
Severe aortic stenosis in this clinical context
Cardiac amyloidosis as the primary diagnosis
Chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension
stages.explanation: She has symptoms and signs of heart failure, an LVEF ≥50%, and objective evidence of elevated filling pressures (E/e' 15, left atrial enlargement, elevated NT-proBNP) — the definition of HFpEF. Her phenotype is classic: elderly, hypertensive, obese, diabetic, with sleep apnea. Note that obesity lowers natriuretic peptide levels, so an NT-proBNP of 820 pg/mL is quite significant in a patient with a BMI of 36. Chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension produces isolated right-sided findings without LV hypertrophy or elevated left-sided filling pressures. Amyloidosis is worth keeping in mind with LVH, but the long hypertension history, ECG showing LVH voltage (amyloid typically shows low voltage), and absence of other red flags make it less likely. The echocardiogram excluded severe aortic stenosis.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which medication has the strongest evidence for reducing heart failure hospitalization in this patient?
options: Empagliflozin
Hydralazine as initial therapy
Digoxin as initial therapy
Isosorbide mononitrate
stages.explanation: SGLT2 inhibitors are the first drug class shown convincingly to improve outcomes in HFpEF: in EMPEROR-Preserved (empagliflozin) and DELIVER (dapagliflozin), they reduced the composite of cardiovascular death and heart failure hospitalization regardless of diabetes status, earning a guideline recommendation. As a bonus, she has type 2 diabetes and CKD, both additional indications. Digoxin has no mortality benefit in any form of heart failure and no established role in HFpEF in sinus rhythm. Isosorbide mononitrate worsened activity levels in HFpEF in the NEAT-HFpEF trial and should be avoided. Hydralazine has evidence only in HFrEF (combined with nitrates in self-identified Black patients) and offers no proven benefit in preserved-EF heart failure.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: In addition to starting the SGLT2 inhibitor, which intervention is most appropriate for her ongoing symptoms?
options: Refer for implantable cardioverter-defibrillator placement in this setting
Stop lisinopril because ACE inhibitors are harmful in HFpEF
Start a low-dose loop diuretic
Start metoprolol succinate for a proven mortality benefit in HFpEF
stages.explanation: Management of HFpEF rests on decongestion and aggressive treatment of contributing comorbidities. She has clinical congestion (crackles, edema, elevated JVP), so a low-dose loop diuretic is indicated for symptom relief, and untreated obstructive sleep apnea both worsens hypertension and contributes to HFpEF pathophysiology — resuming CPAP, along with weight loss and tighter blood pressure control, addresses the drivers of her disease. Beta-blockers have no demonstrated mortality benefit in HFpEF and are not routinely indicated absent another reason (ischemia, atrial fibrillation rate control). An ICD is indicated for EF ≤35% or arrhythmic indications, neither of which applies. ACE inhibitors are not harmful in HFpEF; continuing lisinopril supports the blood pressure control she clearly still needs.

cases.diagnosis: Heart failure with preserved ejection fraction (HFpEF)
pearls: HFpEF is defined by heart failure symptoms, LVEF ≥50%, and objective evidence of elevated filling pressures (elevated E/e', LA enlargement, raised natriuretic peptides).
Obesity lowers natriuretic peptide levels — a 'modestly' elevated BNP or NT-proBNP in an obese patient is more significant than it looks.
SGLT2 inhibitors are the first class with trial-proven benefit in HFpEF (EMPEROR-Preserved, DELIVER) and are recommended regardless of diabetes status.
Cornerstones of HFpEF care: loop diuretics for congestion plus aggressive management of hypertension, obesity, atrial fibrillation, and sleep apnea.

cases.title: Can't lie flat two weeks after delivery
cases.chiefComplaint: I have to sleep sitting up in the recliner — I feel like I'm suffocating when I lie down with the baby.
cases.history: A 31-year-old woman, G2P2, is admitted two weeks after an uncomplicated vaginal delivery of a healthy boy at 39 weeks. Her pregnancy was complicated by gestational hypertension that did not require medication after delivery. Since going home she has had progressive shortness of breath, now provoked by simple tasks such as carrying the baby upstairs. For the past five nights she has slept upright in a recliner because lying flat makes her feel like she is suffocating, and she has woken twice gasping for air. Her family reassured her that exhaustion and swelling are 'normal after having a baby.' She also reports a dry cough and new swelling of both legs. She is breastfeeding. She denies chest pain, fever, unilateral leg pain, or hemoptysis. Past medical history is otherwise unremarkable; her first pregnancy three years ago was uneventful. Medications: prenatal vitamin. No known drug allergies. She does not smoke, drink alcohol, or use drugs. Family history is negative for cardiomyopathy and sudden death.
cases.exam: Tired-appearing woman who becomes visibly dyspneic when supine. JVP 12 cm at 45 degrees. Lungs: crackles over the lower half of both lung fields, no wheezes. Cardiac: tachycardic and regular, PMI displaced toward the anterior axillary line, audible S3, soft 2/6 holosystolic murmur at the apex. Abdomen soft; uterus appropriately involuting; lochia normal. Extremities: 2+ symmetric pitting edema to the knees without calf tenderness or asymmetry. No focal neurologic findings.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step in evaluating her dyspnea?
options: Obtain BNP, 12-lead ECG, chest radiograph,
Start empiric therapeutic heparin for presumed pulmonary embolism
Send a D-dimer to exclude pulmonary embolism as the first step today
Reassure her that dyspnea and edema are expected in the postpartum period
Arrange outpatient overnight pulse oximetry as the first step today
stages.explanation: Her exam is not that of normal postpartum physiology: orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, elevated JVP, diffuse crackles, a displaced PMI, and an S3 are objective signs of heart failure and demand immediate cardiac evaluation with natriuretic peptides, ECG, chest radiograph, and echocardiography. Reassurance is exactly the error that delays this diagnosis — new heart failure in the peripartum window is a leading cause of maternal mortality. D-dimer is unhelpful because it is physiologically elevated for weeks postpartum, and while pulmonary embolism belongs on the differential, her bilateral congestion and cardiomegaly findings point first to a cardiac cause; empiric anticoagulation without a diagnosis is premature. Outpatient oximetry delays workup in a hypoxemic, symptomatic patient who needs evaluation now.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
BNP 1,650 pg/mL
Troponin I 0.05 ng/mL (ULN 0.04)
Hemoglobin 11.2 g/dL
Creatinine 0.8 mg/dL
TSH 1.6 µIU/mL
ECG: sinus tachycardia at 110 bpm, nonspecific T-wave flattening
Chest X-ray: enlarged cardiac silhouette, bilateral interstitial edema, small bilateral pleural effusions
TTE: LVEF 28%, LV end-diastolic diameter 6.1 cm with global hypokinesis, mild functional mitral regurgitation, no LV thrombus, no pericardial effusion

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best explains her presentation?
options: Amniotic fluid embolism in this clinical context
Peripartum cardiomyopathy
Submassive pulmonary embolism
Postpartum preeclampsia with flash pulmonary edema
stages.explanation: Peripartum cardiomyopathy is heart failure with LVEF below 45% presenting toward the end of pregnancy or in the months following delivery, without another identifiable cause — exactly her picture: new dilated, globally hypokinetic LV with EF 28% two weeks postpartum. Risk factors she carries include hypertensive disorder of pregnancy; others include multiparity, multiple gestation, advanced maternal age, and African ancestry. Amniotic fluid embolism is a catastrophic intrapartum or immediately postpartum event with sudden cardiovascular collapse, hypoxemia, and DIC — not a subacute two-week course. Pulmonary embolism does not produce a dilated, diffusely hypokinetic left ventricle or florid left-sided congestion. Postpartum preeclampsia can cause pulmonary edema, but her blood pressure is low-normal and preeclampsia does not explain a severely reduced EF with LV dilation.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She is started on furosemide and metoprolol succinate. Which additional medication is appropriate now but would have been contraindicated had she still been pregnant?
options: Furosemide as initial therapy
Hydralazine as initial therapy
Enalapril
Metoprolol succinate
stages.explanation: ACE inhibitors and ARBs are teratogenic and fetotoxic (renal dysgenesis, oligohydramnios, skull hypoplasia) and are absolutely contraindicated in pregnancy — but after delivery they become a cornerstone of guideline-directed therapy, and enalapril and captopril are considered compatible with breastfeeding because milk transfer is minimal. Furosemide is used cautiously during pregnancy for congestion, so it is not the pregnancy-contraindicated answer. Beta-blockers such as metoprolol are likewise used in pregnancy. Hydralazine is actually the classic vasodilator chosen during pregnancy precisely because ACE inhibitors are forbidden — afterward, it is the less preferred option. She should also be evaluated for anticoagulation given her severely reduced EF and the prothrombotic postpartum state, and bromocriptine remains investigational in many regions.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: Three months later her LVEF is 38% on optimal therapy. She asks whether she can have another baby. What is the most appropriate counseling?
options: Future pregnancy carries no excess risk once her symptoms resolve as the immediate next step
Recommend sterilization regardless of her degree of recovery as the immediate next step
Advise against future pregnancy if her LVEF does not recover to normal , because relapse
She may conceive in six months as long as she continues enalapril through the pregnancy in this setting
stages.explanation: Prognosis in a subsequent pregnancy is driven by recovery of LV function. Women whose EF does not normalize face a high risk of relapse, irreversible decline in LV function, and maternal death with another pregnancy, so guidelines advise against future pregnancy when the EF remains below normal. Even women with full recovery have roughly a 20% risk of recurrent LV dysfunction and need pre-conception cardiology consultation and close surveillance. Saying there is no excess risk is false and dangerous. Mandating sterilization is paternalistic and not guideline-based — effective contraception and shared decision-making are appropriate. Conceiving while continuing enalapril is doubly wrong: the counseling timing is premature given her EF of 38%, and ACE inhibitors must be stopped before conception because of teratogenicity.

cases.diagnosis: Peripartum cardiomyopathy
pearls: Peripartum cardiomyopathy = new heart failure with LVEF <45% in late pregnancy through about 5 months postpartum with no other cause — never dismiss postpartum orthopnea or PND as normal.
ACE inhibitors/ARBs are contraindicated in pregnancy but become first-line postpartum; enalapril and captopril are compatible with breastfeeding.
Risk factors: hypertensive disorders of pregnancy, multiparity, multiple gestation, advanced maternal age, and African ancestry.
Subsequent pregnancy is discouraged if LVEF fails to normalize — relapse and mortality risk are high; even full recovery carries ~20% recurrence risk.

cases.title: Swollen legs and a tight, bloated belly
cases.chiefComplaint: My legs are huge, my belly is tight, and I can't catch my breath walking to the mailbox.
cases.history: A 48-year-old construction worker is admitted with three weeks of progressive exertional dyspnea, orthopnea requiring three pillows, abdominal distension, and bilateral leg swelling to the thighs. He reports early satiety and a 6-kg weight gain. He denies chest pain, fever, cough, or syncope. He has never had similar symptoms. Past medical history includes untreated hypertension noted at a job physical and gastroesophageal reflux. He takes no regular medications and has no drug allergies. He has smoked half a pack daily for 20 years. He drinks 8–10 beers every evening plus whiskey on weekends and has done so for roughly 20 years; his last drink was the night before admission. He denies cocaine, methamphetamine, or other drug use. He has had no recent viral illness. Family history: father with stroke at 70; no premature coronary disease, cardiomyopathy, or sudden death. Review of systems is otherwise notable for poor appetite and disrupted sleep.
cases.exam: Chronically ill-appearing man with temporal wasting. JVP 14 cm with hepatojugular reflux. Lungs: bibasilar crackles. Cardiac: tachycardic regular rhythm, PMI diffuse and displaced to the anterior axillary line, S3 gallop, 2/6 holosystolic murmurs at the apex and left lower sternal border. Abdomen distended with shifting dullness and a tender liver edge 4 cm below the costal margin. Extremities: 3+ pitting edema to the thighs. Bilateral palmar erythema; no asterixis. No spider angiomata on the chest.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which initial workup is most appropriate?
options: Upper endoscopy to evaluate his reflux as the first step today
Renal ultrasound for nephrotic syndrome as the first step today
CT abdomen/pelvis to characterize the ascites
Diagnostic paracentesis as the first test, assuming cirrhotic ascites
Transthoracic echocardiogram, 12-lead ECG,
stages.explanation: His presentation — elevated JVP with hepatojugular reflux, displaced PMI, S3, crackles, edema, and congestive hepatomegaly with ascites — is biventricular heart failure until proven otherwise, so echocardiography with ECG and natriuretic peptides is the essential first step to define ventricular size and function. In a heavy drinker it is tempting to attribute ascites and a tender liver to cirrhosis, but a JVP of 14 cm argues strongly for a cardiac cause: cirrhotic ascites occurs with a flat or normal JVP. CT of the abdomen and paracentesis as first moves would chase the wrong organ; paracentesis may follow later if the etiology of ascites remains unclear (a high SAAG with high ascitic protein suggests cardiac ascites). Endoscopy and renal ultrasound do not address the congested picture in front of you.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
BNP 2,200 pg/mL
AST 88 U/L, ALT 42 U/L (AST:ALT ratio ~2:1)
GGT 310 U/L
MCV 102 fL
Sodium 131 mEq/L
Creatinine 1.2 mg/dL
INR 1.3
Ferritin 210 ng/mL, transferrin saturation 32%
TSH 2.4 µIU/mL
ECG: sinus tachycardia, left atrial abnormality, nonspecific ST-T changes
TTE: LVEF 22%, all four chambers dilated (LVEDD 6.8 cm), global hypokinesis without regional pattern, moderate functional mitral and tricuspid regurgitation, RVSP 48 mmHg
Coronary angiography: no obstructive coronary artery disease (luminal irregularities only)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best accounts for these findings?
options: Ischemic cardiomyopathy
Constrictive pericarditis
Cirrhosis with hepatopulmonary syndrome
Alcohol-induced dilated cardiomyopathy
Hemochromatosis-related cardiomyopathy
stages.explanation: Twenty years of heavy daily alcohol use, a dilated globally hypokinetic ventricle, and exclusion of obstructive coronary disease make alcohol-induced dilated cardiomyopathy the leading diagnosis; the AST:ALT ratio of 2:1, elevated GGT, and macrocytosis corroborate chronic heavy alcohol exposure. Alcoholic cardiomyopathy is a diagnosis reached after excluding ischemia and other causes — which the normal angiogram and labs (TSH, iron studies) have done. Ischemic cardiomyopathy is excluded by the clean coronaries and absence of regional wall motion abnormality. Hepatopulmonary syndrome causes hypoxemia with platypnea in cirrhosis, not an EF of 22% with cardiomegaly. Constrictive pericarditis produces right-sided congestion with preserved systolic function and a small, non-dilated ventricle. Normal iron studies argue against hemochromatosis, which typically causes a mixed or restrictive-then-dilated picture.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: After diuresis, which intervention is most important for recovery of his ventricular function?
options: Start digoxin for inotropic support as initial therapy
Immediate implantable cardioverter-defibrillator placement as initial therapy
Thiamine supplementation alone as initial therapy
Complete abstinence from alcohol combined with guideline-directed medical therapy
Urgent evaluation for heart transplantation as initial therapy
stages.explanation: The therapeutic hook in alcoholic cardiomyopathy is reversibility: with complete abstinence plus guideline-directed therapy (ACE inhibitor/ARNI, evidence-based beta-blocker, mineralocorticoid antagonist, SGLT2 inhibitor), a substantial fraction of patients recover meaningful — sometimes complete — LV function over 6 to 12 months, whereas continued drinking predicts progressive failure and death. Abstinence counseling, addiction medicine referral, and treatment of withdrawal risk are therefore central, not peripheral, to his cardiac care. Digoxin offers symptom control in selected patients but no survival benefit and no role here as the key intervention. An ICD is premature before allowing time for recovery on therapy. Transplant evaluation is reserved for refractory disease after abstinence and optimal therapy fail. Thiamine should indeed be given to a malnourished drinker, but alone it treats beriberi, not this disease.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Regarding defibrillator therapy for his LVEF of 22%, what is the most appropriate plan at discharge?
options: Implant a permanent ICD before he leaves the hospital with appropriate follow-up
Reassess LVEF after at least 3 months of abstinence
A wearable cardioverter-defibrillator is mandatory in all such patients
No follow-up echocardiography is needed once he feels better with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Primary-prevention ICD criteria require a persistently reduced EF (≤35%) despite at least three months of optimal guideline-directed medical therapy in a potentially reversible nonischemic cardiomyopathy — and alcoholic cardiomyopathy is among the most reversible. Implanting a permanent device now would commit him to lifelong hardware he may not need if his EF recovers with abstinence. A wearable defibrillator can be considered case-by-case in higher-risk patients during the recovery window, but it is not mandatory and trial evidence (VEST, in a post-MI population) is unconvincing for routine use. Skipping follow-up echocardiography is wrong because the repeat study at about three months is precisely what determines both prognosis and device candidacy. The correct plan is medical optimization, abstinence support, and a scheduled echo to re-stratify.

cases.diagnosis: Alcohol-induced (alcoholic) dilated cardiomyopathy
pearls: Alcoholic cardiomyopathy is a diagnosis of exclusion: dilated, globally hypokinetic LV in a heavy drinker after ruling out ischemia and other causes.
An elevated JVP separates cardiac ascites from cirrhotic ascites — cirrhosis decompresses the neck veins, heart failure distends them.
Complete abstinence plus GDMT can substantially or fully reverse LV dysfunction; continued drinking predicts progression and death.
Defer primary-prevention ICD decisions until LVEF is reassessed after ≥3 months of abstinence and optimal medical therapy.

cases.title: Chest pain on the day of the funeral
cases.chiefComplaint: Crushing chest pressure that started during my husband's funeral this morning.
cases.history: A 72-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department with two hours of substernal chest pressure radiating to her left arm, accompanied by shortness of breath and nausea. The pain began abruptly during her husband's funeral; he died unexpectedly five days ago after 49 years of marriage. She describes the pressure as 8/10 and unrelieved by rest. She has never had similar pain. Past medical history includes hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and generalized anxiety disorder. Medications: hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily, atorvastatin 20 mg nightly, and sertraline 50 mg daily. No known drug allergies. She has never smoked, drinks a glass of wine on holidays, and walks daily. She is postmenopausal. Family history: mother lived to 94; father died of pneumonia at 80; no premature coronary disease. Review of systems is negative for fever, recent illness, syncope, or palpitations before this episode.
cases.exam: Anxious, tearful woman in moderate distress clutching her chest. JVP 8 cm. Carotids brisk without bruits. Lungs: faint crackles at both bases. Cardiac: tachycardic and regular, normal S1 and S2, no S3, soft 2/6 systolic murmur at the left sternal border, no rub. Abdomen soft and nontender. Extremities warm, no edema, pulses symmetric. Neurologic exam nonfocal. No chest wall tenderness; pain is not reproducible with palpation.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Her 12-lead ECG shows 2-mm ST-segment elevation in V2–V5 with a prolonged QTc of 510 ms. What is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: Treat with lorazepam — this is a panic attack triggered by grief
Give aspirin and heparin and activate the lab for emergent coronary angiography
Administer fibrinolytic therapy and admit to the cardiac unit
Obtain a stat transthoracic echocardiogram before deciding on intervention
stages.explanation: Anterior ST elevation with ongoing ischemic chest pain is a STEMI activation, full stop — she must go emergently to the catheterization lab with aspirin and anticoagulation on the way, because acute LAD occlusion cannot be distinguished from its mimics at the bedside, and time is myocardium. The classic trap in this vignette is anchoring on the emotional trigger: stress cardiomyopathy is a diagnosis made only after coronary angiography excludes obstructive disease, never assumed beforehand. An echocardiogram first wastes critical minutes and cannot exclude LAD occlusion, since both conditions cause anterior wall motion abnormality. Fibrinolysis without angiography is reasonable only when PCI is unavailable within guideline windows — and would be actively harmful if this proves to be takotsubo. Treating presumed panic attack with benzodiazepines in the face of ST elevation is the most dangerous choice offered.

stages.label: CATH LAB & LABS
stages.content:
Coronary angiography: no obstructive disease — 20% mid-LAD plaque, otherwise smooth coronaries
Left ventriculography: akinesis of the mid and apical segments with hyperdynamic basal contraction ('apical ballooning'), LVEF 35%
Troponin I: 1.8 ng/mL at peak (modest relative to the ECG territory)
BNP 850 pg/mL
Potassium 4.0 mEq/L, magnesium 2.0 mg/dL
Repeat ECG: evolving deep symmetric T-wave inversions across the precordium, QTc 520 ms

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best explains the angiographic and ventriculographic findings?
options: Aborted anterior STEMI with spontaneous lysis of an LAD thrombus
Takotsubo (stress) cardiomyopathy
Acute lymphocytic myocarditis in this clinical context
Vasospastic (Prinzmetal) angina
stages.explanation: Takotsubo cardiomyopathy is defined by transient regional wall motion abnormality — classically apical ballooning with basal hyperkinesis — extending beyond a single coronary territory, in the absence of obstructive coronary disease or plaque rupture, typically after intense emotional or physical stress in a postmenopausal woman. The disproportion between her dramatic ECG changes and only modestly elevated troponin, plus the marked QT prolongation and evolving deep T-wave inversions, completes the classic picture; catecholamine surge and microvascular stunning are the proposed mechanisms. An aborted STEMI would generally leave a culprit lesion, plaque rupture, or thrombus, and the wall motion abnormality would respect the LAD territory rather than circumferentially involving the apex. Vasospasm causes transient ST elevation that resolves with the spasm, without persistent ballooning. Myocarditis is possible but usually follows a viral prodrome and lacks this stereotyped apical pattern.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management plan?
options: Dual antiplatelet therapy for 12 months with high-intensity statin as initial therapy
Urgent ICD implantation for the reduced ejection fraction as initial therapy
Lifelong warfarin for all patients with this condition as initial therapy
Supportive care with a beta-blocker
stages.explanation: Takotsubo is usually self-limited: LV function typically normalizes over days to weeks, so treatment is supportive — heart-failure therapy (beta-blocker, ACE inhibitor) while the EF is reduced, telemetry and QT surveillance because torsades is a real early risk with a QTc above 500 ms, and a follow-up echocardiogram to document recovery, which also confirms the diagnosis. Twelve months of dual antiplatelet therapy treats a stented or thrombotic coronary syndrome she does not have; aspirin decisions follow her (minimal) atherosclerosis, not takotsubo. An ICD is not indicated for an acutely reduced EF expected to recover within weeks. Anticoagulation is reasonable in the subset with apical thrombus or severe apical akinesis — for a short course, not lifelong warfarin for all comers. Recurrence occurs in roughly 2–4% per year, so trigger counseling matters.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: On hospital day 1 she becomes hypotensive to 82/50 mmHg with a new harsh systolic murmur. Echo shows systolic anterior motion of the mitral valve with a 70 mmHg intraventricular gradient. What is the best management?
options: Massive pulmonary embolism — administer systemic thrombolysis as the leading concern
Ventricular free-wall rupture — emergent surgical repair as the leading concern
Cardiogenic shock from pump failure — start dobutamine infusion as the leading concern
Dynamic LVOT obstruction
stages.explanation: About a fifth of takotsubo patients develop dynamic left ventricular outflow tract obstruction: the hyperdynamic base squeezes around a relatively empty ventricle, generating systolic anterior motion of the mitral valve, a new murmur, mitral regurgitation, and hypotension. Treatment is counterintuitive for 'shock' — fill the ventricle with careful fluids, slow and weaken basal contraction with a short-acting beta-blocker (e.g., esmolol), and support pressure if needed with a pure vasoconstrictor like phenylephrine. Inotropes and vasodilators worsen the gradient, which is why dobutamine, the reflex choice for cardiogenic shock, is exactly the wrong drug; the echo already demonstrated the mechanism. Free-wall rupture complicates transmural infarction, which angiography excluded, and presents with tamponade rather than an outflow murmur. Pulmonary embolism does not produce an intraventricular gradient with SAM.

cases.diagnosis: Takotsubo (stress) cardiomyopathy
pearls: Takotsubo mimics anterior STEMI — manage as STEMI until emergent angiography shows non-obstructive coronaries with apical ballooning beyond a single vessel territory.
Suspect it in postmenopausal women after acute emotional or physical stress, with troponin elevation disproportionately small for the ECG changes and marked QT prolongation.
Treatment is supportive (beta-blocker, ACE inhibitor, QT monitoring); LV function typically normalizes within days to weeks — document recovery with repeat echo.
If hypotension develops, think dynamic LVOT obstruction from basal hyperkinesis: treat with fluids, beta-blockade, and phenylephrine — avoid dobutamine.

cases.title: Blackout on the basketball court
cases.chiefComplaint: He collapsed and blacked out in the middle of his basketball game yesterday.
cases.history: A 17-year-old varsity basketball player is brought in by his mother after collapsing during a game. Midway through the second quarter, while sprinting on a fast break, he suddenly fell without warning, was unresponsive for roughly 20 seconds by his coach's estimate, and awoke immediately oriented, without confusion, tongue biting, or incontinence. He admits to two prior episodes of lightheadedness during sprints this season and occasional exertional chest tightness he attributed to being 'out of shape,' which he never reported. He denies palpitations preceding the collapse, recent illness, dehydration, or supplement, energy drink, or drug use. Past medical history: none; he passed a routine pre-participation questionnaire last year. Medications: none. No allergies. He does not smoke, drink, or vape. Family history is notable for a paternal uncle who died suddenly at age 38 while swimming, attributed at the time to a 'heart attack'; there was no autopsy. His father has 'thick heart walls' mentioned once at a physical but never followed up.
cases.exam: Well-developed, athletic male in no distress. JVP normal. Carotid upstroke brisk, bifid quality. Lungs clear bilaterally. Cardiac: regular rate and rhythm, nondisplaced but forceful PMI, S4 present, harsh 3/6 crescendo-decrescendo systolic murmur at the left lower sternal border and apex that intensifies with Valsalva and on standing, and softens with squatting and sustained handgrip. No ejection click. No radiation to the carotids. Abdomen benign. No edema; pulses full and symmetric. Neurologic examination normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step in evaluation?
options: Reassurance — exertional syncope in teens is usually vasovagal
12-lead ECG and transthoracic echocardiography
EEG and neurology referral for first seizure
Exercise treadmill stress test today to reproduce the symptoms
Tilt-table testing for neurally mediated syncope
stages.explanation: Exertional syncope without prodrome in a young athlete is a sudden-death red flag and mandates immediate cardiac evaluation — ECG and transthoracic echocardiography are the essential first tests, and the dynamic murmur that grows with Valsalva (decreased preload) already points to a specific structural diagnosis. An exercise stress test in an athlete with possible obstructive physiology and recent syncope is dangerous before imaging defines the anatomy; it may have a role later, performed cautiously, to assess provocable gradients. Tilt-table testing evaluates recurrent unexplained syncope after structural disease is excluded — not before. There were no seizure features (no aura, convulsions, postictal confusion, tongue biting), so EEG is misdirected. Reassurance is the catastrophic error: true vasovagal syncope typically occurs standing with a prodrome, not mid-sprint.

stages.label: ECG & ECHO
stages.content:
ECG: sinus rhythm, voltage criteria for LV hypertrophy, deep narrow Q waves in I, aVL, V5–V6, diffuse T-wave inversions in lateral leads
TTE: asymmetric septal hypertrophy with septal thickness 21 mm (posterior wall 9 mm), systolic anterior motion (SAM) of the mitral valve with posteriorly directed mitral regurgitation, resting LVOT gradient 60 mmHg rising to 95 mmHg with Valsalva, LVEF 70%, left atrial enlargement, no aortic valve abnormality
High-sensitivity troponin: normal
Hemoglobin 15.1 g/dL, TSH 1.9 µIU/mL

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best explains these findings?
options: Hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy
Discrete subaortic membrane
Valvular aortic stenosis
Physiologic remodeling of the athlete's heart
stages.explanation: Asymmetric septal hypertrophy of 21 mm with systolic anterior motion of the mitral valve and a provocable LVOT gradient is diagnostic of hypertrophic cardiomyopathy with obstruction — a genetic sarcomere disease (most commonly beta-myosin heavy chain or myosin-binding protein C mutations) and the leading cause of sudden cardiac death in young athletes in the United States. The family history of sudden death at 38 and a father with 'thick walls' strongly supports it. Athlete's heart causes symmetric, mild hypertrophy that almost never exceeds 13–15 mm, with normal or supranormal diastolic function, no SAM, and no pathologic Q waves or deep T-wave inversions. A subaortic membrane causes fixed obstruction with an early diastolic murmur of AR in many cases and no SAM-dependent dynamic gradient. Valvular aortic stenosis produces an ejection click and carotid radiation with a murmur that softens with Valsalva — the opposite of his.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which management plan is most appropriate now?
options: Start lisinopril to reduce afterload and regress hypertrophy as initial therapy
Start furosemide to lower the outflow gradient as initial therapy
Start verapamil with as-needed sublingual nitroglycerin for chest tightness
Start a nonvasodilating beta-blocker
stages.explanation: First-line pharmacotherapy for obstructive HCM is a nonvasodilating beta-blocker, which slows heart rate, lengthens diastolic filling, and blunts the dynamic gradient. Critically, his unexplained exertional syncope is a major risk factor for sudden cardiac death, making ICD evaluation a guideline-supported priority (family history of sudden death, septal thickness ≥30 mm, nonsustained VT, and abnormal exercise BP response are the other classic markers). Vasodilators such as ACE inhibitors and especially nitrates reduce afterload and preload, worsening obstruction — lisinopril is wrong and the verapamil option is poisoned by the nitroglycerin attached to it (verapamil alone can be an alternative, though it carries risk with severe gradients and hypotension). Diuretics drop preload and intensify SAM, so furosemide aggravates rather than lowers the gradient. He must also avoid dehydration.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What should he and his family be told about sports and screening?
options: He should be restricted from intense competitive sports pending specialist evaluation,
Relatives need evaluation only if they develop symptoms with appropriate follow-up
No family screening is needed because his uncle's death was a heart attack with appropriate follow-up
He may continue competitive basketball as long as he hydrates well and takes his beta-blocker with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: With obstructive HCM and recent exertional syncope, restriction from intense competitive sports pending comprehensive evaluation at an HCM center is the standard recommendation; contemporary guidelines allow shared decision-making about return to play in selected lower-risk patients, but that conversation happens with an expert after full risk stratification and device decisions — not in lieu of them. HCM is autosomal dominant, so every first-degree relative (parents and siblings) needs screening with ECG and echocardiography, repeated periodically in adolescents because hypertrophy can emerge during growth; cascade genetic testing is offered if a pathogenic variant is identified in the proband. Waiting for relatives to become symptomatic forfeits the chance to prevent a first event, which may be sudden death. The uncle's 'heart attack' label is exactly how unrecognized HCM deaths are misattributed in families without autopsy.

cases.diagnosis: Hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy
pearls: A systolic murmur that gets louder with Valsalva or standing (less preload) and softer with squatting or handgrip is HOCM until proven otherwise — the opposite behavior of aortic stenosis.
Exertional syncope without prodrome in a young athlete is a sudden-death red flag: ECG and echo first, never reassurance or treadmill testing.
Avoid preload/afterload reducers (nitrates, diuretics, ACE inhibitors, dihydropyridines) in obstructive HCM; first-line therapy is a nonvasodilating beta-blocker.
HCM is autosomal dominant — screen all first-degree relatives with ECG and echocardiography and consider cascade genetic testing.

cases.title: Fatigue halfway through chemotherapy
cases.chiefComplaint: I'm more worn out than chemo should make me — I get winded just unloading the dishwasher.
cases.history: A 44-year-old woman with node-positive, hormone receptor-positive, HER2-negative left breast cancer is referred by her oncologist midway through adjuvant chemotherapy. She has completed three of four planned cycles of dose-dense doxorubicin and cyclophosphamide (cumulative doxorubicin 180 mg/m² of a planned 240 mg/m²), with paclitaxel and left chest wall radiation to follow. Over the past three weeks she has noticed fatigue beyond what she experienced with earlier cycles, breathlessness climbing one flight of stairs, and a few nights of sleeping on an extra pillow. She denies chest pain, palpitations, syncope, fever, or leg swelling. Her baseline pre-chemotherapy echocardiogram showed an LVEF of 62% with normal strain. Past medical history: hypertension controlled on losartan 50 mg daily. Other medications: ondansetron as needed. No known drug allergies. She has never smoked and rarely drinks. Family history: no premature coronary disease or cardiomyopathy. She works as a school librarian and walks 30 minutes most days, though lately she cannot finish her route.
cases.exam: Well-groomed woman wearing a head scarf, in no acute distress. Right chest port site clean without erythema. JVP 7 cm. Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally. Cardiac: mildly tachycardic, regular rhythm, normal S1 and S2, a soft S3 at the apex, no murmurs or rubs. Left breast lumpectomy scar well healed. Abdomen soft and nontender without hepatomegaly. Extremities with trace pretibial edema, no calf tenderness. No oral mucositis. Pulses 2+ throughout.

stages.label: SURVEILLANCE
stages.question: A surveillance echocardiogram is being performed today. Which parameter is most sensitive for detecting early anthracycline cardiotoxicity, often before the ejection fraction falls?
options: Mitral E/A ratio as the immediate next step
Serum BNP alone as the immediate next step
Visually estimated LV ejection fraction in this setting
QTc interval on the ECG as the immediate next step
Global longitudinal strain on speckle-tracking echocardiography
stages.explanation: Global longitudinal strain detects subclinical myocardial dysfunction before the ejection fraction declines; a relative GLS reduction greater than about 15% from baseline predicts subsequent cardiotoxicity and may prompt cardioprotective therapy while injury is still reversible — which is why guidelines recommend strain and the same imaging modality/technique at every surveillance time point. Visually estimated EF is the least reproducible measure, with inter-reader variability that can exceed the very threshold used to define cardiotoxicity. Natriuretic peptides are useful adjuncts but lack the sensitivity and specificity to stand alone for surveillance. The E/A ratio is load-dependent and nonspecific. QTc monitoring matters for certain other cancer drugs (e.g., arsenic, some tyrosine kinase inhibitors) but does not detect anthracycline myocyte injury.

stages.label: ECHO & LABS
stages.content:
TTE: LVEF 44% by 3D (baseline 62%), global hypokinesis without regional pattern, GLS -13% (baseline -20%), mild mitral regurgitation, no pericardial effusion
Troponin I 0.09 ng/mL (ULN 0.04)
NT-proBNP 610 pg/mL
Hemoglobin 10.8 g/dL
Creatinine 0.8 mg/dL
TSH 2.0 µIU/mL
ECG: sinus rhythm at 94 bpm, low-normal voltage, nonspecific T-wave flattening

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best explains the echocardiographic change?
options: Radiation-induced constrictive pericarditis as the primary diagnosis
Checkpoint inhibitor myocarditis requiring endomyocardial biopsy
Stress (takotsubo) cardiomyopathy from the cancer diagnosis
Anthracycline-induced cardiotoxicity
stages.explanation: She meets the definition of cancer therapy-related cardiac dysfunction: an LVEF decline of more than 10 percentage points from baseline to a value below 50% (62% to 44%), with corroborating GLS deterioration and a positive troponin, occurring on a drug with dose-dependent, cumulative cardiotoxicity. Anthracyclines injure myocytes through topoisomerase 2-beta-mediated DNA damage and oxidative stress; risk climbs steeply with cumulative dose, and her planned chest radiation adds future risk. Takotsubo causes a regional pattern (classically apical ballooning), not the diffuse global hypokinesis seen here, and her acute stressor was months ago. She has received no immune checkpoint inhibitor, so checkpoint myocarditis — which presents with markedly elevated troponin and frequently arrhythmias — is not in play. Radiation constriction is a late complication occurring years after radiotherapy she has not yet received, and it affects the pericardium, not contractility.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management now?
options: Refer immediately for heart transplant evaluation as initial therapy
Permanently discontinue all oncologic treatment given the cardiac risk as initial therapy
Hold the next anthracycline dose, start carvedilol
Continue doxorubicin unchanged and repeat the echocardiogram after the final cycle as initial therapy
stages.explanation: With symptomatic LV dysfunction and an EF now 44%, the anthracycline should be interrupted while guideline-directed heart failure therapy — typically a beta-blocker with evidence in cardio-oncology (carvedilol) plus an ACE inhibitor or ARB — is initiated, since early treatment of anthracycline cardiotoxicity (within months of detection) offers the best chance of EF recovery. The decision is made jointly with oncology, weighing her curable cancer against cardiac risk: this is interruption and reassessment, not unilateral cancellation. Continuing full-dose doxorubicin in a symptomatic patient with a falling EF invites irreversible cardiomyopathy. Abandoning all cancer therapy overcorrects — her oncologist has non-cardiotoxic options, and untreated node-positive breast cancer is lethal. Transplant evaluation is wildly premature for a new, potentially reversible mild-moderate dysfunction that has not yet seen a single heart failure medication.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Her oncologist asks how the remaining systemic therapy should be approached. Which statement is most accurate?
options: Add high-dose vitamin E, which is proven to prevent anthracycline cardiotoxicity as the immediate next step
Cardiotoxicity from anthracyclines is reliably reversible, so the regimen requires no modification as the immediate next step
Prefer completing therapy with non-anthracycline options
Anthracyclines can be resumed at full dose once the troponin normalizes, regardless of EF as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: The guiding principle is to deliver effective cancer therapy by the least cardiotoxic route: she can proceed to her planned paclitaxel, which is not associated with cumulative myocardial injury, while heart failure therapy continues and the EF is re-imaged. If an anthracycline were deemed irreplaceable, risk-mitigation strategies — liposomal doxorubicin formulations, the iron chelator dexrazoxane, or continuous-infusion dosing — measurably lower cardiotoxicity. Resuming full-dose anthracycline keyed only to troponin ignores that her EF and strain define the injury, and rechallenge after symptomatic dysfunction is generally avoided. Calling anthracycline injury 'reliably reversible' is backwards — compared with other causes, recovery is less likely, which is exactly why early detection matters. Antioxidant vitamins, including vitamin E, have failed to show cardioprotection in trials and are not recommended.

cases.diagnosis: Anthracycline-induced cardiomyopathy (cancer therapy-related cardiac dysfunction)
pearls: Cancer therapy-related cardiac dysfunction is commonly defined as an LVEF drop of >10 points to below 50% — always compare against the pre-treatment baseline using the same modality.
Global longitudinal strain (relative decline >15%) and troponin detect anthracycline injury before the EF falls, creating a window for cardioprotection.
Anthracycline cardiotoxicity is cumulative-dose dependent; risk mitigation includes liposomal doxorubicin, dexrazoxane, and continuous infusion.
Early initiation of ACE inhibitor plus carvedilol after detection offers the best chance of EF recovery — delays make anthracycline cardiomyopathy one of the least reversible forms.

cases.title: Winded for weeks with a racing, skipping pulse
cases.chiefComplaint: I've been short of breath for six weeks and my heart has been doing its skipping thing the whole time.
cases.history: A 54-year-old man is admitted from the emergency department with six weeks of progressive exertional dyspnea, two-pillow orthopnea, and ankle swelling. He reports his heart has 'skipped and raced' on and off for at least three years; a walk-in clinic once told him he had an 'irregular heartbeat' and advised follow-up that he never pursued because the palpitations didn't bother him much. He now becomes breathless after one block. He denies chest pain, syncope, fever, or cough. Past medical history: hypertension. Medications: amlodipine 5 mg daily, taken inconsistently. No known drug allergies. He drinks two to three beers on weekends, has never smoked, and denies stimulant or cocaine use. He works as an accountant and has gained 4 kg over the past month. Family history: father with hypertension; no sudden death, cardiomyopathy, or premature coronary disease. Review of systems is negative for heat intolerance, tremor, weight loss, or recent viral illness.
cases.exam: Comfortable at rest but dyspneic walking to the bathroom. JVP 11 cm. Lungs: crackles at both bases. Cardiac: irregularly irregular rhythm with a rapid rate, variable intensity of S1, PMI displaced laterally, no murmur or rub appreciated amid the irregular rhythm. Abdomen soft, mild hepatomegaly without tenderness. Extremities: 2+ pitting edema to the mid-shins. No thyromegaly, lid lag, or tremor. Pulses irregular but symmetric; pulse deficit present at the apex.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which initial workup is most appropriate?
options: Immediate synchronized cardioversion in the emergency department
Empiric amiodarone load before any imaging in this clinical context
12-lead ECG, transthoracic echocardiogram, TSH, electrolytes,
Discharge with a 14-day ambulatory monitor in this clinical context
CT coronary angiography before anything else in this clinical context
stages.explanation: An irregularly irregular tachycardia with new heart failure needs a systematic foundation: ECG to confirm the rhythm, echocardiography to define ventricular function and exclude valve disease, TSH because thyrotoxicosis drives both atrial fibrillation and cardiomyopathy, plus electrolytes and BNP. He is hemodynamically stable, and his arrhythmia is long-standing with unknown anticoagulation status — immediate cardioversion risks thromboembolic stroke without three weeks of anticoagulation or TEE exclusion of atrial thrombus. Empiric amiodarone is similarly hazardous: it can chemically cardiovert an inadequately anticoagulated patient and clouds the picture before thyroid testing. Discharge with a monitor ignores his decompensated heart failure. Coronary evaluation will indeed be part of the cardiomyopathy workup, but it follows the basic assessment rather than preceding the ECG and echo.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
ECG: atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response at 138 bpm, no acute ST-T changes
TTE: LVEF 30%, mildly dilated LV (LVEDD 6.0 cm), global hypokinesis without regional pattern, biatrial enlargement, no significant valve disease
BNP 1,240 pg/mL
TSH 1.8 µIU/mL
Potassium 4.1 mEq/L, magnesium 2.1 mg/dL, creatinine 1.0 mg/dL
Troponin I <0.04 ng/mL
Coronary angiography (hospital day 2): no obstructive coronary artery disease

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best explains his reduced ejection fraction?
options: Ischemic cardiomyopathy from silent infarction as the primary diagnosis
Idiopathic dilated cardiomyopathy coincidentally accompanied by atrial fibrillation
Tachycardia-induced cardiomyopathy from years of rapid atrial fibrillation
Hypertensive heart disease with preserved systolic function as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Years of uncontrolled, persistently rapid atrial fibrillation followed by a dilated, globally hypokinetic ventricle — with normal coronaries, normal TSH, and only mild LV dilation — is the signature of tachycardia-induced cardiomyopathy, in which sustained rapid rates deplete myocardial energetics and cause reversible contractile failure. A clue favoring it over primary dilated cardiomyopathy is the relatively modest LV dilation despite a severely reduced EF; the truth is established retrospectively when the EF recovers after rate or rhythm control, so it is in practice a working diagnosis you must actively pursue. Calling it idiopathic concedes defeat before treating the obvious driver. Hypertensive heart disease with preserved EF is excluded by the EF of 30%. Ischemic cardiomyopathy is excluded by the clean angiogram and the absence of regional wall motion abnormality or Q waves.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: In addition to diuresis and guideline-directed heart failure therapy, what is the best strategy for his atrial fibrillation?
options: Anticoagulation plus a rhythm-control strategy
AV node ablation with pacemaker implantation as the first-line approach as initial therapy
Immediate DC cardioversion without anticoagulation since the AF is causing harm as initial therapy
Rate control alone with digoxin monotherapy as initial therapy
stages.explanation: He needs anticoagulation regardless of strategy (CHA2DS2-VASc at least 2 for hypertension and heart failure), and in atrial fibrillation with HFrEF a rhythm-control strategy is favored: the CASTLE-AF trial showed catheter ablation reduced death and heart failure hospitalization versus medical therapy in this exact population, and restoring sinus rhythm is also the definitive test-and-treatment for suspected tachycardia-induced cardiomyopathy. Cardioversion may proceed once a TEE excludes atrial appendage thrombus or after three weeks of therapeutic anticoagulation. Digoxin monotherapy gives weak rate control, especially with activity, and treats nothing reversible. Cardioverting without anticoagulation in AF of years' duration risks stroke. AV node ablation with pacing is a last-resort 'ablate and pace' option when rate and rhythm control fail — choosing it first forfeits the chance at sinus rhythm and creates pacemaker dependence.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP
stages.question: He undergoes successful cardioversion and is discharged on apixaban, metoprolol succinate, sacubitril/valsartan, and empagliflozin. What is the most appropriate follow-up plan?
options: Stop apixaban the day sinus rhythm is restored as the immediate next step
Implant a primary-prevention ICD before discharge since the LVEF is 30% in this setting
Repeat echocardiography in about 3 months
No follow-up imaging is needed if he feels well as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: The repeat echocardiogram after roughly three months of controlled rhythm and guideline-directed therapy is the linchpin: substantial or complete EF recovery confirms tachycardia-induced cardiomyopathy, dramatically improves prognosis, and usually removes any ICD indication. Implanting an ICD now violates the requirement for at least three months of optimal therapy in a potentially reversible cardiomyopathy — most of these patients recover above the 35% threshold. Skipping imaging forfeits both the diagnostic confirmation and the recurrence surveillance these patients need, since EF can decline again rapidly if AF returns with rapid rates; some clinicians use periodic monitoring for exactly that reason. Anticoagulation must continue at least four weeks after cardioversion because atrial mechanical stunning persists despite electrical sinus rhythm — and given his CHA2DS2-VASc score and high recurrence risk, long-term continuation is generally advised.

cases.diagnosis: Tachycardia-induced cardiomyopathy due to atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response
pearls: Suspect tachycardia-induced cardiomyopathy when a new low EF accompanies a chronically rapid arrhythmia — the diagnosis is confirmed retrospectively by EF recovery after rate/rhythm control.
A severely reduced EF with only modest LV dilation hints at tachycardia-induced rather than primary dilated cardiomyopathy.
In AF with HFrEF, catheter ablation reduced mortality and HF hospitalization in CASTLE-AF — favor rhythm control over rate control alone.
Defer ICD decisions and continue anticoagulation: EF frequently recovers above 35% within 3 months, and atrial stunning persists for weeks after cardioversion.

cases.title: Breathless at 24 weeks pregnant
cases.chiefComplaint: I can't breathe when I walk or lie down — everyone says it's the pregnancy, but this feels wrong.
cases.history: A 34-year-old G1P0 woman at 24 weeks gestation is referred from her obstetrician for dyspnea out of proportion to pregnancy. She immigrated from South Asia five years ago. Since about 18 weeks she has had progressive breathlessness, first on hills, now on a single flight of stairs, with orthopnea and a nocturnal dry cough. Last week she coughed up a teaspoon of pink-tinged sputum, which frightened her. She reports palpitations with exertion but no chest pain or syncope. Her pregnancy has otherwise been uncomplicated, with normal fetal anatomy scan. Childhood history is notable for recurrent severe sore throats and, at age 10, a prolonged febrile illness with painful, swollen joints that moved from knee to ankle, treated at home; she was told she had 'rheumatism.' Past medical history otherwise negative. Medications: prenatal vitamins. No known drug allergies. She has never smoked and does not drink. Family history is noncontributory. She works as a seamstress.
cases.exam: Thin woman with a gravid abdomen, mildly dyspneic climbing onto the exam table. Malar flush noted. JVP 10 cm with prominent a waves. Lungs: fine crackles at both bases. Cardiac: regular tachycardia, loud S1, accentuated P2, an opening snap after S2, and a low-pitched mid-diastolic rumble at the apex heard best in the left lateral decubitus position with the bell. No pansystolic murmur. Fundal height appropriate for dates; fetal heart tones 150 bpm. Mild ankle edema. No clubbing or cyanosis.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?
options: Empiric furosemide without further testing
Right heart catheterization as the first step today
Transthoracic echocardiography
CT pulmonary angiography to exclude pulmonary embolism
stages.explanation: Her auscultatory findings — loud S1, opening snap, and an apical diastolic rumble — point specifically at mitral valve obstruction, and transthoracic echocardiography is the definitive, radiation-free test to confirm the lesion, quantify valve area and gradient, estimate pulmonary pressures, and grade the valve's anatomy for potential intervention. Pregnancy is a prothrombotic state, but her subacute progressive course since mid-pregnancy, bilateral crackles, and a textbook obstructive murmur make pulmonary embolism a poor fit; CT angiography also carries avoidable radiation when the diagnosis is audible at the bedside. Empiric diuresis without defining the lesion treats blindly and may compromise placental perfusion if overdone. Right heart catheterization is invasive, involves fluoroscopy, and is unnecessary when echocardiography can supply the same hemodynamic information noninvasively.

stages.label: ECHOCARDIOGRAM
stages.content:
TTE: thickened mitral leaflets with commissural fusion and diastolic doming ('hockey-stick' anterior leaflet)
Mitral valve area 1.0 cm² by planimetry
Mean transmitral gradient 14 mmHg at heart rate 106 bpm
Severe left atrial enlargement (LA volume index 58 mL/m²)
Mild mitral regurgitation; no other significant valve disease
RVSP estimated 55 mmHg
LVEF 60%
Wilkins score 7 (favorable morphology); no left atrial thrombus seen on TTE
ECG: sinus tachycardia, left atrial abnormality (notched P waves)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best explains her presentation?
options: Severe rheumatic mitral stenosis unmasked by the volume load of pregnancy
Idiopathic pulmonary arterial hypertension as the primary diagnosis
Mitral valve prolapse with severe regurgitation as the primary diagnosis
Left atrial myxoma intermittently obstructing the mitral valve
Peripartum cardiomyopathy as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Commissural fusion with diastolic doming and a valve area of 1.0 cm² is severe rheumatic mitral stenosis; her childhood migratory polyarthritis after recurrent pharyngitis was almost certainly acute rheumatic fever, the dominant cause of mitral stenosis worldwide. Pregnancy unmasks the lesion predictably: blood volume rises 40–50% and heart rate increases, and a fixed orifice cannot accommodate the flow, so symptoms classically emerge or worsen in the second trimester — exactly her course. Peripartum cardiomyopathy occurs in late pregnancy or postpartum with a reduced EF; hers is 60%. Severe mitral prolapse produces a holosystolic murmur, not a diastolic rumble with opening snap. A myxoma can mimic mitral stenosis with a 'tumor plop,' but the echo shows a rheumatic valve and no mass. Pulmonary arterial hypertension does not produce left atrial enlargement or a transmitral gradient — her pulmonary pressures are elevated secondary to the stenosis.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial management?
options: Strict bed rest alone until delivery as initial therapy
Start a cardioselective beta-blocker with a cautious low-dose loop diuretic
Proceed directly to surgical mitral valve replacement this week as initial therapy
Therapeutic warfarin for all pregnant patients with mitral stenosis as initial therapy
Start lisinopril for afterload reduction as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Mitral stenosis is rate-dependent: tachycardia shortens diastole, the only time blood crosses the mitral valve, so a beta-blocker (metoprolol or labetalol, both standard in pregnancy) slows the rate, lengthens diastolic filling, drops the gradient, and often dramatically improves symptoms. Cautious loop diuretics relieve pulmonary congestion — dosed conservatively to protect uteroplacental perfusion — and salt and activity moderation help. ACE inhibitors are contraindicated in pregnancy (fetal renal toxicity). Valve surgery during pregnancy carries substantial fetal mortality from cardiopulmonary bypass and is reserved for refractory cases unsuitable for percutaneous therapy. Anticoagulation in mitral stenosis is indicated for atrial fibrillation, prior embolism, or left atrial thrombus — not routinely for all patients in sinus rhythm. Bed rest alone neither lowers the gradient nor treats congestion and adds thrombosis risk in an already hypercoagulable state.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Despite three weeks of optimized medical therapy she remains NYHA class III. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Percutaneous balloon mitral commissurotomy, ideally performed in the second trimester after TEE excludes left atrial thrombus
Open surgical commissurotomy as the preferred intervention during pregnancy as the immediate next step
Defer any intervention until after delivery regardless of symptom severity as the immediate next step
Urgent delivery at 27 weeks to unload the heart as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: For severe symptomatic mitral stenosis refractory to medical therapy in pregnancy, percutaneous balloon mitral commissurotomy is the intervention of choice when valve morphology is favorable — her Wilkins score of 7, with no more than mild regurgitation, qualifies — and it is ideally performed after the first trimester (organogenesis complete) with abdominal shielding to minimize fetal radiation; a TEE first excludes left atrial thrombus, which would contraindicate the procedure. Outcomes for mother and fetus are excellent in experienced centers. Premature delivery at 27 weeks imposes severe neonatal morbidity and, paradoxically, the hemodynamic shifts of labor and delivery are dangerous in uncorrected severe stenosis. Open commissurotomy or valve replacement requires bypass with significant fetal loss rates and is a fallback, not the preference. Deferring all intervention in a class III patient risks pulmonary edema, atrial fibrillation, and maternal death as volume load peaks toward the third trimester and delivery.

cases.diagnosis: Severe rheumatic mitral stenosis unmasked by pregnancy
pearls: Loud S1 + opening snap + apical diastolic rumble (best heard with the bell, left lateral decubitus) = mitral stenosis; the shorter the S2-to-opening-snap interval, the more severe the stenosis.
Pregnancy's 40–50% rise in blood volume and heart rate unmasks mitral stenosis, classically in the second trimester.
Medical therapy targets heart rate: beta-blockers lengthen diastole and lower the transmitral gradient; diurese cautiously to protect the placenta.
Refractory symptoms with favorable valve morphology (low Wilkins score, no LA thrombus, ≤ mild MR) → percutaneous balloon mitral commissurotomy, ideally in the second trimester.

cases.title: A pounding heartbeat he can feel in his ears
cases.chiefComplaint: I feel my heart pounding all the time, especially lying in bed at night, and lately I tire faster on my bike.
cases.history: A 45-year-old high-school teacher presents for evaluation of a heart murmur first noted years ago but never followed up. Over the past year he has become increasingly aware of a forceful heartbeat — he feels pulsation in his ears and neck when lying in bed and describes his head 'bobbing' with each beat. His weekend cycling endurance has slipped over six months; he stops earlier than usual, though he denies frank dyspnea at rest, chest pain, orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, syncope, or edema. He had a febrile illness with a heart murmur mentioned during college but no formal diagnosis and no known rheumatic fever or endocarditis. Past medical history: mild hypertension, untreated. Medications: none. No known drug allergies. He has never smoked, drinks two beers weekly, and takes no supplements. Family history: no Marfan syndrome, aneurysm, sudden death, or bicuspid valve disease known. Review of systems is otherwise unremarkable.
cases.exam: Well-appearing man. Bounding, abruptly collapsing 'water-hammer' radial and carotid pulses; visible carotid pulsations; subtle head bobbing with systole. Capillary pulsations visible at the nail beds with gentle pressure. JVP normal. Lungs clear. Cardiac: PMI displaced laterally and inferiorly, hyperdynamic; soft S1; high-pitched decrescendo early diastolic murmur (3/4) at the left third intercostal space, loudest with the patient sitting, leaning forward, in end-expiration; soft low-pitched mid-diastolic rumble at the apex; no opening snap. No edema. Femoral pulses prominent with audible to-and-fro sounds.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Invasive aortography as the first step today
Transthoracic echocardiography
Exercise treadmill testing before any imaging
Cardiac MRI as the first imaging test
Three sets of blood cultures for suspected endocarditis
stages.explanation: A decrescendo early diastolic murmur with a wide pulse pressure and the constellation of peripheral signs (water-hammer pulses, head bobbing, Quincke pulsations, to-and-fro femoral sounds) is aortic regurgitation until proven otherwise, and transthoracic echocardiography is the first-line test: it confirms and quantifies the regurgitation, measures the LV dimensions and ejection fraction that drive every management decision, and assesses the aortic root. Cardiac MRI is a valuable second-line tool when echo windows are poor or quantification is discordant — not the entry test. Stress testing can clarify ambiguous symptoms later but should not precede anatomic diagnosis. He is afebrile with a chronic, year-long course, so blood cultures for endocarditis are not indicated. Invasive aortography for grading regurgitation has been supplanted by echocardiography except during catheterization performed for other reasons.

stages.label: ECHOCARDIOGRAM
stages.content:
TTE: trileaflet aortic valve with thickened leaflets and a central regurgitant jet
Severe aortic regurgitation: vena contracta 0.7 cm, holodiastolic flow reversal in the descending thoracic aorta, regurgitant fraction 55%
LVEF 56%
LV end-systolic diameter 52 mm; LV end-diastolic diameter 71 mm
Aortic root 4.0 cm, ascending aorta 3.9 cm
Mild left atrial enlargement; no other valve disease
No vegetations
ECG: sinus rhythm, LVH by voltage with lateral repolarization changes

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best characterizes his condition?
options: Acute severe aortic regurgitation as the primary diagnosis
Patent ductus arteriosus as the primary diagnosis
Chronic severe aortic regurgitation with compensated but dilating left ventricle
Severe primary mitral regurgitation as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: He has chronic severe aortic regurgitation: quantitative criteria are met (vena contracta ≥0.6 cm, holodiastolic aortic flow reversal, regurgitant fraction ≥50%), and the markedly dilated, hyperdynamic ventricle with wide pulse pressure reflects years of compensatory eccentric remodeling that acute AR never has time to develop. Acute severe AR looks entirely different — a small, non-adapted ventricle, narrow pulse pressure, a short soft murmur, tachycardia, and pulmonary edema, typically from endocarditis or dissection. A patent ductus produces a continuous 'machinery' murmur enveloping S2 at the left infraclavicular area, and while it also widens pulse pressure, his murmur is purely diastolic at the left sternal border. Mitral regurgitation gives a holosystolic apical murmur radiating to the axilla; his apical rumble is instead an Austin Flint murmur — the regurgitant jet impinging on the anterior mitral leaflet — not mitral stenosis (note the absence of an opening snap).

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management recommendation?
options: Start nifedipine and repeat echocardiography in two years as initial therapy
Refer for aortic valve replacement
Recommend transcatheter aortic valve replacement as the standard approach for pure AR
Reassure and reassess only if symptoms appear as initial therapy
Surgery is indicated only when the LVEF falls below 40% as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Surgical referral is correct. In chronic severe AR, valve replacement is indicated for symptoms, for LVEF ≤55%, or — even in asymptomatic patients with preserved EF — when the LV end-systolic diameter exceeds 50 mm (or indexed >25 mm/m²), because a ventricle that cannot shrink below that size in systole is decompensating, and waiting risks irreversible dysfunction. His LVESD of 52 mm with an EF drifting toward the lower bound (56%) and early effort intolerance meets the threshold. Vasodilator therapy with two-year surveillance is appropriate only for patients far from surgical triggers; he is at one. Waiting for an EF below 40% guarantees operating on a damaged ventricle with worse postoperative outcomes. TAVR is not standard for pure native AR — the non-calcified annulus provides poor anchoring, so surgery remains the default. Symptom-triggered reassessment ignores that irreversible remodeling precedes symptoms.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: While he awaits surgery, which statement about his medical management is most accurate?
options: Beta-blocker monotherapy is preferred because slowing the heart improves regurgitation before discharge planning
An intra-aortic balloon pump should be placed if he decompensates before surgery as the immediate next step
All afterload-reducing drugs must be avoided in aortic regurgitation as the immediate next step
His hypertension should be treated, preferring an ACE inhibitor, ARB, or dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker
stages.explanation: Hypertension worsens the regurgitant volume by raising the diastolic gradient driving blood back into the ventricle, so treating his systolic pressure of 158 mmHg is clearly indicated, and vasodilating agents — ACE inhibitors, ARBs, or dihydropyridines — are the preferred choices because afterload reduction favors forward flow.
