Beta-blocker monotherapy is physiologically disadvantageous in severe AR: slowing the heart lengthens diastole, the very phase during which regurgitation occurs, increasing regurgitant volume per beat (a notable exception is AR with aortic root disease such as Marfan syndrome, where beta-blockade protects the aorta). An intra-aortic balloon pump is absolutely contraindicated in significant AR — diastolic balloon inflation augments the regurgitation catastrophically. The claim that all afterload reduction must be avoided inverts the truth; vasodilators are the agents of choice when antihypertensive therapy is needed.

cases.diagnosis: Chronic severe aortic regurgitation meeting LV-dimension criteria for valve replacement
pearls: Chronic severe AR: operate for symptoms, LVEF ≤55%, or LV end-systolic diameter >50 mm (>25 mm/m² indexed) — irreversible LV damage precedes symptoms.
Wide pulse pressure with bounding 'water-hammer' pulses, head bobbing, and nail-bed pulsations = peripheral signs of chronic, not acute, AR.
The Austin Flint murmur is an apical diastolic rumble from the AR jet striking the anterior mitral leaflet — no opening snap, unlike mitral stenosis.
Avoid bradycardia (longer diastole = more regurgitation) and never place an intra-aortic balloon pump in significant AR; treat hypertension with vasodilators.

cases.title: A click on an insurance physical
cases.chiefComplaint: The doctor at my insurance physical heard a clicking sound in my heart and told me to get it checked.
cases.history: A 29-year-old software developer presents after an insurance medical examination noted 'an extra heart sound and a murmur.' He feels entirely well: he runs 5-kilometer races, lifts weights three times weekly, and denies chest pain, dyspnea, palpitations, lightheadedness, or syncope at any level of exertion. He has never been told of a murmur before, though he had few medical encounters growing up. Past medical history: none; no rheumatic fever, no recurrent childhood pharyngitis that he recalls. Medications: none. No known drug allergies. He has never smoked, drinks socially, and uses no performance supplements or stimulants. Family history is notable for his father undergoing 'a valve operation' at age 54 — he believes the aortic valve — and a paternal grandfather with an 'aneurysm' of unclear type; there is no Marfan syndrome, sudden death, or congenital heart disease otherwise known. He is planning to begin training for a marathon and asks whether that is safe.
cases.exam: Fit, tall-normal man without marfanoid features (normal arm span, no lens or palate abnormalities). JVP normal. Carotids normal in contour. Lungs clear. Cardiac: regular rhythm; a crisp early systolic ejection click at the apex and base that does not vary with respiration, followed by a 2/6 early-peaking systolic ejection murmur at the right upper sternal border; a faint, brief early diastolic murmur at the left sternal border. Radial and femoral pulses simultaneous and equal, without delay. No edema, no clubbing. Blood pressure equal in both arms.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Reassure him — systolic clicks in lean young men are benign
Transthoracic echocardiography
Cardiac catheterization as the first step today
12-lead ECG alone, with follow-up only if abnormal
CT coronary angiography as the first step today
stages.explanation: A constant (non-respirophasic) systolic ejection click followed by an ejection murmur — with a faint diastolic murmur suggesting accompanying regurgitation — localizes to the aortic valve and warrants transthoracic echocardiography, which will define the valve morphology, quantify any stenosis or regurgitation, and image the aortic root and ascending aorta. The family history of a paternal valve operation in his 50s raises the stakes considerably. Reassurance without imaging is the classic miss: an aortic ejection click is a structural finding, not an innocent variant, and the added diastolic murmur is never innocent. An ECG alone cannot characterize a valve. Catheterization is invasive and unnecessary when echocardiography answers the question, and CT coronary angiography evaluates coronary arteries, which are not the issue in an asymptomatic 29-year-old with a valve click.

stages.label: ECHOCARDIOGRAM
stages.content:
TTE: bicuspid aortic valve with fusion of the right and left coronary cusps and a single fused raphe; systolic doming
Peak aortic velocity 2.4 m/s, mean gradient 11 mmHg (mild stenosis)
Mild aortic regurgitation
LVEF 62%, normal LV wall thickness and dimensions
Aortic root 3.8 cm; ascending aorta 4.4 cm (dilated)
Aortic arch and descending aorta incompletely visualized; no obvious coarctation, but suprasternal views are limited
ECG: sinus bradycardia at 58 bpm, otherwise normal

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which statement best characterizes his condition?
options: Bicuspid aortic valve with associated ascending aortic dilation
An innocent flow murmur of athletic training as the primary diagnosis
Mitral valve prolapse with myxomatous degeneration in this clinical context
Discrete subaortic membrane with secondary valve damage
stages.explanation: He has the most common congenital heart lesion — bicuspid aortic valve, present in 1–2% of the population — currently with only mild stenosis and regurgitation, but with the critical associated finding of a 4.4 cm ascending aorta. Bicuspid aortopathy reflects intrinsic aortic wall pathology (medial degeneration) and not merely post-stenotic flow effects, so the aorta requires surveillance independent of valve severity; the family history (paternal valve surgery, grandfather's aneurysm) fits the heritable clustering of BAV and aortopathy. Mitral prolapse produces a mid-systolic click that moves with maneuvers and a late systolic murmur — his click is constant and aortic. Innocent flow murmurs never come with an ejection click, a diastolic murmur, or a dilated aorta. A subaortic membrane causes fixed subvalvular obstruction, often with AR, but the echo directly visualized a bicuspid valve with doming, not a membrane.

stages.label: SURVEILLANCE
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step in imaging and follow-up?
options: Exercise stress echocardiography before any cross-sectional imaging as the immediate next step
No further imaging until age 50 given the mild valve disease as the immediate next step
Proceed directly to elective ascending aortic replacement as the immediate next step
Transesophageal echocardiography every five years as the immediate next step
Dedicated CT or MR angiography of the thoracic aorta now to confirm dimensions
stages.explanation: Echocardiography commonly truncates or underestimates the ascending aorta and visualized his arch poorly, so a baseline gated CT or MR angiogram is recommended to confirm the 4.4 cm measurement, define the full thoracic aorta, and exclude coarctation — the classic BAV-associated lesion that his equal arm pressures and femoral pulses make unlikely but do not formally exclude. With a dilated aorta (≥4.0 cm), surveillance imaging is repeated at least annually, more often if growth exceeds 0.3 cm/year. Deferring imaging until 50 ignores that aortopathy progresses silently. Surgery is far premature: repair is indicated at ≥5.5 cm, or ≥5.0 cm with risk factors (rapid growth, family history of dissection) or at experienced centers in low-risk patients — he is at 4.4. TEE is invasive and poorly suited to serial aortic measurement, and stress echo addresses valve gradients, not the aorta, which is the pressing issue.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: Which counseling point about family screening and activity is most accurate?
options: Echocardiographic screening of his first-degree relatives is recommended,
Relatives need echocardiograms only if they develop symptoms as the immediate next step
Genetic testing must confirm a causative mutation before any relative is screened as the immediate next step
His future children cannot inherit the condition because it is sporadic as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Bicuspid aortic valve clusters in families — roughly 9% of first-degree relatives are affected, and relatives may harbor aortopathy even with a normal (trileaflet) valve — so guidelines recommend echocardiographic screening of all first-degree relatives, which is especially relevant given his father's valve surgery. Activity counseling is nuanced rather than restrictive: moderate aerobic exercise is safe and encouraged with his mild valve disease, but with an ascending aorta of 4.4 cm, intense isometric straining (maximal weightlifting with Valsalva) raises wall stress and is generally discouraged; his marathon plan can be discussed favorably after the confirmatory aortic imaging. No single gene explains most BAV, so screening is imaging-based — genetic testing is not a prerequisite. Symptom-triggered screening fails because both BAV and aneurysms are silent until late. Heritability is well established; his children should be screened too.

cases.diagnosis: Bicuspid aortic valve with associated ascending aortic dilation (bicuspid aortopathy)
pearls: An aortic ejection click is constant through respiration (unlike a pulmonic click) and signals structural valve disease — never dismiss it, especially with any diastolic murmur.
Bicuspid aortopathy is an intrinsic wall disease: surveil the ascending aorta independently of valve severity; echo underestimates it, so confirm with gated CT or MRA.
Aortic surgery thresholds in BAV: ≥5.5 cm, or ≥5.0 cm with rapid growth or family history of dissection; check for coarctation in every BAV patient.
Screen all first-degree relatives with echocardiography — about 9% will have a bicuspid valve, and aortopathy can occur even with a trileaflet valve.

cases.title: Fever and breathlessness in a young man
cases.chiefComplaint: "I've had fevers and chills for over a week and now I can't catch my breath."
cases.history: A 26-year-old man presents with 10 days of subjective fevers, drenching night sweats, and fatigue, now accompanied by pleuritic chest pain and progressive dyspnea over the past 3 days. He reports a productive cough occasionally streaked with blood. He injects heroin several times daily, most recently this morning, and reuses needles. He denies sick contacts or recent travel. He has had no prior hospitalizations and takes no regular medications. He has no known drug allergies. Social history is notable for unstable housing, ongoing tobacco use (half a pack daily for 8 years), and incarceration 2 years ago. He denies HIV testing in the past. Family history is noncontributory. He has not sought medical care for the fevers, hoping they would resolve. He describes anorexia with a 4 kg unintentional weight loss. He denies headache, neck stiffness, urinary symptoms, abdominal pain, or rash that he has noticed. He has no known cardiac history and reports no prior murmurs.
cases.exam: Ill-appearing, diaphoretic young man in mild respiratory distress. Multiple healed and fresh track marks over both antecubital fossae. Tachycardic with a regular rhythm; a soft holosystolic murmur is heard at the left lower sternal border that augments with inspiration. Lungs reveal scattered bilateral crackles. There is no peripheral edema, and JVP is mildly elevated. Abdomen soft, nontender, no organomegaly. No splinter hemorrhages, Osler nodes, or Janeway lesions identified. Neurologic exam is nonfocal. No conjunctival lesions. Skin is warm without cellulitis at injection sites.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step before starting empiric antibiotics?
options: Begin oral antibiotics and arrange outpatient echocardiography
Start vancomycin immediately and defer cultures until the patient defervesces
Obtain at least three sets of blood cultures from separate venipuncture sites
Order a single peripheral blood culture and a urine culture
stages.explanation: In suspected endocarditis, three sets of blood cultures drawn from separate sites over a short interval maximize the yield for a continuous bacteremia and satisfy a major Duke criterion. Obtaining them before antibiotics is essential because a single dose can sterilize cultures and obscure the diagnosis. Deferring cultures until defervescence (option B) is wrong—an acutely septic IV drug user needs prompt cultures then empiric therapy, not delay. A single culture (option D) is inadequate and cannot establish persistent bacteremia. Oral antibiotics with outpatient follow-up (option E) is dangerously inappropriate for a febrile, hypoxic, tachycardic patient with a new murmur and likely endocarditis requiring admission and IV therapy.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 18.6 ×10³/µL (88% neutrophils)
Hgb 10.4 g/dL
Platelets 168 ×10³/µL
Creatinine 1.1 mg/dL
Lactate 2.4 mmol/L
Blood cultures (3 sets): pending
Chest X-ray: multiple bilateral peripheral nodular opacities, several with central cavitation
ECG: sinus tachycardia, no PR prolongation
HIV 4th-gen Ag/Ab: pending

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given the cavitary lung lesions and tricuspid-region murmur, what is the most likely unifying diagnosis?
options: Community-acquired pneumonia with parapneumonic effusion
Right-sided infective endocarditis with septic pulmonary emboli
Pulmonary thromboembolism with infarction in this clinical context
Reactivation pulmonary tuberculosis as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: An IV drug user with persistent fever, a tricuspid regurgitation murmur (holosystolic at the left lower sternal border, louder with inspiration), and multiple peripheral cavitary nodules has right-sided infective endocarditis throwing septic emboli to the lungs—the classic presentation. Tricuspid vegetations shower the pulmonary circulation, producing multifocal cavitating infarcts. Community-acquired pneumonia (option B) does not explain the new murmur or multifocal cavitary nodules across both lungs. Reactivation TB (option C) favors upper-lobe apical cavities and a more indolent course without a regurgitant murmur. Pulmonary embolism with infarction (option E) can cavitate rarely but does not cause sustained high fevers, leukocytosis, and a new tricuspid murmur in this context.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: While awaiting culture speciation, which empiric antibiotic regimen is most appropriate?
options: Ampicillin plus gentamicin as initial therapy
Oral linezolid as initial therapy
Ceftriaxone monotherapy as initial therapy
Vancomycin to cover methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus
stages.explanation: Staphylococcus aureus, including MRSA, is by far the most common organism in injection-drug-use right-sided endocarditis, so empiric IV vancomycin is the standard initial choice pending speciation and susceptibilities. Ceftriaxone monotherapy (option B) covers streptococci and many gram-negatives but misses MRSA, which is the priority pathogen here. Oral linezolid (option C) has anti-MRSA activity but oral therapy is inappropriate for acute bacteremic endocarditis where reliable bactericidal IV levels are required. Ampicillin plus gentamicin (option D) targets enterococci and viridans streptococci—reasonable for subacute native-valve disease in older adults but not for the staphylococcal disease expected in this IV drug user.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Blood cultures grow methicillin-sensitive S. aureus. What is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?
options: Repeat chest CT in one week to track the nodules in this setting
Transthoracic echocardiography to assess for vegetations
Transesophageal echocardiography only, skipping the transthoracic study
Cardiac MRI as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Transthoracic echocardiography (TTE) is the appropriate first imaging test to confirm vegetations and assess valvular function. In right-sided endocarditis the tricuspid valve is anterior and well visualized by TTE, which has good sensitivity for the typically large right-sided vegetations—so it is the reasonable starting study. Going straight to TEE (option A) is unnecessary first-line for suspected right-sided disease and is more invasive; TEE is reserved for nondiagnostic TTE, suspected left-sided or prosthetic involvement, or complications such as abscess. Cardiac MRI (option C) is not a standard endocarditis tool. Repeating chest CT (option D) tracks emboli but does not establish the cardiac diagnosis or guide therapy now.

cases.diagnosis: Right-sided (tricuspid valve) infective endocarditis due to Staphylococcus aureus with septic pulmonary emboli
pearls: Right-sided endocarditis in people who inject drugs most often involves the tricuspid valve and is caused by S. aureus.
Multiple bilateral peripheral cavitary lung nodules plus fever should prompt a search for tricuspid vegetations causing septic pulmonary emboli.
A tricuspid regurgitation murmur classically increases with inspiration (Carvallo sign).
Draw at least three sets of blood cultures from separate sites before antibiotics to satisfy Duke criteria and capture continuous bacteremia.

cases.title: Weeks of low-grade fever and fatigue
cases.chiefComplaint: "I've felt run down with night sweats and low fevers for about six weeks."
cases.history: A 61-year-old man is admitted after six weeks of intermittent low-grade fevers, malaise, anorexia, and a 5 kg weight loss. He describes drenching night sweats and diffuse arthralgias. Symptoms began roughly two weeks after an extensive dental extraction and crown placement, for which he did not receive antibiotic prophylaxis. He has a history of mild mitral valve prolapse with regurgitation diagnosed years ago and hypertension treated with amlodipine 5 mg daily. He had rheumatic fever as a child. He takes no anticoagulants. He has no known drug allergies. He is a retired accountant, drinks wine socially, and has never used injection drugs. Family history includes coronary disease in his father. He denies cough, dysuria, headache, or focal neurologic symptoms. He saw his primary physician twice and was told he likely had a viral illness, but symptoms persisted and he noticed tender bumps on his fingertips and small dark spots under a fingernail this week, prompting evaluation.
cases.exam: Tired-appearing man, no acute distress. A grade III/VI holosystolic murmur is heard at the apex radiating to the axilla, increased from baseline per old records. Tender, violaceous nodules are present on the pads of two fingers, and there are nontender erythematous macules on the palms. A few splinter hemorrhages are seen under the nails. Funduscopy reveals a retinal hemorrhage with a pale center. Mild splenomegaly is palpable. Lungs are clear. No peripheral edema. No track marks. Neurologic exam is nonfocal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature of this presentation most strongly points toward infective endocarditis rather than a nonspecific viral illness?
options: A history of hypertension treated with amlodipine in this clinical context
The presence of fatigue and weight loss as the first step today
Tender fingertip nodules, splinter hemorrhages,
A normal blood pressure and mild tachycardia as the first step today
stages.explanation: Osler nodes (tender fingertip nodules), Janeway lesions, splinter hemorrhages, and Roth spots (retinal hemorrhages with pale centers) are immune and embolic phenomena highly suggestive of infective endocarditis, especially with a new or changed regurgitant murmur on a previously abnormal valve. Fatigue and weight loss (option A) are nonspecific constitutional symptoms seen in many illnesses. Normal blood pressure with mild tachycardia (option C) is nonspecific. Hypertension on amlodipine (option D) is an unrelated comorbidity. The constellation of peripheral stigmata plus a changing murmur after a dental procedure in a patient with valvular disease is the key clue distinguishing subacute endocarditis from a benign viral syndrome.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 11.8 ×10³/µL
Hgb 10.1 g/dL (normocytic)
ESR 78 mm/hr
CRP 64 mg/L
Rheumatoid factor: positive
Urinalysis: 2+ blood, dysmorphic RBCs, no infection
Blood cultures (3 sets): all growing gram-positive cocci in chains, speciation pending
Transthoracic echo: 9 mm mobile vegetation on the mitral valve with moderate-to-severe regurgitation

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Cultures speciate as viridans group streptococci. Using the modified Duke criteria, how is this case best classified?
options: Definite endocarditis—based solely on the dental history
Definite endocarditis—two major criteria are satisfied
Rejected—the findings are better explained by another diagnosis
Possible endocarditis—only one minor criterion is met
stages.explanation: This patient meets two MAJOR Duke criteria: (1) typical organism (viridans streptococci) from multiple separate blood cultures with persistent bacteremia, and (2) echocardiographic evidence of endocardial involvement (an oscillating vegetation). Two major criteria establish DEFINITE endocarditis, even before counting his several minor criteria (predisposing valve lesion, fever, vascular/immune phenomena like Osler nodes and Roth spots). Possible endocarditis (option A) underestimates the evidence. Rejected (option B) is incorrect because no alternative diagnosis explains the vegetation and persistent typical bacteremia. The dental history alone (option D) is not a Duke criterion—it is only a contextual risk factor, not a major criterion.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For penicillin-susceptible viridans streptococcal native-valve endocarditis, what is the most appropriate definitive antibiotic plan?
options: 4 weeks of IV penicillin G or ceftriaxone
Single-dose ceftriaxone, as used for procedural prophylaxis as initial therapy
Vancomycin for 2 weeks then transition to oral therapy as initial therapy
A 5-day course of oral amoxicillin as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Penicillin-susceptible viridans streptococcal native-valve endocarditis is treated with about 4 weeks of IV penicillin G or ceftriaxone; a 2-week combination with gentamicin is an option only in uncomplicated, carefully selected cases. Prolonged IV bactericidal therapy is required to sterilize the avascular vegetation. A 5-day oral course (option A) is grossly inadequate. Vancomycin (option C) is reserved for true penicillin allergy or resistant organisms, and switching to oral therapy after 2 weeks is not standard here. Single-dose ceftriaxone (option D) is a prophylaxis concept, not treatment of established endocarditis, which needs weeks of therapy.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which finding during treatment would most strongly indicate a need for surgical valve intervention?
options: An ESR that remains elevated at the end of week one
Development of acute heart failure from severe mitral regurgitation
Persistent low-grade fever on day 2 of antibiotics
A single small splinter hemorrhage with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Heart failure caused by valve dysfunction (here, progressive severe mitral regurgitation) is the strongest and most common indication for surgery in endocarditis and should not be delayed. Other surgical indications include uncontrolled infection, abscess, and recurrent emboli with a large persistent vegetation. Persistent low-grade fever on day 2 (option A) is expected early and not an indication for surgery; clinical response can take several days. A single splinter hemorrhage (option B) is a minor peripheral stigma, not a surgical trigger. A still-elevated ESR at one week (option D) reflects slow normalization of inflammatory markers and does not by itself mandate operation.

cases.diagnosis: Subacute bacterial endocarditis of the mitral valve due to viridans group streptococci
pearls: Subacute endocarditis from viridans streptococci classically follows dental work in a patient with pre-existing valvular disease.
Osler nodes (tender) and Janeway lesions (painless), splinter hemorrhages, and Roth spots are the immune/embolic peripheral stigmata of endocarditis.
Two major modified Duke criteria—typical organism with persistent bacteremia plus echocardiographic vegetation—establish definite endocarditis.
Heart failure from valve destruction is the leading indication for surgery in infective endocarditis.

cases.title: Painful, migrating joint swelling in a child
cases.chiefComplaint: "My daughter's joints keep hurting and swelling, and the pain jumps from one joint to another."
cases.history: A 9-year-old girl is brought in with two weeks of migratory joint pain. It began in the right knee with swelling and warmth, improved over a few days, then moved to the left ankle and now the wrists. Each joint is exquisitely tender for a day or two before settling. She has had intermittent low-grade fevers and fatigue. About five weeks ago she had a severe sore throat with fever that was not tested or treated; it resolved on its own. Her mother reports the child has seemed more emotional and clumsy lately, occasionally dropping cups. There is no rash she has noticed beyond a faint pinkish patch on the trunk that comes and goes. Past medical history is unremarkable; immunizations are up to date. She takes no medications and has no allergies. The family recently immigrated and has limited access to care. There is no family history of autoimmune disease. She denies dysuria, diarrhea, or recent tick exposure. No prior joint problems. Appetite is mildly reduced. She attends third grade and lives with two siblings who also had sore throats.
cases.exam: Well-developed girl, mildly uncomfortable. The left wrist and right ankle are swollen, warm, and tender with painful range of motion; the previously affected knee is now normal. Cardiac exam reveals a new high-pitched blowing holosystolic murmur at the apex radiating to the axilla. A faint serpiginous pink rash with central clearing is seen over the trunk. There are no subcutaneous nodules palpable today. Mild choreiform movements of the hands are noted when arms are outstretched. Pharynx is no longer injected. No lymphadenopathy. Lungs clear; no edema.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which laboratory test best supports a preceding group A streptococcal infection given that her sore throat was weeks ago?
options: Rapid antigen detection test of the throat
Blood cultures for Streptococcus pyogenes
Antistreptolysin O and anti-DNase B antibody titers
A single throat culture for group A strep
stages.explanation: Weeks after pharyngitis, the throat is usually no longer colonized, so antistreptococcal antibody titers (ASO and anti-DNase B) are the best way to document recent group A streptococcal infection—a requirement for diagnosing acute rheumatic fever. A rapid antigen test (option A) and throat culture (option D) are often negative by the time rheumatic fever appears 2–4 weeks later, because the organism has cleared. Blood cultures (option C) are useful for invasive bacteremic disease but not for confirming antecedent pharyngeal strep in rheumatic fever, which is a post-infectious immune phenomenon, not active bacteremia.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
ASO titer: markedly elevated
Anti-DNase B: elevated
ESR 62 mm/hr
CRP 48 mg/L
WBC 12.1 ×10³/µL
Throat rapid antigen test: negative
ECG: PR interval 0.22 s (prolonged for age)
Echocardiogram: mild mitral regurgitation, no vegetation

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Using the Jones criteria with documented antecedent strep, which combination here is sufficient to diagnose acute rheumatic fever?
options: Only the prolonged PR interval as the primary diagnosis
One minor criterion (fever) plus elevated ESR in this clinical context
A positive ASO titer alone as the primary diagnosis
Two major criteria
stages.explanation: With evidence of preceding strep infection, the Jones criteria require either two major manifestations OR one major plus two minor. This child has carditis (new mitral regurgitation murmur and regurgitation on echo), migratory polyarthritis, AND Sydenham chorea—three major manifestations—readily satisfying the criteria. The erythema marginatum rash is a fourth major sign. Fever plus elevated ESR (option A) are only minor criteria and insufficient alone. The prolonged PR interval (option C) is a single minor criterion. A positive ASO titer alone (option D) establishes antecedent infection but is not a Jones manifestation; it is the supporting evidence layered onto the major/minor scheme.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate acute treatment for her arthritis and to eradicate residual streptococcus?
options: Systemic corticosteroids as first-line for the joints with no antibiotic
Colchicine and allopurinol as initial therapy
Observation alone since the arthritis is self-limited as initial therapy
High-dose anti-inflammatory therapy plus a course of penicillin
stages.explanation: Acute rheumatic fever arthritis responds dramatically to anti-inflammatory therapy (high-dose aspirin or an NSAID), and all patients receive a course of penicillin to eradicate any residual group A streptococcus regardless of throat culture results. Corticosteroids (option B) are reserved for severe carditis with heart failure, not first-line for arthritis, and omitting antibiotics is wrong. Colchicine and allopurinol (option C) treat gout, which does not cause migratory polyarthritis in a 9-year-old. Observation alone (option D) neglects the need for strep eradication and symptomatic relief and ignores the carditis that requires monitoring.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Because she has carditis, what is the recommended duration of secondary antibiotic prophylaxis?
options: A single 10-day course of oral penicillin only with appropriate follow-up
Until age 21 only, regardless of valve status with appropriate follow-up
No prophylaxis is needed once the acute episode resolves with appropriate follow-up
Long-term prophylaxis with intramuscular benzathine penicillin
stages.explanation: Patients with rheumatic carditis require prolonged secondary prophylaxis—typically intramuscular benzathine penicillin G every 3–4 weeks—to prevent recurrent strep infections that would worsen valve damage. With carditis/residual valve disease, prophylaxis extends for at least 10 years or until age 21 (whichever is longer), and even longer if significant valvular disease persists. No prophylaxis (option A) leaves her at high risk for recurrence and progressive rheumatic heart disease. A single 10-day course (option B) is acute eradication, not secondary prevention. Stopping strictly at age 21 regardless of valve status (option C) is incorrect because persistent valvular disease warrants longer or lifelong prophylaxis.

cases.diagnosis: Acute rheumatic fever with carditis, migratory polyarthritis, and Sydenham chorea following untreated group A streptococcal pharyngitis
pearls: Diagnose acute rheumatic fever with the Jones criteria: evidence of antecedent strep PLUS two major or one major and two minor manifestations.
Major Jones criteria: carditis, migratory polyarthritis, Sydenham chorea, erythema marginatum, and subcutaneous nodules (JONES mnemonic).
Antistreptococcal antibody titers (ASO, anti-DNase B) confirm recent strep when throat cultures have turned negative.
Rheumatic carditis mandates long-term secondary prophylaxis with benzathine penicillin to prevent recurrent disease and worsening valve damage.

cases.title: Tall young man with a family tragedy
cases.chiefComplaint: "My uncle died suddenly at 40 and my doctor said I should get my heart checked because of how I'm built."
cases.history: A 19-year-old college student presents for evaluation after his maternal uncle died suddenly at age 40 from what the family described as a torn aorta. The patient is strikingly tall and thin and has always been told he has long limbs and fingers. He reports occasional mild palpitations but denies chest pain, syncope, or dyspnea. He wears glasses for significant nearsightedness and had a lens problem evaluated by an ophthalmologist last year. He plays competitive basketball and wants clearance to continue. He takes no medications and has no allergies. He has never smoked and drinks alcohol rarely. Beyond the uncle, his mother is tall and was told she has a heart murmur; a grandparent reportedly had eye and joint problems. He denies joint dislocations but bruises easily and has flat feet. He has no learning difficulties. Review of systems is notable for chronic mild low-back discomfort and a history of a collapsed lung in high school that resolved spontaneously. He has no history of hernias surgically repaired.
cases.exam: Tall, thin young man with an arm span exceeding his height and a long, narrow face. The wrist (Walker) and thumb (Steinberg) signs are positive, indicating arachnodactyly. There is pectus excavatum and a high-arched palate with crowded teeth. The chest reveals a soft early diastolic decrescendo murmur at the left sternal border. Skin shows striae over the shoulders. There is mild scoliosis and pes planus. Joints are hypermobile. Lungs are clear. No cyanosis or edema. Peripheral pulses are full and symmetric. Lens subluxation was previously documented; today pupils are equal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most important initial cardiovascular test in this patient?
options: Transthoracic echocardiogram to measure the aortic root diameter
24-hour ambulatory ECG monitor for the palpitations
Exercise treadmill stress test for sports clearance
Coronary CT angiography as the first step today
stages.explanation: In suspected Marfan syndrome the central cardiovascular concern is aortic root dilation at the sinuses of Valsalva, which predisposes to dissection and rupture—the likely cause of his uncle's death. A transthoracic echocardiogram measuring the aortic root is the essential first test for diagnosis, risk stratification, and surveillance. An exercise stress test (option A) addresses ischemia, which is not the issue in a 19-year-old with connective-tissue disease, and could be hazardous before assessing the aorta. Ambulatory ECG (option C) might evaluate palpitations later but misses the life-threatening structural problem. Coronary CTA (option D) evaluates coronary anatomy, not the aortic root pathology that drives prognosis here.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Transthoracic echo: aortic root (sinuses of Valsalva) dilated to 4.6 cm (Z-score markedly elevated for body size)
Aortic valve: mild aortic regurgitation
Mitral valve: mild prolapse with trace regurgitation
LV size and function: normal
Ophthalmology report: superotemporal lens subluxation, both eyes
ECG: normal sinus rhythm

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: His features fit a systemic connective-tissue disorder. The causative gene most classically encodes which protein?
options: Type I collagen
Fibrillin-1
Dystrophin in this clinical context
Type III collagen
stages.explanation: Marfan syndrome is caused by mutations in FBN1, encoding fibrillin-1, a glycoprotein of the extracellular microfibrils; deficient fibrillin leads to excess TGF-β signaling, weakening the aortic wall and the suspensory ligaments of the lens (causing the superotemporal ectopia lentis seen here). Type I collagen defects (option A) cause osteogenesis imperfecta. Dystrophin (option C) is defective in Duchenne muscular dystrophy. Type III collagen defects (option D) cause vascular Ehlers-Danlos syndrome, which also risks arterial rupture but lacks the arachnodactyly and superotemporal lens dislocation pattern characteristic of Marfan. The fibrillin-1 link unifies his skeletal, ocular, and aortic findings.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Besides surveillance imaging, what pharmacologic therapy is recommended to slow aortic root enlargement?
options: A beta-blocker to reduce aortic wall stress
A calcium-channel blocker such as verapamil as initial therapy
Lifelong therapeutic anticoagulation as initial therapy
High-intensity statin therapy as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Beta-blockers reduce the rate of rise of aortic pressure (dP/dt) and wall stress, slowing aortic root dilation in Marfan syndrome; angiotensin-receptor blockers (e.g., losartan) reduce TGF-β signaling and are an accepted alternative or adjunct. A statin (option B) treats atherosclerotic risk and has no role in this aortopathy. Anticoagulation (option C) is not indicated absent mechanical valves or another thrombotic indication and would add bleeding risk. Calcium-channel blockers like verapamil (option D) are generally avoided in Marfan aortic disease because some data suggest they may accelerate aortic complications; they are not the recommended agents.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What is the most appropriate guidance regarding his competitive basketball and activity?
options: He may continue all competitive contact and high-intensity sports without restriction
He should avoid only swimming with appropriate follow-up
He needs complete bed rest until surgery with appropriate follow-up
He should be restricted from intense competitive
stages.explanation: Patients with Marfan syndrome and aortic root dilation should avoid intense competitive sports, contact sports, and heavy isometric/Valsalva-type resistance exertion, all of which spike aortic wall stress and raise dissection risk; low-to-moderate recreational aerobic activity is generally permitted. Allowing unrestricted competitive contact sport (option A) is dangerous given his dilated root and family history of dissection. Restricting only swimming (option B) is arbitrary and misses the high-risk activities. Complete bed rest (option D) is unnecessary and harmful—he can stay active within safe limits and does not yet meet surgical thresholds (root surgery is typically considered around 5.0 cm or with rapid growth/family history of early dissection).

cases.diagnosis: Marfan syndrome (FBN1 mutation) with aortic root dilation at the sinuses of Valsalva
pearls: Marfan syndrome results from FBN1 mutations affecting fibrillin-1, with excess TGF-β signaling weakening the aortic wall.
Superotemporal lens subluxation (ectopia lentis) and aortic root dilation are hallmark features; track the root with serial echocardiography.
Beta-blockers or angiotensin-receptor blockers (losartan) reduce aortic wall stress and slow root dilation.
Restrict intense competitive, contact, and isometric sports; consider elective aortic root repair near 5.0 cm or sooner with a family history of early dissection.

cases.title: Recurrent fainting spells in an older man
cases.chiefComplaint: "I keep blacking out without warning and woke up on the floor again today."
cases.history: An 80-year-old man is brought to the ED after a third episode of sudden loss of consciousness in two weeks. Episodes occur without warning, last under a minute, and are followed by rapid recovery without confusion. There is no aura, tongue-biting, or incontinence. He reports fatigue, lightheadedness, and several near-faints with exertion. He has hypertension and takes amlodipine 5 mg daily; he was recently started on low-dose metoprolol for palpitations. He has no diabetes. He had a prior anterior myocardial infarction five years ago. He takes aspirin and atorvastatin. He has no known drug allergies. He lives with his wife, is a former smoker, and drinks minimal alcohol. Family history is unremarkable. He denies chest pain at present, fevers, or recent illness. He has not changed his medications recently other than the metoprolol added two weeks ago by his cardiologist. Today's episode occurred while he was standing in the kitchen; his wife noted he was pale and briefly limp before awakening spontaneously. No focal weakness or speech difficulty afterward.
cases.exam: Elderly man, alert and oriented, appears well between episodes. Pulse is slow and regular at about 38 bpm. Intermittent prominent pulsations are visible in the neck (cannon A waves). The first heart sound varies in intensity from beat to beat. No murmurs of significant aortic stenosis. Lungs clear. Abdomen benign. No focal neurologic deficits; gait deferred. Extremities warm, no edema. Carotid upstrokes normal without bruits. No signs of trauma except a small forehead abrasion from today's fall.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate diagnostic test?
options: Carotid Doppler ultrasound in this clinical context
A 12-lead ECG with continuous cardiac monitoring
CT of the head to evaluate for stroke
Tilt-table testing as the first step today
stages.explanation: Syncope without prodrome in an elderly man with a markedly slow pulse, cannon A waves, and variable S1 points to a bradyarrhythmia—most likely high-grade AV block—so an immediate 12-lead ECG plus continuous telemetry is essential and will capture the conduction disturbance. A head CT (option A) is low-yield for syncope without focal neurologic signs and would miss the cardiac cause. Carotid Doppler (option C) evaluates for stenosis causing focal stroke symptoms, not abrupt syncope with bradycardia. Tilt-table testing (option D) assesses neurally mediated/vasovagal syncope and is inappropriate and unsafe before excluding a dangerous bradyarrhythmia in an unstable patient.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
ECG: atrial rate 88 bpm, ventricular rate 38 bpm, P waves and QRS complexes completely dissociated; wide QRS escape
Troponin: not elevated
Potassium 4.2 mEq/L, Magnesium 2.0 mg/dL
TSH: normal
Chest X-ray: no acute process

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The ECG shows atria and ventricles beating independently with the atrial rate faster than the ventricular rate. What is the diagnosis?
options: Sinus bradycardia with a junctional escape
First-degree AV block as the primary diagnosis
Complete (third-degree) AV block
Mobitz type I (Wenckebach) second-degree block
stages.explanation: Complete AV dissociation with the atrial rate exceeding the ventricular rate and no relationship between P waves and QRS complexes defines third-degree (complete) heart block; a slow ventricular escape maintains a perfusing rhythm. The cannon A waves arise when the atria contract against closed AV valves during dissociation, and variable S1 reflects changing AV relationships. First-degree block (option A) is merely a prolonged PR with 1:1 conduction. Mobitz I (option B) shows progressive PR lengthening then a dropped beat, with P-QRS still related. Sinus bradycardia with junctional escape (option D) would not show independent atrial activity faster than the ventricles with full dissociation.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He becomes more lightheaded with a recurrent drop in level of consciousness. What is the best immediate management while arranging definitive therapy?
options: Start an amiodarone infusion as initial therapy
Administer IV beta-blocker to stabilize the rhythm as monotherapy
Give IV adenosine as initial therapy
Begin transcutaneous pacing, with atropine as a temporizing measure
stages.explanation: Symptomatic high-grade/complete AV block is treated acutely with temporary pacing—transcutaneous immediately, transitioning to transvenous if needed—to maintain output as a bridge to a permanent pacemaker; atropine may be tried but is often ineffective when the block is infranodal with a wide escape. An IV beta-blocker (option A) would further slow conduction and is dangerous. Adenosine (option C) blocks AV conduction and would worsen the block. Amiodarone (option D) can depress conduction and is not indicated; this is a bradyarrhythmia, not a tachyarrhythmia. His recently added metoprolol should also be stopped, though the structural block likely reflects his prior MI.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What is the definitive treatment for his condition?
options: Lifelong oral theophylline
Coronary artery bypass grafting
Catheter ablation of the AV node
Permanent pacemaker implantation
stages.explanation: Symptomatic acquired complete heart block is a class I indication for permanent pacemaker implantation, which is the definitive therapy and prevents recurrent syncope and sudden death. Oral theophylline (option B) is not a reliable treatment for high-grade AV block. AV node ablation (option C) would deliberately create block and worsen the problem—the opposite of what is needed. Coronary bypass (option D) addresses ischemic coronary disease; while his old MI likely damaged the conduction system, revascularization does not reverse established complete heart block and would not treat the current arrhythmia. Stopping AV-nodal blockers and placing a pacemaker is the correct path.

cases.diagnosis: Complete (third-degree) atrioventricular block presenting with recurrent syncope
pearls: Complete heart block shows AV dissociation with the atrial rate faster than a slower, independent ventricular escape.
Cannon A waves and beat-to-beat variation in S1 intensity are classic bedside signs of AV dissociation.
Atropine often fails in infranodal block with a wide escape—move quickly to transcutaneous/transvenous pacing.
Symptomatic acquired complete heart block is a class I indication for permanent pacemaker implantation.

cases.title: Dizzy spells on the telemetry floor
cases.chiefComplaint: "I keep feeling like I'm going to pass out, and the monitor alarms when it happens."
cases.history: A 73-year-old woman admitted two days ago for community-acquired pneumonia reports recurrent brief episodes of lightheadedness and a sensation that her heart 'skips.' Telemetry technicians have noted intermittent dropped beats. She has no chest pain or dyspnea beyond her resolving pneumonia. She has hypertension and hyperlipidemia, treated with lisinopril and atorvastatin; she takes no AV-nodal blocking agents and no digoxin. She has no diabetes. She is a retired teacher, never smoked, and drinks no alcohol. There is no family history of heart block or sudden death. She had an anterior wall myocardial infarction three years ago treated with a stent and reports she was told afterward she had 'a bit of a bundle branch block.' She has no prior syncope. She denies fevers now, palpitations at rest, or medication changes. Her pneumonia is improving on antibiotics. She is alert and conversant, but the episodes of near-syncope have become more frequent over the last 12 hours, prompting cardiology consultation and closer review of her telemetry strips.
cases.exam: Alert, comfortable older woman. Pulse is regular at rest but with occasional pauses palpable at the wrist. Heart sounds are normal with a consistent S1 intensity; no murmurs, rubs, or gallops. There are no cannon A waves. Jugular venous pressure is normal. Lungs reveal improving right basilar crackles. No peripheral edema. Neurologic exam is nonfocal. Carotid upstrokes are normal. Extremities are warm and well perfused. No signs of volume overload or hypoperfusion.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Telemetry shows occasional non-conducted P waves. What ECG feature most helps distinguish Mobitz II from Mobitz I (Wenckebach) block?
options: Whether the QRS axis is leftward as the first step today
Whether the PR interval progressively lengthens before a dropped beat
Whether the atrial rate is above 100 bpm as the first step today
Whether the patient has chest pain as the first step today
stages.explanation: The defining distinction is the behavior of the PR interval. In Mobitz I (Wenckebach), the PR interval progressively lengthens beat to beat until a QRS is dropped, then the cycle resets. In Mobitz II, conducted beats have a constant (fixed) PR interval and a P wave suddenly fails to conduct without any preceding PR prolongation. Chest pain (option B) is nonspecific and not part of the classification. The atrial rate (option C) does not differentiate the two blocks. The QRS axis (option D) is not the distinguishing criterion, though Mobitz II is frequently associated with a wide QRS from infranodal disease. Tracking the PR interval across conducted beats is the key.

stages.label: TELEMETRY & ECG
stages.content:
12-lead ECG: sinus rhythm, right bundle branch block, constant PR interval (0.18 s) on all conducted beats
Telemetry: intermittent sudden non-conducted P waves with NO preceding PR prolongation; occasional 2:1 conduction
QRS duration: 0.13 s (wide)
Troponin: not elevated
Electrolytes: within normal limits
Echocardiogram: anteroapical hypokinesis, LVEF 45%

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Based on the fixed PR interval with sudden dropped beats and a wide QRS, what is the diagnosis?
options: Sinus arrhythmia in this clinical context
First-degree AV block
Mobitz type II second-degree AV block
Mobitz type I (Wenckebach) second-degree AV block
stages.explanation: A constant PR interval on conducted beats with abrupt, intermittent failure of a P wave to conduct—plus a wide QRS reflecting infranodal (His-Purkinje) disease—defines Mobitz type II second-degree AV block. Her prior anterior MI and bundle branch block fit infranodal conduction disease. Mobitz I (option A) requires progressive PR lengthening before the dropped beat, which is absent here. First-degree block (option C) has a prolonged but constant PR with every P conducting—no dropped beats. Sinus arrhythmia (option D) is a normal phasic variation in sinus rate with respiration and shows every P wave conducting, not non-conducted P waves.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Why does Mobitz II mandate a pacemaker whereas asymptomatic Wenckebach often does not?
options: Mobitz II responds reliably to atropine, so a pacemaker is precautionary as initial therapy
Mobitz II is usually due to reversible vagal tone and resolves spontaneously as initial therapy
Wenckebach always causes syncope and is more dangerous as initial therapy
Mobitz II reflects His-Purkinje disease that can progress unpredictably to complete heart block
stages.explanation: Mobitz II arises from disease below the AV node in the His-Purkinje system, which has poor and unreliable escape rhythms. It can progress abruptly and unpredictably to complete heart block with asystole, so it is a class I indication for permanent pacing even when symptoms are mild. Mobitz I/Wenckebach is usually a block within the AV node, often vagally mediated, with a reliable junctional escape and a benign course—so asymptomatic Wenckebach generally needs no pacemaker. Option 0 describes Wenckebach, not Mobitz II. Option 2 is false—Wenckebach is typically benign. Option 3 is wrong because infranodal (Mobitz II) block characteristically does NOT respond well to atropine and may even worsen.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What is the most appropriate definitive management for this patient?
options: Trial of atropine and discharge if it raises the heart rate
Discontinue all medications and observe off telemetry
Start oral metoprolol to regularize the rhythm
Permanent pacemaker implantation
stages.explanation: Symptomatic Mobitz II second-degree AV block (and even asymptomatic Mobitz II with a wide QRS from structural disease) is a class I indication for permanent pacemaker implantation because of the high risk of progression to complete heart block. Discontinuing meds and removing telemetry (option A) is unsafe; she is not on AV-nodal blockers and the block is structural, not reversible. Metoprolol (option B) further impairs AV conduction and is contraindicated. An atropine trial then discharge (option D) is inappropriate—atropine is unreliable in infranodal block and does not address the need for definitive pacing.

cases.diagnosis: Mobitz type II second-degree atrioventricular block (infranodal) requiring permanent pacemaker
pearls: Mobitz II shows a fixed PR interval with sudden non-conducted P waves; Mobitz I shows progressive PR lengthening before the dropped beat.
Mobitz II reflects His-Purkinje disease and can progress unpredictably to complete heart block—hence it mandates pacing.
A wide QRS or bundle branch block supports an infranodal level of block.
Asymptomatic Wenckebach (Mobitz I) is usually benign and does not require a pacemaker.

cases.title: Racing heart then near-fainting in an older woman
cases.chiefComplaint: "My heart races for a while, then I get so dizzy I nearly black out."
cases.history: A 76-year-old woman presents with several months of intermittent palpitations alternating with episodes of lightheadedness and two near-syncopal spells. The palpitations feel rapid and irregular and last minutes to hours; afterward she sometimes feels her pulse is very slow and she becomes dizzy. She denies chest pain. She has hypertension treated with amlodipine and a history of hypothyroidism well controlled on levothyroxine. She takes no AV-nodal blockers currently. She has no diabetes. She is a retired librarian, never smoked, and rarely drinks alcohol. Family history is noncontributory. She has no prior diagnosed arrhythmia. She is worried because a friend with similar palpitations had a stroke. She reports mild fatigue and reduced exercise tolerance. Her thyroid labs last month were normal. She has not started any new medications. The dizziness episodes seem to come right after the palpitations subside, and on one occasion her home blood-pressure monitor displayed a heart rate in the 30s while she felt faint, prompting this visit.
cases.exam: Well-appearing older woman in no distress. Pulse is irregular and slow at the time of exam. Heart sounds are variable in intensity consistent with an irregular rhythm; no significant murmurs. JVP is normal. Lungs clear. No peripheral edema. Carotid upstrokes normal without bruits. Neurologic exam is nonfocal. No thyromegaly or tremor. Extremities warm and well perfused. No signs of heart failure.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given symptoms that come and go, what is the best initial test to correlate her symptoms with her rhythm?
options: A single resting 12-lead ECG only as the first step today
Ambulatory ECG monitoring to capture symptomatic episodes
Exercise stress echocardiography as the first step today
Electrophysiology study as the first step as the first step today
stages.explanation: Her symptoms are intermittent, so prolonged ambulatory ECG monitoring (a Holter, event recorder, or patch monitor) is the best initial test to correlate palpitations and dizziness with the underlying rhythm—capturing both the tachycardia and the pauses. A single resting ECG (option A) is often normal between episodes and may miss the diagnosis. Exercise stress echocardiography (option C) evaluates ischemia and inducible wall-motion abnormalities, not an intermittent brady-tachy rhythm. An electrophysiology study (option D) is invasive and reserved for cases where noninvasive monitoring is inconclusive or specific inducible arrhythmias must be characterized—not the first step here.

stages.label: AMBULATORY MONITOR
stages.content:
48-hour patch monitor:
- Multiple runs of atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular rates up to 150 bpm (correlating with palpitations)
- On termination of AF, sinus pauses up to 4.2 seconds (correlating with dizziness/near-syncope)
- Baseline sinus bradycardia 40-50 bpm
TSH: normal
Electrolytes: normal
Echocardiogram: normal chamber sizes, LVEF 60%, no significant valve disease

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the diagnosis that unifies her tachycardia, pauses, and baseline bradycardia?
options: Wolff-Parkinson-White syndrome
Paroxysmal AF alone with an incidental normal heart rate
Sick sinus syndrome (tachy-brady syndrome)
Complete heart block as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Alternating atrial fibrillation/flutter with symptomatic sinus pauses and baseline sinus bradycardia is the classic tachy-brady form of sick sinus syndrome—sinus node dysfunction in which the node fails to resume promptly after a tachyarrhythmia terminates, producing long pauses and near-syncope. Isolated paroxysmal AF (option A) does not explain the 4-second sinus pauses or baseline bradycardia. Complete heart block (option C) shows AV dissociation, not sinus pauses after AF termination, and her conducted beats are normally related. Wolff-Parkinson-White (option D) involves a delta wave and pre-excitation, not sinus node failure—she has no pre-excitation on her tracings.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She needs rate control for the AF episodes, but rate-controlling drugs worsen her pauses. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Add a high-dose beta-blocker alone and accept the pauses as initial therapy
Implant a permanent pacemaker, then use rate-controlling/antiarrhythmic drugs
Start digoxin monotherapy and reassure her as initial therapy
Catheter ablation of the AV node without any pacing as initial therapy
stages.explanation: The therapeutic bind in tachy-brady syndrome is that the drugs needed to control the AF (beta-blockers, calcium-channel blockers, antiarrhythmics) aggravate the bradycardia and pauses. The solution is a permanent pacemaker to protect against bradycardia, which then allows safe use of rate-controlling or antiarrhythmic drugs; anticoagulation is added based on stroke risk (her CHA₂DS₂-VASc is elevated by age, sex, and hypertension). A high-dose beta-blocker alone (option A) would deepen dangerous pauses. AV-node ablation without pacing (option C) would leave her without a reliable rhythm—pacing is mandatory if the node is ablated. Digoxin monotherapy (option D) controls rate poorly during exertion and ignores both the pauses and stroke risk.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Regarding stroke prevention, what is the most appropriate recommendation for this patient with documented AF?
options: Anticoagulation only after the pacemaker fails
Oral anticoagulation based on her elevated CHA₂DS₂-VASc score
Aspirin alone is sufficient for stroke prevention
No anticoagulation because her AF is only paroxysmal
stages.explanation: Paroxysmal AF carries the same stroke risk as persistent AF, and anticoagulation decisions are guided by the CHA₂DS₂-VASc score, not by AF burden or pattern. This 76-year-old woman with hypertension already scores high enough to warrant oral anticoagulation (a DOAC is generally preferred). Withholding anticoagulation because the AF is paroxysmal (option A) is a common and dangerous error. Aspirin alone (option B) is inferior and not recommended for stroke prevention in AF with an elevated score. Tying anticoagulation to pacemaker failure (option D) is illogical—stroke prevention is independent of pacing and should begin based on her thromboembolic risk.

cases.diagnosis: Sick sinus syndrome (tachy-brady syndrome) with paroxysmal atrial fibrillation and symptomatic sinus pauses
pearls: Tachy-brady syndrome alternates atrial tachyarrhythmias with symptomatic sinus pauses, often when the tachycardia terminates.
The drugs that control the tachycardia worsen the bradycardia—so a permanent pacemaker is required to enable rate/rhythm control.
Prolonged ambulatory monitoring is key to correlating intermittent symptoms with the rhythm.
Anticoagulate based on CHA₂DS₂-VASc; paroxysmal AF carries the same stroke risk as persistent AF.

cases.title: Breathless and palpitating with a flare-up
cases.chiefComplaint: "My breathing got much worse and now my heart is fluttering."
cases.history: A 71-year-old man with severe COPD is admitted for an acute exacerbation with increased dyspnea, cough, and purulent sputum. On the second hospital day he develops palpitations. He has a 50-pack-year smoking history and is on home oxygen, inhaled tiotropium, and an albuterol/budesonide regimen; during this admission he is receiving frequent nebulized albuterol, ipratropium, systemic corticosteroids, and IV theophylline was recently added. He has cor pulmonale and mild chronic hypoxemia. He has hypertension on amlodipine. He has no prior atrial fibrillation. He has no diabetes. He is a retired machinist, lives alone, and quit alcohol years ago. Family history is noncontributory. He has no chest pain and denies syncope. He is anxious about his breathing. His potassium and magnesium have been running low during the admission. He has not been started on any new antiarrhythmic. The nursing staff note an irregular, rapid pulse on the monitor and request an ECG and cardiology input to characterize the new rhythm.
cases.exam: Thin, barrel-chested man in mild respiratory distress using accessory muscles. Pursed-lip breathing. Heart sounds are irregularly irregular and rapid; no significant murmurs. Lungs reveal diffuse expiratory wheezes and prolonged expiration. There is mild bilateral lower extremity edema and a loud P2 consistent with pulmonary hypertension. JVP is mildly elevated. No focal neurologic deficits. Extremities are warm. Mild clubbing. No calf tenderness.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: An ECG is obtained for the irregular tachycardia. Which finding would specifically establish multifocal atrial tachycardia rather than atrial fibrillation?
options: A regular narrow-complex tachycardia at 150 bpm as the first step today
At least three distinct P-wave morphologies with varying PP, PR,
Absence of any discernible P waves with a chaotic baseline
A sawtooth flutter wave pattern in the inferior leads
stages.explanation: Multifocal atrial tachycardia (MAT) is defined by an irregular atrial rhythm with at least three distinct P-wave morphologies and varying PP, PR, and RR intervals, at a rate over 100 bpm—reflecting multiple competing atrial foci, classically in decompensated pulmonary disease. Absence of P waves with a chaotic baseline (option A) describes atrial fibrillation, which lacks organized P waves entirely. A regular narrow-complex tachycardia at 150 (option C) suggests AVNRT or atrial flutter with 2:1 conduction, not MAT. A sawtooth pattern (option D) is atrial flutter. The hallmark of MAT is multiple discrete P-wave morphologies with discernible, varying P waves before each QRS.

stages.label: ECG & LABS
stages.content:
12-lead ECG: irregular atrial tachycardia, rate ~120 bpm, at least 3 distinct P-wave morphologies, varying PP/PR/RR intervals
Potassium 3.2 mEq/L
Magnesium 1.4 mg/dL
Theophylline level: 18 µg/mL (upper end of range)
ABG: pH 7.36, PaCO₂ 52 mmHg, PaO₂ 60 mmHg
Troponin: not elevated
Chest X-ray: hyperinflation, flattened diaphragms, no infiltrate

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The rhythm is multifocal atrial tachycardia. What is the cornerstone of management?
options: Administer adenosine to terminate the tachycardia as the primary diagnosis
Immediate synchronized cardioversion as the primary diagnosis
Start amiodarone to chemically convert the rhythm as the primary diagnosis
Treat the underlying pulmonary disease/hypoxia
stages.explanation: MAT is driven by the underlying illness, so the cornerstone is treating the precipitant—optimizing oxygenation and the COPD exacerbation, correcting hypokalemia and hypomagnesemia, and reassessing pro-arrhythmic drivers such as excessive beta-agonists and theophylline. The rhythm typically resolves as the lung disease improves. Synchronized cardioversion (option A) is ineffective because MAT is not a reentrant rhythm—it has multiple foci and will recur immediately. Amiodarone (option C) is not first-line and adds toxicity in chronic lung disease. Adenosine (option D) transiently blocks the AV node but does not terminate a multifocal atrial mechanism and is not therapeutic here.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: If rate control with medication becomes necessary and the rhythm persists despite treating the lungs, which agent is generally preferred—and which should be avoided here?
options: Preferred: flecainide; avoid verapamil as initial therapy
Preferred: a nondihydropyridine calcium-channel blocker or selective beta-blocker
Preferred: high-dose nonselective beta-blocker; avoid calcium-channel blockers as initial therapy
Preferred: digoxin; avoid all rate control as initial therapy
stages.explanation: When rate control is needed for persistent MAT, a nondihydropyridine calcium-channel blocker (verapamil or diltiazem) is often preferred, and metoprolol (a cardioselective beta-blocker) can be used if not contraindicated; magnesium may also help suppress ectopy. Nonselective beta-blockers should be avoided because they can provoke bronchospasm in COPD. Option 1 is wrong because it recommends a nonselective beta-blocker and rejects calcium-channel blockers. Digoxin (option C) is a poor choice—it controls rate weakly, is arrhythmogenic at the low potassium and high catecholamine state of MAT. Flecainide (option D) is a class IC agent inappropriate here and is not a standard MAT therapy.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which specific intervention should be prioritized first to reduce the ectopic atrial activity in this patient?
options: Place a temporary transvenous pacemaker as the immediate next step
Begin therapeutic anticoagulation immediately as the immediate next step
Replete magnesium and potassium and minimize excess beta-agonist/theophylline
Increase the theophylline dose for bronchodilation as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Hypokalemia and hypomagnesemia directly promote atrial ectopy, and both beta-agonists and theophylline are arrhythmogenic—so prioritizing electrolyte repletion (especially magnesium) and trimming excess beta-agonist/theophylline directly reduces the multifocal atrial activity. Increasing theophylline (option A) would worsen the arrhythmia, as theophylline toxicity is a classic MAT trigger. Immediate anticoagulation (option C) is not the priority; MAT itself is not a primary thromboembolic rhythm like AF, and stroke prevention is decided separately. A temporary pacemaker (option D) treats bradyarrhythmias, not a multifocal tachycardia, and has no role here.

cases.diagnosis: Multifocal atrial tachycardia in the setting of a COPD exacerbation
pearls: MAT requires ≥3 distinct P-wave morphologies with varying PP/PR/RR intervals at a rate >100 bpm.
MAT is a marker of underlying illness—treat the lung disease, hypoxia, and electrolyte deficits rather than the rhythm itself.
Cardioversion is ineffective in MAT because it is not a reentrant arrhythmia; it recurs immediately.
If rate control is needed, favor verapamil/diltiazem or a cardioselective beta-blocker and replete magnesium; avoid nonselective beta-blockers in bronchospasm.

cases.title: An unexpected finding on a routine pre-op ECG
cases.chiefComplaint: "I'm here for surgery clearance and they said my heart tracing looks unusual."
cases.history: A 23-year-old man presents to the preoperative clinic for clearance before an elective knee arthroscopy. He is asymptomatic and feels well. He denies palpitations, syncope, presyncope, chest pain, or exertional limitation, and he plays recreational soccer without issue. He takes no medications and has no allergies. He has never had cardiac evaluation. He does not smoke, drinks socially, and uses no stimulants or illicit drugs. Family history is notable only for an aunt with 'a fast heart rhythm' that was treated with a procedure; there is no family history of sudden cardiac death. He has no prior ECGs for comparison. The anesthesiology team flagged an abnormality on the screening ECG and asked for cardiology review before proceeding. He is anxious about a possible delay to his surgery but otherwise has no complaints. He reports no recent viral illness, no thyroid disease, and no caffeine-related symptoms. He is a graduate student and physically active.
cases.exam: Healthy-appearing young man in no distress. Heart rate regular without murmurs, rubs, or gallops; normal S1 and S2. No signs of heart failure; JVP normal, lungs clear, no edema. Peripheral pulses normal and symmetric. Neurologic exam nonfocal. No thyromegaly. The remainder of the cardiovascular and general examination is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The screening ECG shows a short PR interval and a slurred upstroke of the QRS. What does this pattern represent?
options: A delta wave from ventricular pre-excitation via an accessory pathway
Left bundle branch block as the first step today
Acute pericarditis as the first step today
Early repolarization, a benign normal variant as the first step today
stages.explanation: A short PR interval with a slurred QRS upstroke (delta wave) and a widened QRS is the classic Wolff-Parkinson-White pattern, indicating ventricular pre-excitation through an accessory atrioventricular pathway (bundle of Kent) that bypasses the normal AV-nodal delay. Left bundle branch block (option A) produces a wide QRS but with a normal or long PR and no delta wave. Early repolarization (option C) causes J-point elevation, not a short PR or delta wave. Acute pericarditis (option D) shows diffuse ST elevation and PR depression, not pre-excitation. The short PR plus delta wave is pathognomonic of an accessory pathway.

stages.label: ECG & DEFINITIONS
stages.content:
12-lead ECG: PR interval 0.10 s, delta waves in multiple leads, QRS 0.12 s, no prior tachyarrhythmia documented
No symptoms of arrhythmia reported (asymptomatic)
Note: 'WPW pattern' = pre-excitation on ECG without arrhythmia; 'WPW syndrome' = pre-excitation PLUS documented tachyarrhythmia
Echocardiogram: structurally normal heart, LVEF 62%
Thyroid function: normal

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: He has the pattern but no documented arrhythmia. How is his condition best characterized, and why does it still matter?
options: Asymptomatic WPW pattern
WPW syndrome—he needs immediate ablation before any surgery as the primary diagnosis
A benign normal variant requiring no further thought as the primary diagnosis
Long QT syndrome requiring beta-blockade as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Because he has pre-excitation on ECG but no documented tachyarrhythmia, this is the asymptomatic WPW pattern, not WPW syndrome. It still matters because if he ever develops atrial fibrillation, the accessory pathway can conduct rapidly to the ventricles and degenerate into ventricular fibrillation—a rare but catastrophic cause of sudden death—so risk stratification is warranted. Calling it WPW syndrome and rushing to ablation before surgery (option A) overstates his current status. Dismissing it as benign with no further thought (option C) ignores the small but real sudden-death risk. Long QT syndrome (option D) is a different entity with a prolonged QT, not pre-excitation, and beta-blockade is its treatment, not relevant here.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step for this asymptomatic young patient with a WPW pattern?
options: Referral to an electrophysiologist for risk stratification
Start a beta-blocker and clear him with no specialist input as initial therapy
No referral; simply avoid caffeine as initial therapy
Lifelong oral amiodarone as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Even asymptomatic patients with a WPW pattern—especially young, active individuals—warrant referral to an electrophysiologist for risk stratification. Exercise testing (looking for abrupt loss of pre-excitation, which suggests a low-risk pathway) or an electrophysiology study assesses the pathway's refractory period and conduction; high-risk features favor catheter ablation, which is curative. Lifelong amiodarone (option A) is inappropriate, with major toxicity, for an asymptomatic young person. No referral and only avoiding caffeine (option C) fails to assess the sudden-death risk. Starting a beta-blocker and clearing him without specialist input (option D) is insufficient and could be hazardous if AF with rapid pathway conduction occurs.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: If this patient ever develops atrial fibrillation with a rapid pre-excited ventricular response, which drug class must be AVOIDED?
options: Intravenous fluids as the immediate next step
Direct-current cardioversion as the immediate next step
Procainamide or ibutilide as the immediate next step
AV-nodal blocking agents such as adenosine, verapamil, diltiazem, beta-blockers,
stages.explanation: In pre-excited atrial fibrillation, AV-nodal blockers (adenosine, verapamil, diltiazem, beta-blockers, digoxin) must be avoided because blocking the AV node preferentially channels conduction down the accessory pathway, accelerating the ventricular rate and risking degeneration into ventricular fibrillation. Procainamide or ibutilide (option A) are appropriate—they slow conduction in the accessory pathway and are recommended when the patient is stable. IV fluids (option C) are neutral and not harmful in this context. Direct-current cardioversion (option D) is the treatment of choice if the patient is hemodynamically unstable, not something to avoid. The teaching point is the danger of AV-nodal blockade with pre-excited AF.

cases.diagnosis: Asymptomatic Wolff-Parkinson-White pattern (ventricular pre-excitation via an accessory pathway)
pearls: WPW pattern = pre-excitation on ECG (short PR, delta wave) without arrhythmia; WPW syndrome adds a documented tachyarrhythmia.
The danger is atrial fibrillation conducting rapidly down the accessory pathway, which can degenerate into ventricular fibrillation.
Even asymptomatic patients warrant EP referral for risk stratification; catheter ablation is curative for high-risk pathways.
In pre-excited AF, avoid AV-nodal blockers (adenosine, calcium-channel blockers, beta-blockers, digoxin); use procainamide/ibutilide or cardiovert if unstable.

cases.title: Sustained racing heart in a man with prior heart attack
cases.chiefComplaint: "My heart suddenly started pounding really fast and won't stop."
cases.history: A 62-year-old man presents to the ED with 40 minutes of a sustained, rapid, regular palpitation that began abruptly while watching television. He feels lightheaded and has mild chest tightness but is conversant and not in extremis. He has ischemic cardiomyopathy with an LVEF of 30% after a large anterior myocardial infarction four years ago. His medications include carvedilol, lisinopril, atorvastatin, aspirin, and furosemide. He has no implantable defibrillator. He has no known drug allergies. He is a former smoker and drinks rarely. He has type 2 diabetes on metformin. Family history includes coronary disease. He denies fever or recent illness and has been adherent to his medications. He reports no prior episodes of sustained palpitations, though he has felt occasional skipped beats. He is anxious but maintains adequate mental status. There has been no syncope. Paramedics noted a wide-complex tachycardia on their monitor en route and gave no medications. He has a palpable pulse and is being moved to a monitored resuscitation bay.
cases.exam: Anxious but alert man, mildly diaphoretic, speaking in full sentences. Rapid regular tachycardia on the monitor. Heart sounds are rapid and regular; difficult to appreciate murmurs at this rate. Lungs have mild bibasilar crackles. JVP shows intermittent prominent (cannon-like) waves. No focal neurologic deficits. Extremities are warm with palpable distal pulses. No peripheral edema beyond trace. He remains hemodynamically borderline but perfusing—mentating well with a measurable blood pressure.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: He has a wide-complex tachycardia with a pulse and is stable enough to mentate and maintain a blood pressure. In a patient with structural heart disease, what should you assume the rhythm is?
options: Sinus tachycardia from anxiety in this clinical context
Ventricular tachycardia until proven otherwise
Artifact from lead movement as the first step today
Supraventricular tachycardia with aberrancy until proven otherwise
stages.explanation: Any wide-complex tachycardia, especially in a patient with structural/ischemic heart disease and prior MI, should be assumed to be ventricular tachycardia until proven otherwise—this assumption is the safest and is correct most of the time. Treating it as SVT with aberrancy (option A) risks giving AV-nodal agents that are ineffective or harmful in VT. Sinus tachycardia from anxiety (option C) would be narrow-complex and rarely reaches 168 with this morphology. Artifact (option D) is excluded by a consistent wide-complex tachycardia with corresponding symptoms and hemodynamics. The cannon A waves suggest AV dissociation, further supporting VT. When in doubt, treat a wide-complex tachycardia as VT.

stages.label: ECG
stages.content:
12-lead ECG: regular wide-complex tachycardia, rate 168 bpm, QRS 0.16 s, monomorphic
Features favoring VT: AV dissociation with intermittent fusion and capture beats, QRS concordance across precordial leads
No prior delta wave; known prior anterior MI with Q waves at baseline
Troponin: mildly elevated
Potassium 4.0 mEq/L, Magnesium 2.0 mg/dL

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which ECG feature is most specific for ventricular tachycardia rather than SVT with aberrancy?
options: A heart rate above 150 bpm
A regular rhythm in this clinical context
A wide QRS alone in this clinical context
AV dissociation with fusion
stages.explanation: AV dissociation—independent atrial and ventricular activity—with fusion and capture beats is highly specific for ventricular tachycardia, because it demonstrates that the ventricles are being driven independently of the atria. A rate over 150 (option A) occurs in both VT and SVT and is nonspecific. A regular rhythm (option B) is seen in monomorphic VT and many SVTs alike, so it does not distinguish them. A wide QRS alone (option D) can result from aberrant conduction or pre-existing bundle branch block in SVT and is therefore not specific. Fusion/capture beats and AV dissociation, along with very wide QRS and precordial concordance, point firmly to VT.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He remains alert with a systolic BP around 100 mmHg (stable monomorphic VT). What is an appropriate next management step?
options: Oral metoprolol and discharge as initial therapy
IV adenosine push as initial therapy
IV antiarrhythmic such as amiodarone , with synchronized cardioversion if he becomes unstable or drugs fail
Immediate unsynchronized defibrillation as initial therapy
stages.explanation: For stable monomorphic VT with a pulse, options are pharmacologic conversion with an IV antiarrhythmic (amiodarone, or procainamide which is often preferred for efficacy) OR elective synchronized cardioversion; if he deteriorates or drugs fail, proceed to synchronized cardioversion. Unsynchronized defibrillation (option A) is reserved for pulseless VT or VF, or unstable polymorphic VT—not a stable patient with a pulse. Adenosine (option C) is for regular SVT and is generally ineffective/inappropriate for VT (though occasionally diagnostic in clearly stable regular wide-complex tachycardia, it is not the treatment for true VT). Oral metoprolol and discharge (option D) is unsafe and fails to terminate the sustained arrhythmia.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After successful conversion, given his ischemic cardiomyopathy with LVEF 30% and sustained VT, what is the most appropriate long-term recommendation?
options: Long-term oral flecainide with appropriate follow-up
Permanent pacemaker only with appropriate follow-up
No further intervention beyond his current medications with appropriate follow-up
Implantable cardioverter-defibrillator for secondary prevention of sudden cardiac death
