stages.explanation: Coarctation of the aorta classically produces radio-femoral delay and an upper-to-lower-extremity blood pressure gradient (arm pressures exceeding leg pressures), so simultaneously palpating brachial and femoral pulses and obtaining four-extremity pressures is the most specific bedside maneuver here. An abdominal bruit (option B) suggests renal artery stenosis, a different secondary-hypertension cause that would not produce diminished femoral pulses. Orthostatic vitals (option D) assess autonomic/volume status, not coarctation. Thyroid palpation (option E) screens for thyroid disease, which is not suggested by his pulse and pressure differential. The arm-leg pressure gradient and delayed femoral pulse are the hallmark findings.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Four-extremity BP: right arm 168/96, left arm 166/94, right leg 120/76, left leg 118/74
ECG: left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage criteria
Chest X-ray: 'figure-3' sign of the aortic contour; bilateral inferior rib notching (3rd-8th ribs)
Echocardiogram: discrete narrowing of the descending thoracic aorta just distal to the left subclavian artery with a peak gradient of 42 mmHg; bicuspid aortic valve; mild concentric LV hypertrophy

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The rib notching seen on his chest film is produced by which mechanism?
options: Healed rib fractures from prior trauma as the primary diagnosis
Direct erosion of the ribs by an enlarged left atrium as the primary diagnosis
Osteopenia from chronic corticosteroid use as the primary diagnosis
Dilated intercostal collateral arteries eroding the inferior rib margins as blood bypasses the coarctation
stages.explanation: In coarctation, blood reaches the distal aorta via collateral channels, prominently the intercostal arteries, which enlarge and become tortuous; their pulsatile pressure erodes the inferior margins of the posterior ribs, producing the classic bilateral rib notching. An enlarged left atrium (option B) does not erode ribs and would not cause this pattern. Corticosteroid-induced osteopenia (option C) causes diffuse demineralization, not focal inferior-margin notching. Healed rib fractures (option E) are focal callus formation, not the symmetric bilateral notching characteristic of coarctation collaterals. The associated bicuspid aortic valve is also a key, very common coexisting finding.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given a discrete coarctation with a 42 mmHg gradient and resistant hypertension, what is the most appropriate definitive management?
options: Long-term beta-blocker monotherapy only as initial therapy
Salt restriction and observation with annual blood pressure checks
Catheter-based balloon angioplasty with stenting to relieve the coarctation
Add a third antihypertensive and avoid any procedure as initial therapy
stages.explanation: A significant coarctation (typically a peak-to-peak gradient ≥20 mmHg, with upper-extremity hypertension and imaging confirmation) warrants mechanical relief — transcatheter balloon angioplasty with stenting in adolescents/adults, or surgical repair — to correct the obstruction, control hypertension, and prevent complications such as heart failure, aortic dissection, and intracranial aneurysm rupture. Simply adding a third drug (option B) leaves the fixed mechanical obstruction and its long-term risks untreated. Beta-blocker monotherapy (option D) does not address the anatomic lesion. Salt restriction and observation (option E) are inadequate for a hemodynamically significant coarctation causing resistant hypertension in a young patient.

stages.label: POST-PROCEDURE
stages.content:
Successful stent placement across the coarctation; residual gradient 6 mmHg.
Right arm BP 138/84 on amlodipine monotherapy at follow-up
Femoral pulses now strong with no radio-femoral delay
Echocardiogram: bicuspid aortic valve persists with mildly dilated ascending aorta (38 mm); no significant aortic stenosis

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which lifelong follow-up issue is most important for this patient after coarctation repair?
options: No further cardiology follow-up is needed once the gradient is relieved with appropriate follow-up
Lifelong anticoagulation because of the aortic stent with appropriate follow-up
Annual coronary angiography to monitor for atherosclerosis with appropriate follow-up
Surveillance for recoarctation, persistent/recurrent hypertension,
stages.explanation: Coarctation is a lifelong condition: even after successful repair, patients need ongoing surveillance for recoarctation (gradient recurrence), residual or recurrent hypertension (which persists in many despite a good anatomic result), and complications of frequently associated lesions — a bicuspid aortic valve (risk of stenosis/regurgitation) and ascending aortic dilation/aneurysm with dissection risk. Discharging him from follow-up (option B) is unsafe given these durable risks. Annual coronary angiography (option C) is not indicated in a young patient without coronary disease. Routine lifelong anticoagulation for an aortic stent (option D) is not standard practice and would not address his actual long-term risks.

cases.diagnosis: Coarctation of the aorta with secondary resistant hypertension and an associated bicuspid aortic valve
pearls: Suspect coarctation in a young patient with resistant upper-extremity hypertension, an arm-leg BP gradient, and radio-femoral delay.
Classic chest film signs: the 'figure-3' aortic contour and bilateral inferior rib notching from dilated intercostal collaterals.
A bicuspid aortic valve coexists in a large proportion of coarctation cases and brings its own ascending aortopathy/valve risks.
Relieve significant coarctation (gradient ≥20 mmHg) with stenting or surgery, then follow lifelong for recoarctation, residual hypertension, and aortic complications.

cases.title: An Unexpected Stroke and a Split Heart Sound
cases.chiefComplaint: Exertional breathlessness and a recent unexplained stroke in a young woman.
cases.history: A 34-year-old woman is referred to cardiology after an embolic stroke with no clear cause. She recovered well with only minor residual right-hand clumsiness. On further questioning she admits to several years of gradually worsening exertional dyspnea and reduced exercise tolerance, which she had attributed to being out of shape. She also notes occasional palpitations. Past medical history is otherwise unremarkable; she takes a combined oral contraceptive pill and now low-dose aspirin started after the stroke. She has no drug allergies. Several weeks before the stroke she had a long-haul flight and a calf that was briefly swollen. She does not smoke, drinks rarely, and uses no drugs. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is positive for mild exertional fatigue and palpitations but negative for chest pain, orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, fever, hemoptysis, or recurrent infections. She has had two uncomplicated pregnancies in the past.
cases.exam: Well-appearing woman in no distress. Jugular venous pressure is normal. There is a left parasternal (right ventricular) heave. On auscultation S2 is widely split and the split does not vary with respiration (fixed splitting). There is a soft grade 2/6 systolic ejection murmur at the left upper sternal border (pulmonary area) and a soft mid-diastolic rumble at the lower left sternal border. There is no loud P2 and no holosystolic murmur. Lungs are clear. The abdomen is soft without organomegaly. There is no peripheral edema and pulses are normal and symmetric. Mild residual right-hand fine-motor clumsiness is noted on neurologic exam.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A young woman with a cryptogenic stroke, exertional dyspnea, and fixed splitting of S2 should be evaluated first with which test?
options: 24-hour ambulatory blood pressure monitoring in this clinical context
Transthoracic echocardiography with agitated-saline contrast study
Pulmonary function testing as the first step today
Coronary angiography as the first step today
stages.explanation: Fixed splitting of S2 with exertional dyspnea and a cryptogenic stroke strongly suggests an atrial septal defect with a left-to-right shunt and the potential for paradoxical embolism. Transthoracic echocardiography with an agitated-saline bubble study is the appropriate first test: it visualizes RV/RA enlargement and pulmonary flow and can demonstrate the interatrial shunt. Coronary angiography (option B) evaluates coronary disease, not relevant in a 34-year-old with this presentation. Ambulatory BP monitoring (option D) addresses hypertension, which she does not have. Pulmonary function testing (option E) evaluates intrinsic lung disease, but her clinical picture points to a cardiac shunt rather than primary pulmonary pathology.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
ECG: incomplete right bundle branch block; rightward axis
Chest X-ray: enlarged right heart and prominent pulmonary arteries with increased pulmonary vascular markings
Echocardiogram: dilated right atrium and right ventricle with RV volume overload (paradoxical septal motion); secundum atrial septal defect, 16 mm, with left-to-right shunt; estimated Qp:Qs 2.1:1; normal LV; estimated RV systolic pressure 38 mmHg
Bubble study: right-to-left passage of bubbles during Valsalva confirming a route for paradoxical embolism

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the physiologic explanation for the fixed splitting of S2 in this patient?
options: Early pulmonic valve closure from severe pulmonary hypertension as the primary diagnosis
The atrial-level shunt equalizes respiratory variation in right
Premature aortic valve closure from aortic stenosis as the primary diagnosis
Delayed left ventricular emptying causing a late A2 as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: In a secundum ASD the left-to-right shunt continuously augments right heart volume; the interatrial communication buffers the normal respiratory variation in systemic venous return relative to the shunt, so RV stroke volume — and therefore the timing of pulmonic valve closure (P2) relative to aortic closure (A2) — does not change appreciably with respiration. This produces the characteristic fixed splitting of S2. Delayed LV emptying (option B) and premature aortic closure (option E) describe other murmurs/sounds and do not explain a respiration-independent split. Early P2 from severe pulmonary hypertension (option D) would narrow or accentuate the split, not fix it across the respiratory cycle.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given a secundum ASD with RV volume overload, a Qp:Qs of 2.1:1, symptoms, and a paradoxical embolic stroke, what is the most appropriate management?
options: Pulmonary vasodilator therapy with no defect closure as initial therapy
Lifelong therapeutic anticoagulation as the sole intervention as initial therapy
Observation alone with repeat echocardiography in five years as initial therapy
Closure of the atrial septal defect
stages.explanation: A hemodynamically significant ASD — evidenced by right heart enlargement and RV volume overload with a Qp:Qs of about 2:1 — is an indication for closure, and a secundum defect with adequate rims is usually amenable to percutaneous device closure, which also removes the conduit for paradoxical embolism that caused her stroke. Observation (option B) allows progressive RV dilation, arrhythmia, and pulmonary vascular disease. Anticoagulation alone (option D) does not correct the shunt or RV overload and leaves the structural defect. Pulmonary vasodilators without closure (option E) are inappropriate here because her pulmonary pressures are not severely elevated and the shunt remains correctable; closure is the definitive therapy.

stages.label: POST-PROCEDURE
stages.content:
Successful percutaneous secundum ASD device closure.
Follow-up echo at 6 months: no residual shunt; right atrial and RV size decreasing; RV systolic pressure normal
Symptoms improved with better exercise tolerance
Started on dual antiplatelet therapy for the closure device per protocol
No recurrent neurologic events

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which counseling point is most appropriate for this patient after device closure?
options: She no longer needs any cardiology follow-up with appropriate follow-up
She should continue antiplatelet therapy for the prescribed post-device period
Endocarditis prophylaxis is never indicated under any circumstance after closure with appropriate follow-up
She must take lifelong warfarin regardless of other factors with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: After percutaneous ASD device closure, patients take antiplatelet therapy (commonly aspirin with a period of dual antiplatelet therapy) while the device endothelializes, and they need follow-up to confirm device position and absence of residual shunt. Given her prior paradoxical embolism and DVT risk, switching away from estrogen-containing contraception toward a non-estrogen method reduces further thromboembolic risk. Discharging her from follow-up (option B) is inappropriate after a device implant. Mandatory lifelong warfarin (option C) is not standard unless another indication exists. Stating prophylaxis is never indicated (option E) is too absolute — antibiotic prophylaxis is generally advised for the first 6 months after device closure.

cases.diagnosis: Ostium secundum atrial septal defect with left-to-right shunt, RV volume overload, and paradoxical embolic stroke
pearls: Fixed splitting of S2 is the classic auscultatory hallmark of an atrial septal defect.
An ASD provides a conduit for paradoxical embolism — suspect it in a young patient with a cryptogenic stroke and a DVT risk factor.
Right heart enlargement with RV volume overload and Qp:Qs ~2:1 marks a hemodynamically significant shunt warranting closure.
Secundum defects with adequate rims are usually closed percutaneously; follow with antiplatelet therapy and short-term endocarditis prophylaxis.

cases.title: A Lifelong Murmur Turns Blue
cases.chiefComplaint: Progressive breathlessness and bluish discoloration in a woman with a known childhood heart defect.
cases.history: A 27-year-old woman presents to clinic with worsening exertional dyspnea, fatigue, and new bluish discoloration of her lips and fingers. She was diagnosed in childhood with a large ventricular septal defect that was never surgically corrected because her family declined surgery and she was later lost to follow-up. Over the past few years she has become progressively limited and now also seeks pregnancy counseling, as she and her partner hope to conceive. Medications are none; she has no drug allergies. She does not smoke, drinks rarely, and uses no drugs. She works part-time as a librarian. Family history is unremarkable. Review of systems is positive for exertional dyspnea, occasional hemoptysis, dizziness, and headaches but negative for chest pain, orthopnea, fevers, or prior thromboembolism. She has noticed her fingertips becoming rounded over the past few years. She is not currently using any contraception.
cases.exam: Comfortable at rest but with central cyanosis and prominent digital clubbing of the fingers and toes. Jugular venous pressure is elevated with a prominent a wave. There is a left parasternal (right ventricular) heave and a palpable, loud P2. Auscultation reveals a single, loud second heart sound with a markedly accentuated pulmonic component and a high-pitched early diastolic murmur of pulmonary regurgitation (Graham Steell). Notably, the previously documented loud holosystolic VSD murmur is now soft or absent. Lungs are clear. There is mild peripheral edema. No prior surgical scar is present.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: In this patient with a long-standing uncorrected VSD who is now cyanotic and clubbed, the disappearance of her previously loud holosystolic murmur most likely indicates what?
options: Spontaneous closure of the ventricular septal defect as the first step today
Equalization of right
Development of severe aortic stenosis as the first step today
Improvement in her cardiac condition as the first step today
stages.explanation: A loud holosystolic VSD murmur depends on a large pressure gradient driving left-to-right flow across the defect. In Eisenmenger physiology, chronic high pulmonary blood flow causes irreversible pulmonary vascular remodeling and a marked rise in pulmonary vascular resistance until right- and left-sided pressures equalize; the gradient — and thus the murmur — diminishes or vanishes as the shunt reverses to right-to-left, producing cyanosis and clubbing. Spontaneous VSD closure (option B) would not cause cyanosis or clubbing. Severe aortic stenosis (option D) would add a new systolic ejection murmur, not explain her hypoxemia. The change reflects worsening, not improvement (option E).

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Hgb 19.6 g/dL (secondary erythrocytosis), Hct 58%
ABG on room air: PaO2 52 mmHg; minimal rise with 100% O2
ECG: right axis deviation; right ventricular hypertrophy with strain
Chest X-ray: enlarged central pulmonary arteries with rapid peripheral 'pruning'; enlarged right heart
Echocardiogram: large unrepaired VSD with bidirectional, predominantly right-to-left shunting; severe RV hypertrophy; estimated RV systolic pressure 98 mmHg (near systemic); flattened interventricular septum

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which finding best confirms that her pulmonary hypertension has become fixed and her shunt has reversed (Eisenmenger syndrome) rather than being correctable?
options: Right-to-left/bidirectional shunting with near-systemic pulmonary pressures
A small restrictive VSD with a large gradient as the primary diagnosis
Improvement of oxygen saturation to normal with supplemental oxygen as the primary diagnosis
A left-to-right shunt with normal pulmonary pressures as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Eisenmenger syndrome is established when long-standing left-to-right shunting drives pulmonary vascular resistance up to systemic levels, causing the shunt to become bidirectional or right-to-left with resulting cyanosis; hypoxemia that fails to correct with 100% oxygen reflects fixed intracardiac right-to-left shunting (not a diffusion problem), confirming the physiology. A persisting left-to-right shunt with normal pressures (option B) and a small restrictive VSD with a large gradient (option E) describe correctable, non-Eisenmenger states. Oxygen saturation normalizing with supplemental oxygen (option D) is the opposite of the shunt physiology and would argue against fixed right-to-left shunting.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management approach now that Eisenmenger physiology is established?
options: Avoid closing the defect
High-flow oxygen alone will correct the hypoxemia and is sufficient therapy as initial therapy
Surgical closure of the VSD now to reverse the cyanosis as initial therapy
Aggressive phlebotomy to normalize the hematocrit in all cases as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Once Eisenmenger physiology is fixed, closing the defect is contraindicated: the right-to-left shunt acts as a 'pop-off' for the suprasystemic pulmonary pressures, and abolishing it precipitates acute right heart failure and death. Management is supportive — preventing dehydration and infection, judicious handling of secondary erythrocytosis (phlebotomy only for symptomatic hyperviscosity, with iron repletion, not routine normalization of hematocrit, which is option D), and consideration of pulmonary arterial hypertension-specific vasodilators (e.g., endothelin receptor antagonists, PDE-5 inhibitors) in appropriate patients. Surgical closure (option B) is harmful. Oxygen alone (option E) does not correct fixed intracardiac right-to-left shunting and is not sufficient therapy.

stages.label: PREGNANCY COUNSELING VISIT
stages.content:
She returns to discuss conception.
Resting SpO2 83%, secondary erythrocytosis persists
Echocardiogram: near-systemic RV pressures, severe RV hypertrophy, preserved RV function for now
She asks specifically about the risks of pregnancy and contraceptive options

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What is the most appropriate counseling regarding pregnancy in a woman with Eisenmenger syndrome?
options: Pregnancy carries very high maternal mortality and is strongly discouraged
She should use only estrogen-containing combined oral contraceptives with appropriate follow-up
Pregnancy risk can be eliminated by closing the VSD before conception with appropriate follow-up
Pregnancy is low-risk and she may proceed without special precautions with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Eisenmenger syndrome is associated with very high maternal mortality (historically on the order of 30-50%), so pregnancy is strongly discouraged and patients require clear counseling and reliable contraception; estrogen-containing methods are generally avoided because of thrombotic risk in an already hyperviscous, cyanotic patient, favoring progestin-only or other approaches with specialist input. Telling her pregnancy is low-risk (option B) is dangerously incorrect. Recommending estrogen-containing combined pills (option C) increases thrombotic risk in this population. Closing the VSD before conception (option E) is contraindicated in established Eisenmenger physiology and would not make pregnancy safe; it would precipitate right heart failure.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which complication is this cyanotic, erythrocytotic patient at especially increased risk of, requiring specific preventive attention?
options: Accelerated coronary atherosclerosis as the dominant risk as the leading concern
Paradoxical embolism, hyperviscosity symptoms,
Recurrent rheumatic fever as the leading concern
Hypocoagulable bleeding only, with no thrombotic risk as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Cyanotic congenital heart disease with a right-to-left shunt creates a paradoxical embolism risk (venous clots/air bubbles entering the systemic circulation), so meticulous IV air-filter precautions are essential, and patients have a complex bleeding-and-thrombosis diathesis along with hyperviscosity symptoms from secondary erythrocytosis. Iron deficiency from over-phlebotomy paradoxically worsens hyperviscosity by producing rigid microcytes, so routine phlebotomy is avoided and iron stores maintained. A purely hypocoagulable state with no thrombotic risk (option B) misstates the balanced diathesis. Accelerated coronary atherosclerosis (option D) and recurrent rheumatic fever (option E) are not the characteristic risks of Eisenmenger physiology.

cases.diagnosis: Eisenmenger syndrome from a long-standing uncorrected ventricular septal defect with reversed (right-to-left) shunt
pearls: Eisenmenger syndrome = a long-standing left-to-right shunt that drives pulmonary vascular resistance to systemic levels, reversing the shunt and causing cyanosis and clubbing.
Disappearance of a previously loud VSD murmur with a loud, single P2 signals pressure equalization and shunt reversal.
Never close the defect once Eisenmenger physiology is fixed — the shunt is a vital pressure pop-off; manage supportively with PAH vasodilators and careful erythrocytosis management.
Pregnancy carries very high maternal mortality; counsel strongly against it, avoid estrogen contraception, use air-filter precautions, and avoid routine phlebotomy/iron deficiency.

cases.title: A Marathoner Who Can No Longer Climb Stairs
cases.chiefComplaint: Progressive breathlessness, fatigue, and near-fainting over the past several months.
cases.history: A 36-year-old woman presents with several months of progressive exertional dyspnea and fatigue. Once an avid recreational runner, she now becomes breathless climbing a single flight of stairs and has had two episodes of near-syncope during exertion. She also reports exertional chest tightness and mild ankle swelling. She was initially treated for presumed asthma and then anxiety without benefit. Past medical history is unremarkable; she takes only an albuterol inhaler (with no relief) and has no drug allergies. She uses no appetite-suppressant drugs, no methamphetamine, and no other stimulants; she does not smoke and drinks rarely. Family history is notable for an aunt who died young of a 'lung-pressure' problem. Review of systems is positive for exertional lightheadedness, palpitations, and early satiety with abdominal bloating but negative for fever, orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, leg pain or swelling suggestive of DVT, joint pains, rashes, or Raynaud phenomenon. She has not been pregnant.
cases.exam: Thin woman, mildly dyspneic with exertion in the hallway. Jugular venous pressure is elevated with a prominent a wave. There is a left parasternal (right ventricular) heave. Auscultation reveals a loud, palpable P2, a right-sided S4, and a holosystolic murmur at the left lower sternal border that increases with inspiration (tricuspid regurgitation). Lungs are clear without crackles or wheezes. The liver edge is mildly enlarged and pulsatile, and there is trace-to-1+ peripheral edema. There is no clubbing, no cyanosis at rest, no rash, and no synovitis. Distal pulses are normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A young woman with progressive dyspnea, exertional near-syncope, a loud P2, and an RV heave should undergo which initial test to screen for the suspected condition?
options: Transthoracic echocardiography
Coronary angiography in this clinical context
Cardiac stress test on a treadmill as the first step
High-resolution chest CT only
stages.explanation: Her findings (loud P2, RV heave, signs of right heart strain, exertional syncope) suggest pulmonary hypertension, and transthoracic echocardiography is the appropriate noninvasive screening test: it estimates pulmonary artery systolic pressure, assesses RV size and function, and excludes left heart disease and structural causes. Coronary angiography (option C) is not the screening test for pulmonary hypertension in a 36-year-old without coronary risk. Chest CT alone (option D) helps evaluate parenchymal lung disease and chronic thromboembolic disease but is not the first screen for the cardiac picture. An unsupervised treadmill stress test first (option E) is risky given her exertional near-syncope and does not characterize the pulmonary circulation.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Echocardiogram: estimated RV systolic pressure 78 mmHg; dilated, hypertrophied right ventricle with reduced function; flattened septum (D-shaped LV); normal LV size and function; no significant left-sided valve disease; small pericardial effusion
ECG: right axis deviation, RV hypertrophy with strain, P pulmonale
NT-proBNP: 1,980 pg/mL
Chest X-ray: enlarged central pulmonary arteries; clear lung fields
V/Q scan: normal (no segmental perfusion defects)
PFTs: mild restriction with reduced DLCO; ANA negative; HIV negative

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: After echocardiography suggests pulmonary hypertension, which test is required to confirm the diagnosis and define the hemodynamic subtype?
options: Repeat transthoracic echocardiography in 6 months
Right heart catheterization
Cardiac MRI as the primary diagnosis
Six-minute walk test
stages.explanation: Right heart catheterization is mandatory to confirm pulmonary hypertension and define its hemodynamic profile — measuring mean pulmonary artery pressure, pulmonary capillary wedge pressure, and pulmonary vascular resistance — to distinguish precapillary (e.g., pulmonary arterial hypertension, with normal wedge pressure and elevated PVR) from postcapillary disease. It also allows acute vasoreactivity testing. Repeat echocardiography (option B) only estimates pressures and cannot confirm the diagnosis or subtype. Cardiac MRI (option D) assesses RV structure/function but does not measure intracardiac pressures or wedge pressure. A six-minute walk test (option E) is a functional/prognostic measure, not a diagnostic confirmation of the hemodynamic category.

stages.label: RIGHT HEART CATHETERIZATION
stages.content:
Mean pulmonary artery pressure: 52 mmHg (normal ≤20)
Pulmonary capillary wedge pressure: 9 mmHg (normal)
Pulmonary vascular resistance: 9 Wood units (markedly elevated)
Cardiac index: 2.0 L/min/m²
Acute vasoreactivity testing with inhaled nitric oxide: negative (non-responder)
Workup negative for connective tissue disease, HIV, chronic thromboembolic disease, portal hypertension, and drug exposures

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: These catheterization values (mean PAP 52, wedge 9, PVR 9 Wood units) with a negative secondary workup are most consistent with which diagnosis?
options: Pulmonary hypertension due to chronic lung disease (Group 3)
Postcapillary pulmonary hypertension from left heart failure
Pulmonary hypertension due to chronic thromboembolic disease (Group 4)
Precapillary pulmonary arterial hypertension, here idiopathic (Group 1)
stages.explanation: An elevated mean PAP with a normal wedge pressure (≤15 mmHg) and markedly elevated pulmonary vascular resistance defines precapillary pulmonary hypertension; with secondary causes excluded (connective tissue disease, HIV, drugs/toxins, portal hypertension, congenital shunts), and a normal V/Q scan ruling out chronic thromboembolic disease, this is idiopathic pulmonary arterial hypertension (Group 1). Postcapillary PH from left heart failure (option B) would show an elevated wedge pressure, which she does not have. Chronic thromboembolic PH (option D) is excluded by the normal V/Q scan. Group 3 PH from lung disease (option E) is not supported by her clear lungs and only mild PFT changes; the hemodynamics and clean workup point to idiopathic PAH.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She is a vasoreactivity non-responder with high-risk features (syncope, markedly elevated PVR, low cardiac index, pericardial effusion). What is the most appropriate initial therapy?
options: Beta-blockers and a loop diuretic alone as initial therapy
Supplemental oxygen alone with reassurance as initial therapy
High-dose calcium channel blocker therapy as initial therapy
Upfront combination pulmonary arterial hypertension-specific therapy , with consideration of a prostacyclin pathway agent
stages.explanation: Because she is a vasoreactivity non-responder with high-risk features, calcium channel blockers are inappropriate (they help only the small subset of acute responders) — option B is therefore wrong and potentially harmful. Guideline-directed care for newly diagnosed PAH, especially intermediate-to-high risk, is upfront combination therapy targeting the endothelin and nitric oxide pathways (endothelin receptor antagonist plus PDE-5 inhibitor), with early addition of a prostacyclin-pathway agent for high-risk disease and referral to an expert PH center. Beta-blockers with a diuretic alone (option D) do not treat the pulmonary vasculopathy and beta-blockers may impair the RV. Oxygen with reassurance (option E) is inadequate for confirmed high-risk PAH.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which additional counseling and monitoring measure is most appropriate for this patient with newly diagnosed idiopathic PAH?
options: Encourage pregnancy soon, since PAH improves during gestation with appropriate follow-up
Plan to stop all therapy once symptoms transiently improve with appropriate follow-up
Recommend high-intensity competitive athletics to build cardiovascular reserve with appropriate follow-up
Strongly advise against pregnancy
stages.explanation: Pregnancy carries very high maternal mortality in pulmonary arterial hypertension and is strongly discouraged, so effective contraception and counseling are essential; routine vaccination (influenza, pneumococcus) reduces decompensation from respiratory infection, and patients are followed with serial multiparameter risk stratification (WHO functional class, 6-minute walk distance, NT-proBNP, and echo/right heart catheterization) to escalate therapy and assess transplant candidacy. Encouraging pregnancy (option B) is dangerous and false. High-intensity competitive athletics (option D) is contraindicated given exertional syncope and severe PAH. Stopping therapy after transient improvement (option E) leads to rapid clinical deterioration in this progressive disease.

cases.diagnosis: Idiopathic pulmonary arterial hypertension (Group 1, precapillary) confirmed by right heart catheterization
pearls: Suspect pulmonary hypertension with progressive dyspnea, exertional syncope, a loud P2, an RV heave, and signs of right heart strain.
Echocardiography screens, but right heart catheterization is required to confirm PH and separate precapillary (normal wedge, high PVR) from postcapillary disease.
Idiopathic PAH (Group 1) is a diagnosis of exclusion: rule out left heart disease, lung disease, chronic thromboembolic disease, connective tissue disease, HIV, drugs, and portal hypertension.

Only acute vasoreactivity responders benefit from calcium channel blockers; non-responders/high-risk patients need PAH-specific combination therapy at a PH center, and pregnancy is strongly discouraged.
